title: A radiologist is training residents on systematic knee MRI reading. Over the first 50 cases, residents complete the full 7-step search pattern on every study. Which outcome best demonstrates successful integration of the pattern into clinical workflow?
text: A radiologist is training residents on systematic knee MRI reading. Over the first 50 cases, residents complete the full 7-step search pattern on every study. Which outcome best demonstrates successful integration of the pattern into clinical workflow? | A. The residents should restrict the search pattern to the clinical question to save time after the first 20 cases. | B. Residents will continue to feel the pattern is slow for the first 100 cases and should expect no improvement in speed until they have completed 200+ studies. | C. Once the pattern is memorized, residents can skip any step if they find an obvious abnormality, since the clinical question has been answered. | D. By cases 40-50, residents complete the search pattern in 8-10 minutes with no increase in errors; the pattern has become automatic and reading speed actually increases because they no longer waste time deciding what to evaluate next. | Correct: D | Systematic search patterns require deliberate practice (50-100 cases) to become automatic. Speed increases with practice as decision-making time is eliminated — the pattern is not a permanent burden but a path to efficiency. Template-driven reporting reinforces the pattern by aligning the reading workflow with the report structure.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When setting up anchor slices for knee MRI reading, which combination of three anchor slices provides the fastest orientation and most reliable navigation for a complete systematic search?
text: When setting up anchor slices for knee MRI reading, which combination of three anchor slices provides the fastest orientation and most reliable navigation for a complete systematic search? | A. Sagittal image through the midline intercondylar notch (to evaluate cruciates and navigate medially/laterally for menisci), coronal mid-condylar image (to see meniscal bodies, collateral ligaments, and alignment), and axial image through the patellofemoral joint (to evaluate patellar articular surface, trochlea, and MPFL). | B. The three anchor slices should always be the most lateral sagittal slice, the posterior coronal slice, and the most superior axial slice, as these show the most pathology. | C. A single anchor slice (sagittal midline) is sufficient if the radiologist has enough experience; multiple anchor slices slow down reading and should be avoided after the first month of training. | D. Anchor slices should be selected differently for each case based on the clinical question, rather than remaining consistent, to adapt the search to the specific pathology being evaluated. | Correct: A | Anchor slices are fixed reference points that remain consistent across every case. Starting at the same anchor slices on every study accelerates orientation and prevents disorientation mid-read. Within weeks of consistent anchor-slice use, readers can rapidly assess whether a case is grossly abnormal just from the appearance of the anchor slice itself. Consistency is the key to speed and reliability.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 22-year-old soccer player sustains a noncontact pivot injury. MRI shows bone marrow edema at the lateral femoral condyle and the posterolateral tibial plateau. Which injury mechanism does this contusion pattern indicate?
text: A 22-year-old soccer player sustains a noncontact pivot injury. MRI shows bone marrow edema at the lateral femoral condyle and the posterolateral tibial plateau. Which injury mechanism does this contusion pattern indicate? | A. Direct blow to the anterior knee (dashboard injury) | B. Lateral patellar dislocation | C. Hyperextension injury | D. Pivot-shift mechanism with likely ACL injury | Correct: D | The combination of lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial plateau marrow edema is the classic pivot-shift contusion pattern. It results from transient anterior subluxation of the lateral tibial compartment and is strongly associated with ACL tears. When you see this pattern, carefully evaluate the ACL and associated meniscal structures.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 35-year-old presents after a motor vehicle accident with a knee-to-dashboard impact. MRI shows marrow edema in the anterior proximal tibia and a large joint effusion. What associated ligamentous injury should you specifically evaluate for?
text: A 35-year-old presents after a motor vehicle accident with a knee-to-dashboard impact. MRI shows marrow edema in the anterior proximal tibia and a large joint effusion. What associated ligamentous injury should you specifically evaluate for? | A. ACL tear | B. PCL tear | C. Lateral collateral ligament tear | D. Patellar tendon rupture | Correct: B | A dashboard injury drives a posteriorly directed force on the flexed proximal tibia, producing a bone contusion at the ANTERIOR proximal tibia (the impact site). This pattern is strongly associated with PCL injury, so the PCL should be carefully evaluated for partial or complete disruption, and the posterolateral corner should also be assessed.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 16-year-old presents after a first-time lateral patellar dislocation. Which bone marrow edema pattern would you expect on MRI?
text: A 16-year-old presents after a first-time lateral patellar dislocation. Which bone marrow edema pattern would you expect on MRI? | A. Lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial plateau | B. Medial patellar facet and anterior lateral femoral condyle | C. Medial and lateral tibial plateaus symmetrically | D. Posterior femoral condyles bilaterally | Correct: B | Lateral patellar dislocation produces a characteristic kissing contusion pattern: paired edema at the medial patellar facet and the anterolateral femoral condyle, produced as the medial patella impacts the condyle during relocation. When this pattern is identified, evaluate for MPFL injury, osteochondral fracture, and loose bodies.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 60-year-old female presents with acute-onset medial knee pain without trauma. MRI shows a low-signal subchondral lesion on T1 with surrounding marrow edema in the medial femoral condyle weight-bearing surface. The overlying articular cartilage is intact. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: A 60-year-old female presents with acute-onset medial knee pain without trauma. MRI shows a low-signal subchondral lesion on T1 with surrounding marrow edema in the medial femoral condyle weight-bearing surface. The overlying articular cartilage is intact. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) | B. Avascular necrosis (AVN) | C. Stress fracture of the femoral condyle | D. Spontaneous insufficiency fracture of the knee (SIFK, formerly SONK) | Correct: D | SIFK (subchondral insufficiency fracture of the knee, previously called SONK) typically presents in older patients with acute onset pain, localized to the weight-bearing surface of the medial femoral condyle with surrounding marrow edema. OCD occurs in younger patients and AVN typically involves a larger geographic area. The subchondral location and clinical presentation are the key distinguishing features.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 45-year-old woman with osteoporosis presents with acute medial knee pain after minor trauma. MRI shows a band of low signal on T1 running through the medial tibial plateau with surrounding marrow edema. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what distinguishes it from a fatigue (stress) fracture?
text: A 45-year-old woman with osteoporosis presents with acute medial knee pain after minor trauma. MRI shows a band of low signal on T1 running through the medial tibial plateau with surrounding marrow edema. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what distinguishes it from a fatigue (stress) fracture? | A. Insufficiency fracture. Insufficiency fractures occur in weakened bone (e.g., osteoporosis) under normal physiologic loading, whereas fatigue fractures occur in normal bone under abnormal repetitive loading. Both appear as a discrete linear low-T1 line within surrounding marrow edema; the distinction is made clinically by bone quality, age, and mechanism, with the osteoporotic history being the key clue here. | B. Fatigue (stress) fracture, which occurs in athletes or military recruits with normal bone density subjected to repetitive abnormal loading and presents with gradual, activity-related onset rather than acute pain after minor trauma. | C. Bone contusion, which shows diffuse reticular marrow edema but lacks the discrete linear low-T1 fracture line, and therefore does not change weight-bearing status. | D. Degenerative joint disease with osteophytes, which produces cartilage loss, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophytes rather than a discrete linear fracture line within marrow edema. | Correct: A | T1 is essential for fracture detection: a discrete low-signal line within marrow edema indicates a fracture (alters weight-bearing status), whereas diffuse edema without a line suggests contusion. Insufficiency and fatigue fractures are both stress fractures; they are distinguished by bone quality and mechanism—normal load in weak (osteoporotic) bone versus repetitive abnormal load in normal bone.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 68-year-old man with knee pain undergoes MRI. Coronal images show near-complete cartilage loss in the medial compartment with subchondral sclerosis and prominent osteophytes, while the lateral compartment cartilage appears relatively preserved. What does this compartmental pattern indicate, and what weight-bearing recommendation would you make?
text: A 68-year-old man with knee pain undergoes MRI. Coronal images show near-complete cartilage loss in the medial compartment with subchondral sclerosis and prominent osteophytes, while the lateral compartment cartilage appears relatively preserved. What does this compartmental pattern indicate, and what weight-bearing recommendation would you make? | A. Valgus malalignment, which would show the opposite pattern: lateral compartment cartilage loss with medial preservation and lateral osteophytes. | B. This pattern is consistent with varus-aligned (medial-compartment-overloaded) knee. The concentrated medial cartilage loss and subchondral sclerosis indicate chronic mechanical overload of the medial compartment. Recommend weight-bearing radiographs to quantify the varus degree, and note in the report that findings are compatible with varus alignment and medial-compartment osteoarthritis—this influences surgical decision-making (unicompartmental vs. total arthroplasty). | C. Normal neutral alignment, which would show symmetric cartilage loss and changes across all three compartments rather than compartment-specific overload. | D. Secondary to a meniscal root tear, which would present with meniscal extrusion and acute subchondral insufficiency fracture rather than chronic degenerative changes and osteophytes. | Correct: B | MRI is not reliable for precise alignment angles—weight-bearing radiographs are the gold standard. However, coronal MRI reveals the CONSEQUENCES of malalignment: varus overload concentrates cartilage loss, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophytes in one compartment (usually medial); valgus concentrates them on the lateral side. Describing the compartmental pattern in your report guides the surgeon toward appropriate procedure selection (unicompartmental vs. total) without attempting angle measurement.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You identify bone marrow edema in the knee on a sagittal PD FS image. Before assigning a diagnosis, you ask several questions about the edema to direct your search for associated injuries. Which question is LEAST useful for directing those next diagnostic steps?
text: You identify bone marrow edema in the knee on a sagittal PD FS image. Before assigning a diagnosis, you ask several questions about the edema to direct your search for associated injuries. Which question is LEAST useful for directing those next diagnostic steps? | A. Where is the edema located? (e.g., posterolateral tibial plateau + lateral femoral condyle edema points to a pivot-shift mechanism, prompting a focused search of the ACL and lateral meniscus). | B. Is the edema on one side of the joint or paired on both opposing surfaces? Paired (kissing) edema implies direct impaction of the two surfaces and should prompt assessment of the apposed bone, cartilage, and adjacent ligaments. | C. What are the patient's age and occupation? These inform baseline risk and pretest probability but do not localize the lesion or redirect the targeted search for associated internal derangement. | D. Does the edema pattern match a recognized injury mechanism (pivot-shift, dashboard, transient patellar dislocation)? This tells you which specific ligaments and structures to scrutinize. | Correct: C | The location, distribution, and mechanism-pattern of bone marrow edema are powerful clues that direct a targeted search for ligament, meniscal, and cartilage injury. Age and occupation refine pretest probability but do not localize the lesion or change the systematic anatomic search, making that question least useful here.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On MRI, you identify a focal area of articular cartilage abnormality in the medial femoral condyle. The cartilage shows an area of signal change that extends deeper than 50% of cartilage thickness but does not reach the subchondral bone. Using the modified ICRS grading system, this lesion is best classified as:
text: On MRI, you identify a focal area of articular cartilage abnormality in the medial femoral condyle. The cartilage shows an area of signal change that extends deeper than 50% of cartilage thickness but does not reach the subchondral bone. Using the modified ICRS grading system, this lesion is best classified as: | A. Grade 1 — superficial softening or fibrillation | B. Grade 2 — defect less than 50% of cartilage thickness | C. Grade 3 — defect greater than 50% of cartilage thickness without full-thickness loss | D. Grade 4 — full-thickness cartilage loss with exposed subchondral bone | Correct: C | ICRS Grade 3 describes a cartilage defect that extends deeper than 50% of the cartilage thickness but does not expose subchondral bone. Accurately grading cartilage lesions helps guide surgical decision-making, as higher-grade lesions may benefit from restorative procedures.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 17-year-old basketball player has an OCD lesion of the medial femoral condyle. Which MRI finding most strongly suggests the fragment is unstable?
text: A 17-year-old basketball player has an OCD lesion of the medial femoral condyle. Which MRI finding most strongly suggests the fragment is unstable? | A. High T2 signal (fluid) between the fragment and parent bone, or a cyst-like lesion at the interface | B. Low signal on T1 images within the lesion | C. The lesion is located on the weight-bearing surface | D. Mild surrounding marrow edema | Correct: A | High T2 signal (fluid) interposed between the OCD fragment and the parent bone, or cyst-like lesions at the interface, strongly suggest fragment instability. Other signs of instability include a displaced fragment and a breach in the overlying articular cartilage. Instability is a key factor in determining surgical vs. conservative management.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A subchondral bone lesion is identified in the knee of a 14-year-old athlete at the classic location on the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. In a 55-year-old with a similar-appearing lesion on the weight-bearing surface of the medial femoral condyle, the leading diagnosis would change to:
text: A subchondral bone lesion is identified in the knee of a 14-year-old athlete at the classic location on the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. In a 55-year-old with a similar-appearing lesion on the weight-bearing surface of the medial femoral condyle, the leading diagnosis would change to: | A. OCD in both cases — age does not influence the differential | B. Stress fracture in the older patient | C. SIFK (subchondral insufficiency fracture) in the older patient | D. AVN in the adolescent | Correct: C | Age and location are critical for differentiating subchondral lesions. OCD typically occurs in adolescents/young adults at the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. SIFK occurs in older patients (typically >55 years) at the weight-bearing surface. Recognizing this age-location pattern prevents misdiagnosis and guides appropriate management.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On MRI of the knee, you identify a well-defined low-signal structure within the joint that is separate from all articular surfaces, seen within the suprapatellar recess. It demonstrates peripheral low signal on all sequences. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: On MRI of the knee, you identify a well-defined low-signal structure within the joint that is separate from all articular surfaces, seen within the suprapatellar recess. It demonstrates peripheral low signal on all sequences. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Meniscal ossicle | B. Loose body (osteochondral fragment) | C. Synovial chondromatosis | D. Fabella | Correct: B | A well-defined structure separate from articular surfaces within the joint space, particularly within a recess, is consistent with an intra-articular loose body. Loose bodies can arise from osteochondral fractures, OCD, or synovial chondromatosis. Reporting their number, size, and location is important as they can cause mechanical symptoms and cartilage damage.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the classic location of knee osteochondritis dissecans (OCD), and which feature most drives surgical decision-making?
text: What is the classic location of knee osteochondritis dissecans (OCD), and which feature most drives surgical decision-making? | A. Patellar median ridge; lesion size | B. Lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle (~70–80%); fragment stability | C. Central weight-bearing lateral femoral condyle; patient age | D. Tibial plateau; presence of effusion | Correct: B | The classic OCD location is the lateral aspect of the MEDIAL femoral condyle (~70–80% of knee OCD). Size and location are reported, but fragment STABILITY is the key determination driving management — a stable lesion may be treated conservatively (especially with open physes), whereas an unstable fragment usually requires surgery.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 13-year-old and a 35-year-old each have a femoral-condyle OCD lesion. Which single imaging check best identifies the better-prognosis (juvenile) form?
text: A 13-year-old and a 35-year-old each have a femoral-condyle OCD lesion. Which single imaging check best identifies the better-prognosis (juvenile) form? | A. Lesion location on the patella | B. Status of the distal femoral physis — open physes = juvenile OCD, with greater healing potential and more frequent conservative success | C. Presence of a joint effusion | D. Signal intensity of the overlying cartilage | Correct: B | Check the distal femoral physis on coronal images: OPEN physes define juvenile OCD, which has substantially better healing potential and is more often managed conservatively; closed physes (adult OCD) heal poorly and more often need surgery. Caveat: in skeletally immature patients a high-T2 rim alone is unreliable for instability (it can be vascular granulation in a stable lesion) — it counts only if it matches fluid signal or a second sign is present.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal PD images, you see increased signal within the posterior horn of the medial meniscus that does not extend to an articular surface. How is this signal classified, and what is the clinical significance?
text: On sagittal PD images, you see increased signal within the posterior horn of the medial meniscus that does not extend to an articular surface. How is this signal classified, and what is the clinical significance? | A. Grade 3 signal — represents a definite meniscal tear | B. Grade 1 signal — always represents an acute traumatic injury | C. Grade 2 signal — requires arthroscopic confirmation of a tear | D. Grade 2 signal — intrameniscal signal without articular surface extension; does not represent a tear | Correct: D | Grade 2 meniscal signal represents linear intrameniscal signal that does not reach any articular surface. It typically represents intrasubstance degeneration, not a true tear. Only grade 3 signal, which unequivocally contacts an articular surface, meets MRI criteria for a meniscal tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When evaluating the meniscus for tears on sagittal MRI, the "two-slice-touch" rule is applied. What does this rule state?
text: When evaluating the meniscus for tears on sagittal MRI, the "two-slice-touch" rule is applied. What does this rule state? | A. A tear is present if abnormal signal is seen on at least two consecutive sagittal images | B. The meniscus must be visible on at least two sagittal slices to be considered normal | C. Grade 3 signal must contact an articular surface on at least two consecutive images to confidently diagnose a tear | D. The meniscal body should be seen on exactly two sagittal bow-tie images | Correct: C | The two-slice-touch rule states that to confidently diagnose a meniscal tear, grade 3 signal (contacting an articular surface) should be identified on at least two consecutive sagittal images. This rule improves specificity and helps avoid false-positive diagnoses from volume averaging or magic angle artifact on a single slice.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A coronal PD FS image shows a linear high signal extending from the inner free edge of the lateral meniscus body perpendicular to the long axis of the meniscus toward the periphery. What type of tear does this represent?
text: A coronal PD FS image shows a linear high signal extending from the inner free edge of the lateral meniscus body perpendicular to the long axis of the meniscus toward the periphery. What type of tear does this represent? | A. Horizontal tear | B. Radial tear | C. Longitudinal-vertical tear | D. Complex tear | Correct: B | A radial tear extends perpendicular to the long axis (circumference) of the meniscus from the free edge toward the periphery. On the coronal or sagittal image where the tear is in-plane, it appears as a linear cleft. Radial tears are biomechanically significant because they disrupt the circumferential collagen fibers that generate hoop stress.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A sagittal MRI shows the "ghost meniscus" sign at the expected location of the medial meniscus posterior root, and the coronal shows meniscal extrusion greater than 3 mm over the medial tibial plateau. Which diagnosis should you strongly consider?
text: A sagittal MRI shows the "ghost meniscus" sign at the expected location of the medial meniscus posterior root, and the coronal shows meniscal extrusion greater than 3 mm over the medial tibial plateau. Which diagnosis should you strongly consider? | A. Medial meniscus posterior root tear | B. Peripheral longitudinal tear of the medial meniscus | C. Normal variant of meniscal morphology | D. Discoid medial meniscus | Correct: A | The ghost meniscus sign (absent meniscal tissue at the root attachment site on sagittal images) combined with meniscal extrusion greater than 3 mm is highly suggestive of a meniscal root tear. Root tears destroy hoop stress and are functionally equivalent to a total meniscectomy, making them a critical finding to identify and report.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You are reviewing a knee MRI and notice only one bow-tie image on the sagittal series where you would normally expect two for the meniscal body. On a coronal image, you see a low-signal band parallel and inferior to the PCL. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: You are reviewing a knee MRI and notice only one bow-tie image on the sagittal series where you would normally expect two for the meniscal body. On a coronal image, you see a low-signal band parallel and inferior to the PCL. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Normal meniscal variant | B. Radial tear of the meniscal body | C. Bucket-handle tear with a displaced fragment (double PCL sign) | D. Discoid meniscus with a horizontal tear | Correct: C | The absent bow-tie sign (only one bow-tie image instead of the expected two) indicates that a meniscal fragment has been displaced. The double PCL sign — a low-signal band in the intercondylar notch parallel to the PCL — represents the displaced inner fragment of a bucket-handle tear. These two signs together are classic for a bucket-handle meniscal tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a knee MRI showing an ACL tear, which easily-missed meniscal injury must you specifically search for at the posterior meniscocapsular junction of the medial meniscus?
text: On a knee MRI showing an ACL tear, which easily-missed meniscal injury must you specifically search for at the posterior meniscocapsular junction of the medial meniscus? | A. Bucket-handle tear of the lateral meniscus | B. Ramp lesion | C. Radial tear of the lateral meniscus posterior root | D. Discoid meniscus | Correct: B | A ramp lesion — a peripheral longitudinal tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus at the meniscocapsular junction — is strongly associated with ACL tears (≈16–24% of ACL-deficient knees) and is notoriously underdiagnosed on MRI (sensitivity ~48–65%). In every ACL case, scrutinize the posteromedial meniscocapsular junction on sagittal and axial fluid-sensitive sequences; it can be repaired at the time of ACL reconstruction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscal extrusion is measured on the coronal mid-body image. Which threshold is pathologic, and what associated tear should it prompt you to search for?
text: Meniscal extrusion is measured on the coronal mid-body image. Which threshold is pathologic, and what associated tear should it prompt you to search for? | A. >1 mm; bucket-handle tear | B. >3 mm; meniscal root tear | C. >8 mm; horizontal cleavage tear | D. Any extrusion; discoid meniscus | Correct: B | Extrusion >3 mm beyond the tibial plateau margin (coronal mid-body slice, reference line at the tibial margin) is pathologic; >5 mm is severe. Significant extrusion should trigger a careful search for a meniscal ROOT tear, which abolishes hoop-stress and is functionally equivalent to a total meniscectomy. Medial extrusion is more clinically significant than lateral.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A parameniscal cyst is identified adjacent to the meniscus. What is it almost always associated with, and what is the treatment implication?
text: A parameniscal cyst is identified adjacent to the meniscus. What is it almost always associated with, and what is the treatment implication? | A. An intact meniscus; the cyst is incidental | B. An underlying (usually horizontal) meniscal tear acting as a one-way valve; the cyst recurs if the tear is not addressed | C. An ACL tear; the cyst resolves after ACL reconstruction | D. Chondrocalcinosis; no treatment needed | Correct: B | Parameniscal cysts almost always communicate with an underlying meniscal tear (typically horizontal/horizontal-cleavage) via a one-way-valve mechanism that pumps joint fluid into the parameniscal soft tissues. Decompression alone is insufficient — the meniscal tear must be addressed or the cyst recurs. A lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head can compress the common peroneal nerve.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On MRI after meniscal repair, which finding most reliably indicates a RE-TEAR rather than expected post-operative healing signal?
text: On MRI after meniscal repair, which finding most reliably indicates a RE-TEAR rather than expected post-operative healing signal? | A. Any intrameniscal signal at the repair site | B. Fluid-bright (T2) signal extending through the full thickness of the repair to the articular surface, or contrast/fluid tracking into the tear on MR arthrography | C. Mild intermediate signal that does not reach a surface | D. A small joint effusion | Correct: B | Repaired menisci normally retain INTERMEDIATE (granulation) signal at the repair site for months to years, so intermediate signal alone does not indicate re-tear. A re-tear is shown by FLUID-BRIGHT (T2) signal extending through the repair to the surface, or by contrast/fluid tracking into the tear on MR arthrography — the most reliable test in the post-operative meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You identify discontinuity and abnormal signal of the ACL on sagittal images. Which of the following secondary signs on MRI would most strongly support the diagnosis of an ACL tear?
text: You identify discontinuity and abnormal signal of the ACL on sagittal images. Which of the following secondary signs on MRI would most strongly support the diagnosis of an ACL tear? | A. Isolated medial tibial plateau marrow edema | B. Isolated prepatellar bursitis | C. Patellar alta | D. Anterior tibial translation relative to the femur, deep lateral femoral notch sign, and Segond fracture | Correct: D | Secondary signs of ACL tear include anterior tibial translation (>5 mm), a deep lateral femoral sulcus (notch sign >1.5 mm), uncovering of the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus, Segond fracture (lateral capsular avulsion), and the pivot-shift contusion pattern. These findings increase diagnostic confidence when the ACL is difficult to evaluate directly.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI, the PCL demonstrates diffuse thickening and increased intrasubstance signal on PD FS but maintains some residual intact fibers without complete discontinuity. How would you characterize this injury?
text: On sagittal MRI, the PCL demonstrates diffuse thickening and increased intrasubstance signal on PD FS but maintains some residual intact fibers without complete discontinuity. How would you characterize this injury? | A. Normal PCL | B. Complete PCL tear (grade 3) | C. Partial PCL tear (grade 1-2) | D. PCL mucoid degeneration | Correct: C | A thickened PCL with increased intrasubstance signal but residual intact fibers represents a partial (incomplete) PCL tear. A complete tear shows full discontinuity or a diffusely amorphous, edematous ligament. PCL mucoid degeneration also causes thickening and increased signal but typically occurs without an acute injury history and has a characteristic striated pattern.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A skier presents after a valgus injury. MRI shows high signal and thickening of the MCL superficial fibers near the femoral origin with surrounding soft-tissue edema. The deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) is intact. What grade MCL injury is this?
text: A skier presents after a valgus injury. MRI shows high signal and thickening of the MCL superficial fibers near the femoral origin with surrounding soft-tissue edema. The deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) is intact. What grade MCL injury is this? | A. Grade 1 — mild sprain with intact fibers | B. Grade 2 — partial tear with some disrupted fibers but the ligament is not completely torn | C. Grade 3 — complete disruption of both superficial and deep MCL | D. Normal MCL with adjacent soft-tissue contusion | Correct: B | MCL Grade 2 injuries show partial disruption of fibers with thickening and high signal within the ligament on fluid-sensitive sequences, but some fibers remain intact. Grade 1 shows edema around an intact ligament; Grade 3 shows complete disruption. The location of injury (femoral, tibial, or mid-substance) should also be reported as it influences surgical planning.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A patient sustains a combined varus and external rotation injury. On MRI, you identify tears of the fibular collateral ligament (LCL), popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament. This constellation of findings represents injury to which anatomic structure?
text: A patient sustains a combined varus and external rotation injury. On MRI, you identify tears of the fibular collateral ligament (LCL), popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament. This constellation of findings represents injury to which anatomic structure? | A. Medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) complex | B. Posterolateral corner (PLC) | C. Posteromedial corner | D. Iliotibial band complex | Correct: B | The posterolateral corner (PLC) consists of three main structures: the fibular (lateral) collateral ligament, the popliteus tendon, and the popliteofibular ligament. PLC injuries are frequently associated with cruciate ligament tears and, if unrecognized, lead to persistent rotational instability and failure of cruciate ligament reconstruction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Following a first-time lateral patellar dislocation, which ligamentous structure is most commonly injured and should be specifically evaluated on axial MRI?
text: Following a first-time lateral patellar dislocation, which ligamentous structure is most commonly injured and should be specifically evaluated on axial MRI? | A. Medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) | B. Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) | C. Lateral collateral ligament (LCL) | D. Posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) | Correct: A | The MPFL is the primary restraint to lateral patellar translation and is torn in over 90% of acute lateral patellar dislocations. It is best evaluated on axial images, and the location of the tear (femoral, mid-substance, or patellar) should be reported as it influences surgical repair strategy.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: An ACL shows diffuse increased intrasubstance signal and is thickened, but its fibers remain CONTINUOUS and parallel to Blumensaat's line, with no secondary signs of instability. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: An ACL shows diffuse increased intrasubstance signal and is thickened, but its fibers remain CONTINUOUS and parallel to Blumensaat's line, with no secondary signs of instability. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Acute complete ACL tear | B. Mucoid degeneration of the ACL ("celery stalk" appearance) | C. Acute partial ACL tear with instability | D. ACL graft re-tear | Correct: B | Mucoid degeneration thickens the ACL with diffuse increased T2 signal between INTACT, continuous fibers — the 'celery stalk' appearance — often with associated intraosseous ganglion cysts at the tibial attachment. The key is preserved fiber continuity and the ABSENCE of secondary signs (no anterior tibial translation, deep notch, or pivot-shift contusions). Do not over-call it as a tear; patients are typically stable with mechanical/extension-loss symptoms.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When evaluating an ACL reconstruction graft, malposition of which tunnel most specifically predicts persistent rotational (pivot-shift) instability?
text: When evaluating an ACL reconstruction graft, malposition of which tunnel most specifically predicts persistent rotational (pivot-shift) instability? | A. A vertical / too-anterior FEMORAL tunnel | B. A posterior femoral tunnel | C. A lateral meniscal tunnel | D. The patellar donor site | Correct: A | A vertical or too-anterior FEMORAL tunnel produces a vertically-oriented graft that controls anterior translation but fails to resist rotation — the classic cause of a persistent pivot shift despite an intact-appearing graft. On sagittal MRI the femoral tunnel should sit in the posterior aspect of the notch. (An anterior TIBIAL tunnel instead causes roof/notch impingement of the graft in extension.) Tunnel malposition predicts failure even when the graft fibers look continuous.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: In a patient with combined ACL + MCL injury, why must you specifically evaluate the posteromedial corner (posterior oblique ligament, semimembranosus expansions)?
text: In a patient with combined ACL + MCL injury, why must you specifically evaluate the posteromedial corner (posterior oblique ligament, semimembranosus expansions)? | A. It is the donor site for ACL reconstruction | B. Combined ACL + MCL + PMC injury produces anteromedial rotatory instability that may need PMC repair in addition to ACL surgery | C. The PMC is the primary restraint to anterior translation | D. PMC injury is purely incidental and does not affect management | Correct: B | The posteromedial corner (posterior oblique ligament + semimembranosus expansions) is a key rotational stabilizer. Combined ACL + MCL + PMC injury creates ANTEROMEDIAL rotatory instability; an unrecognized PMC injury is a cause of residual instability/graft failure and may require PMC repair or reconstruction alongside the ACL. Always scrutinize the PMC when you see an MCL tear with an ACL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A high-energy knee injury shows tears of BOTH cruciates plus a posterolateral corner injury (knee-dislocation pattern). Beyond the ligaments, which structure must be specifically assessed?
text: A high-energy knee injury shows tears of BOTH cruciates plus a posterolateral corner injury (knee-dislocation pattern). Beyond the ligaments, which structure must be specifically assessed? | A. The popliteal artery (and common peroneal nerve) | B. The anterior tibial recurrent artery | C. The saphenous vein | D. The descending genicular artery | Correct: A | A multiligament knee injury (bicruciate ± corner = knee dislocation, even if spontaneously reduced) carries a significant risk of POPLITEAL ARTERY injury (intimal tear, occlusion, transection) and, with PLC injuries, common peroneal nerve injury. Always assess the popliteal vessels (MRI flow voids / CT or conventional angiography) and the peroneal nerve — a missed vascular injury threatens the limb. Report all torn ligaments, the involved corner(s), and the neurovascular status.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI, the quadriceps tendon appears as a trilaminar structure at the superior pole of the patella. Which layer is most often involved in a PARTIAL tear, and why can it be clinically subtle?
text: On sagittal MRI, the quadriceps tendon appears as a trilaminar structure at the superior pole of the patella. Which layer is most often involved in a PARTIAL tear, and why can it be clinically subtle? | A. The superficial layer (rectus femoris); the deep fibers are typically spared | B. The deep layer (vastus intermedius); the intact superficial fibers can preserve active extension, making it deceptively normal on exam | C. The middle layer (vastus medialis/lateralis); the other layers are never involved | D. The tendon is a single homogeneous layer, so tears occur uniformly | Correct: B | The quadriceps tendon is trilaminar: superficial (rectus femoris), middle (vastus medialis/lateralis), and deep (vastus intermedius). Partial tears most commonly involve the deep (vastus intermedius) layer near the patellar insertion. Because the robust superficial rectus femoris fibers often remain intact, the patient may still actively extend the knee, making the injury deceptively subtle — scrutinize the deep aspect of the tendon on sagittal PD/PD-FS images.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 40-year-old male presents with inability to extend the knee after jumping. MRI shows complete discontinuity of the patellar tendon with retraction and a superiorly displaced patella. What additional finding should you evaluate for?
text: A 40-year-old male presents with inability to extend the knee after jumping. MRI shows complete discontinuity of the patellar tendon with retraction and a superiorly displaced patella. What additional finding should you evaluate for? | A. ACL tear | B. Associated meniscal tear | C. Patella alta and degree of tendon retraction | D. Lateral collateral ligament tear | Correct: C | In patellar tendon rupture, the patella retracts superiorly (patella alta) due to the unopposed pull of the quadriceps. Reporting the degree of retraction and the gap size helps the surgeon plan repair. An Insall-Salvati ratio greater than 1.2 supports the diagnosis of patella alta, though comparison with the contralateral side is ideal.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: An MRI shows a well-corticated osseous fragment at the superolateral pole of the patella with smooth margins and no surrounding marrow edema. The patient is asymptomatic in this region. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: An MRI shows a well-corticated osseous fragment at the superolateral pole of the patella with smooth margins and no surrounding marrow edema. The patient is asymptomatic in this region. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Acute patellar sleeve fracture | B. Bipartite patella (normal variant) | C. Osteochondral fracture from patellar dislocation | D. Avulsion fracture of the quadriceps tendon insertion | Correct: B | Bipartite patella is a normal developmental variant, most commonly located at the superolateral pole, with smooth, well-corticated margins and no associated marrow edema. In contrast, an acute fracture would show irregular margins, marrow edema, and surrounding soft-tissue changes. Bipartite patella can occasionally become symptomatic with overuse.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On axial and sagittal MRI, you see a well-defined fluid collection anterior to the patellar tendon, superficial to the tibial tubercle. The patient is a carpet layer who works on his knees. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: On axial and sagittal MRI, you see a well-defined fluid collection anterior to the patellar tendon, superficial to the tibial tubercle. The patient is a carpet layer who works on his knees. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Prepatellar bursitis | B. Superficial infrapatellar bursitis | C. Deep infrapatellar bursitis (behind the patellar tendon) | D. Baker cyst (popliteal cyst) | Correct: B | The superficial infrapatellar bursa is located anterior to the distal patellar tendon and tibial tubercle. It is commonly inflamed in people who frequently kneel (clergyman's knee / carpet layer's knee). The prepatellar bursa is more superior, anterior to the patella itself. Distinguishing these bursae helps correlate imaging findings with the patient's occupational history.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A knee MRI shows a fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa (lipohemarthrosis). What does this finding tell you, and what must you do next?
text: A knee MRI shows a fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa (lipohemarthrosis). What does this finding tell you, and what must you do next? | A. It is a benign reactive effusion; no further action | B. It is pathognomonic of an intra-articular fracture; systematically search for an occult fracture (tibial plateau, patella, femoral condyle) | C. It indicates septic arthritis; aspirate urgently | D. It is a sign of PVNS; obtain a gradient-echo sequence | Correct: B | A lipohemarthrosis — a fat-fluid (or fat-fluid-fluid) level in the suprapatellar bursa — is pathognomonic of an INTRA-ARTICULAR FRACTURE: marrow fat and blood leak into the joint and layer out by density. When you see it, perform a systematic search for an occult fracture of the tibial plateau, patella, or femoral condyle — it may be invisible on initial radiographs.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A popliteal (Baker's) cyst is identified. Between which two structures does it characteristically arise, and what should you always look for?
text: A popliteal (Baker's) cyst is identified. Between which two structures does it characteristically arise, and what should you always look for? | A. Between the biceps femoris and LCL; look for a Segond fracture | B. Between the medial head of gastrocnemius and the semimembranosus tendon; look for an underlying internal derangement (meniscal tear, cartilage loss, synovitis) | C. Anterior to the patellar tendon; look for Hoffa impingement | D. Within the popliteus tendon sheath; look for a PCL tear | Correct: B | A Baker's cyst arises posteromedially between the medial head of gastrocnemius and the semimembranosus tendon. It is usually SECONDARY to intra-articular pathology, so always search for the cause (meniscal tear, cartilage loss, synovitis). A ruptured Baker's cyst dissects inferiorly into the calf and mimics a DVT clinically.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Diffusely low-signal synovial thickening that "blooms" on gradient-echo (GRE) imaging is the hallmark of which condition?
text: Diffusely low-signal synovial thickening that "blooms" on gradient-echo (GRE) imaging is the hallmark of which condition? | A. Synovial (osteo)chondromatosis | B. Pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS) | C. Inflammatory (rheumatoid) synovitis | D. Lipoma arborescens | Correct: B | PVNS deposits HEMOSIDERIN, which is low signal on all sequences and BLOOMS (susceptibility) on gradient-echo. This distinguishes it from synovial chondromatosis (cartilage-signal loose bodies, often calcified, no blooming), inflammatory synovitis (intermediate-signal synovium that enhances), and lipoma arborescens (frond-like FAT-signal synovial proliferation).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 22-year-old sprinter feels a sudden tearing in the back of the thigh at the start of a race and develops posterior thigh ecchymosis. MRI shows complete avulsion of all three hamstring components from the ischial tuberosity, with the retracted stump measuring 3 cm from the footprint and abutting the sciatic nerve. Which reporting element most directly supports the typical decision to refer for surgical repair?
text: A 22-year-old sprinter feels a sudden tearing in the back of the thigh at the start of a race and develops posterior thigh ecchymosis. MRI shows complete avulsion of all three hamstring components from the ischial tuberosity, with the retracted stump measuring 3 cm from the footprint and abutting the sciatic nerve. Which reporting element most directly supports the typical decision to refer for surgical repair? | A. Grade 1 feathery perifascial edema along the biceps femoris | B. Complete avulsion of all three components with ~3 cm retraction | C. An interfascial fluid collection between gastrocnemius and soleus | D. Intact muscle architecture with no discrete fiber gap | Correct: B | Complete avulsion of all three hamstring components (the conjoint tendon — biceps femoris long head plus semitendinosus — together with the semimembranosus) with significant retraction is the injury typically referred for surgical repair, and sciatic-nerve proximity adds weight to that decision; a ~2 cm retraction is a commonly cited guide. Option A (Grade 1 edema) and option D (intact architecture, no gap) describe low-grade strains managed conservatively, and option C describes tennis leg, not a hamstring avulsion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 45-year-old recreational tennis player feels a sudden 'pop' in the calf mid-match and cannot bear weight. MRI shows fluid tracking between the medial gastrocnemius and the soleus aponeurosis at the distal myotendinous junction. Beyond describing the tear, what is the single most important additional structure to evaluate before finalizing the report?
text: A 45-year-old recreational tennis player feels a sudden 'pop' in the calf mid-match and cannot bear weight. MRI shows fluid tracking between the medial gastrocnemius and the soleus aponeurosis at the distal myotendinous junction. Beyond describing the tear, what is the single most important additional structure to evaluate before finalizing the report? | A. The popliteal vein, to exclude deep venous thrombosis | B. The proximal tibiofibular joint, for a ganglion | C. The quadriceps tendon insertion on the patella | D. The adductor longus origin at the pubis | Correct: A | Tennis leg (distal medial gastrocnemius MTJ tear with gastrocnemius–soleus interfascial fluid), DVT, and ruptured Baker's cyst are clinically indistinguishable, so the popliteal vein must be interrogated to exclude DVT before signing off. Options B, C, and D are unrelated structures that do not share this presentation and do not address the critical DVT/Baker's-cyst differential for acute calf pain.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI, you see only one bow-tie configuration of the medial meniscus body instead of the expected two. On the coronal images, there is a low-signal fragment in the intercondylar notch deep to the PCL. What is the unifying diagnosis?
text: On sagittal MRI, you see only one bow-tie configuration of the medial meniscus body instead of the expected two. On the coronal images, there is a low-signal fragment in the intercondylar notch deep to the PCL. What is the unifying diagnosis? | A. Discoid medial meniscus | B. Displaced bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus | C. Medial meniscus posterior root tear | D. Normal variant — the medial meniscus can have variable body width | Correct: B | The absent bow-tie sign (fewer than two bow-tie images on sagittal MRI) combined with a fragment in the intercondylar notch (the double PCL sign) is diagnostic of a displaced bucket-handle tear. This is a surgically urgent finding because the displaced fragment causes mechanical locking and the tear may be repairable if identified early.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 15-year-old presents with knee pain. MRI shows an OCD lesion of the medial femoral condyle with a 1.5 cm fragment. Which finding would make this fragment STABLE and potentially amenable to conservative management?
text: A 15-year-old presents with knee pain. MRI shows an OCD lesion of the medial femoral condyle with a 1.5 cm fragment. Which finding would make this fragment STABLE and potentially amenable to conservative management? | A. Fluid signal (high T2) completely surrounding the fragment | B. Cyst-like lesion at the fragment-bone interface | C. Intact overlying articular cartilage with no fluid at the fragment interface and no underlying cysts | D. Fragment displaced into the joint space | Correct: C | A stable OCD fragment has intact overlying cartilage, no high-T2 signal (fluid) at the fragment-parent bone interface, and no cyst-like lesions underneath. Stable lesions in skeletally immature patients have a high potential for healing with conservative management. Any signs of instability (fluid at the interface, cysts, cartilage breach, or fragment displacement) favor surgical intervention.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a coronal MRI of the knee, you identify a small avulsion fracture at the tip of the fibular head. What associated injury pattern should this finding immediately raise concern for?
text: On a coronal MRI of the knee, you identify a small avulsion fracture at the tip of the fibular head. What associated injury pattern should this finding immediately raise concern for? | A. Isolated LCL sprain | B. Posterolateral corner injury (arcuate sign) — evaluate for associated cruciate ligament tears | C. Proximal tibiofibular joint dislocation | D. Iliotibial band friction syndrome | Correct: B | The arcuate sign is a small avulsion fracture at the fibular styloid process (tip of the fibular head), indicating avulsion of the arcuate ligament complex. This is a hallmark of posterolateral corner injury and is frequently associated with cruciate ligament tears. Missing a PLC injury can lead to failure of cruciate ligament reconstruction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A patient is 6 months post partial meniscectomy and presents with recurrent medial knee pain. On MRI, you see high signal within the residual meniscus on PD FS sequences. How do you differentiate a re-tear from normal post-surgical signal changes?
text: A patient is 6 months post partial meniscectomy and presents with recurrent medial knee pain. On MRI, you see high signal within the residual meniscus on PD FS sequences. How do you differentiate a re-tear from normal post-surgical signal changes? | A. Any high signal in a post-surgical meniscus is always a re-tear | B. Post-surgical menisci never show signal abnormalities on MRI | C. The only reliable way to assess a post-surgical meniscus is with MR arthrography | D. High signal that is unequivocally as bright as fluid on T2/PD FS and extends to an articular surface suggests a re-tear; intermediate signal may represent stable post-surgical change | Correct: D | Post-surgical menisci commonly show grade 2-3 signal changes from scar tissue, granulation tissue, and altered collagen architecture. A true re-tear is suggested when signal within the meniscal remnant is unequivocally as bright as fluid on fluid-sensitive sequences and extends to an articular surface. If there is diagnostic uncertainty, MR arthrography can improve accuracy.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a lateral radiograph and confirmed on sagittal MRI, you identify a fat-fluid level within the suprapatellar bursa (lipohemarthrosis). What is the clinical significance of this finding?
text: On a lateral radiograph and confirmed on sagittal MRI, you identify a fat-fluid level within the suprapatellar bursa (lipohemarthrosis). What is the clinical significance of this finding? | A. This is a normal finding after knee aspiration | B. It indicates a simple joint effusion from synovitis | C. It represents a ruptured Baker cyst | D. It indicates an intra-articular fracture, as fat from the bone marrow has entered the joint space through the fracture site | Correct: D | A lipohemarthrosis (fat-fluid level in the joint) is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture. Fat from the bone marrow cavity enters the joint through a fracture that communicates with the articular surface. When this finding is identified, a careful search for an osteochondral fracture, tibial plateau fracture, or other intra-articular fracture is mandatory.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Degenerative Knee — Tricompartmental Osteoarthritis
text: A 62-year-old retired teacher with progressive bilateral knee pain, worse on the right, for 3 years. Pain is worse with stairs and prolonged walking. BMI 31. No history of trauma. Physical exam shows bilateral varus alignment, small cool effusions, crepitus with ROM, and decreased flexion (0-120°). | Tricompartmental osteoarthritis, most severe medial compartment | Complex degenerative medial meniscal tear with 5mm extrusion | Baker cyst | Mucoid degeneration of ACL | osteoarthritis | degenerative | meniscal degeneration | cartilage loss | subchondral changes | alignment

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Degenerative Knee — Tricompartmental Osteoarthritis model report
text:
BONES AND MARROW: Subchondral sclerosis and cyst formation in the medial compartment involving both the medial femoral condyle and medial tibial plateau. Marginal osteophytes arising from the medial femoral condyle, medial tibial plateau, and patellofemoral joint. No acute fracture. Varus alignment noted on coronal images.

CARTILAGE: Grade 4 (full-thickness) cartilage loss along the medial femoral condyle weight-bearing surface with exposed subchondral bone. Grade 3 cartilage loss at the medial tibial plateau. Grade 2-3 changes in the patellofemoral joint with thinning and fissuring of the trochlear and patellar cartilage. Lateral compartment relatively preserved with Grade 1-2 changes.

MENISCI: Complex degenerative tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus with a horizontal cleavage component. Medial meniscal extrusion measuring 5mm beyond the tibial margin on coronal images. Degenerative signal within the lateral meniscus body without a definite tear extending to an articular surface. It is important to distinguish degenerative intrasubstance signal from a surgically significant tear.

LIGAMENTS: ACL is intact but demonstrates mucoid degeneration with increased intrasubstance signal on all sequences and thickened appearance. PCL is normal. MCL shows mild nonspecific periligamentous signal along its deep fibers, likely related to chronic medial-compartment degeneration and osteophyte change (note: a varus knee tensions the LATERAL collateral, not the MCL). LCL is normal.

EXTENSOR MECHANISM: Moderate patellar tendinosis with thickening and intermediate signal in the proximal patellar tendon. Trochlear cartilage thinning as noted above. Quadriceps tendon is intact.

SYNOVIUM AND BURSAE: Moderate joint effusion. Baker cyst measuring 3.5 cm in greatest dimension communicating with the joint via the posteromedial capsule. Mild synovial thickening. No loose bodies identified. | 1. Advanced medial compartment osteoarthritis with full-thickness cartilage loss and subchondral changes.
2. Complex degenerative tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus with 5 mm extrusion, in the setting of severe medial compartment degeneration.
3. Moderate patellofemoral osteoarthritis.
4. Baker cyst, likely reactive.
5. Mucoid ACL degeneration — ligament functionally intact.

title: ACL Tear + Pivot-Shift Pattern
text: A 22-year-old soccer player presents with acute knee pain and instability after a noncontact pivoting injury during a game. The knee swelled rapidly within 2 hours. | Complete ACL tear | Pivot-shift bone contusion pattern | Possible lateral meniscal root tear | acl | pivot-shift | bone-bruise | meniscus | acute-injury

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: ACL Tear + Pivot-Shift Pattern model report
text:
ACL: Complete discontinuity of the ACL with abnormal signal and morphology. The ligament fibers are lax and irregular.

Bones/Marrow: Characteristic pivot-shift contusion pattern with marrow edema at the posterolateral tibial plateau and lateral femoral condyle sulcus terminalis region.

Menisci: Evaluate carefully for lateral meniscal posterior root tear and medial meniscal ramp lesion (peripheral tear at meniscocapsular junction).

Cartilage: No focal cartilage defects. Subchondral contusion without collapse.

Other ligaments: MCL, LCL, PCL intact. No posterolateral corner injury.

Effusion: Large joint effusion (hemarthrosis); no fat-fluid level — a lipohemarthrosis would require an intra-articular fracture, which is absent here. | 1. Complete ACL tear with classic pivot-shift bone contusion pattern (LFC + posterolateral tibial plateau).
2. Carefully evaluate for associated meniscal injuries (root tear, ramp lesion, bucket-handle) which are commonly associated.
3. Large hemarthrosis; no fat-fluid level to indicate an intra-articular fracture.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: ACL Tear + Pivot-Shift Pattern
text: The pivot-shift contusion pattern (LFC + posterolateral tibial plateau) is the most common bone bruise pattern and is strongly associated with ACL tears.

title: Patellar Dislocation + MPFL Tear
text: A 17-year-old basketball player reports that the knee "popped out" while cutting and spontaneously reduced. There is medial tenderness and a moderate effusion. | Transient lateral patellar dislocation | MPFL tear | Osteochondral injury with possible loose body | patellofemoral | mpfl | dislocation | osteochondral | loose-body

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar Dislocation + MPFL Tear model report
text:
Patellofemoral: Lateral patellar subluxation/tilt. Characteristic bone contusion pattern at the medial patellar facet and anterolateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle ("kissing contusions").

MPFL: Complete tear of the medial patellofemoral ligament at its femoral insertion with surrounding edema. Medial retinacular disruption.

Cartilage: Osteochondral injury at the medial patellar facet with possible chondral shear fragment. Evaluate for loose body in the joint.

Predisposing factors: Assess trochlear morphology (dysplasia?), patella alta (Insall-Salvati ratio), and TT-TG distance.

Effusion: Moderate joint effusion. | 1. Transient lateral patellar dislocation with characteristic kissing contusions (medial patella + anterolateral LFC).
2. Complete MPFL tear at femoral insertion.
3. Osteochondral injury at medial patellar facet — evaluate for loose body.
4. Assess predisposing anatomy (trochlear dysplasia, patella alta).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course

title: Medial Meniscal Root Tear
text: A 55-year-old recreational runner presents with worsening medial knee pain over 3 months without a specific traumatic event. Weight-bearing radiographs show mild medial joint space narrowing. | Posterior medial meniscal root tear | Medial meniscal extrusion | Early medial compartment overload | meniscus | root-tear | extrusion | degenerative | medial-compartment

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Medial Meniscal Root Tear model report
text:
Menisci: Complete radial tear at the posterior root attachment of the medial meniscus. The "ghost meniscus" sign is present on sagittal images — the posterior horn signal is absent at the root attachment level. Coronal images demonstrate meniscal extrusion >3mm beyond the tibial margin.

Bones/Marrow: Subchondral marrow edema at the medial femoral condyle and medial tibial plateau consistent with early overload. No subchondral fracture line.

Cartilage: Grade 2-3 chondral thinning in the medial compartment, weight-bearing surfaces.

Ligaments: All ligaments intact.

Effusion: Small joint effusion. | 1. Posterior medial meniscal root tear — functionally equivalent to total meniscectomy.
2. Meniscal extrusion >3mm indicating loss of hoop stress.
3. Early medial compartment overload with subchondral edema and cartilage thinning.
4. Clinical correlation for surgical candidacy (root repair vs conservative management).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course

Confirm with the radial cleft/defect on coronal and axial images.

title: PCL + PLC Dashboard Injury
text: A 28-year-old presents after a motor vehicle accident with dashboard mechanism. There is posterior sag of the tibia on exam and posterolateral tenderness. | PCL tear | Posterolateral corner injury | Dashboard bone contusion pattern | pcl | plc | dashboard | multiligament | mva

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: PCL + PLC Dashboard Injury model report
text:
PCL: Complete tear with abnormal signal intensity and loss of normal taut morphology. The ligament is thickened and lax.

Bones/Marrow: Dashboard contusion pattern — marrow edema at the anterior proximal tibia from the direct blow mechanism.

Posterolateral corner: Edema and partial tearing of the LCL. Abnormal signal in the popliteus tendon at its femoral origin. Disruption of the popliteofibular ligament and arcuate ligament complex.

Menisci: Medial and lateral menisci intact.

ACL: Intact. MCL: Intact.

Effusion: Moderate joint effusion. | 1. Complete PCL tear with dashboard contusion pattern (anterior tibial marrow edema).
2. Posterolateral corner injury involving the LCL, popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament.
3. Combined PCL + PLC injury — surgical consultation recommended as untreated PLC injuries lead to graft failure.
4. ACL and MCL intact.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bucket-Handle Meniscal Tear
text: A 30-year-old presents with a locked knee after a twisting injury. The patient is unable to fully extend the knee. There is a moderate effusion and joint line tenderness. | Bucket-handle meniscal tear with displaced fragment | Double PCL sign | Donor site identification | meniscus | bucket-handle | locked-knee | displaced-fragment | mechanical-block

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bucket-Handle Meniscal Tear model report
text:
Menisci: Bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus. The displaced inner fragment is flipped into the intercondylar notch, lying anteroinferior to the PCL creating the "double PCL sign" on sagittal images. The donor site shows a truncated and blunted posterior horn (absent bow-tie sign on consecutive sagittal slices). The "fragment in notch" sign is present on coronal images.

ACL: Evaluate carefully — ACL tears coexist in a substantial minority of bucket-handle tears (reported ~10–50%, varying with medial vs lateral location and acuity).

Effusion: Moderate joint effusion.

Bones: No significant marrow edema. | 1. Bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus with displaced fragment into the intercondylar notch.
2. Double PCL sign and absent bow-tie sign confirm the diagnosis.
3. Evaluate ACL integrity — commonly associated injury.
4. Mechanical block explains the locked knee presentation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bucket-Handle Meniscal Tear
text: The "double PCL sign" is highly specific for a MEDIAL-meniscus bucket-handle tear WHEN THE ACL IS INTACT — the intact ACL holds the flipped fragment beneath the PCL, mimicking a second PCL. If the ACL is torn the fragment is less constrained and may not lie in this position, so always confirm ACL integrity.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bucket-Handle Meniscal Tear
text: The absent "bow tie" sign: normally you see 2 consecutive sagittal slices with a bow-tie shaped meniscal body. If fewer, suspect a bucket-handle tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bucket-Handle Meniscal Tear
text: The "fragment in notch" sign on coronal images shows the displaced meniscal fragment in the intercondylar notch.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bucket-Handle Meniscal Tear
text: Bucket-handle tears are the most common cause of a mechanically locked knee in young patients.

title: OCD Stability Assessment
text: A 16-year-old multi-sport athlete presents with activity-related medial knee pain for 4 months. Radiographs show an osteochondral lesion at the medial femoral condyle. Physes remain open. | Juvenile osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) | Stability assessment with juvenile-specific criteria | Possible loose body | ocd | cartilage | juvenile | stability | loose-body

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: OCD Stability Assessment model report
text:
Cartilage/Osteochondral: Well-defined osteochondral lesion at the classic location — lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. The fragment measures approximately 15 x 10 mm. High T2 signal rim surrounds the fragment, extending to the articular surface (rim sign). A thin line of fluid signal is seen between the fragment and the parent bone.

Stability assessment: The high signal rim sign and fluid between fragment and parent bone suggest instability. Evaluate for secondary signs: cyst-like lesions beneath the fragment, disruption of the overlying cartilage.

Bones: Open physes consistent with juvenile form.

Effusion: Small joint effusion. Evaluate for loose bodies in joint recesses. | 1. Juvenile OCD of the medial femoral condyle (classic location) with MRI signs suggesting instability.
2. Rim sign and fluid interface between fragment and parent bone — unstable fragment.
3. Open physes — juvenile form, which has better healing potential than adult OCD.
4. No definite loose body identified. Small effusion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: OCD Stability Assessment
text: Classic OCD location: lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle (~70–80% of cases).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: OCD Stability Assessment
text: Adult (De Smet) MRI instability criteria: (1) high-T2 rim at the fragment–parent-bone interface, (2) fluid (matching fluid signal) undercutting the fragment, (3) cyst-like lesions beneath the fragment, (4) a focal articular-cartilage breach. JUVENILE caveat: in skeletally immature patients the high-T2 rim alone is unreliable — it can be vascular granulation tissue in a STABLE lesion — and counts toward instability only when the high signal matches fluid intensity or a second low-signal line/secondary sign is present.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: OCD Stability Assessment
text: Juvenile OCD (open physes) has better healing potential than adult OCD — but unstable fragments still often need surgery.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: OCD Stability Assessment
text: Always measure the lesion size and report stability features — this directly drives surgical decision-making.

title: SIFK / SONK
text: A 68-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of severe medial knee pain without a specific traumatic event. She has difficulty bearing weight and exam reveals medial femoral condyle tenderness. | Subchondral insufficiency fracture of the knee (SIFK) | Subchondral fracture line | Associated meniscal pathology | sifk | sonk | insufficiency-fracture | marrow-edema | medial-compartment

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: SIFK / SONK model report
text:
Bones/Marrow: Extensive marrow edema centered at the weight-bearing surface of the medial femoral condyle. A low signal subchondral fracture line is present on T1 and T2 sequences, running parallel to the articular surface. Early subchondral flattening/collapse may be present.

Cartilage: Overlying articular cartilage is relatively preserved at the medial femoral condyle — a key feature distinguishing early SIFK from primary osteoarthritis.

Menisci: Associated posterior medial meniscal root tear with meniscal extrusion — this is a common association with SIFK.

Ligaments: Intact.

Effusion: Small to moderate joint effusion. | 1. Subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle (SIFK/SONK) with subchondral fracture line and extensive marrow edema.
2. Evaluate for early subchondral collapse which worsens prognosis.
3. Associated posterior medial meniscal root tear — common association that contributes to accelerated degeneration.
4. Differentiate from OCD (younger patients, different location) and AVN (different signal pattern).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: SIFK / SONK
text: SIFK (formerly SONK) is NOT true osteonecrosis — it is a subchondral insufficiency fracture that may progress to secondary osteonecrosis.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: SIFK / SONK
text: The key MRI finding is a low-signal subchondral fracture line on T1, surrounded by extensive marrow edema.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: SIFK / SONK
text: SIFK is strongly associated with posterior medial meniscal root tears — always check the root.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: SIFK / SONK
text: Differential diagnosis: OCD (younger; classically the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle), AVN (bilateral, multifocal), stress fracture (linear orientation).

title: MCL Distal Avulsion
text: A 25-year-old skier presents after a valgus injury to the knee. There is medial-sided swelling extending distally and tenderness along the distal MCL insertion. | Distal MCL avulsion (Stener-like lesion) | Deep MCL vs superficial MCL involvement | Possible associated medial meniscal injury | mcl | collateral | valgus | avulsion | skiing-injury

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MCL Distal Avulsion model report
text:
MCL: Complete tear of the distal superficial MCL with retraction and displacement superficial to the pes anserinus tendons (Stener-like lesion). The torn distal fibers are folded back upon themselves. Deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) is also disrupted with fluid at the meniscocapsular junction.

Menisci: Peripheral tear at the posterior horn of the medial meniscus at the meniscocapsular junction (associated with deep MCL disruption).

Bones: Mild marrow edema at the medial tibial plateau. No fracture.

ACL/PCL/LCL: Intact.

Effusion: Moderate joint effusion with extensive medial soft tissue edema. | 1. Distal superficial MCL avulsion with Stener-like configuration (displaced superficial to pes anserinus) — this prevents healing and may require surgical repair.
2. Deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) disruption.
3. Associated peripheral medial meniscal tear at meniscocapsular junction.
4. Cruciates and lateral structures intact.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MCL Distal Avulsion
text: A Stener-like lesion of the MCL occurs when the torn distal superficial MCL is displaced superficial to the pes anserinus — this prevents healing.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MCL Distal Avulsion
text: Always differentiate superficial MCL from deep MCL (meniscotibial/meniscofemoral ligaments) — deep MCL injuries affect meniscal stability.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MCL Distal Avulsion
text: MCL injuries are graded 1-3: Grade 1 (edema, intact fibers), Grade 2 (partial tear), Grade 3 (complete disruption).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MCL Distal Avulsion
text: Peripheral medial meniscal / meniscocapsular injury can accompany an MCL tear, because the deep MCL attaches to the meniscus (meniscofemoral and meniscotibial portions). Note: the eponymous "ramp lesion" of the posteromedial meniscocapsular junction is classically associated with ACL tears, not isolated MCL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Multiligament Knee Injury
text: A 35-year-old motorcyclist presents after a high-energy accident with gross knee instability. Clinical exam suggests multiplanar laxity and the vascular status is being monitored. | Knee dislocation (ACL + PCL tears) | Posterolateral corner disruption | Possible popliteal artery injury | Multiple meniscal and chondral injuries | multiligament | dislocation | acl | pcl | plc | vascular | high-energy

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Multiligament Knee Injury model report
text:
ACL: Complete tear with full discontinuity.

PCL: Complete tear — thickened, lax, with abnormal signal throughout.

Posterolateral corner: Complete disruption of the LCL, popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament. Biceps femoris tendon edema.

MCL: High-grade partial tear.

Menisci: Complex tear of the medial meniscus posterior horn. Lateral meniscus body tear.

Bones: Diffuse marrow edema medially and laterally. Possible periarticular fracture fragments.

Cartilage: Multiple chondral injuries.

Vascular: Evaluate for popliteal artery injury — signal abnormality in the popliteal fossa.

Effusion: Large hemarthrosis. | 1. Knee dislocation pattern with bicruciate tear (ACL + PCL).
2. Posterolateral corner disruption (LCL, popliteus, popliteofibular ligament).
3. MCL high-grade partial tear — this is a KD-IV multiligament knee injury (both cruciates plus both collaterals; the medial-side injury places it beyond KD-IIIL, which requires an intact MCL).
4. Multiple meniscal and chondral injuries.
5. CRITICAL: Recommend urgent vascular assessment of the popliteal artery if not already performed.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Multiligament Knee Injury
text: A knee dislocation is defined by bicruciate injury (ACL + PCL) regardless of whether the knee is currently reduced.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Multiligament Knee Injury
text: The Schenck (KD) classification: KD-I (single cruciate torn — ACL or PCL — with the other cruciate intact; ± collateral), KD-II (both cruciates, collaterals intact), KD-III (both cruciates + one collateral; IIIM = medial, IIIL = lateral), KD-IV (both cruciates + both collaterals), KD-V (fracture-dislocation).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Multiligament Knee Injury
text: Popliteal artery injury can be occult in knee dislocation — urgent vascular assessment is required; CTA/MRA is appropriate when pulses, ABI, or clinical findings are abnormal or equivocal.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Multiligament Knee Injury
text: Report every structure systematically — missing a PLC injury in a multiligament knee leads to graft failure.

title: Patellar Tendon Rupture
text: A 32-year-old basketball player felt a pop and sharp pain while landing from a jump. He is unable to perform a straight leg raise. Patella alta is suspected on lateral radiograph. | Complete patellar tendon rupture | Patella alta | Possible bony avulsion fragment from the inferior patellar pole | extensor-mechanism | patellar-tendon | rupture | patella-alta | sports-injury

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar Tendon Rupture model report
text:
Extensor mechanism: Complete rupture of the patellar tendon at its proximal attachment (inferior pole of patella). The torn tendon is retracted distally and may form a "ball" of tissue. Gap between the inferior patella and the proximal tendon stump filled with fluid/hemorrhage.

Patella: Patella alta — the patella rides superiorly due to loss of the distal tether. Insall-Salvati ratio markedly elevated.

Quadriceps tendon: Intact but assess carefully for concomitant injury.

Bones: Possible small avulsion fragment from the inferior pole of the patella.

Effusion: Moderate joint effusion with prepatellar and infrapatellar soft tissue edema. | 1. Complete patellar tendon rupture at its proximal attachment with tendon retraction.
2. Patella alta secondary to the tendon rupture.
3. Intact quadriceps tendon.
4. This is a surgical emergency for the extensor mechanism — urgent repair is recommended.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar Tendon Rupture
text: Patellar tendon rupture is more common in patients <40; quadriceps tendon rupture is more common >40.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar Tendon Rupture
text: Patella alta (high-riding patella) is a hallmark of patellar tendon rupture — measure the Insall-Salvati ratio (normal ~1.0, alta >1.2).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar Tendon Rupture
text: Risk factors: prior tendinopathy, corticosteroid injections, fluoroquinolone use, diabetes, renal disease.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar Tendon Rupture
text: Always evaluate the quadriceps tendon as well — combined injuries can occur.
