course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Post-Meniscus Repair Re-tear
text: A 24-year-old presents 18 months after a medial meniscal repair with new onset of mechanical symptoms and medial joint line pain. The prior repair was performed at the time of ACL reconstruction. | Meniscal re-tear at repair site | Differentiation of healing signal vs re-tear | ACL graft integrity assessment | meniscus | post-surgical | retear | repair | acl-reconstruction

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Post-Meniscus Repair Re-tear model report
text:
Menisci: Post-surgical changes at the medial meniscus with suture artifacts. At the prior repair site, there is recurrent linear high signal extending to the articular surface on proton density sequences. The signal pattern and morphology are concerning for re-tear rather than expected post-repair signal.

ACL graft: The ACL graft is intact with low signal throughout. The tibial and femoral tunnels are appropriately positioned. No tunnel widening.

Post-repair signal interpretation: increased intrameniscal/surface signal can persist for many months after a successful repair, so persistent grade-3 signal alone is not diagnostic of re-tear. Re-tear is favored here by the morphology together with fluid-signal extending into the tear, particularly with new mechanical symptoms; comparison with prior post-operative imaging is ideal.

Effusion: Small to moderate effusion. | 1. Findings concerning for meniscal re-tear at the prior repair site — surface-reaching (grade 3) signal with fluid extending into the tear, in the setting of recurrent mechanical symptoms; comparison with prior post-operative imaging would strengthen the assessment.
2. ACL graft intact with appropriate tunnel positions.
3. Clinical correlation recommended — arthroscopic evaluation may be indicated for definitive diagnosis.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Post-Meniscus Repair Re-tear
text: Post-repair meniscal MRI interpretation is challenging: grade 3 signal within 6 months may be normal healing, but new or persistent signal after 12 months with symptoms suggests re-tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Post-Meniscus Repair Re-tear
text: Clinical correlation is essential — MRI alone cannot always distinguish healing signal from re-tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Post-Meniscus Repair Re-tear
text: Conventional MRI has REDUCED specificity after meniscal repair, because persistent intrasubstance or surface-reaching (grade 3) signal can mimic re-tear even in a healed meniscus. Findings that raise confidence for a true re-tear include displaced fragments, fluid-signal extending into the tear, or new grade-3 signal compared with priors; MR arthrography (contrast imbibing into the tear) is the standard problem-solver. Symptom correlation raises pre-test probability but does not give a fixed accuracy figure.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Post-Meniscus Repair Re-tear
text: Always evaluate the ACL graft when assessing a post-repair meniscus — graft integrity affects meniscal healing.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: ACL Graft Evaluation
text: A 26-year-old presents 10 months after ACL reconstruction with a hamstring autograft. The patient reports a feeling of instability with cutting activities and a recent episode of giving way. | ACL graft failure assessment | Tunnel position evaluation | Possible graft impingement or re-tear | acl | graft | post-surgical | reconstruction | instability

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: ACL Graft Evaluation model report
text:
ACL graft: The hamstring autograft demonstrates abnormal increased signal on all sequences with loss of the normal taut, low-signal morphology. There is discontinuity of the graft fibers in the mid-substance. The graft appears lax with anterior tibial translation.

Tunnel assessment: The femoral tunnel is positioned slightly anterior — this tends to make the graft non-isometric (excessively tight in flexion) and is a recognized contributor to graft stretch/failure. The tibial tunnel position appears acceptable, so roof (intercondylar-notch) impingement — which results from an anteriorly placed TIBIAL tunnel — is not expected here. Mild tunnel widening noted.

Bones: Pivot-shift contusion pattern (LFC + posterolateral tibial plateau) indicating re-injury mechanism.

Menisci: Evaluate for new meniscal tears — common with re-injury.

Effusion: Moderate joint effusion. | 1. ACL graft failure with complete graft tear (abnormal signal, discontinuity, laxity).
2. Pivot-shift bone contusion pattern indicating re-injury through the same mechanism.
3. Femoral tunnel may be slightly anterior — a contributing factor to graft over-tensioning and failure (an anterior tibial tunnel, not femoral, would be the cause of roof impingement).
4. Evaluate menisci for new injuries associated with the instability episode.
5. Revision ACL reconstruction consultation recommended.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: ACL Graft Evaluation
text: A normal ACL graft should be uniformly low signal on all sequences by 12-24 months post-operatively.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: ACL Graft Evaluation
text: Between 6-12 months, the graft undergoes "ligamentization" and may show intermediate signal — this is normal.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: ACL Graft Evaluation
text: Key signs of graft failure: increased signal on all sequences, discontinuity, laxity, anterior tibial translation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: ACL Graft Evaluation
text: Tunnel malposition is a leading technical cause of graft failure: an anterior TIBIAL tunnel causes roof (notch) impingement, while a too-anterior FEMORAL tunnel makes the graft non-isometric (excessively tight in flexion), predisposing to stretch and failure.

text: Hardware artifacts | Tunnel cysts | Post-surgical changes

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Get oriented
text: Sagittal PD fat-sat. Anterior is to the LEFT, posterior to the RIGHT; fluid is bright, fat is suppressed. You scroll medial → lateral. This slice is through the intercondylar notch — the deepest part of the joint.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral condyle
text: The large rounded bone with smooth low-signal cortex and bright fatty marrow. Its articular surface should be covered by a thin, even layer of cartilage. | Femoral condyle

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial plateau
text: The flat top of the tibia below the joint line. Marrow signal should be uniform — a discrete dark line within it would signal a fracture. | Tibial plateau

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Extensor mechanism — top
text: The quadriceps tendon (striated, multi-layered) inserts on the superior pole of the patella. Trace it down to the patella, then to the patellar tendon below. | Quadriceps tendon | Patella

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar tendon
text: Runs from the inferior pole of the patella to the tibial tubercle — uniformly low signal and a few mm thick. Hoffa's fat pad sits just behind it. | Patellar tendon

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Hoffa's fat pad
text: The fat behind the patellar tendon and in front of the joint. Normally homogeneous; edema here is a clue to anterior impingement. | Hoffa's (infrapatellar) fat pad

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscus — the dark bow-ties
text: On a compartment slice the meniscus appears as two low-signal triangles (anterior and posterior horns). The posterior horn is normally the larger of the two medially. Persistent high signal that reaches an articular surface on adjacent slices favors a tear; isolated single-slice clefts deserve correlation. Three normal lateral-meniscus relationships mimic tears: the transverse (intermeniscal) ligament at the anterior horn, the meniscofemoral ligament (Humphry/Wrisberg) at the posterior horn, and the popliteal hiatus/popliteomeniscal fascicles posterolaterally near the popliteus tendon. Trace the band or hiatus across adjacent slices before calling a tear. | Anterior horn (meniscus) | Posterior horn (meniscus)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Cartilage & joint fluid
text: Cartilage is the smooth intermediate-signal layer on the bone ends; a thin bright rim of joint fluid outlines it. A small amount of fluid is normal. | Articular cartilage / joint fluid

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Anterior cruciate ligament
text: On this lateral notch slice the ACL is a straight, striated band running parallel to the roof of the notch (Blumensaat line), from the lateral femoral condyle down to the anterior tibial spine — more fibrillar and a touch brighter than the smooth, dark PCL. It is partly volume-averaged on any one slice, so trace it across a couple: normal fibers stay continuous and parallel to the notch roof. | Anterior cruciate ligament

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Posterior cruciate ligament
text: The PCL is a smooth, continuous low-signal band arcing through the posterior notch — a thick, dark, curved structure on this midline slice. Loss of its smooth arc suggests injury. | Posterior cruciate ligament

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Popliteal vessels
text: The popliteal artery and vein run vertically in the popliteal fossa behind the joint — a useful posterior landmark. Don't mistake the flow-related signal for pathology. | Popliteal vessels

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the straight striated band marked here, paralleling the roof of the intercondylar notch?
text: What is the straight striated band marked here, paralleling the roof of the intercondylar notch? | Anterior cruciate ligament | Posterior cruciate ligament | Meniscofemoral ligament | Posterior joint capsule | Correct: Anterior cruciate ligament | On this lateral notch slice, the straight, slightly striated band running parallel to Blumensaat's line from the lateral femoral condyle to the anterior tibial spine is the ACL. The PCL is smoother, darker, more sharply curved, and sits more posteriorly and medially in the notch, so it would not lie along the notch roof on this lateral slice.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Femoral condyle | Patella | Tibial plateau | Fabella | Correct: Femoral condyle | The large rounded bone forming the top of the joint, with low-signal cortex and bright fatty marrow, is the femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Femoral condyle | Tibial plateau | Patella | Fibular head | Correct: Tibial plateau | The flat bone below the joint line is the tibial plateau (proximal tibia).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Patella | Femoral condyle | Quadriceps tendon | Tibial tubercle | Correct: Patella | The anterior bone at the front of the knee, with marrow signal, is the patella.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Quadriceps tendon | Patellar tendon | Iliotibial band | Prepatellar bursa | Correct: Quadriceps tendon | The striated multi-layered tendon inserting on the superior pole of the patella is the quadriceps tendon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Patellar tendon | Quadriceps tendon | ACL | Popliteus tendon | Correct: Patellar tendon | The uniformly low-signal band from the inferior pole of the patella to the tibial tubercle is the patellar tendon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Hoffa's infrapatellar fat pad | Joint effusion | Baker's cyst | Prefemoral fat | Correct: Hoffa's infrapatellar fat pad | The fat behind the patellar tendon and in front of the joint is Hoffa's (infrapatellar) fat pad.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Posterior horn of the meniscus | PCL | Posterior joint capsule | Popliteus tendon | Correct: Posterior horn of the meniscus | The low-signal triangle at the posterior joint line is the posterior horn of the meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Anterior horn of the meniscus | Hoffa's fat pad | ACL | Transverse ligament | Correct: Anterior horn of the meniscus | The low-signal triangle at the anterior joint line is the anterior horn of the meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The bright line outlining the joint surface here is normal — what is it?
text: The bright line outlining the joint surface here is normal — what is it? | Articular cartilage with a thin rim of joint fluid | A meniscal tear | Subchondral edema | Synovitis | Correct: Articular cartilage with a thin rim of joint fluid | Smooth cartilage over the bone ends, outlined by a thin bright rim of joint fluid, is the normal articular surface. A small amount of fluid is expected.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Popliteal vessels | PCL | Baker's cyst | Semimembranosus tendon | Correct: Popliteal vessels | The vertical tubular structures in the popliteal fossa behind the joint are the popliteal artery and vein.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You've found this meniscus on the sagittal. To judge how far it extrudes (a root-tear clue), which plane do you switch to?
text: You've found this meniscus on the sagittal. To judge how far it extrudes (a root-tear clue), which plane do you switch to? | Coronal | Axial | A different sagittal slice | Extrusion can't be measured on MRI | Correct: Coronal | Meniscal extrusion is graded on the CORONAL image at the mid-body — >3 mm past the tibial margin is significant and should trigger a deliberate posterior-root search. Build the habit of correlating planes.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Get oriented
text: Coronal PD fat-sat through the mid-joint. Superior is up, inferior down; medial is to the LEFT and lateral to the RIGHT. Coronal's strength is comparing the two compartments side-by-side.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Medial femoral condyle
text: Sits to the left here, articulating with the (concave) medial tibial plateau. Smooth cortex, uniform fatty marrow. | Medial femoral condyle

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Lateral femoral condyle
text: On the right, articulating with the convex lateral tibial plateau (the medial plateau is concave) — coronal is where you compare the two side-by-side. | Lateral femoral condyle

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial plateau
text: The proximal tibia below the joint line. Compare the medial (concave) and lateral (convex) plateaus. | Tibial plateau

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial spines (intercondylar eminence)
text: The central bony peaks (intercondylar eminence) — your landmark for the true mid-joint coronal slice. The cruciates anchor in the intercondylar areas just beside the spines, not on the peaks themselves. | Tibial spines / eminence

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Menisci — the wedges
text: Each meniscus is a low-signal triangle pointing into the joint. On coronal, check that the medial body doesn't extrude past the tibial margin — >3 mm is significant, but it is a clue to inspect the posterior root rather than a root-tear diagnosis by itself. Laterally, do not mistake the normal popliteus hiatus/popliteomeniscal fascicle region for a peripheral tear. | Medial meniscus (body) | Lateral meniscus (body)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Weight-bearing cartilage
text: The smooth intermediate-signal layer on the condyle and plateau, outlined by a thin bright rim of joint fluid. Compare medial vs lateral for symmetry. | Articular cartilage

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Medial collateral ligament
text: A thin, taut low-signal band along the medial edge from the femoral epicondyle to the proximal tibia. It has two layers — the superficial MCL (the long outer band) and the deep MCL (meniscofemoral + meniscotibial fibers anchoring the meniscus); fluid between the layers is the first sign of a sprain. | Medial collateral ligament

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Lateral collateral ligament (LCL)
text: The cord-like LCL runs down the lateral edge from the lateral femoral epicondyle to the fibular head — the lateral counterpart of the MCL. The popliteus and posterolateral corner are confirmed on more posterior slices. | Lateral collateral ligament (LCL)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure running along the medial edge of the joint?
text: What is the marked structure running along the medial edge of the joint? | Medial collateral ligament | Pes anserine tendons | Medial meniscus body | Semimembranosus tendon | Correct: Medial collateral ligament | The thin, taut low-signal band coursing along the medial margin from the medial femoral epicondyle to the proximal tibia is the medial collateral ligament. The pes anserine tendons lie more superficial and anteromedial and insert lower on the tibia, not as a single taut femorotibial band at the joint line.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the cord-like structure at the lateral margin coursing toward the fibular head?
text: What is the cord-like structure at the lateral margin coursing toward the fibular head? | Lateral (fibular) collateral ligament | Iliotibial band | Popliteus tendon | Biceps femoris tendon | Correct: Lateral (fibular) collateral ligament | The discrete cord-like ligament descending from the lateral femoral epicondyle to the fibular head is the lateral (fibular) collateral ligament. The iliotibial band inserts more anteriorly at Gerdy tubercle on the tibia, not the fibular head, so it would not converge on the fibular apex like the marked structure.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the central bony peaks marked here at the mid-joint?
text: What are the central bony peaks marked here at the mid-joint? | Tibial spines (intercondylar eminence) | Femoral intercondylar notch roof | Avulsed cruciate fragment | Osteophytes of the tibial plateau | Correct: Tibial spines (intercondylar eminence) | The paired central peaks rising from the tibia between the condyles are the tibial spines (intercondylar eminence), the landmark for the true mid-joint coronal slice. They are a normal osseous structure, not an avulsed fragment; an eminence avulsion would show a displaced bone fragment with a donor-site defect and marrow edema.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Medial femoral condyle | Lateral femoral condyle | Medial tibial plateau | Patella | Correct: Medial femoral condyle | The condyle on the medial (left) side, over the medial tibial plateau, is the medial femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Lateral femoral condyle | Medial femoral condyle | Lateral tibial plateau | Fibular head | Correct: Lateral femoral condyle | The condyle on the lateral (right) side is the lateral femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Tibial plateau | Femoral condyle | Tibial spine | Fibula | Correct: Tibial plateau | The flat bone below the joint line is the tibial plateau.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Medial meniscus (body) | Lateral meniscus (body) | MCL | Medial femoral condyle | Correct: Medial meniscus (body) | The low-signal triangle at the medial joint margin is the body of the medial meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Lateral meniscus (body) | Medial meniscus (body) | LCL | Popliteus tendon | Correct: Lateral meniscus (body) | The low-signal triangle at the lateral joint margin is the body of the lateral meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The bright line outlining the joint surface here is normal — what is it?
text: The bright line outlining the joint surface here is normal — what is it? | Articular cartilage with a thin rim of joint fluid | A chondral defect | Subchondral cyst | Meniscal tear | Correct: Articular cartilage with a thin rim of joint fluid | Smooth cartilage over the bone ends, outlined by a thin bright fluid rim, is the normal articular surface.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: This is the medial meniscus. Beyond how much extrusion past the tibial margin is considered significant and should trigger a root search?
text: This is the medial meniscus. Beyond how much extrusion past the tibial margin is considered significant and should trigger a root search? | >3 mm | >1 mm | >8 mm | Any extrusion at all | Correct: >3 mm | Medial meniscal extrusion >3 mm beyond the tibial margin is significant and should make you inspect the posterior root, but extrusion alone does not prove a root tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The cruciate ligaments arise near these central tibial spines. On which plane do you actually trace the ACL along Blumensaat's line?
text: The cruciate ligaments arise near these central tibial spines. On which plane do you actually trace the ACL along Blumensaat's line? | Sagittal | Coronal | Axial | All planes equally | Correct: Sagittal | The ACL is best traced on the SAGITTAL plane, paralleling Blumensaat's line (the intercondylar roof); coronal and axial confirm. Correlate planes for every ligament.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Get oriented
text: Axial T2 fat-sat at the patellofemoral joint. Anterior (the patella) is up, posterior is down; medial is to the LEFT, lateral to the RIGHT. The axial is THE plane for the patella, trochlea, retinacula/MPFL, and patellar tracking.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patella
text: The anterior sesamoid bone, with its own articular cartilage on the deep surface. Note its position and tilt relative to the trochlea. | Patella

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral trochlea
text: The grooved anterior femur the patella tracks in. The lateral facet is normally longer, forming a higher buttress against the patella; loss of that asymmetry — a flat, shallow groove — is trochlear dysplasia. | Femoral trochlea

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Trochlear groove
text: The deepest point of the trochlea. A shallow, flat groove is trochlear dysplasia — a key predisposing factor for patellar instability. | Trochlear groove (sulcus)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellofemoral cartilage
text: The cartilage on the patellar and trochlear surfaces, outlined by a thin bright rim of fluid. Assess thickness and symmetry of the facets. | Patellofemoral cartilage

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MPFL / medial retinaculum
text: The medial patellofemoral ligament runs from the medial patella toward the adductor tubercle — the main restraint against lateral dislocation, and what tears after a patellar dislocation. | MPFL / medial retinaculum

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Lateral retinaculum
text: The lateral retinacular soft tissues tethering the patella laterally. | Lateral retinaculum

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral condyles
text: Behind the trochlea, the two condyles flank the intercondylar notch (where the cruciates are seen in cross-section). | Medial femoral condyle | Lateral femoral condyle

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Popliteal vessels
text: The popliteal artery and vein in the popliteal fossa posteriorly — a reliable posterior landmark. | Popliteal vessels

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked grooved anterior femoral structure the patella tracks within?
text: What is the marked grooved anterior femoral structure the patella tracks within? | Femoral trochlea | Intercondylar notch | Median patellar ridge | Tibial tuberosity | Correct: Femoral trochlea | The grooved anterior femur against which the patella articulates is the femoral trochlea; its lateral facet is normally longer, forming a buttress against lateral patellar subluxation. The median patellar ridge belongs to the patella (the anterior bone), not the femur, so it is on the opposite side of the patellofemoral joint.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked soft-tissue band on the lateral side of the patella?
text: What is the marked soft-tissue band on the lateral side of the patella? | Lateral retinaculum | MPFL (medial retinaculum) | Iliotibial band | Vastus lateralis tendon | Correct: Lateral retinaculum | The retinacular soft tissue tethering the patella on its lateral aspect is the lateral retinaculum. The MPFL is the principal medial restraint and lies on the opposite (medial) side of the patella, so a lateral marker cannot be the MPFL.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Patella | Femoral trochlea | Quadriceps tendon | Tibial tubercle | Correct: Patella | The anterior sesamoid bone at the front of the knee is the patella.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The bright line between the patella and the trochlea is normal — what is it?
text: The bright line between the patella and the trochlea is normal — what is it? | Patellofemoral articular cartilage with joint fluid | A chondral fissure | Synovitis | Plica | Correct: Patellofemoral articular cartilage with joint fluid | Smooth patellar and trochlear cartilage outlined by a thin bright fluid rim is the normal patellofemoral articulation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Trochlear groove (sulcus) | Intercondylar notch | Median patellar ridge | Trochlear cartilage defect | Correct: Trochlear groove (sulcus) | The deepest central point of the trochlea is the trochlear groove (sulcus); flattening here indicates dysplasia.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Which medial restraint of the patella is marked (the structure torn after a lateral patellar dislocation)?
text: Which medial restraint of the patella is marked (the structure torn after a lateral patellar dislocation)? | MPFL / medial retinaculum | Lateral retinaculum | Patellar tendon | Iliotibial band | Correct: MPFL / medial retinaculum | The medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) is the main restraint against lateral dislocation and the structure injured after a patellar dislocation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Medial femoral condyle | Lateral femoral condyle | Medial tibial plateau | Patella | Correct: Medial femoral condyle | The condyle on the medial (left) side behind the trochlea is the medial femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Lateral femoral condyle | Medial femoral condyle | Fibular head | Lateral meniscus | Correct: Lateral femoral condyle | The condyle on the lateral (right) side is the lateral femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Popliteal vessels | PCL | Baker's cyst | Semimembranosus tendon | Correct: Popliteal vessels | The tubular structures in the popliteal fossa posteriorly are the popliteal artery and vein.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A shallow, flat trochlear groove on the axial images predisposes to which problem?
text: A shallow, flat trochlear groove on the axial images predisposes to which problem? | Patellar instability / dislocation | ACL tear | Meniscal root tear | Baker's cyst | Correct: Patellar instability / dislocation | A flat/shallow trochlea (trochlear dysplasia) is a key predisposing factor for recurrent patellar instability and lateral dislocation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: This is the MPFL. After a transient lateral patellar dislocation, where do you expect the classic bone bruises?
text: This is the MPFL. After a transient lateral patellar dislocation, where do you expect the classic bone bruises? | Medial patella + anterolateral femoral condyle | Posterolateral tibia + lateral femoral condyle | Anterior tibial plateau | Medial femoral condyle alone | Correct: Medial patella + anterolateral femoral condyle | Lateral patellar dislocation classically bruises the INFEROMEDIAL patellar facet and the ANTEROLATERAL femoral condyle as the patella relocates — and tears the MPFL, most often at its femoral (adductor tubercle) origin (patellar-sided and mid-substance tears also occur).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Get oriented
text: Sagittal T1 (non-fat-sat). Fat and bone marrow are BRIGHT; joint fluid is dark — the reverse of the PD-FS. Anterior is left, posterior right. T1's strength is marrow and anatomy; read it alongside the PD-FS (fluid/edema) at the same level.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral condyle
text: Low-signal cortex with BRIGHT fatty marrow (unsuppressed on T1). T1 is the most sensitive sequence for the marrow itself. | Femoral condyle

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial plateau
text: Uniform bright marrow below the joint. A discrete DARK line within the bright marrow is the classic T1 sign of a fracture. | Tibial plateau

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Extensor mechanism — top
text: The quadriceps tendon inserts on the superior pole of the patella. Tendons are low signal on T1, just as on PD-FS. | Quadriceps tendon | Patella

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar tendon
text: Uniformly low signal from the inferior pole of the patella to the tibial tubercle. | Patellar tendon

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Hoffa's fat pad
text: Bright fat (unsuppressed on T1) behind the patellar tendon. | Hoffa's (infrapatellar) fat pad

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscus
text: Low-signal triangles at the joint line — dark on T1, as on PD-FS. | Anterior horn (meniscus) | Posterior horn (meniscus)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bone marrow — T1's job
text: Normal marrow is uniformly bright (fatty) on T1. The key abnormal finding is FOCAL DARK signal — a fracture line, infiltration, or a lesion. (Joint fluid is dark on T1, so it is the PD-FS — not T1 — that you use for cartilage and effusion.) | Bone marrow

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Posterior cruciate ligament
text: The PCL is a smooth, continuous low-signal arc through the posterior notch. | Posterior cruciate ligament

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Popliteal vessels
text: The popliteal artery and vein in the popliteal fossa posteriorly. | Popliteal vessels

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Femoral condyle | Patella | Tibial plateau | Fabella | Correct: Femoral condyle | The large rounded bone with low-signal cortex and bright fatty marrow is the femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Tibial plateau | Femoral condyle | Patella | Fibular head | Correct: Tibial plateau | The bone below the joint line is the tibial plateau.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Patella | Femoral condyle | Quadriceps tendon | Tibial tubercle | Correct: Patella | The anterior bone at the front of the knee is the patella.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Quadriceps tendon | Patellar tendon | Iliotibial band | Prepatellar bursa | Correct: Quadriceps tendon | The tendon inserting on the superior pole of the patella is the quadriceps tendon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Patellar tendon | Quadriceps tendon | ACL | Popliteus tendon | Correct: Patellar tendon | The low-signal band from the inferior patella to the tibial tubercle is the patellar tendon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Hoffa's infrapatellar fat pad | Joint effusion | Baker's cyst | Prefemoral fat | Correct: Hoffa's infrapatellar fat pad | The bright fat behind the patellar tendon is Hoffa's (infrapatellar) fat pad.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Posterior horn of the meniscus | PCL | Posterior joint capsule | Popliteus tendon | Correct: Posterior horn of the meniscus | The low-signal triangle at the posterior joint line is the posterior horn of the meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Anterior horn of the meniscus | Hoffa's fat pad | ACL | Transverse ligament | Correct: Anterior horn of the meniscus | The low-signal triangle at the anterior joint line is the anterior horn of the meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this T1 image, how does normal bone marrow appear?
text: On this T1 image, how does normal bone marrow appear? | Bright (fatty) | Dark (low signal) | The same signal as joint fluid | Bright only with fat suppression | Correct: Bright (fatty) | Normal marrow is bright on T1 because fatty marrow is not suppressed. Focal DARK marrow signal (e.g., a fracture line) is the key abnormality T1 detects.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | Popliteal vessels | PCL | Baker's cyst | Semimembranosus tendon | Correct: Popliteal vessels | The tubular structures in the popliteal fossa posteriorly are the popliteal artery and vein.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On T1 you spot a faint dark line in the bright marrow. Which sequence best confirms the surrounding marrow edema?
text: On T1 you spot a faint dark line in the bright marrow. Which sequence best confirms the surrounding marrow edema? | Fluid-sensitive PD/T2 fat-sat | T1 (this sequence) | Gradient echo | The localizer | Correct: Fluid-sensitive PD/T2 fat-sat | T1 shows the fracture line and marrow replacement; the fat-suppressed fluid-sensitive sequence (PD/T2 FS) best shows the surrounding bright edema. Read marrow on both.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral condyle
text: Trace the cartilage as an even gray layer over the condyle — focal thinning, a fissure, or a flap is a chondral injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial plateau
text: Scan the marrow for a discrete dark fracture line; pivot-shift contusions sit at the posterolateral plateau.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Extensor mechanism — top
text: The quadriceps tendon is trilaminar — partial tears hide in the DEEP (vastus intermedius) layer with intact superficial fibers.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar tendon
text: Normally a few mm thick and uniformly dark; focal thickening = tendinopathy (usually proximal), a fluid-filled gap = rupture.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Hoffa's fat pad
text: Edema in the superolateral fat pad is the clue to anterior (Hoffa) impingement — easy to overlook.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscus — the dark bow-ties
text: Count the bow-ties on the body slices, then trace the lateral transverse/meniscofemoral/popliteal-hiatus relationships before calling a tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscus
text: Count the bow-ties on the body slices, then inspect the lateral popliteal hiatus so a normal fascicle/hiatus does not become a false-positive tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Cartilage & joint fluid
text: A small effusion is physiologic; the cartilage surface should be smooth and uniform — follow it slice to slice.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Anterior cruciate ligament
text: A normal ACL parallels Blumensaat's line; if the fibers bow, sag below the roof, or go wavy/edematous, suspect a tear. Secondary signs — a deep lateral notch sign, anterior tibial translation, and pivot-shift contusions — clinch it.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Posterior cruciate ligament
text: Loss of the smooth PCL arc or a buckled contour points to anterior tibial translation from an ACL tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Popliteal vessels
text: Don't mistake flow-related signal for thrombus; a Baker's cyst sits medially, between the medial gastrocnemius and semimembranosus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial spines (intercondylar eminence)
text: Use the spines as your landmark for the true mid-joint slice and the cruciate origins.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Menisci — the wedges
text: Measure medial extrusion at the mid-body — >3 mm beyond the tibial margin is significant and should prompt a deliberate root/cartilage search; extrusion alone is not a root tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Weight-bearing cartilage
text: Compare medial vs lateral cartilage thickness directly — coronal makes early, subtle loss obvious.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Medial collateral ligament
text: Grade by depth (superficial vs deep) and note the level — proximal/femoral injuries scar; distal (Stener-like) may not.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Lateral collateral ligament (LCL)
text: The posterolateral corner is commonly missed — trace the LCL, popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament on the more posterior slices.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patella
text: Check patellar tilt and the cartilage of the median ridge and facets — the thickest cartilage in the body.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral trochlea
text: A shallow or flat trochlea (dysplasia) is the key predisposing factor for lateral patellar dislocation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Trochlear groove
text: Flattening of the sulcus is the cardinal dysplasia sign — assess the sulcus angle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellofemoral cartilage
text: Scrutinize the median ridge for fissures; patellar chondral loss is a common cause of anterior knee pain.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MPFL / medial retinaculum
text: After a dislocation the MPFL tears at the medial patella or the femoral (adductor tubercle) end — look at both.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bone marrow — T1's job
text: A dark band within bright marrow = fracture; diffuse marrow darkening = infiltration. T1 is your marrow sequence.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Anterior cruciate ligament
text: variant: A thick, bulbous ACL with increased signal but CONTINUOUS, parallel 'celery-stalk' fibers and no secondary signs is mucoid degeneration, not a tear — don't operate. | measure: Quantify the secondary signs of a tear: anterior tibial translation >5 mm (lateral sagittal through the mid-lateral compartment) or a lateral femoral sulcus >1.5 mm deep.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Posterior cruciate ligament
text: variant: A PCL that is thickened with diffusely increased signal but keeps its general contour is a partial/interstitial tear, not simple contusion — correlate with the posterior drawer.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Medial collateral ligament
text: measure: The superficial MCL runs from the medial femoral epicondyle to ~5–7 cm below the joint line on the tibia — trace its full length on coronal before calling it intact.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscus — the dark bow-ties
text: measure: Three or more contiguous bow-tie slices (4–5 mm sagittal) — or a central coronal body width >13–15 mm — is a discoid meniscus, not normal.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscus
text: variant: Linear grade-2 signal that does NOT reach an articular surface is degeneration, common after 40 — call a tear only when surface-reaching signal is seen on two consecutive slices (two-slice-touch). At the lateral meniscus, the popliteal hiatus and popliteomeniscal fascicles can create a smooth normal gap near the popliteus tendon; loss of fascicles, peripheral fluid, or hypermobile-meniscus context is abnormal.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patella
text: variant: A subchondral lucency in the SUPEROLATERAL quadrant with intact overlying cartilage is the benign dorsal defect of the patella — don't call it an osteochondral lesion or infection.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral trochlea
text: measure: Trochlear dysplasia and elevated TT-TG are separate instability risk markers. TT-TG >20 mm is the classic abnormal cutoff, but MRI values vary with landmarks, slice choice, and knee position — do not use one number as a standalone surgical rule.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Trochlear groove
text: measure: Read the crossing sign on the sagittal/lateral view — the trochlear-floor line crossing the anterior cortex of the lateral femoral condyle marks a flat (dysplastic) groove.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MPFL / medial retinaculum
text: measure: Screen for predisposing anatomy: patella alta (Insall-Salvati >1.2) and elevated TT-TG. The classic >20 mm TT-TG cutoff is a risk marker, but MRI technique and landmarks can shift the value by a few millimeters.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bone marrow — T1's job
text: variant: Patchy marrow that stays BRIGHTER than muscle and disc on T1, is symmetric, and spares the epiphyses is benign red-marrow reconversion — signal at/below muscle is the red flag for infiltration.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral condyle
text: variant: An OCD interface line only as bright as cartilage (not fluid), especially in a skeletally immature knee, suggests granulation tissue and a STABLE fragment; a fluid-bright (T2) rim fully undercutting it means unstable.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Cartilage & joint fluid
text: variant: Focal meniscal/cartilage signal near 55° to B0 (e.g., posterior horn lateral meniscus by the popliteal hiatus) that brightens on short-TE PD but fades on T2 is magic-angle artifact — don't call it a tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellofemoral cartilage
text: variant: A thin low-signal line paralleling the cartilage surface that does NOT reach a surface and shifts when the phase-encode direction is changed is Gibbs (truncation) artifact, not a fissure or delamination.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Anterior cruciate ligament
text: ultrasound: seen: false | appearance: The cruciates are deep and intra-articular, so standard knee ultrasound cannot reliably image the ACL — MRI (or arthroscopy) is the modality. US may show only indirect clues such as an effusion or a Segond cortical avulsion fragment.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Posterior cruciate ligament
text: ultrasound: seen: false | appearance: Like the ACL, the PCL sits deep in the notch and is not assessable on routine US; a posterior approach occasionally glimpses the proximal PCL, but MRI is the modality.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Medial collateral ligament
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: A striated, three-layer band over the medial joint line running from the medial femoral condyle to the proximal tibia — the superficial MCL is the key layer. A tear shows hypoechoic swelling, fibre disruption, or valgus laxity. | tip: Coronal long-axis over the medial joint line; gentle valgus stress can unmask laxity. | image: src: /images/teaching/us/mcl.jpg | caption: Normal MCL — striated band over the medial joint line (coronal long-axis). | attribution: Lutz et al., KSSTA 2021 (CC BY 4.0)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Lateral collateral ligament (LCL)
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: A discrete cord-like ligament from the lateral femoral condyle to the fibular head. Trace the rest of the posterolateral corner on adjacent images — the biceps femoris, popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament. | tip: Coronal long-axis toward the fibular head; a crossed-leg ('figure-4') position tightens and highlights it. | image: src: /images/teaching/us/lcl.jpg | caption: Normal LCL — cord-like ligament toward the fibular head (long-axis). | attribution: Wu et al., Insights into Imaging 2024 (CC BY 4.0)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Extensor mechanism — top
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: A fibrillar, hyperechoic, trilaminar tendon inserting on the superior pole of the patella; partial tears hide in the deep (vastus intermedius) layer. Tilt the probe to defeat anisotropy (an artefactual hypoechoic band that mimics a tear). | tip: Longitudinal over the suprapatellar region, knee slightly flexed to put the tendon under tension. | image: src: /images/teaching/us/quad-tendon.jpg | caption: Normal quadriceps tendon — fibrillar, hyperechoic, inserting on the patella (long-axis). | attribution: Vomer et al., Cureus 2023 (CC BY 4.0)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar tendon
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: A uniform fibrillar band a few millimetres thick from the inferior patella to the tibial tubercle. Focal proximal hypoechoic thickening — often with neovascularity on Doppler — is patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee); a fluid-filled gap is a tear. | tip: Longitudinal from the inferior patellar pole down to the tibial tubercle; flex the knee to reduce anisotropy. | image: src: /images/teaching/us/patellar-tendon.jpg | caption: Normal patellar tendon — uniform fibrillar band (long-axis). | attribution: Priyanka et al., Cureus 2026 (CC BY 4.0)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patella
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: US shows the patellar cortex and the attached quadriceps and patellar tendons; the retropatellar cartilage and patellar tracking are MRI/CT questions, not US. | tip: Slide longitudinally from the quadriceps tendon across the patella to the patellar tendon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral trochlea
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: With the knee maximally flexed, the anterior trochlear hyaline cartilage appears as a smooth anechoic band over the trochlea — useful for anterior chondral loss. Trochlear DYSPLASIA (sulcus depth/shape) is judged on axial MRI/CT, not US. | tip: Maximal knee flexion, transverse suprapatellar window over the trochlea. | image: src: /images/teaching/us/trochlear-cartilage.jpg | caption: Normal trochlear hyaline cartilage — anechoic band (*) over the trochlea (transverse, knee flexed). | attribution: Kauppinen et al., 2021 (CC BY 4.0)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Trochlear groove
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: The anterior trochlear cartilage is visible as an anechoic band with the knee flexed, but the sulcus angle/depth that defines dysplasia is an axial MRI/CT measurement. | tip: Maximal knee flexion, transverse suprapatellar window.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscus — the dark bow-ties
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: US reaches only the PERIPHERAL meniscus and parameniscal cysts at the joint line (a triangular hyperechoic wedge); the body, roots, and intrasubstance tears are an MRI domain. | tip: Coronal over the medial or lateral joint line, leg slightly stressed open.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscus
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: US reaches only the PERIPHERAL meniscus and parameniscal cysts at the joint line; the body, roots, and intrasubstance signal are MRI territory. | tip: Coronal over the joint line.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Menisci — the wedges
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: Only the peripheral meniscus and a parameniscal cyst are accessible on US; meniscal extrusion can be measured at the joint line, but root and intrasubstance tears need MRI. | tip: Coronal over the mid-joint line to assess peripheral extrusion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Cartilage & joint fluid
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: US excels at JOINT FLUID — anechoic distension of the suprapatellar recess, and it is more sensitive than the exam for a small effusion. For CARTILAGE, only the anterior femoral (trochlear) surface is reachable (knee flexed); the weight-bearing condylar surface stays hidden. | tip: Suprapatellar recess long-axis for effusion; maximal flexion + transverse for trochlear cartilage. | image: src: /images/teaching/us/effusion.jpg | caption: Joint effusion (star) distending the suprapatellar recess — anechoic fluid (long-axis). | attribution: Vomer et al., Cureus 2023 (CC BY 4.0)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Weight-bearing cartilage
text: ultrasound: seen: false | appearance: The weight-bearing femoral condylar cartilage is screened behind the patella and tibia on US — only the anterior trochlear cartilage is reachable (knee flexed). MRI is the modality for weight-bearing chondral loss.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral condyle
text: ultrasound: seen: false | appearance: The condylar weight-bearing cartilage is not accessible on standard US; MRI grades chondral injury. (The anterior trochlear cartilage IS visible with the knee flexed.)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial plateau
text: ultrasound: seen: false | appearance: Subchondral and marrow injury of the plateau are MRI findings; US sees only the cortical surface and adjacent soft tissue, not the marrow or a non-displaced fracture line.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bone marrow — T1's job
text: ultrasound: seen: false | appearance: Ultrasound cannot assess marrow — sound does not penetrate cortical bone. Marrow oedema, contusions, and occult fractures are MRI-only (T1 plus a fluid-sensitive sequence).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Popliteal vessels
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: US is first-line here: the popliteal vein and artery (compress the vein to exclude DVT), and a Baker's cyst — an anechoic collection with a neck between the medial gastrocnemius and semimembranosus. Look for rupture (fluid dissecting distally), a classic DVT mimic. | tip: Transverse in the popliteal fossa; compress the vein to assess for thrombus. | image: src: /images/teaching/us/bakers-cyst.jpg | caption: Baker's (popliteal) cyst — well-defined anechoic fluid collection on B-mode US. | attribution: Intermedichbo, Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 3.0)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MPFL / medial retinaculum
text: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: The MPFL runs from the superomedial patella toward the adductor tubercle; after a lateral patellar dislocation US can show a tear at the patellar attachment as hypoechoic disruption. | tip: Transverse at the superomedial patella, following the retinaculum toward the femur.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 19-year-old soccer player sustains a non-contact pivoting injury. Radiographs show a small vertically oriented cortical avulsion off the proximal lateral tibia just below the joint line. Which intra-articular injury is most strongly associated with this radiographic finding?
text: ACL — Segond fracture / associated injury | A 19-year-old soccer player sustains a non-contact pivoting injury. Radiographs show a small vertically oriented cortical avulsion off the proximal lateral tibia just below the joint line. Which intra-articular injury is most strongly associated with this radiographic finding? | Anterior cruciate ligament tear | Posterior cruciate ligament tear | Quadriceps tendon rupture | Lateral meniscus discoid variant | Correct: Anterior cruciate ligament tear | A vertical cortical avulsion of the lateral tibia just below the joint line is a Segond fracture, an avulsion at the anterolateral capsular/iliotibial attachment that is highly associated (>75-90%) with ACL rupture. A PCL tear is instead linked with a posterior (reverse Segond) medial tibial avulsion, not this lateral fragment, so it is the weaker association.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 28-year-old restrained front-seat passenger strikes the dashboard with a flexed knee in a head-on collision. He has a posterior sag of the tibia on exam and a posterior drawer is positive. Which finding on MRI would best confirm the suspected primary ligament injury?
text: PCL — dashboard mechanism / next step | A 28-year-old restrained front-seat passenger strikes the dashboard with a flexed knee in a head-on collision. He has a posterior sag of the tibia on exam and a posterior drawer is positive. Which finding on MRI would best confirm the suspected primary ligament injury? | Discontinuity and increased signal within the thick curved band in the posterior intercondylar notch | A straight striated band paralleling Blumensaat's line that is wavy and lax | Fluid signal at the posteromedial meniscocapsular junction | A cortical avulsion at the anterolateral tibia | Correct: Discontinuity and increased signal within the thick curved band in the posterior intercondylar notch | The dashboard mechanism drives the proximal tibia posteriorly and tears the PCL, the thick curved low-signal band in the posterior notch; disruption or increased intrasubstance signal there confirms it. A wavy band along Blumensaat's line describes the ACL, and the anterolateral tibial avulsion is a Segond fracture tied to ACL rather than PCL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 23-year-old with a locked knee that will not fully extend has a sagittal MRI showing a low-signal band lying anteriorly within the intercondylar notch, paralleling and just inferior to the PCL. Body-slice images show fewer than two consecutive meniscal bow-ties. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: Meniscus — bucket-handle / displaced fragment | A 23-year-old with a locked knee that will not fully extend has a sagittal MRI showing a low-signal band lying anteriorly within the intercondylar notch, paralleling and just inferior to the PCL. Body-slice images show fewer than two consecutive meniscal bow-ties. What is the most likely diagnosis? | Bucket-handle meniscal tear with a displaced fragment | Mucoid degeneration of the ACL | Normal meniscofemoral ligament of Humphry | Cyclops lesion after ACL reconstruction | Correct: Bucket-handle meniscal tear with a displaced fragment | A displaced inner meniscal fragment lying in the notch inferior and parallel to the PCL produces the double-PCL sign, and an absent bow-tie (fewer than two consecutive) confirms a bucket-handle tear that mechanically blocks extension. The meniscofemoral ligament of Humphry runs anterior to the PCL but is a thin discrete band that does not abolish the bow-tie count or lock the knee.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 56-year-old reports a pop while rising from a squat and now has medial joint-line pain. Coronal MRI shows the medial meniscus body displaced 5 mm beyond the tibial margin, and the posterior horn near the root appears truncated. Which feature most reliably supports a posterior medial meniscal root tear?
text: Meniscal root tear — two-slice-touch / extrusion | A 56-year-old reports a pop while rising from a squat and now has medial joint-line pain. Coronal MRI shows the medial meniscus body displaced 5 mm beyond the tibial margin, and the posterior horn near the root appears truncated. Which feature most reliably supports a posterior medial meniscal root tear? | Coronal extrusion of 5 mm with a truncated (ghost) posterior root | Intrasubstance grade 2 signal not reaching a surface | A double-PCL sign in the notch | A parameniscal cyst at the anterior horn | Correct: Coronal extrusion of 5 mm with a truncated (ghost) posterior root | Medial coronal extrusion of at least 3 mm together with an absent or truncated 'ghost' root at the posterior attachment is the signature of a posterior root tear, which functionally behaves like a total meniscectomy. Intrasubstance grade 2 signal that does not reach a surface is degeneration, not a full-thickness root detachment, so it does not establish the diagnosis.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 15-year-old gymnast has an osteochondritis dissecans lesion at the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. On fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed images, which finding would most specifically indicate that the fragment is unstable and may warrant surgical fixation?
text: Cartilage / OCD stability | A 15-year-old gymnast has an osteochondritis dissecans lesion at the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. On fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed images, which finding would most specifically indicate that the fragment is unstable and may warrant surgical fixation? | A fluid-signal-intensity line completely encircling and undercutting the fragment | Mild marrow edema in the parent bone deep to the lesion | A granulation interface line only as bright as articular cartilage | An en-face fragment diameter of 6 mm with intact overlying cartilage | Correct: A fluid-signal-intensity line completely encircling and undercutting the fragment | A rim of fluid-intensity signal that completely surrounds and undercuts the fragment indicates synovial fluid dissecting the interface and is the most reliable De Smet sign of instability. A high-signal interface line only as bright as cartilage usually reflects vascular granulation tissue, especially in skeletally immature patients, and is not by itself a sign of instability.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 65-year-old woman has sudden atraumatic medial knee pain. MRI shows a subchondral low-signal line in the weight-bearing medial femoral condyle paralleling and concave toward the articular surface, with surrounding diffuse marrow edema. What is the best diagnosis?
text: SIFK vs osteonecrosis / marrow | A 65-year-old woman has sudden atraumatic medial knee pain. MRI shows a subchondral low-signal line in the weight-bearing medial femoral condyle paralleling and concave toward the articular surface, with surrounding diffuse marrow edema. What is the best diagnosis? | Subchondral insufficiency fracture | Primary spontaneous osteonecrosis with epiphyseal infarct | Transient bone marrow edema syndrome | Medial femoral condyle osteochondroma | Correct: Subchondral insufficiency fracture | A subchondral fracture line paralleling the articular surface with adjacent marrow edema in an older patient with atraumatic pain is a subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIFK). Primary osteonecrosis instead shows a serpentine double-line sign demarcating an infarct away from the subchondral plate, so the subchondral, surface-parallel line favors SIFK over a true epiphyseal infarct.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 16-year-old sustains a first-time lateral patellar dislocation that has spontaneously reduced. MRI shows an inferomedial patellar facet and anterolateral femoral condyle bone bruise and an MPFL tear, with no displaced osteochondral fragment and a TT-TG distance of 12 mm. What is the most appropriate initial management?
text: Patellar dislocation / MPFL management | A 16-year-old sustains a first-time lateral patellar dislocation that has spontaneously reduced. MRI shows an inferomedial patellar facet and anterolateral femoral condyle bone bruise and an MPFL tear, with no displaced osteochondral fragment and a TT-TG distance of 12 mm. What is the most appropriate initial management? | Nonoperative rehabilitation with bracing and quadriceps strengthening | Acute MPFL reconstruction with tibial tubercle osteotomy | Lateral retinacular release alone | Immediate patellectomy | Correct: Nonoperative rehabilitation with bracing and quadriceps strengthening | A first-time dislocation without a loose osteochondral body and with a normal TT-TG (well under the ~20 mm threshold) is managed nonoperatively with bracing and rehabilitation. Tibial tubercle osteotomy is reserved for an elevated TT-TG, so adding it here would be over-treatment given the normal alignment measurement.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 27-year-old has an MRI-confirmed posterolateral corner injury with fibular collateral ligament, popliteus, and popliteofibular ligament disruption after a varus hyperextension blow. The surgeon plans cruciate reconstruction. Why is recognizing and addressing this posterolateral corner injury before isolated cruciate surgery most important?
text: Posterolateral corner — combined injury / graft failure | A 27-year-old has an MRI-confirmed posterolateral corner injury with fibular collateral ligament, popliteus, and popliteofibular ligament disruption after a varus hyperextension blow. The surgeon plans cruciate reconstruction. Why is recognizing and addressing this posterolateral corner injury before isolated cruciate surgery most important? | Unrecognized posterolateral corner injury is a leading cause of cruciate graft failure from residual rotational and varus instability | Posterolateral corner injuries reliably heal nonoperatively, so cruciate surgery can simply be delayed | The posterolateral corner has no effect on cruciate graft loading once the cruciate is reconstructed | Posterolateral corner injuries occur in isolation, so they rarely change the surgical plan | Correct: Unrecognized posterolateral corner injury is a leading cause of cruciate graft failure from residual rotational and varus instability | The posterolateral corner is the primary restraint to varus and external-rotation, and a missed combined injury leaves residual rotational/varus laxity that overloads and stretches out a cruciate graft, a recognized cause of reconstruction failure, so it must be addressed concurrently. The distractors are wrong because high-grade posterolateral corner injuries usually require repair or reconstruction rather than healing alone, and they very commonly accompany (rather than spare) cruciate injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 24-year-old presents after a pivoting injury. Sagittal MRI shows the ACL is poorly visualized. To use the posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) line sign as a secondary indicator of ACL tear, you draw a line along the straight distal segment of the PCL and extend it proximally and anteriorly. In an intact knee, where should this line intersect?
text: Cruciate ligaments (ACL/PCL) | A 24-year-old presents after a pivoting injury. Sagittal MRI shows the ACL is poorly visualized. To use the posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) line sign as a secondary indicator of ACL tear, you draw a line along the straight distal segment of the PCL and extend it proximally and anteriorly. In an intact knee, where should this line intersect? | It should intersect the femur, crossing the distal femoral metaphysis/medullary canal | It should intersect the tibial plateau just anterior to the tibial tubercle | It should fail to intersect the femur and instead pass anterior to it | It should intersect the patella at its lower pole | Correct: It should intersect the femur, crossing the distal femoral metaphysis/medullary canal | Normally the extended PCL line intersects the femoral medullary cavity; with a torn ACL the PCL becomes more buckled/redundant (more sharply curved) from anterior tibial translation, so the line falls short and fails to intersect the femur (a positive sign). It is a secondary sign best combined with primary findings.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On MRI of a knee with an acute ACL tear, you measure anterior tibial translation by referencing the posterior margins of the lateral femoral condyle and lateral tibial plateau on a sagittal image. Which threshold of anterior tibial subluxation is most specific for a complete ACL tear?
text: Cruciate ligaments (ACL/PCL) | On MRI of a knee with an acute ACL tear, you measure anterior tibial translation by referencing the posterior margins of the lateral femoral condyle and lateral tibial plateau on a sagittal image. Which threshold of anterior tibial subluxation is most specific for a complete ACL tear? | ≥ 1 mm | ≥ 3 mm | ≥ 5 mm | ≥ 10 mm | Correct: ≥ 5 mm | Anterior translation of the lateral tibial plateau of ≥ 5 mm relative to the lateral femoral condyle is highly specific (though not very sensitive) for a complete ACL tear; smaller degrees overlap with normal laxity.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 52-year-old with chronic, atraumatic knee pain has an ACL that is diffusely thickened and ill-defined with intermediate-to-high T2 signal, yet the individual fibers remain continuous and parallel, producing a 'celery-stalk' appearance. There is often an associated finding in the adjacent bone. Which is the single best diagnosis and its characteristic associated feature?
text: Cruciate ligaments (ACL/PCL) | A 52-year-old with chronic, atraumatic knee pain has an ACL that is diffusely thickened and ill-defined with intermediate-to-high T2 signal, yet the individual fibers remain continuous and parallel, producing a 'celery-stalk' appearance. There is often an associated finding in the adjacent bone. Which is the single best diagnosis and its characteristic associated feature? | Acute complete ACL tear with a Segond fracture of the lateral tibia | ACL mucoid degeneration, frequently associated with subchondral/intraosseous ganglion cysts at the tibial or femoral attachment | ACL graft impingement with a cyclops lesion in Hoffa's fat pad | ACL ganglion arising from the PCL with a deep lateral femoral notch sign | Correct: ACL mucoid degeneration, frequently associated with subchondral/intraosseous ganglion cysts at the tibial or femoral attachment | Mucoid degeneration shows a thickened ACL with continuous, intact fibers ('celery stalk' / increased signal but preserved striations) and is commonly accompanied by intraosseous ganglion cysts at the ligament insertions — distinguishing it from a tear, where fibers are discontinuous.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Reviewing the radiograph and MRI of an acute knee injury, you identify a deep lateral femoral notch (terminal/condylopatellar sulcus). Beyond simply suggesting an ACL tear, an abnormally deep notch is considered a useful marker. Which depth threshold is the accepted cutoff that strongly suggests an associated ACL injury?
text: Cruciate ligaments (ACL/PCL) | Reviewing the radiograph and MRI of an acute knee injury, you identify a deep lateral femoral notch (terminal/condylopatellar sulcus). Beyond simply suggesting an ACL tear, an abnormally deep notch is considered a useful marker. Which depth threshold is the accepted cutoff that strongly suggests an associated ACL injury? | Greater than 0.5 mm | Greater than 1.5 mm | Greater than 4 mm | Greater than 8 mm | Correct: Greater than 1.5 mm | A lateral femoral (condylopatellar) sulcus depth greater than 1.5 mm is the classic threshold strongly associated with ACL disruption, reflecting an osteochondral impaction injury as the anteriorly subluxed tibia engages the lateral femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MR images with 4-5 mm slice thickness, which finding most reliably distinguishes a discoid lateral meniscus from a normal lateral meniscus?
text: Menisci | On sagittal MR images with 4-5 mm slice thickness, which finding most reliably distinguishes a discoid lateral meniscus from a normal lateral meniscus? | A minimum coronal body width of 6-8 mm on the central coronal image | The 'bow-tie' (continuous body) appearance on 3 or more contiguous sagittal slices | Absence of the normal meniscofemoral ligament on the Wrisberg side | Increased intrameniscal grade 2 signal in the posterior horn | Correct: The 'bow-tie' (continuous body) appearance on 3 or more contiguous sagittal slices | A normal meniscus shows the contiguous 'bow-tie' body on at most 2 consecutive 4-5 mm sagittal slices; 3 or more contiguous bow-tie segments (or a central coronal width >13-15 mm) indicates a discoid meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 58-year-old has acute medial knee pain. Coronal MRI shows the posterior root signal disrupted with the meniscal body displaced 4 mm beyond the tibial margin. Regarding the 3 mm extrusion threshold for a medial meniscal posterior root tear, which statement is most accurate?
text: Menisci | A 58-year-old has acute medial knee pain. Coronal MRI shows the posterior root signal disrupted with the meniscal body displaced 4 mm beyond the tibial margin. Regarding the 3 mm extrusion threshold for a medial meniscal posterior root tear, which statement is most accurate? | The 3 mm threshold is a useful high-specificity clue but relatively insensitive, so extrusion <3 mm does not exclude a root tear | The 3 mm threshold is both highly sensitive and highly specific for root tears | Extrusion is best and most reproducibly measured on sagittal rather than coronal images | Meniscal extrusion of this degree is a normal finding and requires no root evaluation | Correct: The 3 mm threshold is a useful high-specificity clue but relatively insensitive, so extrusion <3 mm does not exclude a root tear | Coronal medial meniscal extrusion >=3 mm is a significant clue in many root-tear studies, but it is only modestly sensitive and should not be treated as diagnostic by itself. Sub-3 mm extrusion does not exclude a root tear; the 'ghost' (absent/blunted root) sign should also be sought.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: During ACL reconstruction planning, you are asked about a suspected ramp lesion. Which statement is most accurate regarding its MRI and arthroscopic evaluation?
text: Menisci | During ACL reconstruction planning, you are asked about a suspected ramp lesion. Which statement is most accurate regarding its MRI and arthroscopic evaluation? | It is a radial tear of the posterior horn lateral meniscus best seen on coronal images | Standard anterior-portal arthroscopy reliably visualizes it, so MRI adds little | Fluid signal between the posterior horn medial meniscus and the joint capsule on fluid-sensitive sequences is a key sign, and the lesion is frequently missed without posteromedial probing | It is defined as extrusion of the medial meniscus body greater than 5 mm | Correct: Fluid signal between the posterior horn medial meniscus and the joint capsule on fluid-sensitive sequences is a key sign, and the lesion is frequently missed without posteromedial probing | A ramp lesion is a tear at the posteromedial meniscocapsular junction; high T2 fluid signal/edema posterior to the posterior horn raises suspicion, and a substantial proportion are missed on standard anterior-portal viewing without a posteromedial (trans-notch) probing approach.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI of a knee with an ACL tear, you see vertical signal at the junction of the posterior horn lateral meniscus and an obliquely coursing structure passing toward the PCL. Which feature best favors a true posterior horn tear over a meniscofemoral (Wrisberg) ligament pseudotear?
text: Menisci | On sagittal MRI of a knee with an ACL tear, you see vertical signal at the junction of the posterior horn lateral meniscus and an obliquely coursing structure passing toward the PCL. Which feature best favors a true posterior horn tear over a meniscofemoral (Wrisberg) ligament pseudotear? | The vertical signal can be traced contiguously into the PCL through the intercondylar notch | The apparent meniscal cleft persists 4 or more sagittal images lateral to the PCL | The structure lies posterior to the PCL on the Wrisberg side | The signal is seen on only a single sagittal image near the midline | Correct: The apparent meniscal cleft persists 4 or more sagittal images lateral to the PCL | A Wrisberg/Humphrey pseudotear is created where the meniscofemoral ligament inserts on the posterior horn; an apparent cleft extending >=4 sagittal images lateral to the PCL exceeds the ligament's reach and should be treated as a true tear, especially with an ACL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 16-year-old soccer player has a juvenile osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) lesion at the classic lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. On fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed sequences, which finding is MOST specific for an UNSTABLE fragment?
text: Cartilage & osteochondral | A 16-year-old soccer player has a juvenile osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) lesion at the classic lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. On fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed sequences, which finding is MOST specific for an UNSTABLE fragment? | A thin, uninterrupted line of high T2 signal isointense to articular cartilage along the fragment interface | A high-T2-signal rim completely surrounding the fragment that follows fluid signal intensity and undercuts it | Mild bone-marrow edema in the parent bone deep to the lesion | An en-face fragment diameter measuring 6 mm with intact overlying cartilage | Correct: A high-T2-signal rim completely surrounding the fragment that follows fluid signal intensity and undercuts it | A fluid-signal-intensity rim that completely encircles and undercuts the fragment indicates synovial fluid dissecting the interface and is the most reliable De Smet sign of instability; a granulation-tissue interface line is only as bright as cartilage, not fluid. Secondary signs include subchondral cysts >5 mm and a focal cartilage defect.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When applying the De Smet MRI criteria for OCD fragment instability, the SPECIFICITY of a T2-bright line at the fragment-parent interface improves substantially when which additional qualifier is present?
text: Cartilage & osteochondral | When applying the De Smet MRI criteria for OCD fragment instability, the SPECIFICITY of a T2-bright line at the fragment-parent interface improves substantially when which additional qualifier is present? | The patient is skeletally immature with open physes | The high-signal line is matched by a breach (defect) in the overlying articular cartilage on fluid-sensitive images | The lesion is located in the patellofemoral compartment | The fragment measures less than 5 mm in greatest dimension | Correct: The high-signal line is matched by a breach (defect) in the overlying articular cartilage on fluid-sensitive images | In skeletally immature patients a T2-bright interface line frequently reflects vascular granulation tissue rather than fluid, lowering specificity; instability is far more reliable when that line is accompanied by a discontinuity in the overlying cartilage, confirming communication with the joint.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A surgeon asks you to distinguish chondral DELAMINATION from a simple partial-thickness fissure on knee MRI. Which finding is MOST characteristic of delamination?
text: Cartilage & osteochondral | A surgeon asks you to distinguish chondral DELAMINATION from a simple partial-thickness fissure on knee MRI. Which finding is MOST characteristic of delamination? | A linear high-signal cleft extending perpendicularly from the articular surface into but not through the cartilage | A fluid-signal line at the cartilage-bone (osteochondral) junction with the overlying cartilage surface still intact | Focal full-thickness cartilage loss exposing subchondral bone (ICRS grade 4) | Diffuse intrachondral T2 hyperintensity without a discrete defect | Correct: A fluid-signal line at the cartilage-bone (osteochondral) junction with the overlying cartilage surface still intact | Delamination is a shear separation of cartilage from subchondral bone at the deep tidemark/calcified-cartilage zone, seen as a fluid-signal line paralleling the bone with a deceptively intact articular surface; it can be missed at arthroscopic probing of the surface.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A sports-medicine fellow measures a tibial tubercle–trochlear groove (TT-TG) distance of 22 mm on an axial MRI in a patient with recurrent lateral patellar instability. Above which commonly cited threshold does the TT-TG distance become a recognized risk factor for patellar instability and an indication potentially favoring tibial tubercle medialization?
text: Patellofemoral & extensor | A sports-medicine fellow measures a tibial tubercle–trochlear groove (TT-TG) distance of 22 mm on an axial MRI in a patient with recurrent lateral patellar instability. Above which commonly cited threshold does the TT-TG distance become a recognized risk factor for patellar instability and an indication potentially favoring tibial tubercle medialization? | 8 mm | 12 mm | 20 mm | 30 mm | Correct: 20 mm | A TT-TG distance >=20 mm is the classic abnormal threshold associated with patellar instability, but it is a risk marker rather than a standalone surgical rule.
