text: Most parameniscal cysts have an underlying meniscal tear acting as a one-way valve. Trace the cyst back to the meniscus to find the likely causative tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: The deep MCL (capsular ligament) has two parts — the meniscofemoral and meniscotibial ligaments — both attaching to the medial meniscus. The meniscotibial portion tethers the meniscus to the tibia, so its injury can cause meniscal displacement.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: Post-meniscal repair: only fluid-bright signal matching joint fluid on T2 that reaches the surface is a definitive re-tear. Intermediate signal may represent healing.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: Count your meniscal body segments on sagittal images. Fewer than two bow-ties = absent bow-tie sign = bucket-handle until proven otherwise.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: Satisfaction of search: after finding the obvious ACL tear, force yourself to complete all 7 steps. 30–40% of cases have additional clinically relevant pathology.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: The Segond fracture (small lateral tibial avulsion) is pathognomonic for ACL injury. Look for it on coronal images.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: Bone marrow edema on T1 appears dark, but a discrete low-signal LINE within the edema = fracture (not just contusion). This changes weight-bearing protocol.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: OCD stability: fluid signal at the fragment-parent bone interface, cystic change, or displacement = unstable. Surrounding edema alone does NOT mean unstable.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: Large lateral parameniscal cysts near the fibular head can compress the common peroneal nerve, presenting with foot drop.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: In multi-ligament knee injuries (ACL + PCL), always evaluate the posterolateral corner (LCL, popliteus, popliteofibular ligament). PLC injury changes surgical timing.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: Lead your impression with the most clinically significant diagnosis. Surgeons scan the impression first — don’t bury the ACL tear in paragraph three.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: Bipartite patella: smooth, corticated margins, classically superolateral. Fracture: irregular, non-corticated margins with edema and effusion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: The quadriceps tendon is trilaminar. Partial tears most commonly involve the deep (vastus intermedius) layer; the robust superficial rectus femoris fibers often remain intact, so the tear can preserve active extension and be deceptively subtle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: When measuring meniscal extrusion, use the coronal image where the medial meniscal body is widest, with a reference line at the tibial plateau margin.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: Lipohemarthrosis (fat-fluid level on axial images) indicates an intra-articular fracture, even if you can’t see the fracture line.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
text: Window/level: widen for meniscal internal signal evaluation, narrow for cartilage surface defects. Adjusting as you move through your search pattern improves sensitivity.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Which imaging plane is the workhorse of knee MRI, and what structures does it best evaluate?
text: Which imaging plane is the workhorse of knee MRI, and what structures does it best evaluate? | Sagittal. It is the primary plane for evaluating the cruciate ligaments, the meniscal horns (the "bow-tie" body in cross-section), cartilage surfaces, and the extensor mechanism. (Meniscal body extrusion is measured on coronal.)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you confirm medial versus lateral orientation on coronal knee MRI images?
text: How do you confirm medial versus lateral orientation on coronal knee MRI images? | Identify the fibular head, which is always on the lateral side.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the single most important sequence for knee MRI, and why?
text: What is the single most important sequence for knee MRI, and why? | PD FS (proton density fat-suppressed). Fat suppression removes bright fat signal so edema and fluid stand out. It detects bone marrow edema, meniscal tears, ligament injuries, cartilage defects, and effusion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On T1-weighted images, what does normal marrow look like and what causes it to appear dark?
text: On T1-weighted images, what does normal marrow look like and what causes it to appear dark? | Normal marrow is fatty and therefore bright on T1. Any process that replaces marrow fat (fracture, tumor, infection, edema) appears dark on T1.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A finding is dark on T1 and bright on PD FS. What does this signal combination represent?
text: A finding is dark on T1 and bright on PD FS. What does this signal combination represent? | Almost always edema or fluid. A finding bright on both T1 and PD FS could be subacute blood (methemoglobin) or proteinaceous fluid.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is magic angle artifact, and how do you confirm it is not a true meniscal tear?
text: What is magic angle artifact, and how do you confirm it is not a true meniscal tear? | Magic angle artifact causes increased signal in structures oriented at 55 degrees to the main magnetic field on short TE sequences (T1, PD). It disappears on T2. If meniscal signal on PD disappears on T2, it is artifact, not a tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What artifact produces a linear bright line within the meniscus parallel to its surface, mimicking a horizontal tear?
text: What artifact produces a linear bright line within the meniscus parallel to its surface, mimicking a horizontal tear? | Truncation (Gibbs) artifact. It runs parallel to, and a fixed short distance from, the meniscal surface and does NOT contact the articular surface (a true tear reaches a surface). Reduce it by increasing the acquisition matrix or changing the phase-encode direction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How should you adjust window/level differently when evaluating menisci versus cartilage?
text: How should you adjust window/level differently when evaluating menisci versus cartilage? | Widen the window for meniscal evaluation to reveal internal signal changes within the dark meniscus. Narrow the window for cartilage to maximize contrast between the cartilage surface and adjacent fluid.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Name the three compartments of the knee.
text: Name the three compartments of the knee. | Medial tibiofemoral, lateral tibiofemoral, and patellofemoral.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the Segond fracture and what is its clinical significance?
text: What is the Segond fracture and what is its clinical significance? | A small avulsion fracture of the lateral tibial rim. It is pathognomonic for ACL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the significance of a tibial eminence fracture in an adolescent?
text: What is the significance of a tibial eminence fracture in an adolescent? | It represents an avulsion equivalent of an ACL tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why is the medial meniscus more susceptible to tears than the lateral meniscus?
text: Why is the medial meniscus more susceptible to tears than the lateral meniscus? | The medial meniscus is firmly attached to the joint capsule and deep MCL, giving it limited mobility. The lateral meniscus is more circular, more mobile, and detaches from the capsule at the popliteal hiatus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the functional consequence of a meniscal root tear?
text: What is the functional consequence of a meniscal root tear? | A root tear is functionally equivalent to total meniscectomy because it eliminates the ability to distribute circumferential hoop stresses.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI, what is the expected appearance and orientation of a normal ACL?
text: On sagittal MRI, what is the expected appearance and orientation of a normal ACL? | A taut, low-signal band extending from the lateral femoral condyle to the anterior tibial plateau, oriented approximately parallel to the Blumensaat line (intercondylar roof).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the two layers of the MCL and what structure does the deep layer attach to?
text: What are the two layers of the MCL and what structure does the deep layer attach to? | Superficial MCL (primary valgus stabilizer, extends 5-7 cm below the joint line) and deep MCL (meniscofemoral and meniscotibial ligaments that attach directly to the medial meniscus).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you distinguish the popliteal hiatus from a lateral meniscal tear on sagittal images?
text: How do you distinguish the popliteal hiatus from a lateral meniscal tear on sagittal images? | Look for the round popliteus tendon sitting within the gap. If the tendon is present, it is the normal popliteal hiatus, not a tear. Confirm by following the tendon on consecutive slices.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where do the meniscofemoral ligaments of Humphrey and Wrisberg run in relation to the PCL?
text: Where do the meniscofemoral ligaments of Humphrey and Wrisberg run in relation to the PCL? | Humphrey passes anterior to the PCL; Wrisberg passes posterior to the PCL. Both run from the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus to the medial femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the three layers of the quadriceps tendon from superficial to deep?
text: What are the three layers of the quadriceps tendon from superficial to deep? | Rectus femoris (superficial), vastus medialis and lateralis (middle), and vastus intermedius (deep).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the MPFL and what is its primary function?
text: What is the MPFL and what is its primary function? | The medial patellofemoral ligament runs from the adductor tubercle region to the superomedial patella. It is the primary soft tissue restraint preventing lateral patellar translation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why are most missed findings on knee MRI not due to lesion invisibility?
text: Why are most missed findings on knee MRI not due to lesion invisibility? | Studies show that missed findings are rarely invisible. In most cases, the reader simply never looked at the area where the lesion was located. A systematic search pattern forces evaluation of every structure every time.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is satisfaction of search and how does it lead to missed diagnoses?
text: What is satisfaction of search and how does it lead to missed diagnoses? | Once you find a significant abnormality, your brain becomes psychologically satisfied and less attentive to additional findings. The antidote is to complete your entire search pattern before constructing the report.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You correctly diagnose an ACL tear. Name three commonly associated injuries you must still actively search for.
text: You correctly diagnose an ACL tear. Name three commonly associated injuries you must still actively search for. | Lateral meniscal posterior horn tear, medial meniscal ramp lesion, posterolateral corner injury. Also: bone contusions in the lateral compartment and cartilage damage.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: List the 7 steps of the knee MRI search pattern in order.
text: List the 7 steps of the knee MRI search pattern in order. | 1) Overall assessment (effusion, alignment, marrow), 2) Bones and marrow, 3) Cartilage and osteochondral surfaces, 4) Menisci, 5) Ligaments, 6) Extensor mechanism and soft tissues, 7) Final review (return to clinical question).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is an anchor slice and what are the three key anchor slices for knee MRI?
text: What is an anchor slice and what are the three key anchor slices for knee MRI? | An anchor slice is a specific slice where you know normal anatomy and can navigate from. Key anchors: sagittal midline intercondylar notch (cruciates), coronal mid-condylar (meniscal bodies, collaterals), and axial patellofemoral joint (MPFL, trochlea).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you differentiate a bone contusion from a fracture on MRI?
text: How do you differentiate a bone contusion from a fracture on MRI? | Both show bone marrow edema on fluid-sensitive sequences. A fracture has a discrete low-signal line on T1 within the edema; a contusion does not. This distinction changes weight-bearing status.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the pivot-shift bone contusion pattern and what injury does it suggest?
text: What is the pivot-shift bone contusion pattern and what injury does it suggest? | Edema in the posterolateral tibial plateau and the lateral femoral condyle sulcus. This pattern is present in many acute ACL tears and should trigger direct ACL evaluation plus a search for associated meniscal injuries.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the dashboard bone contusion pattern?
text: What is the dashboard bone contusion pattern? | Edema in the anterior tibial plateau from a posterior-directed force on the flexed knee. This pattern is associated with PCL tears.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the classic bone bruise pattern of lateral patellar dislocation?
text: What is the classic bone bruise pattern of lateral patellar dislocation? | Edema on the medial patellar facet and the anterolateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle. This pattern results from the patella impacting the lateral condyle during transient dislocation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is SIFK (SONK) and how does it differ from OCD?
text: What is SIFK (SONK) and how does it differ from OCD? | SIFK (subchondral insufficiency fracture of the knee) occurs in older patients (typically women >55) with acute-onset pain and shows subchondral low-signal fracture line with surrounding edema, often in the medial femoral condyle. OCD occurs in younger patients (10-25) at the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle with a well-defined osteochondral fragment.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the modified ICRS grades for cartilage loss on MRI?
text: What are the modified ICRS grades for cartilage loss on MRI? | Grade 1: signal abnormality with intact surface. Grade 2: partial-thickness defect (<50%). Grade 3: partial-thickness defect (>50%) or full-thickness without bone exposure. Grade 4: full-thickness with subchondral bone exposure.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where is the most common location for OCD in the knee?
text: Where is the most common location for OCD in the knee? | The lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle, accounting for approximately 70–80% of cases.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability?
text: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability? | A high-signal-intensity rim (fluid signal on T2) surrounding the fragment at the interface with parent bone, indicating fluid undermining the lesion. A low-signal rim on T1 is nonspecific and can represent fibrous healing or a cleavage plane.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Name four MRI signs of OCD instability.
text: Name four MRI signs of OCD instability. | Fluid-signal line at fragment-bone interface on T2, fluid-filled cyst beneath the lesion, displaced or partially displaced fragment, and disruption of the overlying articular cartilage surface.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where should you search for loose bodies on knee MRI?
text: Where should you search for loose bodies on knee MRI? | Posteromedial and posterolateral recesses, the intercondylar notch, and the suprapatellar pouch. Loose bodies preferentially settle in dependent recesses.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How should you systematically report knee articular cartilage?
text: How should you systematically report knee articular cartilage? | Compartment by compartment — medial and lateral tibiofemoral plus patellofemoral — assessing BOTH surfaces of each. Patellofemoral cartilage is best seen on axial images; the patellar median ridge and lateral facet are common defect sites. The medial compartment bears the greater load, so early loss often shows there first.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What single imaging check separates juvenile from adult OCD, and why does it matter?
text: What single imaging check separates juvenile from adult OCD, and why does it matter? | The distal femoral physis on coronal images: OPEN physes = juvenile OCD (greater healing potential, more often managed conservatively); CLOSED physes = adult OCD (poorer healing, more often surgical). Caveat: in skeletally immature patients a high-T2 rim alone is unreliable for instability — it can be vascular granulation in a stable lesion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What grade of intrameniscal signal reaches the articular surface?
text: What grade of intrameniscal signal reaches the articular surface? | Grade 3. Grade 1 is a globular dot of mucinous degeneration, Grade 2 is linear intrameniscal signal not reaching the surface, and Grade 3 contacts the articular surface (= tear).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On PD / short-TE images, does intrameniscal signal have to be as bright as fluid to call a tear?
text: On PD / short-TE images, does intrameniscal signal have to be as bright as fluid to call a tear? | No — on PD / short-TE, surface-reaching intermediate-to-high signal is enough; it need not approach fluid intensity. (Requiring fluid-bright signal is the T2 / long-TE criterion, which is why T2 is less sensitive.) The key is signal unequivocally reaching an articular surface on ≥2 consecutive images.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the two-slice-touch rule?
text: What is the two-slice-touch rule? | Meniscal signal must contact the articular surface on at least two consecutive sagittal images (at standard 3-4 mm slice thickness) to be diagnosed as a tear. This reduces false positives from volume averaging and truncation artifact.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why does the two-slice-touch rule require caution at 3T?
text: Why does the two-slice-touch rule require caution at 3T? | At 3T, magic angle effect and magnetization transfer can cause artifactually increased intrameniscal signal, particularly in the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus near the popliteal hiatus, mimicking tears.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why are radial meniscal tears biomechanically significant even when small?
text: Why are radial meniscal tears biomechanically significant even when small? | Radial tears disrupt the circumferential hoop fibers of the meniscus, eliminating its ability to distribute axial load. This is true regardless of tear size.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the "ghost meniscus" sign and what tear type does it suggest?
text: What is the "ghost meniscus" sign and what tear type does it suggest? | The meniscus appears absent or truncated on images through the tear. It is seen with radial tears (where the tear is parallel to the imaging plane) and with root tears on sagittal images.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the most common degenerative meniscal tear pattern and where does it typically originate?
text: What is the most common degenerative meniscal tear pattern and where does it typically originate? | Horizontal (cleavage) tear, running parallel to the tibial plateau and splitting the meniscus into upper and lower leaves. It most commonly originates in the posterior horn of the medial meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A meniscal root tear is functionally equivalent to what procedure?
text: A meniscal root tear is functionally equivalent to what procedure? | Total meniscectomy. Root tears completely disrupt the circumferential hoop stresses that allow the meniscus to distribute axial load.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the root tear triad?
text: What is the root tear triad? | Posterior medial root tear + meniscal extrusion (>3 mm) + subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle or tibial plateau. The root tear is the underlying biomechanical cause.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the "ghost sign" on sagittal images in the context of a root tear?
text: What is the "ghost sign" on sagittal images in the context of a root tear? | Absence of the normal dark posterior horn root attachment adjacent to the PCL insertion on sagittal images. It indicates the root has been avulsed or disrupted.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is a ramp lesion and how common is it in ACL-injured knees?
text: What is a ramp lesion and how common is it in ACL-injured knees? | A peripheral longitudinal tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus at the meniscocapsular junction. It is found in approximately 16-24% of ACL-deficient knees.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the reported MRI sensitivity for detecting ramp lesions?
text: What is the reported MRI sensitivity for detecting ramp lesions? | Only 48-65%. Ramp lesions are notoriously underdiagnosed on preoperative MRI, missed in up to half of cases. In every ACL tear, actively search the posterior meniscocapsular junction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the three cardinal signs of a bucket-handle tear?
text: What are the three cardinal signs of a bucket-handle tear? | Absent bow-tie sign (fewer than two consecutive body sagittal images), double PCL sign (displaced fragment parallel to the PCL), and flipped meniscus sign (fragment adjacent to the anterior horn making it appear disproportionately large).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How many consecutive sagittal images should show the normal bow-tie configuration of the meniscal body?
text: How many consecutive sagittal images should show the normal bow-tie configuration of the meniscal body? | At least two. Fewer than two consecutive bow-tie images suggests a bucket-handle tear with displacement of the central fragment.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How is meniscal extrusion measured, and what threshold is pathologic?
text: How is meniscal extrusion measured, and what threshold is pathologic? | Measured on coronal images at the meniscal mid-body: distance from the outer tibial plateau margin to the outermost meniscal edge. Greater than 3 mm is pathologic; greater than 5 mm suggests severe extrusion with major loss of hoop stress function.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: At what meniscal extrusion threshold should you actively search for a meniscal root tear?
text: At what meniscal extrusion threshold should you actively search for a meniscal root tear? | Extrusion >3 mm is pathologic and should prompt an active search for a meniscal root tear on coronal and axial images; >5 mm indicates severe meniscal dysfunction with major loss of hoop-stress function.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the relationship between parameniscal cysts and meniscal tears?
text: What is the relationship between parameniscal cysts and meniscal tears? | Most parameniscal cysts have an underlying meniscal tear acting as a one-way valve. The tear allows joint fluid to decompress into parameniscal tissues. The cyst may recur if only the cyst is treated without addressing the causative tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What complication can a large lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head cause?
text: What complication can a large lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head cause? | Compression of the common peroneal nerve, presenting with foot drop.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After meniscal repair, what signal criterion distinguishes re-tear from expected postoperative healing?
text: After meniscal repair, what signal criterion distinguishes re-tear from expected postoperative healing? | Fluid-bright signal (matching joint fluid on T2) reaching the articular surface indicates re-tear. Intermediate signal at the repair site represents expected healing granulation tissue and should not be overcalled.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After partial meniscectomy, what does the normal meniscal remnant look like on MRI?
text: After partial meniscectomy, what does the normal meniscal remnant look like on MRI? | Truncated and blunted with a smooth, well-defined free edge and uniformly low signal. Persistent grade 3 signal extending to the new articular surface suggests re-tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the primary MRI signs of an ACL tear?
text: What are the primary MRI signs of an ACL tear? | Discontinuity of ligament fibers, abnormal morphology (wavy, lax, or horizontally oriented), and abnormal signal intensity (edema/hemorrhage on T2). A complete tear shows amorphous edematous tissue replacing the ligament.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Name three secondary signs of an ACL tear.
text: Name three secondary signs of an ACL tear. | Pivot-shift bone contusions (posterolateral tibial plateau + lateral femoral condyle sulcus), anterior tibial translation >5 mm, and PCL buckling. Others include deepened lateral femoral sulcus >1.5 mm, Segond fracture, and uncovered posterior horn sign.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the Segond fracture and what is its association with ACL tears?
text: What is the Segond fracture and what is its association with ACL tears? | A small avulsion of the lateral tibial rim representing anterolateral ligament avulsion. It is pathognomonic for ACL tear, with ACL injury present in approximately 75-100% of cases.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How does ACL mucoid degeneration differ from an ACL tear on MRI?
text: How does ACL mucoid degeneration differ from an ACL tear on MRI? | Mucoid degeneration shows a thickened, bulbous ACL with diffuse T2 signal but intact, continuous fibers ("celery stalk" appearance). Unlike tears, fiber tension and orientation are preserved, and secondary signs (bone contusions, tibial translation) are absent.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the ACL graft ligamentization period, and how does it appear on MRI?
text: What is the ACL graft ligamentization period, and how does it appear on MRI? | During the first 6-12 months, the graft undergoes revascularization and remodeling with increased T2 signal. This is expected and should not be confused with graft failure. A mature graft (12-24 months) should be uniformly low signal.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the most common technical error leading to ACL graft failure?
text: What is the most common technical error leading to ACL graft failure? | Tunnel malposition. The two mechanisms differ: an anteriorly placed TIBIAL tunnel causes roof (notch) impingement in extension, while a too-anterior or vertical FEMORAL tunnel produces a non-isometric/vertical graft with persistent rotational (pivot-shift) instability. On sagittal MRI, the femoral tunnel should sit in the posterior aspect of the intercondylar notch.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the classic mechanism for a PCL tear?
text: What is the classic mechanism for a PCL tear? | A posterior-directed force on the proximal tibia with the knee flexed -- the "dashboard injury." Bone contusions are typically on the anterior tibial plateau.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When does a PCL buckle anteriorly, and why is this important to recognize?
text: When does a PCL buckle anteriorly, and why is this important to recognize? | The PCL may buckle anteriorly in the setting of an ACL tear with anterior tibial translation. This should not be confused with a primary PCL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the three grades of MCL injury and their MRI findings?
text: What are the three grades of MCL injury and their MRI findings? | Grade I (sprain): periligamentous edema with intact fibers. Grade II (partial tear): partial discontinuity with surrounding edema. Grade III (complete tear): complete fiber disruption with fluid filling the gap.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why is it important to specify whether an MCL injury involves the superficial or deep layer?
text: Why is it important to specify whether an MCL injury involves the superficial or deep layer? | The deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) attaches directly to the medial meniscus. Deep MCL tears can cause meniscocapsular separation, which may require surgical repair -- a different management path than an isolated superficial MCL sprain.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the three primary posterolateral corner (PLC) structures?
text: What are the three primary posterolateral corner (PLC) structures? | LCL (fibular collateral ligament), popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the arcuate fracture and what does it indicate?
text: What is the arcuate fracture and what does it indicate? | Avulsion of the fibular styloid. It is pathognomonic for a posterolateral corner injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why is an unrecognized PLC injury an important, frequently overlooked cause of cruciate graft failure?
text: Why is an unrecognized PLC injury an important, frequently overlooked cause of cruciate graft failure? | Unrecognized PLC injury allows persistent external rotation and varus laxity, which overloads cruciate ligament reconstructions. PLC injuries must be identified and addressed for successful graft outcomes.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the primary structure of the posteromedial corner and what instability pattern does PMC injury create?
text: What is the primary structure of the posteromedial corner and what instability pattern does PMC injury create? | The posterior oblique ligament (POL). PMC injury contributes to anteromedial rotatory instability (AMRI), and when combined with ACL tear, creates greater rotational instability than an isolated ACL tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What bone bruise pattern is classic for transient lateral patellar dislocation?
text: What bone bruise pattern is classic for transient lateral patellar dislocation? | Edema on the medial patellar facet and the anterolateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: At what location is the MPFL most commonly torn?
text: At what location is the MPFL most commonly torn? | At its attachments far more than mid-substance. Classic adult teaching is femoral-sided predominance (~50%, Balcarek 2010), but in skeletally immature patients the patellar attachment predominates (~80-95%, Askenberger 2016) — scrutinize BOTH ends.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What three anatomic risk factors for recurrent patellar dislocation should be assessed?
text: What three anatomic risk factors for recurrent patellar dislocation should be assessed? | Patella alta (Insall-Salvati ratio >1.2), trochlear dysplasia (Dejour classification), and increased TT-TG distance (>20 mm is pathologic).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What combination of ligament tears constitutes a knee dislocation until proven otherwise?
text: What combination of ligament tears constitutes a knee dislocation until proven otherwise? | Combined ACL + PCL disruption, even if the knee has spontaneously reduced. This carries a limb-threatening risk of occult popliteal artery injury, requiring urgent vascular assessment.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What vascular injury must be excluded in a multiligament knee injury, and how should it be assessed?
text: What vascular injury must be excluded in a multiligament knee injury, and how should it be assessed? | Popliteal artery injury must be excluded urgently. MRI signs include loss of the normal flow void, perivascular hematoma, and intimal irregularity; CTA/MRA is used when pulses, ABI, or clinical findings are abnormal or equivocal, or per trauma protocol.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Which layer of the quadriceps tendon is most commonly involved in partial tears?
text: Which layer of the quadriceps tendon is most commonly involved in partial tears? | The deep layer (vastus intermedius). On sagittal images, look for a fluid-signal defect at the deep aspect of the tendon near the patellar insertion. The superficial fibers may appear intact.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What finding on MRI distinguishes a complete quadriceps tendon tear from a partial tear?
text: What finding on MRI distinguishes a complete quadriceps tendon tear from a partial tear? | A complete tear shows full-thickness discontinuity with proximal retraction of the tendon stump, patella baja (low-riding patella), and often a large hemorrhagic joint effusion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How does patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee) differ from a partial patellar tendon tear on MRI?
text: How does patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee) differ from a partial patellar tendon tear on MRI? | Tendinopathy shows thickening and intermediate signal (not fluid-bright on T2) with intact continuity. A partial tear shows a discrete fluid-signal defect within the tendon. This distinction matters: tendinopathy is managed conservatively; significant partial tears may need surgery.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After bone-patellar tendon-bone graft harvest for ACL reconstruction, what is the expected appearance of the patellar tendon?
text: After bone-patellar tendon-bone graft harvest for ACL reconstruction, what is the expected appearance of the patellar tendon? | A thinned central portion of the tendon with surrounding scar tissue. This is a normal postoperative finding and should not be called a tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you differentiate bipartite patella from a patellar fracture?
text: How do you differentiate bipartite patella from a patellar fracture? | Bipartite patella has smooth, well-corticated margins, is located at the superolateral pole (~75% of cases), and is bilateral in ~50%. Fractures have sharp, irregular margins, most commonly occur at the mid-patella, and are usually unilateral.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is a painful bipartite patella and what finding indicates it on MRI?
text: What is a painful bipartite patella and what finding indicates it on MRI? | When a bipartite patella becomes symptomatic from stress or trauma, bone marrow edema is seen at the synchondrosis between the accessory ossicle and the patella.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What does a lipohemarthrosis indicate and how does it appear on MRI?
text: What does a lipohemarthrosis indicate and how does it appear on MRI? | Lipohemarthrosis is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture. It appears as a fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa: the superior fat layer is bright on T1, and the inferior layer is serous or hemorrhagic fluid.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When you identify a lipohemarthrosis but no obvious fracture, what should you do?
text: When you identify a lipohemarthrosis but no obvious fracture, what should you do? | Perform a dedicated second-look fracture search of the tibial plateaus, patella, and femoral condyles. Subtle lateral tibial plateau depression fractures are the most commonly missed finding.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where does a Baker's cyst arise and what associated pathology should you search for?
text: Where does a Baker's cyst arise and what associated pathology should you search for? | Baker's cysts arise between the medial head of the gastrocnemius and the semimembranosus tendon posteromedially. They are commonly secondary to intra-articular pathology (meniscal tears, cartilage degeneration, inflammatory arthropathy).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What clinical condition does a ruptured Baker's cyst mimic?
text: What clinical condition does a ruptured Baker's cyst mimic? | Deep venous thrombosis (pseudothrombophlebitis). On MRI, a ruptured Baker's cyst shows inferior extension of fluid along the medial gastrocnemius fascial planes.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where is the pes anserine bursa located and what clinical condition does pes anserine bursitis mimic?
text: Where is the pes anserine bursa located and what clinical condition does pes anserine bursitis mimic? | Located approximately 4-5 cm below the medial joint line, deep to the sartorius/gracilis/semitendinosus tendons. It commonly mimics medial meniscal tear or MCL injury clinically.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the hallmark MRI finding of PVNS (tenosynovial giant cell tumor)?
text: What is the hallmark MRI finding of PVNS (tenosynovial giant cell tumor)? | Low signal intensity on all pulse sequences due to hemosiderin deposition, with "blooming" artifact on gradient echo sequences. Diffuse PVNS also shows bone erosions on both sides of the joint with relative preservation of joint space.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you distinguish PVNS from synovial chondromatosis on MRI?
text: How do you distinguish PVNS from synovial chondromatosis on MRI? | PVNS shows low signal on all sequences with GRE blooming (hemosiderin). Synovial chondromatosis shows cartilage-signal bodies (intermediate T1, high T2) without blooming, often with ring-and-arc calcification.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On fluid-sensitive MRI, how do you distinguish a Grade 1 from a Grade 3 muscle strain?
text: On fluid-sensitive MRI, how do you distinguish a Grade 1 from a Grade 3 muscle strain? | Grade 1: feathery/stellate perifascial edema with intact architecture and NO fiber gap. Grade 3: complete fiber discontinuity with a retracted wavy/serpentine stump and hematoma filling the gap.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the two tendinous components of the proximal hamstring origin at the ischial tuberosity, and what must the report specify in an avulsion?
text: What are the two tendinous components of the proximal hamstring origin at the ischial tuberosity, and what must the report specify in an avulsion? | (1) The conjoint tendon (biceps femoris long head + semitendinosus fused into one tendon) and (2) the separate semimembranosus tendon. Report which/how many components are torn, the retraction distance in cm, and proximity to the sciatic nerve.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the signature MRI finding of medial gastrocnemius 'tennis leg,' and which two diagnoses must always be on the differential?
text: What is the signature MRI finding of medial gastrocnemius 'tennis leg,' and which two diagnoses must always be on the differential? | Fluid tracking between the medial gastrocnemius and the soleus aponeurosis at the distal myotendinous junction. Always exclude DVT (check the popliteal vein) and ruptured Baker's cyst (plantaris rupture is a less common mimic).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why is a missed posterior medial meniscal root tear so consequential?
text: Why is a missed posterior medial meniscal root tear so consequential? | It is functionally equivalent to total meniscectomy, disrupting circumferential hoop stresses. If missed, it leads to subchondral insufficiency fractures, accelerated cartilage loss, progressive varus malalignment, and eventual arthroplasty.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When you see a subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle, what must you evaluate?
text: When you see a subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle, what must you evaluate? | The posterior medial meniscal root. The root tear is the underlying biomechanical cause. If you only report the fracture without identifying the root tear, the underlying problem will not be addressed.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What should you include in every ACL tear report regarding ramp lesions?
text: What should you include in every ACL tear report regarding ramp lesions? | A specific statement about the presence or absence of a ramp lesion. Since MRI sensitivity is only ~50-65%, recommend intraoperative assessment through a posteromedial portal when findings are equivocal.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: If you see fewer than two bow-tie images on sagittal sequences, what should you do next?
text: If you see fewer than two bow-tie images on sagittal sequences, what should you do next? | Immediately suspect a bucket-handle tear and search for the displaced fragment: in the intercondylar notch (double PCL sign) or flipped against the anterior horn (flipped meniscus sign). Check coronal images for a truncated donor site.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability, and why is T1 signal unreliable for this assessment?
text: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability, and why is T1 signal unreliable for this assessment? | Fluid-signal intensity (matching joint fluid) on T2 at the fragment-bone interface indicates undermining and instability. A low-signal rim on T1 is nonspecific -- it can represent either healing fibrous tissue or an unstable cleavage plane.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why is failure to diagnose a PLC injury the leading cause of cruciate graft failure?
text: Why is failure to diagnose a PLC injury the leading cause of cruciate graft failure? | Unrecognized PLC injury allows persistent external rotation and varus laxity, which overloads the cruciate graft. All three PLC structures (LCL, popliteus tendon, popliteofibular ligament) must be systematically evaluated in every cruciate injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What fracture is pathognomonic for posterolateral corner injury?
text: What fracture is pathognomonic for posterolateral corner injury? | Arcuate fracture -- avulsion of the fibular styloid. This finding should never be dismissed as incidental.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After diagnosing lateral patellar dislocation with MPFL tear, what is the complete evaluation checklist?
text: After diagnosing lateral patellar dislocation with MPFL tear, what is the complete evaluation checklist? | MPFL tear location, osteochondral injury and loose bodies (check suprapatellar pouch, lateral gutter, posterior recesses), trochlear dysplasia, patella alta (Insall-Salvati >1.2), and TT-TG distance (>20 mm pathologic).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What finding is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture?
text: What finding is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture? | Lipohemarthrosis (fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa). When identified, perform a systematic search for fractures of the tibial plateau, patella, and femoral condyles.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is a Segond fracture, and how does a reverse Segond fracture differ in its ligamentous association?
text: What is a Segond fracture, and how does a reverse Segond fracture differ in its ligamentous association? | Segond fracture: lateral tibial rim avulsion, pathognomonic for ACL tear. Reverse Segond fracture: medial tibial rim avulsion, associated with PCL tears rather than ACL tears.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why does combined ACL + PCL disruption require emergent vascular assessment?
text: Why does combined ACL + PCL disruption require emergent vascular assessment? | Combined cruciate disruption equals a knee dislocation pattern until proven otherwise. Popliteal artery injury can be occult after spontaneous reduction, so urgent vascular assessment is required; CTA/MRA is used when pulses, ABI, or clinical findings are abnormal or equivocal, or per trauma protocol.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How long after meniscal repair can increased signal at the repair site persist as a normal finding?
text: How long after meniscal repair can increased signal at the repair site persist as a normal finding? | At least 12 months, and it may persist indefinitely. Only fluid-bright signal (matching joint fluid on T2) reaching the articular surface constitutes a definitive re-tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: At what time point should a mature ACL graft appear uniformly low signal, and what signal indicates failure?
text: At what time point should a mature ACL graft appear uniformly low signal, and what signal indicates failure? | By 12-18 months. Fluid-signal intensity within the graft substance, discontinuity, or recurrence of secondary signs (anterior tibial translation, new pivot-shift contusions) indicates graft failure.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What three key questions should your knee MRI report answer for the surgeon?
text: What three key questions should your knee MRI report answer for the surgeon? | (1) What is the diagnosis? (2) What associated injuries are present? (3) Does anything change my planned approach? Anticipate the surgeon's decision points and address them proactively.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: For meniscal tears, what factors should you address to help the surgeon determine repairability?
text: For meniscal tears, what factors should you address to help the surgeon determine repairability? | Location (peripheral red zone = repairable vs. central white zone = resection), morphology (vertical longitudinal = repairable vs. complex = usually resection), displacement status, and acuity.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Name four findings on knee MRI that require urgent or direct communication with the referring clinician.
text: Name four findings on knee MRI that require urgent or direct communication with the referring clinician. | Knee dislocation/multiligament injury (needs urgent vascular assessment), locked bucket-handle tear, displaced osteochondral fragments in a weight-bearing compartment, and suspected septic arthritis.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What must you document per ACR practice parameters when directly communicating a significant finding?
text: What must you document per ACR practice parameters when directly communicating a significant finding? | Who was contacted, the method of communication, and the time. This documentation should be included in your report.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You are reviewing a knee MRI and want to evaluate the ACL in its full length. Which is the primary clinical plane used to evaluate the ACL from femoral origin to tibial insertion?
text: You are reviewing a knee MRI and want to evaluate the ACL in its full length. Which is the primary clinical plane used to evaluate the ACL from femoral origin to tibial insertion? | A. Axial | B. Coronal | C. Sagittal | D. Sagittal oblique (parallel to the intercondylar roof) | C | The sagittal plane is the primary plane for evaluating the ACL, as it displays the ligament along its course from the lateral femoral condyle to the anterior tibial insertion. While sagittal oblique sequences can be helpful, standard sagittal images are the workhorse for ACL assessment in routine clinical practice.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A fellow notices that a structure near the posterior tibial plateau appears abnormally bright on a single sagittal image but looks normal on all other sequences. The structure is oriented approximately 55 degrees to the main magnetic field. What is the most likely explanation?
text: A fellow notices that a structure near the posterior tibial plateau appears abnormally bright on a single sagittal image but looks normal on all other sequences. The structure is oriented approximately 55 degrees to the main magnetic field. What is the most likely explanation? | A. Partial-thickness tear of the structure | B. Mucoid degeneration | C. Magic angle artifact | D. Motion artifact from patient movement | C | The magic angle artifact causes increased signal in structures oriented at approximately 55 degrees to B0, particularly on short-TE sequences (T1, PD). It disappears on long-TE sequences (T2), which is the key distinguishing feature from true pathology. This commonly affects the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus and portions of tendons.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: In a routine knee MRI protocol, which fluid-sensitive sequence is the standard workhorse for evaluating marrow edema, soft-tissue edema, and meniscal/ligamentous pathology?
text: In a routine knee MRI protocol, which fluid-sensitive sequence is the standard workhorse for evaluating marrow edema, soft-tissue edema, and meniscal/ligamentous pathology? | A. Proton density fat-suppressed (PD FS) | B. T1-weighted | C. Gradient echo | D. Short tau inversion recovery (STIR) | A | PD FS (proton density fat-suppressed) is the fluid-sensitive workhorse of knee MRI: it shows marrow and soft-tissue edema while also giving the SNR and spatial resolution needed for meniscal and ligamentous detail, so most protocols use it as the primary edema-sensitive sequence. STIR is also fluid-sensitive and gives more uniform fat suppression (so it is comparably sensitive for marrow edema in isolation), but its lower SNR and resolution make it a supplement rather than the routine workhorse. T1 excels at anatomy and marrow replacement but does not highlight edema; gradient echo is prone to susceptibility/T2* effects and is not used as the primary edema sequence.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A knee MRI is acquired with T1, PD FS, and T2 sequences. When evaluating bone marrow for traumatic contusions, which sequence combination best distinguishes the extent of injury and detects marrow edema with optimal sensitivity and resolution?
text: A knee MRI is acquired with T1, PD FS, and T2 sequences. When evaluating bone marrow for traumatic contusions, which sequence combination best distinguishes the extent of injury and detects marrow edema with optimal sensitivity and resolution? | A. T1 alone, because normal marrow is bright and any pathology appears dark | B. T1 to detect discrete fracture lines within edema (dark line in bright marrow), and PD FS to visualize the full extent of marrow edema with good spatial resolution and signal-to-noise ratio for soft-tissue detail. | C. T2 alone, because it shows fluid/edema the brightest and is the most sensitive sequence | D. PD FS alone, because it is the single most important sequence and eliminates the need for other sequences | B | Each sequence has complementary roles: T1 reveals marrow replacement and fracture lines (dark lines against bright fat); PD FS shows edema and soft-tissue pathology with excellent resolution; T2 characterizes fluid collections. A complete evaluation uses all three strategically.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You are evaluating a knee MRI for a suspected meniscal tear on a PD/T2 sequence. The meniscus appears uniformly dark (low signal) at your current window/level, making subtle internal signal hard to detect. How should you adjust window width and level to best reveal faint intrasubstance meniscal signal?
text: You are evaluating a knee MRI for a suspected meniscal tear on a PD/T2 sequence. The meniscus appears uniformly dark (low signal) at your current window/level, making subtle internal signal hard to detect. How should you adjust window width and level to best reveal faint intrasubstance meniscal signal? | A. Narrow the window width and set the level low, centered on the dark meniscus, so the small range of low signal intensities is spread across the full gray scale and faint internal gradations become visible. | B. Widen the window as much as possible, since a larger window width always increases contrast between adjacent low-signal voxels. | C. Raise the level to the maximum and keep the window wide, making the whole image brighter to expose hidden signal. | D. Leave the window/level unchanged and instead rely solely on the radial and sagittal images, since window/level settings do not affect detection of internal signal. | A | Detecting faint internal meniscal signal is a contrast task. Narrowing the window width and centering a low level on the dark meniscus spreads a small range of low intensities across the full gray scale, amplifying subtle gradations. Widening the window compresses gray levels and reduces contrast; simply raising overall brightness does not improve low-signal discrimination.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When evaluating the menisci on sagittal MRI, you note that the medial meniscus posterior horn is significantly larger than its anterior horn. In contrast, the lateral meniscus anterior and posterior horns appear roughly equal in size. Which statement best characterizes normal meniscal anatomy?
text: When evaluating the menisci on sagittal MRI, you note that the medial meniscus posterior horn is significantly larger than its anterior horn. In contrast, the lateral meniscus anterior and posterior horns appear roughly equal in size. Which statement best characterizes normal meniscal anatomy? | A. Both menisci are symmetric C-shaped structures with equal-sized horns | B. The lateral meniscus is C-shaped with a larger posterior horn; the medial meniscus is O-shaped | C. Both menisci are O-shaped with larger anterior horns | D. The medial meniscus is C-shaped with a larger posterior horn; the lateral meniscus is more circular (O-shaped) with roughly equal-sized horns | D | The medial meniscus is C-shaped with a wider posterior horn relative to its anterior horn, while the lateral meniscus is more circular (O-shaped) with anterior and posterior horns of roughly equal size. Recognizing this normal asymmetry is critical so that a normal large medial posterior horn is not mistaken for pathology.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On axial images at the level of the joint line, you notice a gap in the posterolateral capsule where no meniscal tissue is attached. This area is adjacent to the popliteus tendon. What is the correct interpretation?
text: On axial images at the level of the joint line, you notice a gap in the posterolateral capsule where no meniscal tissue is attached. This area is adjacent to the popliteus tendon. What is the correct interpretation? | A. Peripheral meniscal tear of the lateral meniscus | B. Normal popliteal hiatus | C. Lateral meniscal root avulsion | D. Parameniscal cyst indicating an underlying tear | B | The popliteal hiatus is a normal anatomic opening in the posterolateral capsule where the popliteus tendon passes from its intracapsular course to its femoral origin. It is a common mimic of a peripheral lateral meniscal tear and should not be reported as pathology.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a sagittal MRI, the ACL should be evaluated for its expected course and signal. In a normal knee, the ACL fibers course from the posteromedial aspect of the lateral femoral condyle to the anterior tibial plateau. What is the expected signal of a normal ACL on PD FS images?
text: On a sagittal MRI, the ACL should be evaluated for its expected course and signal. In a normal knee, the ACL fibers course from the posteromedial aspect of the lateral femoral condyle to the anterior tibial plateau. What is the expected signal of a normal ACL on PD FS images? | A. Uniformly bright (high signal) throughout | B. Uniformly dark (low signal) like the PCL | C. Predominantly low signal with some intermediate signal striations from interposed fat and fascicles | D. Absent signal (the normal ACL is not visible on MRI) | C | The normal ACL has a striated appearance with predominantly low signal fibers interspersed with intermediate signal from interfascicular fat and connective tissue. This is in contrast to the PCL, which is typically uniformly dark (low signal). Recognizing normal ACL signal helps avoid overcalling partial tears.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The meniscal roots are critical attachment points. Where does the posterior root of the medial meniscus insert?
text: The meniscal roots are critical attachment points. Where does the posterior root of the medial meniscus insert? | A. Anterior to the ACL tibial insertion | B. Just posterior to the tibial spine, between the attachments of the lateral meniscus posterior root and the PCL | C. On the posterior tibial cortex, below the joint line | D. On the medial femoral condyle, adjacent to the MCL | B | The medial meniscus posterior root inserts on the posterior tibial plateau just anterior to the PCL insertion and posterior to the lateral meniscus posterior root. This area is best evaluated on coronal and sagittal images, and injury here (root tear) is functionally equivalent to a total meniscectomy.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 23-year-old soccer player sustains a noncontact knee injury. Coronal MRI shows a small cortical avulsion fracture at the lateral tibial plateau. Which ligamentous injury is this fracture pathognomonic for?
text: A 23-year-old soccer player sustains a noncontact knee injury. Coronal MRI shows a small cortical avulsion fracture at the lateral tibial plateau. Which ligamentous injury is this fracture pathognomonic for? | A. LCL tear; indicates posterolateral corner injury | B. Segond fracture; pathognomonic for ACL injury | C. MCL tear; indicates medial compartment varus stress | D. Patellar dislocation; indicates MPFL injury | B | The Segond fracture (lateral tibial rim avulsion) is a key radiographic sign of ACL injury that should raise suspicion even when the ACL is not directly visible or appears intact on initial assessment.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When organizing a knee MRI report, you evaluate cartilage loss, osteophytes, and meniscal pathology in three separate anatomic units. Which statement best describes this compartment-based approach?
text: When organizing a knee MRI report, you evaluate cartilage loss, osteophytes, and meniscal pathology in three separate anatomic units. Which statement best describes this compartment-based approach? | A. It is redundant because all three compartments always degenerate together | B. It is designed to minimize the report length by grouping findings | C. This approach independently assesses the medial tibiofemoral, lateral tibiofemoral, and patellofemoral compartments — essential because pathology often clusters within one compartment, and isolated compartment disease has different surgical implications than multi-compartment involvement. | D. It is only necessary if varus or valgus malalignment is present | C | Compartment-based evaluation guides surgical decision-making: unicompartmental arthroplasty vs. total knee arthroplasty depends on whether disease is isolated or multi-compartment.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI, you identify a smooth, linear structure connecting the anterior horns of the medial and lateral menisci. This structure can mimic a tear of the anterior horn of the lateral meniscus. What is this normal anatomic variant?
text: On sagittal MRI, you identify a smooth, linear structure connecting the anterior horns of the medial and lateral menisci. This structure can mimic a tear of the anterior horn of the lateral meniscus. What is this normal anatomic variant? | A. Meniscofemoral ligament (Wrisberg); runs from posterior horn of lateral meniscus to medial femoral condyle posterior to the PCL | B. ACL tibial attachment; represents the ACL insertion site | C. Anterior intermeniscal ligament avulsion; indicates an acute meniscal injury | D. Transverse meniscal ligament; a normal interconnecting ligament between anterior meniscal horns that should not be reported as pathology | D | The transverse ligament is a key normal variant that can be misinterpreted as an anterior horn lateral meniscal tear if not recognized—it is linear, smooth, and does not disrupt normal meniscal morphology.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 45-year-old with knee pain undergoes MRI. Coronal images show decreased space and cartilage loss predominantly in the medial tibiofemoral compartment with relative preservation of the lateral compartment. The tibial plateau shows a mild varus inclination. What does this pattern indicate?
text: A 45-year-old with knee pain undergoes MRI. Coronal images show decreased space and cartilage loss predominantly in the medial tibiofemoral compartment with relative preservation of the lateral compartment. The tibial plateau shows a mild varus inclination. What does this pattern indicate? | A. Varus malalignment with medial compartment overload; shifts load to the medial side, explaining the compartment-specific cartilage loss pattern | B. Normal aging without malalignment; compartmental asymmetry is expected in all knees | C. Valgus malalignment; shifts load laterally, which would cause lateral cartilage loss | D. Patellofemoral joint disease; does not explain the asymmetric tibiofemoral cartilage loss | A | MRI can demonstrate the consequences of malalignment through a compartment-specific cartilage loss pattern. Varus knees show medial overload; valgus knees show lateral overload. This informs conservative management and surgical planning.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A fellow reads a knee MRI and correctly identifies an ACL tear but fails to notice a displaced bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus on the same study. This type of diagnostic error is best described as:
text: A fellow reads a knee MRI and correctly identifies an ACL tear but fails to notice a displaced bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus on the same study. This type of diagnostic error is best described as: | A. Satisfaction of search | B. Anchoring bias | C. Availability bias | D. Premature closure | A | Satisfaction of search occurs when identification of one abnormality reduces the vigilance for detecting additional findings on the same study. Using a systematic checklist-based approach to every knee MRI helps prevent this error by ensuring all structures are evaluated regardless of initial findings.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A sports medicine fellow wants to adopt a systematic approach to reading knee MRI. Which strategy is most effective for avoiding missed findings?
text: A sports medicine fellow wants to adopt a systematic approach to reading knee MRI. Which strategy is most effective for avoiding missed findings? | A. Focus on the area of clinical concern first, then briefly scan the rest of the knee | B. Use a consistent structured checklist that evaluates every anatomic compartment in a fixed order, regardless of clinical history | C. Read only the sequences that are most relevant to the clinical question | D. Start with the radiologist report and then verify the findings on the images | B | A consistent, structured search pattern that evaluates every compartment in the same order on every study is the most effective way to minimize missed findings. While clinical history provides important context, it should not limit which structures are evaluated. The goal is to look at everything, every time.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Which approach to a systematic knee MRI search is the most reliable way to organize the evaluation so that nothing is missed?
text: Which approach to a systematic knee MRI search is the most reliable way to organize the evaluation so that nothing is missed? | A. Read one plane at a time — every structure on the sagittals, then the coronals, then the axials — before integrating the findings. | B. Work through the knee one anatomic category at a time (bones, cartilage, menisci, ligaments, extensor mechanism, soft tissues), checking each across all relevant sequences and planes. | C. Evaluate only the structures implicated by the clinical history and request, deferring the rest unless symptoms warrant. | D. Start from the most conspicuous abnormality and work outward, stopping once a finding explains the symptoms. | B | A robust search organizes by anatomic category and reviews each category across every relevant sequence and plane — this guarantees coverage and forces multi-plane correlation. Reading one plane fully before the next breaks that correlation; restricting the search to the clinical question or stopping at the first conspicuous finding both invite satisfaction-of-search misses.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A radiologist is training residents on systematic knee MRI reading. Over the first 50 cases, residents complete the full 7-step search pattern on every study. Which outcome best demonstrates successful integration of the pattern into clinical workflow?
text: A radiologist is training residents on systematic knee MRI reading. Over the first 50 cases, residents complete the full 7-step search pattern on every study. Which outcome best demonstrates successful integration of the pattern into clinical workflow? | A. The residents should restrict the search pattern to the clinical question to save time after the first 20 cases. | B. Residents will continue to feel the pattern is slow for the first 100 cases and should expect no improvement in speed until they have completed 200+ studies. | C. Once the pattern is memorized, residents can skip any step if they find an obvious abnormality, since the clinical question has been answered. | D. By cases 40-50, residents complete the search pattern in 8-10 minutes with no increase in errors; the pattern has become automatic and reading speed actually increases because they no longer waste time deciding what to evaluate next. | D | Systematic search patterns require deliberate practice (50-100 cases) to become automatic. Speed increases with practice as decision-making time is eliminated — the pattern is not a permanent burden but a path to efficiency. Template-driven reporting reinforces the pattern by aligning the reading workflow with the report structure.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When setting up anchor slices for knee MRI reading, which combination of three anchor slices provides the fastest orientation and most reliable navigation for a complete systematic search?
text: When setting up anchor slices for knee MRI reading, which combination of three anchor slices provides the fastest orientation and most reliable navigation for a complete systematic search? | A. Sagittal image through the midline intercondylar notch (to evaluate cruciates and navigate medially/laterally for menisci), coronal mid-condylar image (to see meniscal bodies, collateral ligaments, and alignment), and axial image through the patellofemoral joint (to evaluate patellar articular surface, trochlea, and MPFL). | B. The three anchor slices should always be the most lateral sagittal slice, the posterior coronal slice, and the most superior axial slice, as these show the most pathology. | C. A single anchor slice (sagittal midline) is sufficient if the radiologist has enough experience; multiple anchor slices slow down reading and should be avoided after the first month of training. | D. Anchor slices should be selected differently for each case based on the clinical question, rather than remaining consistent, to adapt the search to the specific pathology being evaluated. | A | Anchor slices are fixed reference points that remain consistent across every case. Starting at the same anchor slices on every study accelerates orientation and prevents disorientation mid-read. Within weeks of consistent anchor-slice use, readers can rapidly assess whether a case is grossly abnormal just from the appearance of the anchor slice itself. Consistency is the key to speed and reliability.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 22-year-old soccer player sustains a noncontact pivot injury. MRI shows bone marrow edema at the lateral femoral condyle and the posterolateral tibial plateau. Which injury mechanism does this contusion pattern indicate?
text: A 22-year-old soccer player sustains a noncontact pivot injury. MRI shows bone marrow edema at the lateral femoral condyle and the posterolateral tibial plateau. Which injury mechanism does this contusion pattern indicate? | A. Direct blow to the anterior knee (dashboard injury) | B. Lateral patellar dislocation | C. Hyperextension injury | D. Pivot-shift mechanism with likely ACL injury | D | The combination of lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial plateau marrow edema is the classic pivot-shift contusion pattern. It results from transient anterior subluxation of the lateral tibial compartment and is strongly associated with ACL tears. When you see this pattern, carefully evaluate the ACL and associated meniscal structures.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 35-year-old presents after a motor vehicle accident with a knee-to-dashboard impact. MRI shows marrow edema in the anterior proximal tibia and a large joint effusion. What associated ligamentous injury should you specifically evaluate for?
text: A 35-year-old presents after a motor vehicle accident with a knee-to-dashboard impact. MRI shows marrow edema in the anterior proximal tibia and a large joint effusion. What associated ligamentous injury should you specifically evaluate for? | A. ACL tear | B. PCL tear | C. Lateral collateral ligament tear | D. Patellar tendon rupture | B | A dashboard injury drives a posteriorly directed force on the flexed proximal tibia, producing a bone contusion at the ANTERIOR proximal tibia (the impact site). This pattern is strongly associated with PCL injury, so the PCL should be carefully evaluated for partial or complete disruption, and the posterolateral corner should also be assessed.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 16-year-old presents after a first-time lateral patellar dislocation. Which bone marrow edema pattern would you expect on MRI?
text: A 16-year-old presents after a first-time lateral patellar dislocation. Which bone marrow edema pattern would you expect on MRI? | A. Lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial plateau | B. Medial patellar facet and anterior lateral femoral condyle | C. Medial and lateral tibial plateaus symmetrically | D. Posterior femoral condyles bilaterally | B | Lateral patellar dislocation produces a characteristic kissing contusion pattern: paired edema at the medial patellar facet and the anterolateral femoral condyle, produced as the medial patella impacts the condyle during relocation. When this pattern is identified, evaluate for MPFL injury, osteochondral fracture, and loose bodies.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 60-year-old female presents with acute-onset medial knee pain without trauma. MRI shows a low-signal subchondral lesion on T1 with surrounding marrow edema in the medial femoral condyle weight-bearing surface. The overlying articular cartilage is intact. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: A 60-year-old female presents with acute-onset medial knee pain without trauma. MRI shows a low-signal subchondral lesion on T1 with surrounding marrow edema in the medial femoral condyle weight-bearing surface. The overlying articular cartilage is intact. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) | B. Avascular necrosis (AVN) | C. Stress fracture of the femoral condyle | D. Spontaneous insufficiency fracture of the knee (SIFK, formerly SONK) | D | SIFK (subchondral insufficiency fracture of the knee, previously called SONK) typically presents in older patients with acute onset pain, localized to the weight-bearing surface of the medial femoral condyle with surrounding marrow edema. OCD occurs in younger patients and AVN typically involves a larger geographic area. The subchondral location and clinical presentation are the key distinguishing features.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 45-year-old woman with osteoporosis presents with acute medial knee pain after minor trauma. MRI shows a band of low signal on T1 running through the medial tibial plateau with surrounding marrow edema. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what distinguishes it from a fatigue (stress) fracture?
text: A 45-year-old woman with osteoporosis presents with acute medial knee pain after minor trauma. MRI shows a band of low signal on T1 running through the medial tibial plateau with surrounding marrow edema. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what distinguishes it from a fatigue (stress) fracture? | A. Insufficiency fracture. Insufficiency fractures occur in weakened bone (e.g., osteoporosis) under normal physiologic loading, whereas fatigue fractures occur in normal bone under abnormal repetitive loading. Both appear as a discrete linear low-T1 line within surrounding marrow edema; the distinction is made clinically by bone quality, age, and mechanism, with the osteoporotic history being the key clue here. | B. Fatigue (stress) fracture, which occurs in athletes or military recruits with normal bone density subjected to repetitive abnormal loading and presents with gradual, activity-related onset rather than acute pain after minor trauma. | C. Bone contusion, which shows diffuse reticular marrow edema but lacks the discrete linear low-T1 fracture line, and therefore does not change weight-bearing status. | D. Degenerative joint disease with osteophytes, which produces cartilage loss, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophytes rather than a discrete linear fracture line within marrow edema. | A | T1 is essential for fracture detection: a discrete low-signal line within marrow edema indicates a fracture (alters weight-bearing status), whereas diffuse edema without a line suggests contusion. Insufficiency and fatigue fractures are both stress fractures; they are distinguished by bone quality and mechanism—normal load in weak (osteoporotic) bone versus repetitive abnormal load in normal bone.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 68-year-old man with knee pain undergoes MRI. Coronal images show near-complete cartilage loss in the medial compartment with subchondral sclerosis and prominent osteophytes, while the lateral compartment cartilage appears relatively preserved. What does this compartmental pattern indicate, and what weight-bearing recommendation would you make?
text: A 68-year-old man with knee pain undergoes MRI. Coronal images show near-complete cartilage loss in the medial compartment with subchondral sclerosis and prominent osteophytes, while the lateral compartment cartilage appears relatively preserved. What does this compartmental pattern indicate, and what weight-bearing recommendation would you make? | A. Valgus malalignment, which would show the opposite pattern: lateral compartment cartilage loss with medial preservation and lateral osteophytes. | B. This pattern is consistent with varus-aligned (medial-compartment-overloaded) knee. The concentrated medial cartilage loss and subchondral sclerosis indicate chronic mechanical overload of the medial compartment. Recommend weight-bearing radiographs to quantify the varus degree, and note in the report that findings are compatible with varus alignment and medial-compartment osteoarthritis—this influences surgical decision-making (unicompartmental vs. total arthroplasty). | C. Normal neutral alignment, which would show symmetric cartilage loss and changes across all three compartments rather than compartment-specific overload. | D. Secondary to a meniscal root tear, which would present with meniscal extrusion and acute subchondral insufficiency fracture rather than chronic degenerative changes and osteophytes. | B | MRI is not reliable for precise alignment angles—weight-bearing radiographs are the gold standard. However, coronal MRI reveals the CONSEQUENCES of malalignment: varus overload concentrates cartilage loss, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophytes in one compartment (usually medial); valgus concentrates them on the lateral side. Describing the compartmental pattern in your report guides the surgeon toward appropriate procedure selection (unicompartmental vs. total) without attempting angle measurement.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You identify bone marrow edema in the knee on a sagittal PD FS image. Before assigning a diagnosis, you ask several questions about the edema to direct your search for associated injuries. Which question is LEAST useful for directing those next diagnostic steps?
text: You identify bone marrow edema in the knee on a sagittal PD FS image. Before assigning a diagnosis, you ask several questions about the edema to direct your search for associated injuries. Which question is LEAST useful for directing those next diagnostic steps? | A. Where is the edema located? (e.g., posterolateral tibial plateau + lateral femoral condyle edema points to a pivot-shift mechanism, prompting a focused search of the ACL and lateral meniscus). | B. Is the edema on one side of the joint or paired on both opposing surfaces? Paired (kissing) edema implies direct impaction of the two surfaces and should prompt assessment of the apposed bone, cartilage, and adjacent ligaments. | C.
