What are the patient's age and occupation? These inform baseline risk and pretest probability but do not localize the lesion or redirect the targeted search for associated internal derangement. | D. Does the edema pattern match a recognized injury mechanism (pivot-shift, dashboard, transient patellar dislocation)? This tells you which specific ligaments and structures to scrutinize. | C | The location, distribution, and mechanism-pattern of bone marrow edema are powerful clues that direct a targeted search for ligament, meniscal, and cartilage injury. Age and occupation refine pretest probability but do not localize the lesion or change the systematic anatomic search, making that question least useful here.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On MRI, you identify a focal area of articular cartilage abnormality in the medial femoral condyle. The cartilage shows an area of signal change that extends deeper than 50% of cartilage thickness but does not reach the subchondral bone. Using the modified ICRS grading system, this lesion is best classified as:
text: On MRI, you identify a focal area of articular cartilage abnormality in the medial femoral condyle. The cartilage shows an area of signal change that extends deeper than 50% of cartilage thickness but does not reach the subchondral bone. Using the modified ICRS grading system, this lesion is best classified as: | A. Grade 1 — superficial softening or fibrillation | B. Grade 2 — defect less than 50% of cartilage thickness | C. Grade 3 — defect greater than 50% of cartilage thickness without full-thickness loss | D. Grade 4 — full-thickness cartilage loss with exposed subchondral bone | C | ICRS Grade 3 describes a cartilage defect that extends deeper than 50% of the cartilage thickness but does not expose subchondral bone. Accurately grading cartilage lesions helps guide surgical decision-making, as higher-grade lesions may benefit from restorative procedures.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 17-year-old basketball player has an OCD lesion of the medial femoral condyle. Which MRI finding most strongly suggests the fragment is unstable?
text: A 17-year-old basketball player has an OCD lesion of the medial femoral condyle. Which MRI finding most strongly suggests the fragment is unstable? | A. High T2 signal (fluid) between the fragment and parent bone, or a cyst-like lesion at the interface | B. Low signal on T1 images within the lesion | C. The lesion is located on the weight-bearing surface | D. Mild surrounding marrow edema | A | High T2 signal (fluid) interposed between the OCD fragment and the parent bone, or cyst-like lesions at the interface, strongly suggest fragment instability. Other signs of instability include a displaced fragment and a breach in the overlying articular cartilage. Instability is a key factor in determining surgical vs. conservative management.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A subchondral bone lesion is identified in the knee of a 14-year-old athlete at the classic location on the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. In a 55-year-old with a similar-appearing lesion on the weight-bearing surface of the medial femoral condyle, the leading diagnosis would change to:
text: A subchondral bone lesion is identified in the knee of a 14-year-old athlete at the classic location on the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. In a 55-year-old with a similar-appearing lesion on the weight-bearing surface of the medial femoral condyle, the leading diagnosis would change to: | A. OCD in both cases — age does not influence the differential | B. Stress fracture in the older patient | C. SIFK (subchondral insufficiency fracture) in the older patient | D. AVN in the adolescent | C | Age and location are critical for differentiating subchondral lesions. OCD typically occurs in adolescents/young adults at the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. SIFK occurs in older patients (typically >55 years) at the weight-bearing surface. Recognizing this age-location pattern prevents misdiagnosis and guides appropriate management.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On MRI of the knee, you identify a well-defined low-signal structure within the joint that is separate from all articular surfaces, seen within the suprapatellar recess. It demonstrates peripheral low signal on all sequences. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: On MRI of the knee, you identify a well-defined low-signal structure within the joint that is separate from all articular surfaces, seen within the suprapatellar recess. It demonstrates peripheral low signal on all sequences. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Meniscal ossicle | B. Loose body (osteochondral fragment) | C. Synovial chondromatosis | D. Fabella | B | A well-defined structure separate from articular surfaces within the joint space, particularly within a recess, is consistent with an intra-articular loose body. Loose bodies can arise from osteochondral fractures, OCD, or synovial chondromatosis. Reporting their number, size, and location is important as they can cause mechanical symptoms and cartilage damage.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the classic location of knee osteochondritis dissecans (OCD), and which feature most drives surgical decision-making?
text: What is the classic location of knee osteochondritis dissecans (OCD), and which feature most drives surgical decision-making? | A. Patellar median ridge; lesion size | B. Lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle (~70–80%); fragment stability | C. Central weight-bearing lateral femoral condyle; patient age | D. Tibial plateau; presence of effusion | B | The classic OCD location is the lateral aspect of the MEDIAL femoral condyle (~70–80% of knee OCD). Size and location are reported, but fragment STABILITY is the key determination driving management — a stable lesion may be treated conservatively (especially with open physes), whereas an unstable fragment usually requires surgery.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 13-year-old and a 35-year-old each have a femoral-condyle OCD lesion. Which single imaging check best identifies the better-prognosis (juvenile) form?
text: A 13-year-old and a 35-year-old each have a femoral-condyle OCD lesion. Which single imaging check best identifies the better-prognosis (juvenile) form? | A. Lesion location on the patella | B. Status of the distal femoral physis — open physes = juvenile OCD, with greater healing potential and more frequent conservative success | C. Presence of a joint effusion | D. Signal intensity of the overlying cartilage | B | Check the distal femoral physis on coronal images: OPEN physes define juvenile OCD, which has substantially better healing potential and is more often managed conservatively; closed physes (adult OCD) heal poorly and more often need surgery. Caveat: in skeletally immature patients a high-T2 rim alone is unreliable for instability (it can be vascular granulation in a stable lesion) — it counts only if it matches fluid signal or a second sign is present.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal PD images, you see increased signal within the posterior horn of the medial meniscus that does not extend to an articular surface. How is this signal classified, and what is the clinical significance?
text: On sagittal PD images, you see increased signal within the posterior horn of the medial meniscus that does not extend to an articular surface. How is this signal classified, and what is the clinical significance? | A. Grade 3 signal — represents a definite meniscal tear | B. Grade 1 signal — always represents an acute traumatic injury | C. Grade 2 signal — requires arthroscopic confirmation of a tear | D. Grade 2 signal — intrameniscal signal without articular surface extension; does not represent a tear | D | Grade 2 meniscal signal represents linear intrameniscal signal that does not reach any articular surface. It typically represents intrasubstance degeneration, not a true tear. Only grade 3 signal, which unequivocally contacts an articular surface, meets MRI criteria for a meniscal tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When evaluating the meniscus for tears on sagittal MRI, the "two-slice-touch" rule is applied. What does this rule state?
text: When evaluating the meniscus for tears on sagittal MRI, the "two-slice-touch" rule is applied. What does this rule state? | A. A tear is present if abnormal signal is seen on at least two consecutive sagittal images | B. The meniscus must be visible on at least two sagittal slices to be considered normal | C. Grade 3 signal must contact an articular surface on at least two consecutive images to confidently diagnose a tear | D. The meniscal body should be seen on exactly two sagittal bow-tie images | C | The two-slice-touch rule states that to confidently diagnose a meniscal tear, grade 3 signal (contacting an articular surface) should be identified on at least two consecutive sagittal images. This rule improves specificity and helps avoid false-positive diagnoses from volume averaging or magic angle artifact on a single slice.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A coronal PD FS image shows a linear high signal extending from the inner free edge of the lateral meniscus body perpendicular to the long axis of the meniscus toward the periphery. What type of tear does this represent?
text: A coronal PD FS image shows a linear high signal extending from the inner free edge of the lateral meniscus body perpendicular to the long axis of the meniscus toward the periphery. What type of tear does this represent? | A. Horizontal tear | B. Radial tear | C. Longitudinal-vertical tear | D. Complex tear | B | A radial tear extends perpendicular to the long axis (circumference) of the meniscus from the free edge toward the periphery. On the coronal or sagittal image where the tear is in-plane, it appears as a linear cleft. Radial tears are biomechanically significant because they disrupt the circumferential collagen fibers that generate hoop stress.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A sagittal MRI shows the "ghost meniscus" sign at the expected location of the medial meniscus posterior root, and the coronal shows meniscal extrusion greater than 3 mm over the medial tibial plateau. Which diagnosis should you strongly consider?
text: A sagittal MRI shows the "ghost meniscus" sign at the expected location of the medial meniscus posterior root, and the coronal shows meniscal extrusion greater than 3 mm over the medial tibial plateau. Which diagnosis should you strongly consider? | A. Medial meniscus posterior root tear | B. Peripheral longitudinal tear of the medial meniscus | C. Normal variant of meniscal morphology | D. Discoid medial meniscus | A | The ghost meniscus sign (absent meniscal tissue at the root attachment site on sagittal images) combined with meniscal extrusion greater than 3 mm is highly suggestive of a meniscal root tear. Root tears destroy hoop stress and are functionally equivalent to a total meniscectomy, making them a critical finding to identify and report.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You are reviewing a knee MRI and notice only one bow-tie image on the sagittal series where you would normally expect two for the meniscal body. On a coronal image, you see a low-signal band parallel and inferior to the PCL. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: You are reviewing a knee MRI and notice only one bow-tie image on the sagittal series where you would normally expect two for the meniscal body. On a coronal image, you see a low-signal band parallel and inferior to the PCL. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Normal meniscal variant | B. Radial tear of the meniscal body | C. Bucket-handle tear with a displaced fragment (double PCL sign) | D. Discoid meniscus with a horizontal tear | C | The absent bow-tie sign (only one bow-tie image instead of the expected two) indicates that a meniscal fragment has been displaced. The double PCL sign — a low-signal band in the intercondylar notch parallel to the PCL — represents the displaced inner fragment of a bucket-handle tear. These two signs together are classic for a bucket-handle meniscal tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a knee MRI showing an ACL tear, which easily-missed meniscal injury must you specifically search for at the posterior meniscocapsular junction of the medial meniscus?
text: On a knee MRI showing an ACL tear, which easily-missed meniscal injury must you specifically search for at the posterior meniscocapsular junction of the medial meniscus? | A. Bucket-handle tear of the lateral meniscus | B. Ramp lesion | C. Radial tear of the lateral meniscus posterior root | D. Discoid meniscus | B | A ramp lesion — a peripheral longitudinal tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus at the meniscocapsular junction — is strongly associated with ACL tears (≈16–24% of ACL-deficient knees) and is notoriously underdiagnosed on MRI (sensitivity ~48–65%). In every ACL case, scrutinize the posteromedial meniscocapsular junction on sagittal and axial fluid-sensitive sequences; it can be repaired at the time of ACL reconstruction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscal extrusion is measured on the coronal mid-body image. Which threshold is pathologic, and what associated tear should it prompt you to search for?
text: Meniscal extrusion is measured on the coronal mid-body image. Which threshold is pathologic, and what associated tear should it prompt you to search for? | A. >1 mm; bucket-handle tear | B. >3 mm; meniscal root tear | C. >8 mm; horizontal cleavage tear | D. Any extrusion; discoid meniscus | B | Extrusion >3 mm beyond the tibial plateau margin (coronal mid-body slice, reference line at the tibial margin) is pathologic; >5 mm is severe. Significant extrusion should trigger a careful search for a meniscal ROOT tear, which abolishes hoop-stress and is functionally equivalent to a total meniscectomy. Medial extrusion is more clinically significant than lateral.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A parameniscal cyst is identified adjacent to the meniscus. What is it almost always associated with, and what is the treatment implication?
text: A parameniscal cyst is identified adjacent to the meniscus. What is it almost always associated with, and what is the treatment implication? | A. An intact meniscus; the cyst is incidental | B. An underlying (usually horizontal) meniscal tear acting as a one-way valve; the cyst recurs if the tear is not addressed | C. An ACL tear; the cyst resolves after ACL reconstruction | D. Chondrocalcinosis; no treatment needed | B | Parameniscal cysts almost always communicate with an underlying meniscal tear (typically horizontal/horizontal-cleavage) via a one-way-valve mechanism that pumps joint fluid into the parameniscal soft tissues. Decompression alone is insufficient — the meniscal tear must be addressed or the cyst recurs. A lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head can compress the common peroneal nerve.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On MRI after meniscal repair, which finding most reliably indicates a RE-TEAR rather than expected post-operative healing signal?
text: On MRI after meniscal repair, which finding most reliably indicates a RE-TEAR rather than expected post-operative healing signal? | A. Any intrameniscal signal at the repair site | B. Fluid-bright (T2) signal extending through the full thickness of the repair to the articular surface, or contrast/fluid tracking into the tear on MR arthrography | C. Mild intermediate signal that does not reach a surface | D. A small joint effusion | B | Repaired menisci normally retain INTERMEDIATE (granulation) signal at the repair site for months to years, so intermediate signal alone does not indicate re-tear. A re-tear is shown by FLUID-BRIGHT (T2) signal extending through the repair to the surface, or by contrast/fluid tracking into the tear on MR arthrography — the most reliable test in the post-operative meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You identify discontinuity and abnormal signal of the ACL on sagittal images. Which of the following secondary signs on MRI would most strongly support the diagnosis of an ACL tear?
text: You identify discontinuity and abnormal signal of the ACL on sagittal images. Which of the following secondary signs on MRI would most strongly support the diagnosis of an ACL tear? | A. Isolated medial tibial plateau marrow edema | B. Isolated prepatellar bursitis | C. Patellar alta | D. Anterior tibial translation relative to the femur, deep lateral femoral notch sign, and Segond fracture | D | Secondary signs of ACL tear include anterior tibial translation (>5 mm), a deep lateral femoral sulcus (notch sign >1.5 mm), uncovering of the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus, Segond fracture (lateral capsular avulsion), and the pivot-shift contusion pattern. These findings increase diagnostic confidence when the ACL is difficult to evaluate directly.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI, the PCL demonstrates diffuse thickening and increased intrasubstance signal on PD FS but maintains some residual intact fibers without complete discontinuity. How would you characterize this injury?
text: On sagittal MRI, the PCL demonstrates diffuse thickening and increased intrasubstance signal on PD FS but maintains some residual intact fibers without complete discontinuity. How would you characterize this injury? | A. Normal PCL | B. Complete PCL tear (grade 3) | C. Partial PCL tear (grade 1-2) | D. PCL mucoid degeneration | C | A thickened PCL with increased intrasubstance signal but residual intact fibers represents a partial (incomplete) PCL tear. A complete tear shows full discontinuity or a diffusely amorphous, edematous ligament. PCL mucoid degeneration also causes thickening and increased signal but typically occurs without an acute injury history and has a characteristic striated pattern.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A skier presents after a valgus injury. MRI shows high signal and thickening of the MCL superficial fibers near the femoral origin with surrounding soft-tissue edema. The deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) is intact. What grade MCL injury is this?
text: A skier presents after a valgus injury. MRI shows high signal and thickening of the MCL superficial fibers near the femoral origin with surrounding soft-tissue edema. The deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) is intact. What grade MCL injury is this? | A. Grade 1 — mild sprain with intact fibers | B. Grade 2 — partial tear with some disrupted fibers but the ligament is not completely torn | C. Grade 3 — complete disruption of both superficial and deep MCL | D. Normal MCL with adjacent soft-tissue contusion | B | MCL Grade 2 injuries show partial disruption of fibers with thickening and high signal within the ligament on fluid-sensitive sequences, but some fibers remain intact. Grade 1 shows edema around an intact ligament; Grade 3 shows complete disruption. The location of injury (femoral, tibial, or mid-substance) should also be reported as it influences surgical planning.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A patient sustains a combined varus and external rotation injury. On MRI, you identify tears of the fibular collateral ligament (LCL), popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament. This constellation of findings represents injury to which anatomic structure?
text: A patient sustains a combined varus and external rotation injury. On MRI, you identify tears of the fibular collateral ligament (LCL), popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament. This constellation of findings represents injury to which anatomic structure? | A. Medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) complex | B. Posterolateral corner (PLC) | C. Posteromedial corner | D. Iliotibial band complex | B | The posterolateral corner (PLC) consists of three main structures: the fibular (lateral) collateral ligament, the popliteus tendon, and the popliteofibular ligament. PLC injuries are frequently associated with cruciate ligament tears and, if unrecognized, lead to persistent rotational instability and failure of cruciate ligament reconstruction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Following a first-time lateral patellar dislocation, which ligamentous structure is most commonly injured and should be specifically evaluated on axial MRI?
text: Following a first-time lateral patellar dislocation, which ligamentous structure is most commonly injured and should be specifically evaluated on axial MRI? | A. Medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) | B. Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) | C. Lateral collateral ligament (LCL) | D. Posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) | A | The MPFL is the primary restraint to lateral patellar translation and is torn in over 90% of acute lateral patellar dislocations. It is best evaluated on axial images, and the location of the tear (femoral, mid-substance, or patellar) should be reported as it influences surgical repair strategy.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: An ACL shows diffuse increased intrasubstance signal and is thickened, but its fibers remain CONTINUOUS and parallel to Blumensaat's line, with no secondary signs of instability. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: An ACL shows diffuse increased intrasubstance signal and is thickened, but its fibers remain CONTINUOUS and parallel to Blumensaat's line, with no secondary signs of instability. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Acute complete ACL tear | B. Mucoid degeneration of the ACL ("celery stalk" appearance) | C. Acute partial ACL tear with instability | D. ACL graft re-tear | B | Mucoid degeneration thickens the ACL with diffuse increased T2 signal between INTACT, continuous fibers — the 'celery stalk' appearance — often with associated intraosseous ganglion cysts at the tibial attachment. The key is preserved fiber continuity and the ABSENCE of secondary signs (no anterior tibial translation, deep notch, or pivot-shift contusions). Do not over-call it as a tear; patients are typically stable with mechanical/extension-loss symptoms.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When evaluating an ACL reconstruction graft, malposition of which tunnel most specifically predicts persistent rotational (pivot-shift) instability?
text: When evaluating an ACL reconstruction graft, malposition of which tunnel most specifically predicts persistent rotational (pivot-shift) instability? | A. A vertical / too-anterior FEMORAL tunnel | B. A posterior femoral tunnel | C. A lateral meniscal tunnel | D. The patellar donor site | A | A vertical or too-anterior FEMORAL tunnel produces a vertically-oriented graft that controls anterior translation but fails to resist rotation — the classic cause of a persistent pivot shift despite an intact-appearing graft. On sagittal MRI the femoral tunnel should sit in the posterior aspect of the notch. (An anterior TIBIAL tunnel instead causes roof/notch impingement of the graft in extension.) Tunnel malposition predicts failure even when the graft fibers look continuous.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: In a patient with combined ACL + MCL injury, why must you specifically evaluate the posteromedial corner (posterior oblique ligament, semimembranosus expansions)?
text: In a patient with combined ACL + MCL injury, why must you specifically evaluate the posteromedial corner (posterior oblique ligament, semimembranosus expansions)? | A. It is the donor site for ACL reconstruction | B. Combined ACL + MCL + PMC injury produces anteromedial rotatory instability that may need PMC repair in addition to ACL surgery | C. The PMC is the primary restraint to anterior translation | D. PMC injury is purely incidental and does not affect management | B | The posteromedial corner (posterior oblique ligament + semimembranosus expansions) is a key rotational stabilizer. Combined ACL + MCL + PMC injury creates ANTEROMEDIAL rotatory instability; an unrecognized PMC injury is a cause of residual instability/graft failure and may require PMC repair or reconstruction alongside the ACL. Always scrutinize the PMC when you see an MCL tear with an ACL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A high-energy knee injury shows tears of BOTH cruciates plus a posterolateral corner injury (knee-dislocation pattern). Beyond the ligaments, which structure must be specifically assessed?
text: A high-energy knee injury shows tears of BOTH cruciates plus a posterolateral corner injury (knee-dislocation pattern). Beyond the ligaments, which structure must be specifically assessed? | A. The popliteal artery (and common peroneal nerve) | B. The anterior tibial recurrent artery | C. The saphenous vein | D. The descending genicular artery | A | A multiligament knee injury (bicruciate ± corner = knee dislocation, even if spontaneously reduced) carries a significant risk of POPLITEAL ARTERY injury (intimal tear, occlusion, transection) and, with PLC injuries, common peroneal nerve injury. Always assess the popliteal vessels (MRI flow voids / CT or conventional angiography) and the peroneal nerve — a missed vascular injury threatens the limb. Report all torn ligaments, the involved corner(s), and the neurovascular status.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI, the quadriceps tendon appears as a trilaminar structure at the superior pole of the patella. Which layer is most often involved in a PARTIAL tear, and why can it be clinically subtle?
text: On sagittal MRI, the quadriceps tendon appears as a trilaminar structure at the superior pole of the patella. Which layer is most often involved in a PARTIAL tear, and why can it be clinically subtle? | A. The superficial layer (rectus femoris); the deep fibers are typically spared | B. The deep layer (vastus intermedius); the intact superficial fibers can preserve active extension, making it deceptively normal on exam | C. The middle layer (vastus medialis/lateralis); the other layers are never involved | D. The tendon is a single homogeneous layer, so tears occur uniformly | B | The quadriceps tendon is trilaminar: superficial (rectus femoris), middle (vastus medialis/lateralis), and deep (vastus intermedius). Partial tears most commonly involve the deep (vastus intermedius) layer near the patellar insertion. Because the robust superficial rectus femoris fibers often remain intact, the patient may still actively extend the knee, making the injury deceptively subtle — scrutinize the deep aspect of the tendon on sagittal PD/PD-FS images.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 40-year-old male presents with inability to extend the knee after jumping. MRI shows complete discontinuity of the patellar tendon with retraction and a superiorly displaced patella. What additional finding should you evaluate for?
text: A 40-year-old male presents with inability to extend the knee after jumping. MRI shows complete discontinuity of the patellar tendon with retraction and a superiorly displaced patella. What additional finding should you evaluate for? | A. ACL tear | B. Associated meniscal tear | C. Patella alta and degree of tendon retraction | D. Lateral collateral ligament tear | C | In patellar tendon rupture, the patella retracts superiorly (patella alta) due to the unopposed pull of the quadriceps. Reporting the degree of retraction and the gap size helps the surgeon plan repair. An Insall-Salvati ratio greater than 1.2 supports the diagnosis of patella alta, though comparison with the contralateral side is ideal.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: An MRI shows a well-corticated osseous fragment at the superolateral pole of the patella with smooth margins and no surrounding marrow edema. The patient is asymptomatic in this region. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: An MRI shows a well-corticated osseous fragment at the superolateral pole of the patella with smooth margins and no surrounding marrow edema. The patient is asymptomatic in this region. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Acute patellar sleeve fracture | B. Bipartite patella (normal variant) | C. Osteochondral fracture from patellar dislocation | D. Avulsion fracture of the quadriceps tendon insertion | B | Bipartite patella is a normal developmental variant, most commonly located at the superolateral pole, with smooth, well-corticated margins and no associated marrow edema. In contrast, an acute fracture would show irregular margins, marrow edema, and surrounding soft-tissue changes. Bipartite patella can occasionally become symptomatic with overuse.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On axial and sagittal MRI, you see a well-defined fluid collection anterior to the patellar tendon, superficial to the tibial tubercle. The patient is a carpet layer who works on his knees. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: On axial and sagittal MRI, you see a well-defined fluid collection anterior to the patellar tendon, superficial to the tibial tubercle. The patient is a carpet layer who works on his knees. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Prepatellar bursitis | B. Superficial infrapatellar bursitis | C. Deep infrapatellar bursitis (behind the patellar tendon) | D. Baker cyst (popliteal cyst) | B | The superficial infrapatellar bursa is located anterior to the distal patellar tendon and tibial tubercle. It is commonly inflamed in people who frequently kneel (clergyman's knee / carpet layer's knee). The prepatellar bursa is more superior, anterior to the patella itself. Distinguishing these bursae helps correlate imaging findings with the patient's occupational history.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A knee MRI shows a fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa (lipohemarthrosis). What does this finding tell you, and what must you do next?
text: A knee MRI shows a fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa (lipohemarthrosis). What does this finding tell you, and what must you do next? | A. It is a benign reactive effusion; no further action | B. It is pathognomonic of an intra-articular fracture; systematically search for an occult fracture (tibial plateau, patella, femoral condyle) | C. It indicates septic arthritis; aspirate urgently | D. It is a sign of PVNS; obtain a gradient-echo sequence | B | A lipohemarthrosis — a fat-fluid (or fat-fluid-fluid) level in the suprapatellar bursa — is pathognomonic of an INTRA-ARTICULAR FRACTURE: marrow fat and blood leak into the joint and layer out by density. When you see it, perform a systematic search for an occult fracture of the tibial plateau, patella, or femoral condyle — it may be invisible on initial radiographs.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A popliteal (Baker's) cyst is identified. Between which two structures does it characteristically arise, and what should you always look for?
text: A popliteal (Baker's) cyst is identified. Between which two structures does it characteristically arise, and what should you always look for? | A. Between the biceps femoris and LCL; look for a Segond fracture | B. Between the medial head of gastrocnemius and the semimembranosus tendon; look for an underlying internal derangement (meniscal tear, cartilage loss, synovitis) | C. Anterior to the patellar tendon; look for Hoffa impingement | D. Within the popliteus tendon sheath; look for a PCL tear | B | A Baker's cyst arises posteromedially between the medial head of gastrocnemius and the semimembranosus tendon. It is usually SECONDARY to intra-articular pathology, so always search for the cause (meniscal tear, cartilage loss, synovitis). A ruptured Baker's cyst dissects inferiorly into the calf and mimics a DVT clinically.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Diffusely low-signal synovial thickening that "blooms" on gradient-echo (GRE) imaging is the hallmark of which condition?
text: Diffusely low-signal synovial thickening that "blooms" on gradient-echo (GRE) imaging is the hallmark of which condition? | A. Synovial (osteo)chondromatosis | B. Pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS) | C. Inflammatory (rheumatoid) synovitis | D. Lipoma arborescens | B | PVNS deposits HEMOSIDERIN, which is low signal on all sequences and BLOOMS (susceptibility) on gradient-echo. This distinguishes it from synovial chondromatosis (cartilage-signal loose bodies, often calcified, no blooming), inflammatory synovitis (intermediate-signal synovium that enhances), and lipoma arborescens (frond-like FAT-signal synovial proliferation).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 22-year-old sprinter feels a sudden tearing in the back of the thigh at the start of a race and develops posterior thigh ecchymosis. MRI shows complete avulsion of all three hamstring components from the ischial tuberosity, with the retracted stump measuring 3 cm from the footprint and abutting the sciatic nerve. Which reporting element most directly supports the typical decision to refer for surgical repair?
text: A 22-year-old sprinter feels a sudden tearing in the back of the thigh at the start of a race and develops posterior thigh ecchymosis. MRI shows complete avulsion of all three hamstring components from the ischial tuberosity, with the retracted stump measuring 3 cm from the footprint and abutting the sciatic nerve. Which reporting element most directly supports the typical decision to refer for surgical repair? | A. Grade 1 feathery perifascial edema along the biceps femoris | B. Complete avulsion of all three components with ~3 cm retraction | C. An interfascial fluid collection between gastrocnemius and soleus | D. Intact muscle architecture with no discrete fiber gap | B | Complete avulsion of all three hamstring components (the conjoint tendon — biceps femoris long head plus semitendinosus — together with the semimembranosus) with significant retraction is the injury typically referred for surgical repair, and sciatic-nerve proximity adds weight to that decision; a ~2 cm retraction is a commonly cited guide. Option A (Grade 1 edema) and option D (intact architecture, no gap) describe low-grade strains managed conservatively, and option C describes tennis leg, not a hamstring avulsion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 45-year-old recreational tennis player feels a sudden 'pop' in the calf mid-match and cannot bear weight. MRI shows fluid tracking between the medial gastrocnemius and the soleus aponeurosis at the distal myotendinous junction. Beyond describing the tear, what is the single most important additional structure to evaluate before finalizing the report?
text: A 45-year-old recreational tennis player feels a sudden 'pop' in the calf mid-match and cannot bear weight. MRI shows fluid tracking between the medial gastrocnemius and the soleus aponeurosis at the distal myotendinous junction. Beyond describing the tear, what is the single most important additional structure to evaluate before finalizing the report? | A. The popliteal vein, to exclude deep venous thrombosis | B. The proximal tibiofibular joint, for a ganglion | C. The quadriceps tendon insertion on the patella | D. The adductor longus origin at the pubis | A | Tennis leg (distal medial gastrocnemius MTJ tear with gastrocnemius–soleus interfascial fluid), DVT, and ruptured Baker's cyst are clinically indistinguishable, so the popliteal vein must be interrogated to exclude DVT before signing off. Options B, C, and D are unrelated structures that do not share this presentation and do not address the critical DVT/Baker's-cyst differential for acute calf pain.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI, you see only one bow-tie configuration of the medial meniscus body instead of the expected two. On the coronal images, there is a low-signal fragment in the intercondylar notch deep to the PCL. What is the unifying diagnosis?
text: On sagittal MRI, you see only one bow-tie configuration of the medial meniscus body instead of the expected two. On the coronal images, there is a low-signal fragment in the intercondylar notch deep to the PCL. What is the unifying diagnosis? | A. Discoid medial meniscus | B. Displaced bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus | C. Medial meniscus posterior root tear | D. Normal variant — the medial meniscus can have variable body width | B | The absent bow-tie sign (fewer than two bow-tie images on sagittal MRI) combined with a fragment in the intercondylar notch (the double PCL sign) is diagnostic of a displaced bucket-handle tear. This is a surgically urgent finding because the displaced fragment causes mechanical locking and the tear may be repairable if identified early.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 15-year-old presents with knee pain. MRI shows an OCD lesion of the medial femoral condyle with a 1.5 cm fragment. Which finding would make this fragment STABLE and potentially amenable to conservative management?
text: A 15-year-old presents with knee pain. MRI shows an OCD lesion of the medial femoral condyle with a 1.5 cm fragment. Which finding would make this fragment STABLE and potentially amenable to conservative management? | A. Fluid signal (high T2) completely surrounding the fragment | B. Cyst-like lesion at the fragment-bone interface | C. Intact overlying articular cartilage with no fluid at the fragment interface and no underlying cysts | D. Fragment displaced into the joint space | C | A stable OCD fragment has intact overlying cartilage, no high-T2 signal (fluid) at the fragment-parent bone interface, and no cyst-like lesions underneath. Stable lesions in skeletally immature patients have a high potential for healing with conservative management. Any signs of instability (fluid at the interface, cysts, cartilage breach, or fragment displacement) favor surgical intervention.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a coronal MRI of the knee, you identify a small avulsion fracture at the tip of the fibular head. What associated injury pattern should this finding immediately raise concern for?
text: On a coronal MRI of the knee, you identify a small avulsion fracture at the tip of the fibular head. What associated injury pattern should this finding immediately raise concern for? | A. Isolated LCL sprain | B. Posterolateral corner injury (arcuate sign) — evaluate for associated cruciate ligament tears | C. Proximal tibiofibular joint dislocation | D. Iliotibial band friction syndrome | B | The arcuate sign is a small avulsion fracture at the fibular styloid process (tip of the fibular head), indicating avulsion of the arcuate ligament complex. This is a hallmark of posterolateral corner injury and is frequently associated with cruciate ligament tears. Missing a PLC injury can lead to failure of cruciate ligament reconstruction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A patient is 6 months post partial meniscectomy and presents with recurrent medial knee pain. On MRI, you see high signal within the residual meniscus on PD FS sequences. How do you differentiate a re-tear from normal post-surgical signal changes?
text: A patient is 6 months post partial meniscectomy and presents with recurrent medial knee pain. On MRI, you see high signal within the residual meniscus on PD FS sequences. How do you differentiate a re-tear from normal post-surgical signal changes? | A. Any high signal in a post-surgical meniscus is always a re-tear | B. Post-surgical menisci never show signal abnormalities on MRI | C. The only reliable way to assess a post-surgical meniscus is with MR arthrography | D. High signal that is unequivocally as bright as fluid on T2/PD FS and extends to an articular surface suggests a re-tear; intermediate signal may represent stable post-surgical change | D | Post-surgical menisci commonly show grade 2-3 signal changes from scar tissue, granulation tissue, and altered collagen architecture. A true re-tear is suggested when signal within the meniscal remnant is unequivocally as bright as fluid on fluid-sensitive sequences and extends to an articular surface. If there is diagnostic uncertainty, MR arthrography can improve accuracy.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a lateral radiograph and confirmed on sagittal MRI, you identify a fat-fluid level within the suprapatellar bursa (lipohemarthrosis). What is the clinical significance of this finding?
text: On a lateral radiograph and confirmed on sagittal MRI, you identify a fat-fluid level within the suprapatellar bursa (lipohemarthrosis). What is the clinical significance of this finding? | A. This is a normal finding after knee aspiration | B. It indicates a simple joint effusion from synovitis | C. It represents a ruptured Baker cyst | D. It indicates an intra-articular fracture, as fat from the bone marrow has entered the joint space through the fracture site | D | A lipohemarthrosis (fat-fluid level in the joint) is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture. Fat from the bone marrow cavity enters the joint through a fracture that communicates with the articular surface. When this finding is identified, a careful search for an osteochondral fracture, tibial plateau fracture, or other intra-articular fracture is mandatory.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the straight striated band marked here, paralleling the roof of the intercondylar notch?
text: What is the straight striated band marked here, paralleling the roof of the intercondylar notch? | A. Anterior cruciate ligament | B. Posterior cruciate ligament | C. Meniscofemoral ligament | D. Posterior joint capsule | A | On this lateral notch slice, the straight, slightly striated band running parallel to Blumensaat's line from the lateral femoral condyle to the anterior tibial spine is the ACL. The PCL is smoother, darker, more sharply curved, and sits more posteriorly and medially in the notch, so it would not lie along the notch roof on this lateral slice.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Femoral condyle | B. Patella | C. Tibial plateau | D. Fabella | A | The large rounded bone forming the top of the joint, with low-signal cortex and bright fatty marrow, is the femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Femoral condyle | B. Tibial plateau | C. Patella | D. Fibular head | B | The flat bone below the joint line is the tibial plateau (proximal tibia).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Patella | B. Femoral condyle | C. Quadriceps tendon | D. Tibial tubercle | A | The anterior bone at the front of the knee, with marrow signal, is the patella.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Quadriceps tendon | B. Patellar tendon | C. Iliotibial band | D. Prepatellar bursa | A | The striated multi-layered tendon inserting on the superior pole of the patella is the quadriceps tendon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Patellar tendon | B. Quadriceps tendon | C. ACL | D. Popliteus tendon | A | The uniformly low-signal band from the inferior pole of the patella to the tibial tubercle is the patellar tendon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Hoffa's infrapatellar fat pad | B. Joint effusion | C. Baker's cyst | D. Prefemoral fat | A | The fat behind the patellar tendon and in front of the joint is Hoffa's (infrapatellar) fat pad.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Posterior horn of the meniscus | B. PCL | C. Posterior joint capsule | D. Popliteus tendon | A | The low-signal triangle at the posterior joint line is the posterior horn of the meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Anterior horn of the meniscus | B. Hoffa's fat pad | C. ACL | D. Transverse ligament | A | The low-signal triangle at the anterior joint line is the anterior horn of the meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The bright line outlining the joint surface here is normal — what is it?
text: The bright line outlining the joint surface here is normal — what is it? | A. Articular cartilage with a thin rim of joint fluid | B. A meniscal tear | C. Subchondral edema | D. Synovitis | A | Smooth cartilage over the bone ends, outlined by a thin bright rim of joint fluid, is the normal articular surface. A small amount of fluid is expected.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Popliteal vessels | B. PCL | C. Baker's cyst | D. Semimembranosus tendon | A | The vertical tubular structures in the popliteal fossa behind the joint are the popliteal artery and vein.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You've found this meniscus on the sagittal. To judge how far it extrudes (a root-tear clue), which plane do you switch to?
text: You've found this meniscus on the sagittal. To judge how far it extrudes (a root-tear clue), which plane do you switch to? | A. Coronal | B. Axial | C. A different sagittal slice | D. Extrusion can't be measured on MRI | A | Meniscal extrusion is graded on the CORONAL image at the mid-body — >3 mm past the tibial margin is significant and should trigger a deliberate posterior-root search. Build the habit of correlating planes.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure running along the medial edge of the joint?
text: What is the marked structure running along the medial edge of the joint? | A. Medial collateral ligament | B. Pes anserine tendons | C. Medial meniscus body | D. Semimembranosus tendon | A | The thin, taut low-signal band coursing along the medial margin from the medial femoral epicondyle to the proximal tibia is the medial collateral ligament. The pes anserine tendons lie more superficial and anteromedial and insert lower on the tibia, not as a single taut femorotibial band at the joint line.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the cord-like structure at the lateral margin coursing toward the fibular head?
text: What is the cord-like structure at the lateral margin coursing toward the fibular head? | A. Lateral (fibular) collateral ligament | B. Iliotibial band | C. Popliteus tendon | D. Biceps femoris tendon | A | The discrete cord-like ligament descending from the lateral femoral epicondyle to the fibular head is the lateral (fibular) collateral ligament. The iliotibial band inserts more anteriorly at Gerdy tubercle on the tibia, not the fibular head, so it would not converge on the fibular apex like the marked structure.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the central bony peaks marked here at the mid-joint?
text: What are the central bony peaks marked here at the mid-joint? | A. Tibial spines (intercondylar eminence) | B. Femoral intercondylar notch roof | C. Avulsed cruciate fragment | D. Osteophytes of the tibial plateau | A | The paired central peaks rising from the tibia between the condyles are the tibial spines (intercondylar eminence), the landmark for the true mid-joint coronal slice. They are a normal osseous structure, not an avulsed fragment; an eminence avulsion would show a displaced bone fragment with a donor-site defect and marrow edema.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Medial femoral condyle | B. Lateral femoral condyle | C. Medial tibial plateau | D. Patella | A | The condyle on the medial (left) side, over the medial tibial plateau, is the medial femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Lateral femoral condyle | B. Medial femoral condyle | C. Lateral tibial plateau | D. Fibular head | A | The condyle on the lateral (right) side is the lateral femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Tibial plateau | B. Femoral condyle | C. Tibial spine | D. Fibula | A | The flat bone below the joint line is the tibial plateau.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Medial meniscus (body) | B. Lateral meniscus (body) | C. MCL | D. Medial femoral condyle | A | The low-signal triangle at the medial joint margin is the body of the medial meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Lateral meniscus (body) | B. Medial meniscus (body) | C. LCL | D. Popliteus tendon | A | The low-signal triangle at the lateral joint margin is the body of the lateral meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The bright line outlining the joint surface here is normal — what is it?
text: The bright line outlining the joint surface here is normal — what is it? | A. Articular cartilage with a thin rim of joint fluid | B. A chondral defect | C. Subchondral cyst | D. Meniscal tear | A | Smooth cartilage over the bone ends, outlined by a thin bright fluid rim, is the normal articular surface.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: This is the medial meniscus. Beyond how much extrusion past the tibial margin is considered significant and should trigger a root search?
text: This is the medial meniscus. Beyond how much extrusion past the tibial margin is considered significant and should trigger a root search? | A. >3 mm | B. >1 mm | C. >8 mm | D. Any extrusion at all | A | Medial meniscal extrusion >3 mm beyond the tibial margin is significant and should make you inspect the posterior root, but extrusion alone does not prove a root tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The cruciate ligaments arise near these central tibial spines. On which plane do you actually trace the ACL along Blumensaat's line?
text: The cruciate ligaments arise near these central tibial spines. On which plane do you actually trace the ACL along Blumensaat's line? | A. Sagittal | B. Coronal | C. Axial | D. All planes equally | A | The ACL is best traced on the SAGITTAL plane, paralleling Blumensaat's line (the intercondylar roof); coronal and axial confirm. Correlate planes for every ligament.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked grooved anterior femoral structure the patella tracks within?
text: What is the marked grooved anterior femoral structure the patella tracks within? | A. Femoral trochlea | B. Intercondylar notch | C. Median patellar ridge | D. Tibial tuberosity | A | The grooved anterior femur against which the patella articulates is the femoral trochlea; its lateral facet is normally longer, forming a buttress against lateral patellar subluxation. The median patellar ridge belongs to the patella (the anterior bone), not the femur, so it is on the opposite side of the patellofemoral joint.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked soft-tissue band on the lateral side of the patella?
text: What is the marked soft-tissue band on the lateral side of the patella? | A. Lateral retinaculum | B. MPFL (medial retinaculum) | C. Iliotibial band | D. Vastus lateralis tendon | A | The retinacular soft tissue tethering the patella on its lateral aspect is the lateral retinaculum. The MPFL is the principal medial restraint and lies on the opposite (medial) side of the patella, so a lateral marker cannot be the MPFL.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Patella | B. Femoral trochlea | C. Quadriceps tendon | D. Tibial tubercle | A | The anterior sesamoid bone at the front of the knee is the patella.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The bright line between the patella and the trochlea is normal — what is it?
text: The bright line between the patella and the trochlea is normal — what is it? | A. Patellofemoral articular cartilage with joint fluid | B. A chondral fissure | C. Synovitis | D. Plica | A | Smooth patellar and trochlear cartilage outlined by a thin bright fluid rim is the normal patellofemoral articulation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Trochlear groove (sulcus) | B. Intercondylar notch | C. Median patellar ridge | D. Trochlear cartilage defect | A | The deepest central point of the trochlea is the trochlear groove (sulcus); flattening here indicates dysplasia.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Which medial restraint of the patella is marked (the structure torn after a lateral patellar dislocation)?
text: Which medial restraint of the patella is marked (the structure torn after a lateral patellar dislocation)? | A. MPFL / medial retinaculum | B. Lateral retinaculum | C. Patellar tendon | D. Iliotibial band | A | The medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) is the main restraint against lateral dislocation and the structure injured after a patellar dislocation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Medial femoral condyle | B. Lateral femoral condyle | C. Medial tibial plateau | D. Patella | A | The condyle on the medial (left) side behind the trochlea is the medial femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Lateral femoral condyle | B. Medial femoral condyle | C. Fibular head | D. Lateral meniscus | A | The condyle on the lateral (right) side is the lateral femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Popliteal vessels | B. PCL | C. Baker's cyst | D. Semimembranosus tendon | A | The tubular structures in the popliteal fossa posteriorly are the popliteal artery and vein.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A shallow, flat trochlear groove on the axial images predisposes to which problem?
text: A shallow, flat trochlear groove on the axial images predisposes to which problem? | A. Patellar instability / dislocation | B. ACL tear | C. Meniscal root tear | D. Baker's cyst | A | A flat/shallow trochlea (trochlear dysplasia) is a key predisposing factor for recurrent patellar instability and lateral dislocation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: This is the MPFL. After a transient lateral patellar dislocation, where do you expect the classic bone bruises?
text: This is the MPFL. After a transient lateral patellar dislocation, where do you expect the classic bone bruises? | A. Medial patella + anterolateral femoral condyle | B. Posterolateral tibia + lateral femoral condyle | C. Anterior tibial plateau | D. Medial femoral condyle alone | A | Lateral patellar dislocation classically bruises the INFEROMEDIAL patellar facet and the ANTEROLATERAL femoral condyle as the patella relocates — and tears the MPFL, most often at its femoral (adductor tubercle) origin (patellar-sided and mid-substance tears also occur).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Femoral condyle | B. Patella | C. Tibial plateau | D. Fabella | A | The large rounded bone with low-signal cortex and bright fatty marrow is the femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Tibial plateau | B. Femoral condyle | C. Patella | D. Fibular head | A | The bone below the joint line is the tibial plateau.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Patella | B. Femoral condyle | C. Quadriceps tendon | D. Tibial tubercle | A | The anterior bone at the front of the knee is the patella.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Quadriceps tendon | B. Patellar tendon | C. Iliotibial band | D. Prepatellar bursa | A | The tendon inserting on the superior pole of the patella is the quadriceps tendon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Patellar tendon | B. Quadriceps tendon | C. ACL | D. Popliteus tendon | A | The low-signal band from the inferior patella to the tibial tubercle is the patellar tendon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Hoffa's infrapatellar fat pad | B. Joint effusion | C. Baker's cyst | D. Prefemoral fat | A | The bright fat behind the patellar tendon is Hoffa's (infrapatellar) fat pad.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Posterior horn of the meniscus | B. PCL | C. Posterior joint capsule | D. Popliteus tendon | A | The low-signal triangle at the posterior joint line is the posterior horn of the meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Anterior horn of the meniscus | B. Hoffa's fat pad | C. ACL | D. Transverse ligament | A | The low-signal triangle at the anterior joint line is the anterior horn of the meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this T1 image, how does normal bone marrow appear?
text: On this T1 image, how does normal bone marrow appear? | A. Bright (fatty) | B. Dark (low signal) | C. The same signal as joint fluid | D. Bright only with fat suppression | A | Normal marrow is bright on T1 because fatty marrow is not suppressed. Focal DARK marrow signal (e.g., a fracture line) is the key abnormality T1 detects.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Popliteal vessels | B. PCL | C. Baker's cyst | D. Semimembranosus tendon | A | The tubular structures in the popliteal fossa posteriorly are the popliteal artery and vein.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On T1 you spot a faint dark line in the bright marrow. Which sequence best confirms the surrounding marrow edema?
text: On T1 you spot a faint dark line in the bright marrow. Which sequence best confirms the surrounding marrow edema? | A. Fluid-sensitive PD/T2 fat-sat | B. T1 (this sequence) | C. Gradient echo | D. The localizer | A | T1 shows the fracture line and marrow replacement; the fat-suppressed fluid-sensitive sequence (PD/T2 FS) best shows the surrounding bright edema. Read marrow on both.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Humeral head | B. Glenoid | C. Acromion | D. Coracoid | A | On a lateral sagittal slice the round ball capped by the cuff is the humeral head.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Which cuff tendon drapes over the superior humeral head?
text: Which cuff tendon drapes over the superior humeral head? | A. Supraspinatus | B. Subscapularis | C. Teres minor | D. Deltoid | A | The supraspinatus tendon caps the superior humeral head, running to the greater-tuberosity footprint.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Which cuff muscle is anterior, the largest, and inserts on the lesser tuberosity?
text: Which cuff muscle is anterior, the largest, and inserts on the lesser tuberosity? | A. Subscapularis | B. Infraspinatus | C. Supraspinatus | D. Teres minor | A | Subscapularis is the anterior and largest cuff muscle; its tendon inserts on the lesser tuberosity.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Infraspinatus / teres minor | B. Subscapularis | C. Supraspinatus | D. Deltoid | A | The posterior cuff muscles are infraspinatus (superior) and teres minor (inferior).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Between supraspinatus and subscapularis, this space holds the biceps and its pulley — what is it?
text: Between supraspinatus and subscapularis, this space holds the biceps and its pulley — what is it? | A. Rotator interval | B. Subacromial bursa | C. Axillary recess | D. Quadrilateral space | A | The rotator interval (between supraspinatus and subscapularis) contains the long head of biceps and the coracohumeral/superior glenohumeral ligament pulley.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A hooked or down-sloping shape of which structure can narrow the supraspinatus outlet?
text: A hooked or down-sloping shape of which structure can narrow the supraspinatus outlet? | A. Acromion | B. Coracoid | C. Greater tuberosity | D. Clavicle | A | The acromion can narrow the subacromial outlet when hooked, down-sloping, or unfused as an os acromiale. Treat that morphology as a risk factor to correlate with symptoms, dynamic exam, bursal change, and cuff findings.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Glenoid & labrum | B. Humeral head | C. Acromion | D. Coracoid | A | The socket of the scapula rimmed by the low-signal labrum is the glenoid and labrum.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Deltoid | B. Supraspinatus | C. Infraspinatus | D. Trapezius | A | The deltoid is the outer muscular envelope, NOT a rotator cuff (SITS) muscle; it lies superficial to the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this scapular-Y view, which rotator cuff muscle fills the fossa ABOVE the scapular spine?
text: On this scapular-Y view, which rotator cuff muscle fills the fossa ABOVE the scapular spine? | A. Supraspinatus | B. Infraspinatus | C. Subscapularis | D. Teres minor | A | On the scapular-Y the supraspinatus occupies the supraspinous fossa above the scapular spine; it is the most superior cuff muscle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Most degenerative tears of the marked supraspinatus tendon begin ~1 cm from its footprint, in which region?
text: Most degenerative tears of the marked supraspinatus tendon begin ~1 cm from its footprint, in which region? | A. Critical zone | B. Rotator interval | C. Footprint enthesis | D. Bare area | A | The hypovascular 'critical zone' ~1 cm proximal to the greater-tuberosity footprint is where most degenerative supraspinatus tears begin.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The marked rotator interval houses the biceps pulley. Which two ligaments form that pulley?
text: The marked rotator interval houses the biceps pulley. Which two ligaments form that pulley? | A. Coracohumeral + superior glenohumeral ligaments | B. Coracoacromial + transverse ligaments | C. Inferior glenohumeral ligament complex | D. Middle glenohumeral + spiral glenoid ligaments | A | The rotator-interval biceps pulley is the coracohumeral (CHL) plus superior glenohumeral (SGHL) ligament; a pulley tear lets the biceps subluxate medially.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this en-face glenoid, a tear of the anteroinferior labrum (3-6 o'clock) is which lesion?
text: On this en-face glenoid, a tear of the anteroinferior labrum (3-6 o'clock) is which lesion? | A. Bankart (anteroinferior labral) lesion | B. SLAP tear | C. Hill-Sachs lesion | D. Bennett lesion | A | On the sagittal-oblique en-face glenoid clock-face, the anteroinferior labrum (3-6 o'clock) is the Bankart zone torn after anterior dislocation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked dependent inferior pouch of the joint capsule below the humeral head?
text: What is the marked dependent inferior pouch of the joint capsule below the humeral head? | A. Axillary recess | B. Subacromial-subdeltoid bursa | C. Bicipital groove | D. Subscapular recess | A | The dependent inferior capsular pouch below the humeral head is the axillary recess, formed by the inferior glenohumeral ligament complex; it is the site to assess for capsular thickening/edema in adhesive capsulitis. The subacromial-subdeltoid bursa lies superficial to the cuff, above the head, not inferior to it.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked lateral bony footprint onto which the supraspinatus and infraspinatus insert?
text: What is the marked lateral bony footprint onto which the supraspinatus and infraspinatus insert? | A. Greater tuberosity | B. Lesser tuberosity | C. Glenoid | D. Coracoid | A | The greater tuberosity is the lateral footprint for the supraspinatus and infraspinatus; a subcortical cyst or marrow edema here is a soft sign of an adjacent cuff tear. The lesser tuberosity, anteriorly, is the subscapularis insertion, not the supraspinatus/infraspinatus footprint.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The cuff tendon followed to its footprint here is the…
text: The cuff tendon followed to its footprint here is the… | A. Supraspinatus | B. Subscapularis | C. Biceps | D. Deltoid | A | On coronal the supraspinatus tendon is followed along its length to the greater-tuberosity footprint.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Fluid undercuts the superior labrum with a smooth, medially-directed margin paralleling the glenoid. What is this most likely?
text: Fluid undercuts the superior labrum with a smooth, medially-directed margin paralleling the glenoid. What is this most likely? | A. Sublabral recess (normal variant) | B. Type II SLAP tear | C. Bankart lesion | D. Paralabral cyst | A | A smooth, medially-oriented cleft paralleling the glenoid is the normal sublabral recess; a laterally-curved, irregular cleft extending into the labral substance favors a SLAP tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The thin fat/fluid plane between the cuff and the acromion/deltoid is the…
text: The thin fat/fluid plane between the cuff and the acromion/deltoid is the… | A. Subacromial–subdeltoid bursa | B. Glenohumeral joint | C. Axillary recess | D. Rotator interval | A | The plane between the cuff and the overlying acromion/deltoid is the subacromial–subdeltoid bursa.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Acromion / AC joint | B. Greater tuberosity | C. Humeral head | D. Coracoid | A | The bony arch and its joint with the distal clavicle is the acromion / AC joint.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Humeral head | B. Glenoid | C. Acromion | D. Coracoid | A | The round proximal humerus capped by the supraspinatus tendon on the coronal is the humeral head.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Deltoid | B. Supraspinatus | C. Trapezius | D. Subscapularis | A | The deltoid drapes the greater tuberosity laterally as the outer envelope, separated from the cuff by the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Fluid in the marked subacromial-subdeltoid bursa AND the glenohumeral joint together most strongly suggests what?
text: Fluid in the marked subacromial-subdeltoid bursa AND the glenohumeral joint together most strongly suggests what? | A. Full-thickness rotator cuff tear | B. Isolated subacromial bursitis | C. Adhesive capsulitis | D. Normal finding | A | Communicating fluid in BOTH the SASD bursa and the joint strongly suggests a full-thickness cuff tear; isolated bursal fluid is nonspecific and does not diagnose a full-thickness tear by itself.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A subcortical cyst or marrow edema at the marked greater-tuberosity footprint is a soft sign of what?
text: A subcortical cyst or marrow edema at the marked greater-tuberosity footprint is a soft sign of what? | A. Adjacent rotator cuff tear | B. AC joint arthrosis | C. Acute fracture | D. Normal apophysis | A | A subcortical cyst or marrow edema at the greater-tuberosity footprint is a soft sign of an adjacent rotator cuff tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The coronal is the best plane to measure which feature of this torn supraspinatus tendon?
text: The coronal is the best plane to measure which feature of this torn supraspinatus tendon? | A. Tendon gap / retraction (Patte stage) | B. Which cuff tendons are torn | C. Fatty muscle atrophy (Goutallier) | D. Labral integrity | A | Coronal measures the tendon GAP and retraction (Patte); the sagittal-Y shows WHICH tendons are torn and T1 shows fatty atrophy (Goutallier).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On the distal supraspinatus, why does the rotator cable/crescent concept matter?
text: On the distal supraspinatus, why does the rotator cable/crescent concept matter? | A. An intact cable can preserve load transfer across a thin crescent tear, while cable disruption can make a tear more functionally important | B. The cable is a normal labral variant and should not affect cuff interpretation | C. The crescent is the biceps pulley, so crescent injury predicts medial biceps dislocation | D. The cable is assessed only on axial images and is unrelated to the supraspinatus footprint | A | The rotator cable is the thicker articular-sided bundle just medial to the thinner distal cuff crescent. Crescent tears are common near the footprint, but an intact cable can help preserve force transmission; cable disruption, especially anteriorly, makes the tear more clinically important.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked anteromedial bony process of the scapula on this axial slice?
text: What is the marked anteromedial bony process of the scapula on this axial slice? | A. Coracoid process | B. Lesser tuberosity | C. Acromion | D. Spine of the scapula | A | The hook-like anteromedial process of the scapula on the higher axial slices is the coracoid, the origin of the conjoint tendon and the anchor for the coracoclavicular and coracoacromial ligaments. The lesser tuberosity is on the humerus (laterally), not the scapula.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this axial slice with anterior at the top, what is the marked dark triangle at the lower glenoid rim?
text: On this axial slice with anterior at the top, what is the marked dark triangle at the lower glenoid rim? | A. Posterior labrum | B. Anterior labrum | C. Glenoid articular cartilage | D. Infraspinatus tendon | A | With anterior at the top of the axial image, the dark fibrocartilage triangle at the LOWER (posterior) glenoid rim is the posterior labrum; blunting or a tear suggests posterior instability or a reverse Bankart. The anterior labrum is the dark triangle at the upper (anterior) rim on the same slice.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Humeral head | B. Glenoid | C. Coracoid process | D. Acromion | A | The round ball with bright marrow and a thin dark cortex, lateral in the image, is the humeral head.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Glenoid | B. Humeral head | C. Coracoid | D. Acromion | A | The shallow comma-shaped articular face of the scapula, medial to the head, is the glenoid.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Anterior labrum | B. Posterior labrum | C. Articular cartilage | D. Subscapularis tendon | A | The dark triangle at the anterior glenoid rim is the anterior labrum — its anteroinferior part is the Bankart site of anterior instability.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The small dark dot seated between the tuberosities here is the…
text: The small dark dot seated between the tuberosities here is the… | A. Long head of biceps tendon in its groove | B. Subscapularis tendon | C. Anterior labrum | D. Joint fluid only | A | A small low-signal dot in the bicipital groove between the tuberosities is the long head of biceps. An empty groove signals a torn or dislocated tendon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: This anterior tendon coursing toward the lesser tuberosity is the…
text: This anterior tendon coursing toward the lesser tuberosity is the… | A. Subscapularis | B. Supraspinatus | C. Infraspinatus | D. Long head of biceps | A | The anterior cuff tendon running to the lesser tuberosity is the subscapularis; its tears are easily missed — look for medial biceps subluxation as a clue.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: If the marked bicipital groove were EMPTY, what would that imply?
text: If the marked bicipital groove were EMPTY, what would that imply? | A. Torn or medially dislocated long head of biceps | B. Normal fluid in the groove | C. Os acromiale | D. SLAP tear | A | An empty bicipital groove means the long head of biceps is torn or medially dislocated; medial dislocation implies a torn subscapularis or biceps pulley.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A tear of the marked anteroinferior labrum, classically after anterior dislocation, is which lesion?
text: A tear of the marked anteroinferior labrum, classically after anterior dislocation, is which lesion? | A. Bankart lesion | B. SLAP tear | C. Buford complex | D. Hill-Sachs lesion | A | A torn anteroinferior labrum after anterior dislocation is a Bankart lesion; an attached osseous fragment makes it a bony Bankart.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A torn posterior labrum (marked) paired with a reverse Hill-Sachs is the signature of what?
text: A torn posterior labrum (marked) paired with a reverse Hill-Sachs is the signature of what? | A. Posterior instability (often post-seizure/electric shock) | B. Anterior Bankart instability | C. SLAP tear | D. AC joint separation | A | A blunted or torn posterior labrum plus a reverse Hill-Sachs on the anteromedial head signals posterior instability, classically after a seizure or electric shock.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A tear of the marked subscapularis tendon most directly destabilizes which structure?
text: A tear of the marked subscapularis tendon most directly destabilizes which structure? | A. Long head of biceps (medial subluxation/dislocation) | B. Supraspinatus tendon | C. Posterior labrum | D. Deltoid | A | A subscapularis tear at the lesser-tuberosity insertion disrupts the biceps pulley, so the long head subluxates or dislocates medially.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked bony shelf that separates the supraspinatus above from the infraspinatus below on this scapular-Y slice?
text: What is the marked bony shelf that separates the supraspinatus above from the infraspinatus below on this scapular-Y slice? | A. Spine of the scapula | B. Coracoid process | C. Acromion | D. Clavicle | A | The scapular spine is the keystone of the Y, dividing the supraspinous fossa (supraspinatus, above) from the infraspinous fossa (infraspinatus, below); use it to be certain which muscle you are grading. The acromion is the lateral continuation of the spine forming the outlet roof, not the dividing shelf between the two fossae.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this lateral sagittal T1 slice, what is the marked rounded structure filled with bright fatty marrow?
text: On this lateral sagittal T1 slice, what is the marked rounded structure filled with bright fatty marrow? | A. Humeral head | B. Glenoid | C. Acromion | D. Coracoid | A | On the lateral T1 slices the humeral head fills the field with uniformly bright fatty marrow; a focal DARK region is the abnormal finding (edema, fracture, or a marrow-replacing lesion). The glenoid is the shallow scapular socket seen on the more medial slices, not this lateral rounded ball.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this scapular-'Y' slice, which cuff muscle sits ABOVE the scapular spine?
text: On this scapular-'Y' slice, which cuff muscle sits ABOVE the scapular spine? | A. Supraspinatus | B. Infraspinatus | C. Subscapularis | D. Teres minor | A | Supraspinatus occupies the supraspinatus fossa above the scapular spine; infraspinatus lies below it.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The large anterior cuff muscle filling the anterior fossa is the…
text: The large anterior cuff muscle filling the anterior fossa is the… | A. Subscapularis | B. Supraspinatus | C. Infraspinatus | D. Deltoid | A | Subscapularis is the anterior rotator cuff; it fills the subscapular fossa and inserts on the lesser tuberosity.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this T1 image, normal humeral-head marrow appears…
text: On this T1 image, normal humeral-head marrow appears… | A. Bright (fatty marrow) | B. Dark | C. Identical to muscle | D. Bright only after contrast | A | Normal marrow is bright on T1 (fat is not suppressed); a focal dark area signals edema, fracture, or a lesion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Glenoid & labrum | B. Humeral head | C. Acromion | D. Coracoid | A | The pear-shaped scapular glenoid rimmed by the low-signal labrum is seen en face on this slice.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: T1 is the best sequence to grade which feature of a chronic cuff tear?
text: T1 is the best sequence to grade which feature of a chronic cuff tear? | A. Fatty muscle atrophy (Goutallier) | B. Subacromial bursal fluid | C. Bone marrow edema | D. Cartilage thickness | A | T1 shows fatty infiltration of the cuff muscles (Goutallier–Fuchs); high-grade atrophy predicts an irreparable tear and changes surgical planning.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
