text: What is the marked structure? | A. Acromion | B. Coracoid | C. Clavicle | D. Greater tuberosity | A | The flat bone forming the roof of the outlet superiorly is the acromion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Infraspinatus | B. Supraspinatus | C. Teres minor | D. Subscapularis | A | The infraspinatus is the large posterior cuff muscle filling the infraspinous fossa below the scapular spine; supraspinatus lies above the spine.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Teres minor | B. Infraspinatus | C. Subscapularis | D. Deltoid | A | Teres minor is the small posterior cuff muscle just below infraspinatus; isolated fatty atrophy points to axillary-nerve (quadrilateral-space) denervation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure?
text: What is the marked structure? | A. Coracoid process | B. Acromion | C. Glenoid | D. Clavicle | A | The coracoid is the anterior hook-like process, origin of the conjoint tendon and the coracoclavicular/coracoacromial ligaments, and a reliable 'anterior' landmark.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why is T1 the sequence used to grade rotator-cuff fatty atrophy (Goutallier)?
text: Why is T1 the sequence used to grade rotator-cuff fatty atrophy (Goutallier)? | A. Fat is bright and unsuppressed on T1, so fatty muscle infiltration is conspicuous | B. Fluid is brightest on T1 | C. Fat is suppressed on T1 | D. T1 has the highest spatial resolution | A | On T1 fat is bright and unsuppressed, so Goutallier fatty infiltration of the cuff muscles stands out -- and its grade predicts whether a tear is repairable.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this coronal T2 FS Dixon image, what is the marked structure seated within the acetabulum?
text: On this coronal T2 FS Dixon image, what is the marked structure seated within the acetabulum? | A. Femoral head | B. Greater trochanter | C. Acetabular roof (sourcil) | D. Femoral neck | A | The spherical epiphysis articulating within the acetabular cup is the femoral head, the surface you screen for AVN and subchondral insufficiency fracture of the femoral head (SIFH). The greater trochanter is the lateral cortical prominence at the abductor footprint — well lateral to and below the joint, not seated within the acetabulum.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The dense subchondral weight-bearing roof of the acetabulum marked here is best termed the:
text: The dense subchondral weight-bearing roof of the acetabulum marked here is best termed the: | A. Triradiate cartilage | B. Sourcil (acetabular roof) | C. Acetabular labrum | D. Fovea capitis | B | The sclerotic superolateral weight-bearing roof of the acetabulum is the sourcil, the surface you assess for coverage and stress change. The acetabular labrum is the triangular fibrocartilage at the rim peripheral to the bony roof — a soft-tissue structure, not the bony subchondral plate.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The small triangular low-signal structure capping the superolateral acetabular rim is the:
text: The small triangular low-signal structure capping the superolateral acetabular rim is the: | A. Ligamentum teres | B. Transverse acetabular ligament | C. Acetabular labrum | D. Zona orbicularis | C | The triangular fibrocartilage seating on the bony rim and deepening the socket is the acetabular labrum, the anterosuperior tear site worked up with MR arthrography. The transverse acetabular ligament bridges the inferior acetabular notch, not the superolateral rim seen on this slice; the zona orbicularis is the circular capsular waist around the neck, not a rim structure.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The thin intermediate-signal layer marked between the femoral head cortex and the acetabular cortex represents the:
text: The thin intermediate-signal layer marked between the femoral head cortex and the acetabular cortex represents the: | A. Joint effusion | B. Hyaline articular cartilage | C. Acetabular labrum | D. Subchondral marrow | B | The smooth intermediate-signal stripe covering the bony surfaces is hyaline articular cartilage, scrutinized for focal chondral loss in FAI. A joint effusion would be bright fluid distending the capsule and recesses rather than a thin layer conforming to the subchondral cortex.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The bony segment connecting the femoral head to the trochanters, marked here, is the:
text: The bony segment connecting the femoral head to the trochanters, marked here, is the: | A. Femoral neck | B. Intertrochanteric line | C. Acetabular column | D. Femoral shaft | A | The segment between the head and the trochanteric region is the femoral neck, where you must clear the superolateral tension-side cortex for stress fracture. The intertrochanteric line lies distal to the neck between the greater and lesser trochanters, not the head-neck connecting segment marked here.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The tendons marked inserting onto the greater trochanter — the abductor 'rotator cuff of the hip' — are the:
text: The tendons marked inserting onto the greater trochanter — the abductor 'rotator cuff of the hip' — are the: | A. Iliopsoas and rectus femoris | B. Gluteus medius and minimus | C. Piriformis and obturator internus | D. Tensor fasciae latae and sartorius | B | The gluteus medius and minimus insert on the greater trochanter and are the hip abductor 'rotator cuff' implicated in GTPS. The iliopsoas inserts on the lesser trochanter (and rectus femoris arises anteriorly at the AIIS), so neither forms the greater-trochanteric abductor footprint marked here.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The midline fibrocartilaginous joint marked inferiorly between the two pubic bones is the:
text: The midline fibrocartilaginous joint marked inferiorly between the two pubic bones is the: | A. Sacroiliac joint | B. Pubic symphysis | C. Hip joint | D. Iliopubic eminence | B | The midline fibrocartilaginous articulation between the pubic bones is the pubic symphysis, the focus of the athletic pubalgia workup with the adductor aponeurosis. The sacroiliac joint lies posterosuperiorly between the sacrum and ilium — a separate joint, not in this anterior midline location.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The paired articulations marked between the sacrum and the iliac bones on this posterior coronal slice are the:
text: The paired articulations marked between the sacrum and the iliac bones on this posterior coronal slice are the: | A. Hip (femoroacetabular) joints | B. Sacroiliac joints | C. Pubic symphysis | D. Lumbosacral facet joints | B | The articulations between the sacrum and the ilia are the sacroiliac joints, screened here for sacroiliitis and sacral insufficiency fracture. The femoroacetabular (hip) joints lie laterally between the femoral heads and acetabula — separate, more anterolateral joints not formed by the sacrum.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this coronal T2 FS Dixon image, the marked lateral bony prominence of the proximal femur — the footprint of the gluteus medius and minimus tendons — is which structure?
text: On this coronal T2 FS Dixon image, the marked lateral bony prominence of the proximal femur — the footprint of the gluteus medius and minimus tendons — is which structure? | A. Greater trochanter | B. Lesser trochanter | C. Femoral head | D. Iliac crest | A | The greater trochanter is the lateral proximal-femoral prominence and the abductor (gluteus medius/minimus) footprint, with the trochanteric/subgluteus bursae normally collapsed superficial to the tendons — the GTPS hot spot. The lesser trochanter is also a proximal-femoral apophysis but lies posteromedially as the iliopsoas insertion, not the lateral abductor footprint.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked spherical structure seated in the acetabular socket?
text: What is the marked spherical structure seated in the acetabular socket? | A. Greater trochanter | B. Femoral head | C. Femoral neck | D. Ischial tuberosity | B | The round ball articulating within the acetabulum is the femoral head. The femoral neck is the narrower column connecting the head to the trochanters and is not spherical, so it is the best distractor but wrong here.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked low-signal triangular structure at the rim of the acetabulum?
text: What is the marked low-signal triangular structure at the rim of the acetabulum? | A. Acetabular labrum | B. Ligamentum teres | C. Transverse acetabular ligament | D. Iliofemoral ligament | A | The triangular low-signal fibrocartilage capping the bony rim is the acetabular labrum. The ligamentum teres runs centrally from the fovea to the acetabular notch, not at the peripheral rim, so it is the closest distractor but incorrect.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this axial image, the marked intact bony column behind the femoral head is the:
text: On this axial image, the marked intact bony column behind the femoral head is the: | A. Anterior wall/column of the acetabulum | B. Posterior wall/column of the acetabulum | C. Quadrilateral plate | D. Teardrop | B | Behind (posterior to) the femoral head the marked bone is the posterior wall/column of the acetabulum. The anterior wall/column lies in front of the head; the quadrilateral plate is the medial wall and the teardrop is a medial-floor radiographic structure, so all three are plausible but wrong for a posterior marker.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The marked round low-signal tendon lying directly anterior to the femoral head is the:
text: The marked round low-signal tendon lying directly anterior to the femoral head is the: | A. Rectus femoris tendon | B. Sartorius tendon | C. Iliopsoas tendon | D. Gluteus minimus tendon | C | The tendon immediately anterior to the hip joint/femoral head is the iliopsoas tendon. The rectus femoris (direct head at the AIIS, indirect head along the acetabular rim) lies more anterolateral and superficial, so it is the best distractor but not in this position.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The marked muscles converging on the greater trochanter — the hip's abductor 'rotator cuff' — are the:
text: The marked muscles converging on the greater trochanter — the hip's abductor 'rotator cuff' — are the: | A. Gluteus medius & minimus | B. Piriformis & obturator internus | C. Tensor fasciae latae & sartorius | D. Quadratus femoris & obturator externus | A | Gluteus medius and minimus are the abductors that insert on the greater trochanter and form the hip's 'rotator cuff.' Piriformis (superomedial trochanter) and obturator internus (medial trochanter) attach in the same region and are short external rotators, making them the strongest distractor, but they are not the abductors.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The marked fascicular structure posterior to the ischium, between the ischial tuberosity and greater trochanter, is the:
text: The marked fascicular structure posterior to the ischium, between the ischial tuberosity and greater trochanter, is the: | A. Femoral nerve | B. Obturator nerve | C. Pudendal nerve | D. Sciatic nerve | D | The large fascicular nerve coursing posterior to the hip between the ischial tuberosity and greater trochanter is the sciatic nerve. The femoral nerve lies anteriorly within the iliopsoas groove lateral to the femoral artery, so it is a plausible but anatomically opposite distractor.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked spherical epiphyseal structure seated in the acetabulum?
text: What is the marked spherical epiphyseal structure seated in the acetabulum? | A. Greater trochanter | B. Acetabular dome (sourcil) | C. Femoral head | D. Ischial tuberosity | C | The large, round, cartilage-capped epiphysis articulating with the acetabular cup is the femoral head; its sphericity and marrow are the key normal checks. The acetabular dome (sourcil) is the curved acetabular roof ABOVE the head, not the rounded ball itself, so it is the closest miss.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked structure connecting the femoral head to the shaft, seen here in profile?
text: What is the marked structure connecting the femoral head to the shaft, seen here in profile? | A. Femoral neck & head-neck junction | B. Acetabular labrum | C. Iliopsoas tendon | D. Anterior inferior iliac spine | A | The waisted column of bone linking the round head to the trochanteric region is the femoral neck and its head-neck junction, where you judge anterior offset (cam) and tension-side stress. The acetabular labrum is the small triangular fibrocartilage at the socket rim, not the tubular bony bridge, making it the tempting distractor.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked low-signal triangle along the acetabular rim?
text: What is the marked low-signal triangle along the acetabular rim? | A. Ligamentum teres | B. Transverse acetabular ligament | C. Acetabular labrum | D. Zona orbicularis | C | The triangular low-signal fibrocartilage seated on the bony acetabular rim is the labrum, with the anterosuperior segment the classic tear site read on MR arthrography. The ligamentum teres runs from the fovea to the acetabular notch INSIDE the joint, not along the outer rim, so it is the best distractor.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked rounded low-signal tendon coursing anterior to the hip joint toward the lesser trochanter?
text: What is the marked rounded low-signal tendon coursing anterior to the hip joint toward the lesser trochanter? | A. Iliopsoas tendon | B. Rectus femoris tendon | C. Sartorius | D. Gluteus minimus tendon | A | The rounded tendon gliding over the anterior capsule and femoral head toward the lesser trochanter is the iliopsoas — the internal-snapping and anterior bursa structure. The rectus femoris (direct head off the AIIS, indirect head off the acetabular rim) lies more superolateral on the anterior pelvis, so it is the closest anatomic mimic.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked tendon group inserting on the greater trochanter (the 'rotator cuff of the hip')?
text: What is the marked tendon group inserting on the greater trochanter (the 'rotator cuff of the hip')? | A. Piriformis tendon | B. Iliopsoas tendon | C. Gluteus medius & minimus (abductors) | D. Quadratus femoris | C | The tendons converging on the greater trochanter are the gluteus medius and minimus — the hip abductors, analogous to the shoulder rotator cuff and the source of GTPS. The piriformis also reaches the trochanter but inserts on its superomedial tip and is an external rotator, not the lateral-facet abductor footprint, so it is the strongest distractor.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the marked thin fluid-containing space along the anterior femoral neck beneath the capsule?
text: What is the marked thin fluid-containing space along the anterior femoral neck beneath the capsule? | A. Anterior joint recess | B. Iliopsoas bursa | C. Zona orbicularis | D. Rectus femoris origin | A | The thin capsular space hugging the anterior head-neck deep to the iliofemoral ligament is the anterior joint recess, the first site effusion collects and the access point for aspiration. The iliopsoas bursa lies superficial to the capsule around the iliopsoas tendon and can communicate with the joint, but it is extracapsular, making it the most plausible miss.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this sagittal PD FS image, the marked anterior fibrous layer draped over the femoral head-neck and reinforced by the Y ligament of Bigelow is which structure?
text: On this sagittal PD FS image, the marked anterior fibrous layer draped over the femoral head-neck and reinforced by the Y ligament of Bigelow is which structure? | A. Acetabular labrum | B. Joint capsule with iliofemoral ligament | C. Ligamentum teres | D. Iliopsoas tendon | B | The anterior joint capsule reinforced by the iliofemoral ligament forms the roof of the anterior synovial recess, and its distension by fluid indicates effusion/synovitis — also the target for image-guided aspiration/injection. The acetabular labrum is the triangular fibrocartilage rim at the acetabular margin (tears are anterosuperior, best shown on MR arthrography), not the fibrous capsular layer draped over the femoral neck.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this coronal T2 FS image, what is the marked rounded lateral condyle of the distal humerus?
text: On this coronal T2 FS image, what is the marked rounded lateral condyle of the distal humerus? | A. Trochlea | B. Capitellum | C. Radial head | D. Olecranon | B | The rounded LATERAL condyle articulating with the radial head is the capitellum — the site of osteochondritis dissecans in the young thrower/gymnast. The trochlea is the spool-shaped MEDIAL articular surface that articulates with the ulna.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The thin dark band running from the medial epicondyle toward the sublime tubercle is the:
text: The thin dark band running from the medial epicondyle toward the sublime tubercle is the: | A. Lateral ulnar collateral ligament | B. Anterior bundle of the UCL | C. Annular ligament | D. Common extensor tendon | B | The anterior bundle of the UCL is the primary valgus restraint, inserting on the sublime tubercle — where the thrower's undersurface (T-sign) tear classically occurs. The LUCL is the lateral restraint to posterolateral rotatory instability.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tendinosis of the marked origin off the lateral epicondyle is the lesion in lateral epicondylitis. Which adjacent ligament must you always trace here?
text: Tendinosis of the marked origin off the lateral epicondyle is the lesion in lateral epicondylitis. Which adjacent ligament must you always trace here? | A. The anterior bundle of the UCL | B. The LUCL to the supinator crest | C. The annular ligament around the radial neck | D. The transverse (Cooper) ligament | B | The LUCL/RCL origin lies deep to the common extensor tendon, so a deep extensor tear can take the LUCL and produce posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI) — trace the LUCL to the supinator crest before signing off any high-grade lateral epicondylitis.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The marked nerve sits posterior to the medial epicondyle. Mild T2 brightness here can be normal — what anchors a diagnosis of cubital tunnel syndrome?
text: The marked nerve sits posterior to the medial epicondyle. Mild T2 brightness here can be normal — what anchors a diagnosis of cubital tunnel syndrome? | A. T2 signal alone | B. Caliber change + muscle denervation + clinical correlation | C. Any anterior position of the nerve | D. The presence of a normal Osborne retinaculum | B | The ulnar nerve in the cubital tunnel can show mild T2 brightness normally, so anchor the call to nerve enlargement PLUS FCU/ulnar-intrinsic denervation PLUS clinical correlation — and look for a structural cause (subluxation, accessory muscle) that can change operative planning.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why does the common flexor-pronator origin (medial epicondylitis) travel with ulnar neuritis?
text: Why does the common flexor-pronator origin (medial epicondylitis) travel with ulnar neuritis? | A. They share a blood supply | B. The cubital tunnel and ulnar nerve sit immediately posterior to the flexor-pronator origin | C. The flexor origin is innervated by the ulnar nerve | D. Both are restraints to valgus stress | B | The cubital tunnel sits just posterior to the common flexor-pronator origin, so medial epicondylitis and ulnar neuritis coexist — when you call medial epicondylitis, always interrogate the ulnar nerve and the UCL (the medial triad).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After elbow trauma with a normal radiograph, which fat-pad finding signals an occult fracture until proven otherwise?
text: After elbow trauma with a normal radiograph, which fat-pad finding signals an occult fracture until proven otherwise? | A. A visible anterior fat pad | B. A displaced/elevated POSTERIOR fat pad | C. Absence of the anterior fat pad | D. A small joint effusion alone | B | The posterior fat pad is normally hidden in the olecranon fossa; a visible/displaced posterior fat pad after trauma means a hemarthrosis from an occult intra-articular fracture (most often the radial head) until proven otherwise. The anterior fat pad is normally seen.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: The marked anterior beak of the proximal ulna is the keystone of post-traumatic stability. A small fracture of which part implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability?
text: The marked anterior beak of the proximal ulna is the keystone of post-traumatic stability. A small fracture of which part implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability? | A. The olecranon tip | B. The anteromedial coronoid facet | C. The radial styloid | D. The sublime tubercle only | B | An anteromedial coronoid facet fracture may look small on radiographs but can imply varus posteromedial rotatory instability (VPMRI) and needs instability-focused surgical review — 'small does not mean stable' at the coronoid. The sublime tubercle (medial) is the UCL's distal attachment.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A thin posterolateral radiocapitellar synovial fold on this sagittal elbow MRI is best interpreted how?
text: A thin posterolateral radiocapitellar synovial fold on this sagittal elbow MRI is best interpreted how? | A. A normal plica/fold unless thickened, edematous, or associated with snapping/catching | B. A displaced loose body until proven otherwise | C. A capitellar OCD instability sign by itself | D. A UCL T-sign seen on the wrong plane | A | A posterolateral radiocapitellar plica/synovial fold can be a normal thin fold in the radiocapitellar recess. Do not call it a loose body or OCD instability sign by itself; worry when it is thickened/edematous or matches mechanical snapping/catching.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 19-year-old soccer player sustains a non-contact pivoting injury. Radiographs show a small vertically oriented cortical avulsion off the proximal lateral tibia just below the joint line. Which intra-articular injury is most strongly associated with this radiographic finding?
text: A 19-year-old soccer player sustains a non-contact pivoting injury. Radiographs show a small vertically oriented cortical avulsion off the proximal lateral tibia just below the joint line. Which intra-articular injury is most strongly associated with this radiographic finding? | A. Anterior cruciate ligament tear | B. Posterior cruciate ligament tear | C. Quadriceps tendon rupture | D. Lateral meniscus discoid variant | A | A vertical cortical avulsion of the lateral tibia just below the joint line is a Segond fracture, an avulsion at the anterolateral capsular/iliotibial attachment that is highly associated (>75-90%) with ACL rupture. A PCL tear is instead linked with a posterior (reverse Segond) medial tibial avulsion, not this lateral fragment, so it is the weaker association.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 28-year-old restrained front-seat passenger strikes the dashboard with a flexed knee in a head-on collision. He has a posterior sag of the tibia on exam and a posterior drawer is positive. Which finding on MRI would best confirm the suspected primary ligament injury?
text: A 28-year-old restrained front-seat passenger strikes the dashboard with a flexed knee in a head-on collision. He has a posterior sag of the tibia on exam and a posterior drawer is positive. Which finding on MRI would best confirm the suspected primary ligament injury? | A. Discontinuity and increased signal within the thick curved band in the posterior intercondylar notch | B. A straight striated band paralleling Blumensaat's line that is wavy and lax | C. Fluid signal at the posteromedial meniscocapsular junction | D. A cortical avulsion at the anterolateral tibia | A | The dashboard mechanism drives the proximal tibia posteriorly and tears the PCL, the thick curved low-signal band in the posterior notch; disruption or increased intrasubstance signal there confirms it. A wavy band along Blumensaat's line describes the ACL, and the anterolateral tibial avulsion is a Segond fracture tied to ACL rather than PCL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 23-year-old with a locked knee that will not fully extend has a sagittal MRI showing a low-signal band lying anteriorly within the intercondylar notch, paralleling and just inferior to the PCL. Body-slice images show fewer than two consecutive meniscal bow-ties. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: A 23-year-old with a locked knee that will not fully extend has a sagittal MRI showing a low-signal band lying anteriorly within the intercondylar notch, paralleling and just inferior to the PCL. Body-slice images show fewer than two consecutive meniscal bow-ties. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Bucket-handle meniscal tear with a displaced fragment | B. Mucoid degeneration of the ACL | C. Normal meniscofemoral ligament of Humphry | D. Cyclops lesion after ACL reconstruction | A | A displaced inner meniscal fragment lying in the notch inferior and parallel to the PCL produces the double-PCL sign, and an absent bow-tie (fewer than two consecutive) confirms a bucket-handle tear that mechanically blocks extension. The meniscofemoral ligament of Humphry runs anterior to the PCL but is a thin discrete band that does not abolish the bow-tie count or lock the knee.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 56-year-old reports a pop while rising from a squat and now has medial joint-line pain. Coronal MRI shows the medial meniscus body displaced 5 mm beyond the tibial margin, and the posterior horn near the root appears truncated. Which feature most reliably supports a posterior medial meniscal root tear?
text: A 56-year-old reports a pop while rising from a squat and now has medial joint-line pain. Coronal MRI shows the medial meniscus body displaced 5 mm beyond the tibial margin, and the posterior horn near the root appears truncated. Which feature most reliably supports a posterior medial meniscal root tear? | A. Coronal extrusion of 5 mm with a truncated (ghost) posterior root | B. Intrasubstance grade 2 signal not reaching a surface | C. A double-PCL sign in the notch | D. A parameniscal cyst at the anterior horn | A | Medial coronal extrusion of at least 3 mm together with an absent or truncated 'ghost' root at the posterior attachment is the signature of a posterior root tear, which functionally behaves like a total meniscectomy. Intrasubstance grade 2 signal that does not reach a surface is degeneration, not a full-thickness root detachment, so it does not establish the diagnosis.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 15-year-old gymnast has an osteochondritis dissecans lesion at the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. On fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed images, which finding would most specifically indicate that the fragment is unstable and may warrant surgical fixation?
text: A 15-year-old gymnast has an osteochondritis dissecans lesion at the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. On fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed images, which finding would most specifically indicate that the fragment is unstable and may warrant surgical fixation? | A. A fluid-signal-intensity line completely encircling and undercutting the fragment | B. Mild marrow edema in the parent bone deep to the lesion | C. A granulation interface line only as bright as articular cartilage | D. An en-face fragment diameter of 6 mm with intact overlying cartilage | A | A rim of fluid-intensity signal that completely surrounds and undercuts the fragment indicates synovial fluid dissecting the interface and is the most reliable De Smet sign of instability. A high-signal interface line only as bright as cartilage usually reflects vascular granulation tissue, especially in skeletally immature patients, and is not by itself a sign of instability.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 65-year-old woman has sudden atraumatic medial knee pain. MRI shows a subchondral low-signal line in the weight-bearing medial femoral condyle paralleling and concave toward the articular surface, with surrounding diffuse marrow edema. What is the best diagnosis?
text: A 65-year-old woman has sudden atraumatic medial knee pain. MRI shows a subchondral low-signal line in the weight-bearing medial femoral condyle paralleling and concave toward the articular surface, with surrounding diffuse marrow edema. What is the best diagnosis? | A. Subchondral insufficiency fracture | B. Primary spontaneous osteonecrosis with epiphyseal infarct | C. Transient bone marrow edema syndrome | D. Medial femoral condyle osteochondroma | A | A subchondral fracture line paralleling the articular surface with adjacent marrow edema in an older patient with atraumatic pain is a subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIFK). Primary osteonecrosis instead shows a serpentine double-line sign demarcating an infarct away from the subchondral plate, so the subchondral, surface-parallel line favors SIFK over a true epiphyseal infarct.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 16-year-old sustains a first-time lateral patellar dislocation that has spontaneously reduced. MRI shows an inferomedial patellar facet and anterolateral femoral condyle bone bruise and an MPFL tear, with no displaced osteochondral fragment and a TT-TG distance of 12 mm. What is the most appropriate initial management?
text: A 16-year-old sustains a first-time lateral patellar dislocation that has spontaneously reduced. MRI shows an inferomedial patellar facet and anterolateral femoral condyle bone bruise and an MPFL tear, with no displaced osteochondral fragment and a TT-TG distance of 12 mm. What is the most appropriate initial management? | A. Nonoperative rehabilitation with bracing and quadriceps strengthening | B. Acute MPFL reconstruction with tibial tubercle osteotomy | C. Lateral retinacular release alone | D. Immediate patellectomy | A | A first-time dislocation without a loose osteochondral body and with a normal TT-TG (well under the ~20 mm threshold) is managed nonoperatively with bracing and rehabilitation. Tibial tubercle osteotomy is reserved for an elevated TT-TG, so adding it here would be over-treatment given the normal alignment measurement.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 27-year-old has an MRI-confirmed posterolateral corner injury with fibular collateral ligament, popliteus, and popliteofibular ligament disruption after a varus hyperextension blow. The surgeon plans cruciate reconstruction. Why is recognizing and addressing this posterolateral corner injury before isolated cruciate surgery most important?
text: A 27-year-old has an MRI-confirmed posterolateral corner injury with fibular collateral ligament, popliteus, and popliteofibular ligament disruption after a varus hyperextension blow. The surgeon plans cruciate reconstruction. Why is recognizing and addressing this posterolateral corner injury before isolated cruciate surgery most important? | A. Unrecognized posterolateral corner injury is a leading cause of cruciate graft failure from residual rotational and varus instability | B. Posterolateral corner injuries reliably heal nonoperatively, so cruciate surgery can simply be delayed | C. The posterolateral corner has no effect on cruciate graft loading once the cruciate is reconstructed | D. Posterolateral corner injuries occur in isolation, so they rarely change the surgical plan | A | The posterolateral corner is the primary restraint to varus and external-rotation, and a missed combined injury leaves residual rotational/varus laxity that overloads and stretches out a cruciate graft, a recognized cause of reconstruction failure, so it must be addressed concurrently. The distractors are wrong because high-grade posterolateral corner injuries usually require repair or reconstruction rather than healing alone, and they very commonly accompany (rather than spare) cruciate injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 24-year-old presents after a pivoting injury. Sagittal MRI shows the ACL is poorly visualized. To use the posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) line sign as a secondary indicator of ACL tear, you draw a line along the straight distal segment of the PCL and extend it proximally and anteriorly. In an intact knee, where should this line intersect?
text: A 24-year-old presents after a pivoting injury. Sagittal MRI shows the ACL is poorly visualized. To use the posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) line sign as a secondary indicator of ACL tear, you draw a line along the straight distal segment of the PCL and extend it proximally and anteriorly. In an intact knee, where should this line intersect? | A. It should intersect the femur, crossing the distal femoral metaphysis/medullary canal | B. It should intersect the tibial plateau just anterior to the tibial tubercle | C. It should fail to intersect the femur and instead pass anterior to it | D. It should intersect the patella at its lower pole | A | Normally the extended PCL line intersects the femoral medullary cavity; with a torn ACL the PCL becomes more buckled/redundant (more sharply curved) from anterior tibial translation, so the line falls short and fails to intersect the femur (a positive sign). It is a secondary sign best combined with primary findings.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On MRI of a knee with an acute ACL tear, you measure anterior tibial translation by referencing the posterior margins of the lateral femoral condyle and lateral tibial plateau on a sagittal image. Which threshold of anterior tibial subluxation is most specific for a complete ACL tear?
text: On MRI of a knee with an acute ACL tear, you measure anterior tibial translation by referencing the posterior margins of the lateral femoral condyle and lateral tibial plateau on a sagittal image. Which threshold of anterior tibial subluxation is most specific for a complete ACL tear? | A. ≥ 1 mm | B. ≥ 3 mm | C. ≥ 5 mm | D. ≥ 10 mm | C | Anterior translation of the lateral tibial plateau of ≥ 5 mm relative to the lateral femoral condyle is highly specific (though not very sensitive) for a complete ACL tear; smaller degrees overlap with normal laxity.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 52-year-old with chronic, atraumatic knee pain has an ACL that is diffusely thickened and ill-defined with intermediate-to-high T2 signal, yet the individual fibers remain continuous and parallel, producing a 'celery-stalk' appearance. There is often an associated finding in the adjacent bone. Which is the single best diagnosis and its characteristic associated feature?
text: A 52-year-old with chronic, atraumatic knee pain has an ACL that is diffusely thickened and ill-defined with intermediate-to-high T2 signal, yet the individual fibers remain continuous and parallel, producing a 'celery-stalk' appearance. There is often an associated finding in the adjacent bone. Which is the single best diagnosis and its characteristic associated feature? | A. Acute complete ACL tear with a Segond fracture of the lateral tibia | B. ACL mucoid degeneration, frequently associated with subchondral/intraosseous ganglion cysts at the tibial or femoral attachment | C. ACL graft impingement with a cyclops lesion in Hoffa's fat pad | D. ACL ganglion arising from the PCL with a deep lateral femoral notch sign | B | Mucoid degeneration shows a thickened ACL with continuous, intact fibers ('celery stalk' / increased signal but preserved striations) and is commonly accompanied by intraosseous ganglion cysts at the ligament insertions — distinguishing it from a tear, where fibers are discontinuous.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Reviewing the radiograph and MRI of an acute knee injury, you identify a deep lateral femoral notch (terminal/condylopatellar sulcus). Beyond simply suggesting an ACL tear, an abnormally deep notch is considered a useful marker. Which depth threshold is the accepted cutoff that strongly suggests an associated ACL injury?
text: Reviewing the radiograph and MRI of an acute knee injury, you identify a deep lateral femoral notch (terminal/condylopatellar sulcus). Beyond simply suggesting an ACL tear, an abnormally deep notch is considered a useful marker. Which depth threshold is the accepted cutoff that strongly suggests an associated ACL injury? | A. Greater than 0.5 mm | B. Greater than 1.5 mm | C. Greater than 4 mm | D. Greater than 8 mm | B | A lateral femoral (condylopatellar) sulcus depth greater than 1.5 mm is the classic threshold strongly associated with ACL disruption, reflecting an osteochondral impaction injury as the anteriorly subluxed tibia engages the lateral femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MR images with 4-5 mm slice thickness, which finding most reliably distinguishes a discoid lateral meniscus from a normal lateral meniscus?
text: On sagittal MR images with 4-5 mm slice thickness, which finding most reliably distinguishes a discoid lateral meniscus from a normal lateral meniscus? | A. A minimum coronal body width of 6-8 mm on the central coronal image | B. The 'bow-tie' (continuous body) appearance on 3 or more contiguous sagittal slices | C. Absence of the normal meniscofemoral ligament on the Wrisberg side | D. Increased intrameniscal grade 2 signal in the posterior horn | B | A normal meniscus shows the contiguous 'bow-tie' body on at most 2 consecutive 4-5 mm sagittal slices; 3 or more contiguous bow-tie segments (or a central coronal width >13-15 mm) indicates a discoid meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 58-year-old has acute medial knee pain. Coronal MRI shows the posterior root signal disrupted with the meniscal body displaced 4 mm beyond the tibial margin. Regarding the 3 mm extrusion threshold for a medial meniscal posterior root tear, which statement is most accurate?
text: A 58-year-old has acute medial knee pain. Coronal MRI shows the posterior root signal disrupted with the meniscal body displaced 4 mm beyond the tibial margin. Regarding the 3 mm extrusion threshold for a medial meniscal posterior root tear, which statement is most accurate? | A. The 3 mm threshold is a useful high-specificity clue but relatively insensitive, so extrusion <3 mm does not exclude a root tear | B. The 3 mm threshold is both highly sensitive and highly specific for root tears | C. Extrusion is best and most reproducibly measured on sagittal rather than coronal images | D. Meniscal extrusion of this degree is a normal finding and requires no root evaluation | A | Coronal medial meniscal extrusion >=3 mm is a significant clue in many root-tear studies, but it is only modestly sensitive and should not be treated as diagnostic by itself. Sub-3 mm extrusion does not exclude a root tear; the 'ghost' (absent/blunted root) sign should also be sought.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: During ACL reconstruction planning, you are asked about a suspected ramp lesion. Which statement is most accurate regarding its MRI and arthroscopic evaluation?
text: During ACL reconstruction planning, you are asked about a suspected ramp lesion. Which statement is most accurate regarding its MRI and arthroscopic evaluation? | A. It is a radial tear of the posterior horn lateral meniscus best seen on coronal images | B. Standard anterior-portal arthroscopy reliably visualizes it, so MRI adds little | C. Fluid signal between the posterior horn medial meniscus and the joint capsule on fluid-sensitive sequences is a key sign, and the lesion is frequently missed without posteromedial probing | D. It is defined as extrusion of the medial meniscus body greater than 5 mm | C | A ramp lesion is a tear at the posteromedial meniscocapsular junction; high T2 fluid signal/edema posterior to the posterior horn raises suspicion, and a substantial proportion are missed on standard anterior-portal viewing without a posteromedial (trans-notch) probing approach.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI of a knee with an ACL tear, you see vertical signal at the junction of the posterior horn lateral meniscus and an obliquely coursing structure passing toward the PCL. Which feature best favors a true posterior horn tear over a meniscofemoral (Wrisberg) ligament pseudotear?
text: On sagittal MRI of a knee with an ACL tear, you see vertical signal at the junction of the posterior horn lateral meniscus and an obliquely coursing structure passing toward the PCL. Which feature best favors a true posterior horn tear over a meniscofemoral (Wrisberg) ligament pseudotear? | A. The vertical signal can be traced contiguously into the PCL through the intercondylar notch | B. The apparent meniscal cleft persists 4 or more sagittal images lateral to the PCL | C. The structure lies posterior to the PCL on the Wrisberg side | D. The signal is seen on only a single sagittal image near the midline | B | A Wrisberg/Humphrey pseudotear is created where the meniscofemoral ligament inserts on the posterior horn; an apparent cleft extending >=4 sagittal images lateral to the PCL exceeds the ligament's reach and should be treated as a true tear, especially with an ACL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 16-year-old soccer player has a juvenile osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) lesion at the classic lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. On fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed sequences, which finding is MOST specific for an UNSTABLE fragment?
text: A 16-year-old soccer player has a juvenile osteochondritis dissecans (OCD) lesion at the classic lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle. On fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed sequences, which finding is MOST specific for an UNSTABLE fragment? | A. A thin, uninterrupted line of high T2 signal isointense to articular cartilage along the fragment interface | B. A high-T2-signal rim completely surrounding the fragment that follows fluid signal intensity and undercuts it | C. Mild bone-marrow edema in the parent bone deep to the lesion | D. An en-face fragment diameter measuring 6 mm with intact overlying cartilage | B | A fluid-signal-intensity rim that completely encircles and undercuts the fragment indicates synovial fluid dissecting the interface and is the most reliable De Smet sign of instability; a granulation-tissue interface line is only as bright as cartilage, not fluid. Secondary signs include subchondral cysts >5 mm and a focal cartilage defect.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When applying the De Smet MRI criteria for OCD fragment instability, the SPECIFICITY of a T2-bright line at the fragment-parent interface improves substantially when which additional qualifier is present?
text: When applying the De Smet MRI criteria for OCD fragment instability, the SPECIFICITY of a T2-bright line at the fragment-parent interface improves substantially when which additional qualifier is present? | A. The patient is skeletally immature with open physes | B. The high-signal line is matched by a breach (defect) in the overlying articular cartilage on fluid-sensitive images | C. The lesion is located in the patellofemoral compartment | D. The fragment measures less than 5 mm in greatest dimension | B | In skeletally immature patients a T2-bright interface line frequently reflects vascular granulation tissue rather than fluid, lowering specificity; instability is far more reliable when that line is accompanied by a discontinuity in the overlying cartilage, confirming communication with the joint.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A surgeon asks you to distinguish chondral DELAMINATION from a simple partial-thickness fissure on knee MRI. Which finding is MOST characteristic of delamination?
text: A surgeon asks you to distinguish chondral DELAMINATION from a simple partial-thickness fissure on knee MRI. Which finding is MOST characteristic of delamination? | A. A linear high-signal cleft extending perpendicularly from the articular surface into but not through the cartilage | B. A fluid-signal line at the cartilage-bone (osteochondral) junction with the overlying cartilage surface still intact | C. Focal full-thickness cartilage loss exposing subchondral bone (ICRS grade 4) | D. Diffuse intrachondral T2 hyperintensity without a discrete defect | B | Delamination is a shear separation of cartilage from subchondral bone at the deep tidemark/calcified-cartilage zone, seen as a fluid-signal line paralleling the bone with a deceptively intact articular surface; it can be missed at arthroscopic probing of the surface.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A sports-medicine fellow measures a tibial tubercle–trochlear groove (TT-TG) distance of 22 mm on an axial MRI in a patient with recurrent lateral patellar instability. Above which commonly cited threshold does the TT-TG distance become a recognized risk factor for patellar instability and an indication potentially favoring tibial tubercle medialization?
text: A sports-medicine fellow measures a tibial tubercle–trochlear groove (TT-TG) distance of 22 mm on an axial MRI in a patient with recurrent lateral patellar instability. Above which commonly cited threshold does the TT-TG distance become a recognized risk factor for patellar instability and an indication potentially favoring tibial tubercle medialization? | A. 8 mm | B. 12 mm | C. 20 mm | D. 30 mm | C | A TT-TG distance >=20 mm is the classic abnormal threshold associated with patellar instability, but it is a risk marker rather than a standalone surgical rule. MRI values measured on cartilaginous landmarks tend to run a few millimeters lower than CT-based bony measurements, so landmarks and clinical context matter.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Which of the following is the most reliable and reproducible radiographic/MRI index for diagnosing patella alta because it is independent of patellar tendon variability and uses the articular cartilage surface of the patella relative to the tibial plateau?
text: Which of the following is the most reliable and reproducible radiographic/MRI index for diagnosing patella alta because it is independent of patellar tendon variability and uses the articular cartilage surface of the patella relative to the tibial plateau? | A. Insall-Salvati ratio | B. Caton-Deschamps index | C. Blackburne-Peel index measured to the patellar tendon insertion | D. Modified Insall-Salvati ratio measured to the inferior pole of the patella | B | The Caton-Deschamps index (distance from the inferior articular margin of the patella to the anterosuperior tibial plateau divided by patellar articular surface length; alta if >1.2) is favored because it avoids errors from a variable or thickened patellar tendon that confound the Insall-Salvati ratio.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a true lateral radiograph or sagittal MRI of a knee with trochlear dysplasia, the 'crossing sign' refers to which specific finding indicating a flat or shallow trochlea?
text: On a true lateral radiograph or sagittal MRI of a knee with trochlear dysplasia, the 'crossing sign' refers to which specific finding indicating a flat or shallow trochlea? | A. The line of the trochlear floor crosses the anterior cortex of the lateral femoral condyle | B. The patellar tendon crosses the physeal scar at the level of the tibial tubercle | C. The medial and lateral trochlear facet lines cross at the superior pole of the patella | D. The supratrochlear spur crosses the Blumensaat line | A | The crossing sign is present when the line representing the floor of the trochlear groove crosses the anterior border of the lateral femoral condyle, meaning the groove becomes flat (flush with the condyle) at that point—a hallmark of trochlear dysplasia. A supratrochlear spur and double contour are additional Dejour signs of higher-grade dysplasia.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 16-year-old presents after a transient lateral patellar dislocation. Beyond the classic medial patellar and lateral femoral condyle bone bruise pattern and MPFL injury, which additional finding is a well-described NORMAL VARIANT of the patella that should NOT be mistaken for an osteochondral injury or osteomyelitis?
text: A 16-year-old presents after a transient lateral patellar dislocation. Beyond the classic medial patellar and lateral femoral condyle bone bruise pattern and MPFL injury, which additional finding is a well-described NORMAL VARIANT of the patella that should NOT be mistaken for an osteochondral injury or osteomyelitis? | A. Dorsal defect of the patella in the superolateral quadrant | B. Bipartite patella fragment in the inferomedial pole | C. Osteochondral fracture of the median ridge | D. Avulsion at the adductor tubercle MPFL origin | A | The dorsal defect of the patella is a benign subchondral lucency in the superolateral quadrant of the patella with intact overlying cartilage on MRI; recognizing the intact cartilage and typical location prevents misdiagnosis as an osteochondral lesion or infection. In lateral patellar dislocation the MPFL can tear at its patellar attachment, its femoral (adductor tubercle) attachment, or both — the tear site is variable.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a coronal MRI of an acutely injured knee, you identify a curvilinear bone fragment avulsed from the fibula and displaced superomedially, accompanied by edema in the popliteus and fibular collateral ligament. Which anatomic structure is most specifically responsible for this 'arcuate sign' avulsion fragment?
text: On a coronal MRI of an acutely injured knee, you identify a curvilinear bone fragment avulsed from the fibula and displaced superomedially, accompanied by edema in the popliteus and fibular collateral ligament. Which anatomic structure is most specifically responsible for this 'arcuate sign' avulsion fragment? | A. The arcuate ligament / conjoined insertion of the posterolateral corner at the fibular styloid | B. The proximal tibiofibular ligament at the fibular head | C. The fibular insertion of the lateral head of gastrocnemius | D. The fabellofibular ligament alone at the fibular apex | A | The arcuate sign is a small avulsion at the fibular styloid, the conjoined attachment site of the posterolateral corner (arcuate ligament complex), which is why it signals a high rate of associated cruciate (especially PCL) injury and should never be dismissed as trivial.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A fellow shows you a coronal proton-density image with ossification along the expected course of the proximal MCL, paralleling the medial femoral condyle, in a patient with a remote valgus injury and no acute edema. To distinguish a healed grade I-II MCL injury (Pellegrini-Stieda) from an acute deep MCL tear, which feature most reliably indicates the chronic, healed process?
text: A fellow shows you a coronal proton-density image with ossification along the expected course of the proximal MCL, paralleling the medial femoral condyle, in a patient with a remote valgus injury and no acute edema. To distinguish a healed grade I-II MCL injury (Pellegrini-Stieda) from an acute deep MCL tear, which feature most reliably indicates the chronic, healed process? | A. Curvilinear ossification at the femoral origin without surrounding marrow or soft-tissue edema | B. Fluid signal interposed between the deep MCL and the medial meniscus | C. A wavy, lax superficial MCL with surrounding T2-hyperintense fluid | D. High T2 signal tracking along the meniscofemoral and meniscotibial ligaments | A | Pellegrini-Stieda represents post-traumatic ossification/calcification at the femoral attachment of the MCL; the absence of marrow and soft-tissue edema confirms a chronic healed lesion rather than acute injury, where edema and fluid would dominate.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When grading a medial collateral ligament injury on MRI for a sports-medicine fellow, which finding is MOST consistent with a high-grade (grade III) complete tear rather than a grade II partial injury?
text: When grading a medial collateral ligament injury on MRI for a sports-medicine fellow, which finding is MOST consistent with a high-grade (grade III) complete tear rather than a grade II partial injury? | A. Complete discontinuity of the superficial MCL fibers with fluid spanning the full ligament width and a wavy, retracted free edge | B. Edema superficial to an intact, thickened MCL with preserved continuous low-signal fibers | C. Periligamentous edema with partial fiber thickening but no fluid-filled defect | D. Increased T2 signal within the deep MCL with intact superficial fibers | A | Grade III is defined by complete fiber discontinuity with a fluid-filled gap and often a wavy/retracted ligament, whereas grade I shows only periligamentous edema over intact fibers and grade II shows partial-thickness fiber disruption or thickening with preserved continuity.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On axial and sagittal MRI at the level of the lateral knee, you trace a tendon to the fibular head, where it blends with the fibular (lateral) collateral ligament to form a 'conjoined tendon.' Recognizing this conjoined insertion is key because injury here implicates posterolateral instability. Which structure forms this conjoined insertion with the fibular collateral ligament?
text: On axial and sagittal MRI at the level of the lateral knee, you trace a tendon to the fibular head, where it blends with the fibular (lateral) collateral ligament to form a 'conjoined tendon.' Recognizing this conjoined insertion is key because injury here implicates posterolateral instability. Which structure forms this conjoined insertion with the fibular collateral ligament? | A. The biceps femoris tendon, whose long-head direct and anterior arms blend with the FCL at the fibular head | B. The iliotibial band, which inserts at Gerdy tubercle on the lateral tibia and does not conjoin with the FCL | C. The popliteus tendon, which passes deep to the FCL to insert on the lateral femoral condyle | D. The lateral patellar retinaculum, which extends to the fibular head | A | The long head of the biceps femoris and the fibular (lateral) collateral ligament merge into a conjoined tendon at the fibular head; the IT band inserts separately at Gerdy tubercle and the popliteus inserts on the femur, so recognizing the conjoined insertion helps localize posterolateral corner injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 24-year-old presents after a non-contact pivoting injury. MRI shows ACL discontinuity. Regarding the classic pivot-shift contusion pattern, which paired location of marrow edema is MOST specific for this mechanism?
text: A 24-year-old presents after a non-contact pivoting injury. MRI shows ACL discontinuity. Regarding the classic pivot-shift contusion pattern, which paired location of marrow edema is MOST specific for this mechanism? | A. Posterolateral tibial plateau and mid-lateral femoral condyle (near the terminal sulcus) | B. Anteromedial femoral condyle and anteromedial tibial plateau | C. Posteromedial tibial plateau and medial femoral condyle | D. Anterior tibia and anterior femur with patellar contusion | A | The pivot-shift (valgus + internal tibial rotation + anterior translation) drives the posterolateral tibial plateau against the mid-portion of the lateral femoral condyle at the condylopatellar (terminal) sulcus, producing the kissing contusions highly associated with ACL tears. A reciprocal posteromedial tibial plateau bruise from the tibia snapping back is a secondary sign.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 67-year-old woman has acute medial knee pain without trauma. MRI shows a subchondral focus in the weight-bearing medial femoral condyle. Which finding BEST distinguishes a subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIFK/SONK) from primary osteonecrosis (AVN)?
text: A 67-year-old woman has acute medial knee pain without trauma. MRI shows a subchondral focus in the weight-bearing medial femoral condyle. Which finding BEST distinguishes a subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIFK/SONK) from primary osteonecrosis (AVN)? | A. A subchondral T2-hypointense/low-signal line that is concave toward and roughly parallel to the subchondral plate, with adjacent diffuse marrow edema | B. A serpentine double-line sign enclosing a fat-signal center remote from the articular surface | C. Geographic fat-equivalent marrow signal bounded by a sclerotic rim | D. Diffuse homogeneous marrow edema with no discrete line and an intact cortex | A | SIFK is identified by a subchondral low-signal fracture line paralleling the articular surface with surrounding marrow edema, whereas primary AVN shows the serpiginous double-line sign demarcating an epiphyseal infarct away from the subchondral plate. Line length/depth and overlying condylar collapse predict progression.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 31-year-old marathon runner has knee MRI showing patchy, slightly T1-hypointense marrow in the bilateral distal femoral and proximal tibial metadiaphyses, sparing the epiphyses. To confirm this is benign red-marrow reconversion rather than infiltrative disease, the MOST reliable feature is that the marrow signal remains:
text: A 31-year-old marathon runner has knee MRI showing patchy, slightly T1-hypointense marrow in the bilateral distal femoral and proximal tibial metadiaphyses, sparing the epiphyses. To confirm this is benign red-marrow reconversion rather than infiltrative disease, the MOST reliable feature is that the marrow signal remains: | A. Higher (brighter) than adjacent skeletal muscle and intervertebral disc on T1-weighted images | B. Markedly lower than muscle on T1 but suppresses completely on STIR | C. Isointense to fluid on T2 with avid homogeneous enhancement | D. Lower than muscle on T1 with diffuse restricted diffusion | A | Normal hematopoietic (red) marrow always retains interspersed fat, so on T1 it stays brighter than skeletal muscle and disc; signal at or below muscle should raise concern for marrow-replacing infiltration. Reconversion is also typically symmetric, spares the epiphyses/apophyses, and follows the reverse order of normal conversion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After an acute lateral patellar dislocation that has spontaneously reduced, which combination of marrow contusions on MRI is the characteristic signature of this mechanism?
text: After an acute lateral patellar dislocation that has spontaneously reduced, which combination of marrow contusions on MRI is the characteristic signature of this mechanism? | A. Inferomedial patellar facet and anterolateral femoral condyle (lateral aspect) | B. Superolateral patella and posteromedial tibial plateau | C. Lateral patellar facet and lateral tibial plateau | D. Medial patellar facet and medial femoral condyle | A | As the patella dislocates laterally and relocates, the medial patellar facet impacts the anterolateral (lateral) femoral condyle, producing edema at both sites along with medial patellofemoral ligament (MPFL) injury (tear site variable — patellar, femoral, or both). Recognizing this bruise pattern lets you infer a transient dislocation even when the patella is reduced.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a sagittal intermediate-TE PD sequence, the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus shows a focal increase in internal signal that does not extend to an articular surface. The signal is most conspicuous where the meniscus curves and the collagen fibers are oriented near 55 degrees to the main magnetic field. Which feature most reliably distinguishes this magic-angle phenomenon from a true intrasubstance degeneration/tear?
text: On a sagittal intermediate-TE PD sequence, the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus shows a focal increase in internal signal that does not extend to an articular surface. The signal is most conspicuous where the meniscus curves and the collagen fibers are oriented near 55 degrees to the main magnetic field. Which feature most reliably distinguishes this magic-angle phenomenon from a true intrasubstance degeneration/tear? | A. The signal reaches an articular surface on at least one image | B. The increased signal disappears or markedly decreases on a long-TE (T2-weighted) sequence | C. The signal is brightest on the fat-suppressed T2 image | D. The signal is associated with a parameniscal cyst | B | Magic-angle signal arises from T2 lengthening in collagen oriented near 55 degrees and is seen only at short/intermediate TE; it fades or vanishes at long TE, whereas a true tear/degeneration persists. This is why a meniscal signal that does not extend to the surface and resolves on T2 should not be called a tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A sagittal PD image of the knee shows a thin, sharply defined low-signal line paralleling the high-signal articular cartilage surface, mimicking a delamination or a surface fissure. The line is most pronounced at high-contrast interfaces and its position shifts if the phase- and frequency-encoding directions are swapped. What is the most effective way to reduce this truncation (Gibbs) artifact?
text: A sagittal PD image of the knee shows a thin, sharply defined low-signal line paralleling the high-signal articular cartilage surface, mimicking a delamination or a surface fissure. The line is most pronounced at high-contrast interfaces and its position shifts if the phase- and frequency-encoding directions are swapped. What is the most effective way to reduce this truncation (Gibbs) artifact? | A. Decrease the receiver bandwidth | B. Increase the matrix size (more phase-encoding steps) to improve spatial resolution | C. Add fat suppression | D. Increase the echo train length | B | Truncation (Gibbs ringing) results from finite sampling of high-contrast edges and produces parallel lines that can mimic cartilage or meniscal abnormality; increasing the matrix/spatial resolution narrows the oscillations and reduces the artifact. It is most pronounced along the phase-encode direction and shifts when the encoding axes are swapped.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After ACL reconstruction with an interference screw, fluid-sensitive images near the tibial tunnel are degraded by susceptibility artifact obscuring the graft. Which single parameter change does the MOST to reduce metallic susceptibility distortion?
text: After ACL reconstruction with an interference screw, fluid-sensitive images near the tibial tunnel are degraded by susceptibility artifact obscuring the graft. Which single parameter change does the MOST to reduce metallic susceptibility distortion? | A. Increasing the receiver bandwidth | B. Switching from fast spin-echo to gradient-echo acquisition | C. Using spectral (chemical-shift selective) fat saturation instead of STIR | D. Increasing the echo time (TE) | A | Higher receiver bandwidth shortens the readout and compresses the per-pixel frequency shift, directly reducing metal-induced geometric distortion and signal pile-up. Gradient-echo worsens susceptibility, spectral fat-sat fails near metal (favoring STIR/Dixon), and longer TE increases dephasing.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On a coronal fat-suppressed T2 image of the knee, the marrow on the lateral side of the field appears bright and inhomogeneously 'unsuppressed,' while the medial side is well suppressed. There is no corresponding finding on T1. What is the most likely explanation for this fat-suppression failure?
text: On a coronal fat-suppressed T2 image of the knee, the marrow on the lateral side of the field appears bright and inhomogeneously 'unsuppressed,' while the medial side is well suppressed. There is no corresponding finding on T1. What is the most likely explanation for this fat-suppression failure? | A. Bone marrow edema from a stress reaction | B. B0 field inhomogeneity at the periphery causing the fat resonance to fall outside the saturation band | C. A true infiltrative marrow process | D. Chemical-shift artifact of the second kind | B | Spectral fat saturation depends on B0 homogeneity; toward the edges of the FOV (or near large off-isocenter curvature) the fat peak drifts and is incompletely saturated, producing regional 'failed' fat-sat that mimics marrow signal but has no T1 correlate. STIR or Dixon techniques, which are insensitive to B0 inhomogeneity, are the remedy.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 24-year-old gymnast has lateral patellar maltracking on physical exam. On a sagittal MRI you measure the patellar tendon length and the greatest diagonal length of the patella to calculate the Insall-Salvati ratio. Which value confirms patella alta?
text: A 24-year-old gymnast has lateral patellar maltracking on physical exam. On a sagittal MRI you measure the patellar tendon length and the greatest diagonal length of the patella to calculate the Insall-Salvati ratio. Which value confirms patella alta? | A. >1.2 | B. >0.8 | C. >1.0 | D. >2.0 | A | An Insall-Salvati ratio (patellar tendon length divided by patellar length) >1.2 indicates patella alta, a predisposing factor for instability; <0.8 indicates patella baja, and the normal range is approximately 0.8-1.2.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 64-year-old retired carpenter has a chronic full-thickness supraspinatus tear. On the sagittal-oblique Y-view, the supraspinatus muscle belly lies entirely below a line drawn from the superior border of the scapular spine to the superior border of the coracoid, and the muscle shows more fat than muscle on T1. Which finding most strongly predicts that an anatomic primary cuff repair will fail?
text: A 64-year-old retired carpenter has a chronic full-thickness supraspinatus tear. On the sagittal-oblique Y-view, the supraspinatus muscle belly lies entirely below a line drawn from the superior border of the scapular spine to the superior border of the coracoid, and the muscle shows more fat than muscle on T1. Which finding most strongly predicts that an anatomic primary cuff repair will fail? | A. Positive tangent sign with Goutallier grade 3-4 fatty infiltration | B. Tendon retraction to the apex of the humeral head | C. A trace of fluid in the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa | D. A type II (curved) acromion on the outlet view | A | A positive tangent sign (supraspinatus belly below the spine-coracoid line) plus high-grade Goutallier fatty infiltration (grade 3-4) reflects irreversible muscle degeneration and is the strongest predictor of repair failure and re-tear, often shifting management toward debridement, superior capsular reconstruction, or reverse arthroplasty. Tendon retraction to the humeral apex is concerning but, without advanced fatty atrophy, is more often still reparable; acromial shape and a trace of bursal fluid do not by themselves predict repair failure.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 47-year-old falls onto an outstretched, externally rotated arm and now has anterior shoulder pain and weakness with the lift-off and belly-press tests. MRI shows the long head of the biceps displaced medially, lying over (superficial to) the lesser tuberosity. Which associated injury should you most specifically look for on the axial images?
text: A 47-year-old falls onto an outstretched, externally rotated arm and now has anterior shoulder pain and weakness with the lift-off and belly-press tests. MRI shows the long head of the biceps displaced medially, lying over (superficial to) the lesser tuberosity. Which associated injury should you most specifically look for on the axial images? | A. A subscapularis tendon tear | B. A SLAP type II tear | C. An anteroinferior (Bankart) labral tear | D. A supraspinatus footprint tear | A | Medial subluxation/dislocation of the long head of the biceps is the companion sign of a subscapularis tendon tear, because the subscapularis and the deep capsular fibers form the medial wall of the biceps pulley; positive lift-off and belly-press tests localize to the subscapularis. A SLAP tear involves the superior labral-biceps anchor, not the groove, and does not displace the biceps medially; a Bankart is an instability lesion of the anteroinferior labrum unrelated to biceps position.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 28-year-old overhead athlete has deep shoulder pain and a positive O'Brien test. MR arthrogram shows superior labral contrast that is irregular and laterally curved, extending lateral to the biceps anchor into the labral substance, and measuring more than 2-3 mm wide. Which is the most appropriate interpretation?
text: A 28-year-old overhead athlete has deep shoulder pain and a positive O'Brien test. MR arthrogram shows superior labral contrast that is irregular and laterally curved, extending lateral to the biceps anchor into the labral substance, and measuring more than 2-3 mm wide. Which is the most appropriate interpretation? | A. Type II SLAP tear | B. Normal sublabral recess | C. Buford complex | D. Sublabral foramen | A | Contrast with an irregular, laterally curved contour that extends lateral to the biceps anchor into the labrum (the load-bearing morphologic finding, reinforced by a width greater than ~2-3 mm) is a type II SLAP tear. A normal sublabral recess is a smooth, thin, medially directed cleft that parallels the glenoid surface. The Buford complex (absent anterosuperior labrum with a cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament) and the sublabral foramen are anterosuperior variants, not superior labral tears, and should not be repaired.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 21-year-old contact athlete has had four anterior dislocations. CT shows 24% anteroinferior glenoid bone loss, and MRI shows an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion. Which surgical option gives the lowest recurrence risk?
text: A 21-year-old contact athlete has had four anterior dislocations. CT shows 24% anteroinferior glenoid bone loss, and MRI shows an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion. Which surgical option gives the lowest recurrence risk? | A. Bony augmentation such as a coracoid transfer (Latarjet) | B. Isolated arthroscopic Bankart soft-tissue repair | C. Capsular plication without bony augmentation | D. Physical therapy with activity modification | A | With substantial/critical glenoid bone loss (often ~20-25%, with lower subcritical thresholds relevant in high-risk contact athletes) and an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion, isolated arthroscopic Bankart repair has a high recurrence rate, so bony augmentation such as Latarjet is favored to restore the glenoid track and lower recurrence; remplissage may be added depending on the humeral defect strategy. Isolated Bankart repair and capsular plication do not address the bone deficit, and recurrent dislocations in a young contact athlete are not reliably controlled by nonoperative management.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 26-year-old with recurrent anterior instability after multiple dislocations has a non-arthrogram MRI showing the anteroinferior labrum medially displaced and rotated down onto the scapular neck, healed in a medialized position, with an intact but stripped anterior scapular periosteum. Which lesion does this best describe?
text: A 26-year-old with recurrent anterior instability after multiple dislocations has a non-arthrogram MRI showing the anteroinferior labrum medially displaced and rotated down onto the scapular neck, healed in a medialized position, with an intact but stripped anterior scapular periosteum. Which lesion does this best describe? | A. ALPSA lesion | B. Classic soft-tissue Bankart lesion | C. Perthes lesion | D. GLAD lesion | A | An ALPSA (anterior labroligamentous periosteal sleeve avulsion) shows medial and inferior displacement of the labroligamentous complex with an intact but stripped periosteum, so the labrum heals chronically in a medialized position - typical of recurrent instability. A classic Bankart has a torn periosteum with the labrum free at the rim; a Perthes has a nondisplaced labrum with intact periosteum that lifts only with stress; a GLAD is a superficial labral tear with an articular cartilage defect and is not an instability lesion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 53-year-old woman with diabetes has 4 months of progressive shoulder stiffness with global loss of both active and passive motion and no antecedent trauma. Non-arthrogram fat-suppressed T2 MRI is most likely to show which combination of findings?
text: A 53-year-old woman with diabetes has 4 months of progressive shoulder stiffness with global loss of both active and passive motion and no antecedent trauma. Non-arthrogram fat-suppressed T2 MRI is most likely to show which combination of findings? | A. Thickening and edema of the axillary recess capsule with soft tissue obliterating the subcoracoid fat triangle in the rotator interval | B. A full-thickness supraspinatus tear with fluid in the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa | C. An anteroinferior labral tear with a Hill-Sachs lesion | D. Fatty atrophy of the infraspinatus with a spinoglenoid notch cyst | A | Adhesive capsulitis produces capsular and synovial thickening with T2 edema in the axillary recess, plus rotator-interval soft-tissue thickening that obliterates the subcoracoid fat triangle - a highly specific sign - matching the clinical picture of painful global motion loss in a diabetic patient. A cuff tear, a labral-Hill-Sachs instability pattern, and a spinoglenoid notch cyst with infraspinatus denervation each produce focal mechanical or neurologic findings, not the diffuse capsular thickening of a frozen shoulder.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 34-year-old volleyball player has posterior shoulder aching and mild external-rotation weakness. MRI shows a paralabral cyst extending into the spinoglenoid notch but not reaching the suprascapular notch, with a posterosuperior labral tear. Which muscle is most likely to show denervation edema or atrophy?
text: A 34-year-old volleyball player has posterior shoulder aching and mild external-rotation weakness. MRI shows a paralabral cyst extending into the spinoglenoid notch but not reaching the suprascapular notch, with a posterosuperior labral tear. Which muscle is most likely to show denervation edema or atrophy? | A. Infraspinatus | B. Supraspinatus | C. Teres minor | D. Both supraspinatus and infraspinatus | A | At the spinoglenoid notch the suprascapular nerve has already given off its supraspinatus branches more proximally, so compression there denervates the infraspinatus alone, producing isolated infraspinatus edema or atrophy. A lesion at the more proximal suprascapular notch would affect both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus; the teres minor is supplied by the axillary nerve in the quadrilateral space, not the suprascapular nerve.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 58-year-old returns 9 months after arthroscopic supraspinatus repair with recurrent pain and weakness. On fat-suppressed T2 imaging, which finding most reliably indicates a recurrent full-thickness re-tear rather than expected postoperative change?
text: A 58-year-old returns 9 months after arthroscopic supraspinatus repair with recurrent pain and weakness. On fat-suppressed T2 imaging, which finding most reliably indicates a recurrent full-thickness re-tear rather than expected postoperative change? | A. A fluid-signal-intensity gap spanning the full tendon thickness with tendon retraction from the footprint | B. Intermediate T2 signal within the tendon at the repair site | C. Low-signal suture-anchor artifact at the greater tuberosity | D. A small amount of fluid in the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa | A | A full-thickness, fluid-bright gap with tendon retraction off the footprint indicates a recurrent re-tear, because fluid-equivalent signal traversing the entire tendon is the same criterion used in the native cuff. Intermediate (not fluid-bright) intratendinous signal, susceptibility from suture anchors, and a small amount of bursal fluid are all expected postoperative findings and do not by themselves indicate a re-tear; granulation tissue can keep signal intermediate for months.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 58-year-old laborer has a chronic full-thickness supraspinatus tear. On the sagittal-oblique T1-weighted image at the level of the scapular Y, the supraspinatus muscle belly does not cross a line drawn from the superior margin of the scapular spine to the superior margin of the coracoid. According to the tangent sign, this finding most specifically indicates which of the following?
text: A 58-year-old laborer has a chronic full-thickness supraspinatus tear. On the sagittal-oblique T1-weighted image at the level of the scapular Y, the supraspinatus muscle belly does not cross a line drawn from the superior margin of the scapular spine to the superior margin of the coracoid. According to the tangent sign, this finding most specifically indicates which of the following? | A. Tendon retraction to the level of the glenoid (Patte stage 3) | B. Goutallier grade 1 fatty infiltration | C. Supraspinatus muscle atrophy | D. An irreparable subscapularis tear | C | The tangent sign is positive when the supraspinatus muscle belly fails to cross (lies below) the line connecting the superior borders of the scapular spine and coracoid on the Y-shaped sagittal image, indicating significant supraspinatus muscle atrophy and predicting poorer repair outcomes. It assesses bulk/atrophy, distinct from tendon retraction (Patte) or fatty infiltration grading (Goutallier).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On an oblique-coronal fat-suppressed T2-weighted image, you identify focal high signal at the articular surface of the supraspinatus immediately adjacent to its insertion on the greater tuberosity footprint, with intact bursal-side fibers. This 'rim-rent' (PASTA-type) tear is most often UNDERESTIMATED on conventional MRI for which reason?
