The acromion will show a type III hooked morphology | A | This is the subscapularis tendon; a tear here disrupts the medial wall of the biceps pulley, so the most specifically associated finding is medial subluxation or dislocation of the long head of the biceps out of the bicipital groove - the companion clue to a subscapularis tear. A supraspinatus footprint gap, a posterior (reverse Bankart) labral tear, and acromial hooking are not the direct consequence of a subscapularis tendon tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 23-year-old sustained an anterior shoulder dislocation in a wrestling match and now has apprehension. On this axial slice (anterior at the top), the dark triangle at the anterior glenoid rim is the anterior labrum.
text: A 23-year-old sustained an anterior shoulder dislocation in a wrestling match and now has apprehension. On this axial slice (anterior at the top), the dark triangle at the anterior glenoid rim is the anterior labrum. | A. A posterosuperolateral humeral head impaction (Hill-Sachs lesion) | B. A superior labral tear extending into the biceps anchor (SLAP) | C. An os acromiale at the meso-meta junction | D. Distal clavicular osteolysis | A | A torn anteroinferior labrum (Bankart lesion) from anterior dislocation is classically paired with a Hill-Sachs lesion, the impaction fracture of the posterosuperolateral humeral head where it strikes the anterior glenoid rim. A SLAP tear involves the superior labral-biceps anchor (a different mechanism, overhead athletes); os acromiale and distal clavicular osteolysis are unrelated to the instability event shown here.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 55-year-old has chronic shoulder pain and weakness. On this coronal slice, the thin plane between the rotator cuff and the overlying acromion/deltoid is the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa.
text: A 55-year-old has chronic shoulder pain and weakness. On this coronal slice, the thin plane between the rotator cuff and the overlying acromion/deltoid is the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. | A. Combined with joint fluid, distension of this bursa suggests a full-thickness cuff tear | B. Isolated distension here is diagnostic of adhesive capsulitis | C. Fluid here indicates a SLAP tear at the biceps anchor | D. Fluid here localizes a quadrilateral-space syndrome | A | This is the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa; communicating fluid in BOTH this bursa and the glenohumeral joint strongly suggests a full-thickness rotator cuff tear that lets fluid pass from the joint into the bursa. A trace of isolated bursal fluid is nonspecific and does not diagnose a full-thickness tear or frozen shoulder (which thickens the axillary recess capsule); the bursa is unrelated to the superior labral anchor (SLAP) or the quadrilateral space.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 52-year-old diabetic woman has months of painful global stiffness with no trauma. On this coronal slice, the dependent inferior pouch of the joint capsule is the axillary recess.
text: A 52-year-old diabetic woman has months of painful global stiffness with no trauma. On this coronal slice, the dependent inferior pouch of the joint capsule is the axillary recess. | A. Capsular thickening and edema here support adhesive capsulitis | B. Fluid distension here is a normal finding requiring no follow-up | C. Thickening here indicates a full-thickness supraspinatus tear | D. Edema here localizes a spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst | A | This is the axillary recess; thickening and T2 edema of the axillary recess capsule (together with rotator-interval soft-tissue thickening that obliterates the subcoracoid fat) is the MRI signature of adhesive capsulitis, fitting this diabetic patient with non-traumatic global stiffness. The axillary recess is not where a supraspinatus tear or a spinoglenoid notch cyst is assessed, and capsular thickening here is not a normal finding to ignore.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 60-year-old with a chronic cuff tear is being evaluated for repair. This sagittal T1 scapular-Y slice shows the four cuff muscle bellies; the muscle filling the supraspinous fossa above the scapular spine is the supraspinatus.
text: A 60-year-old with a chronic cuff tear is being evaluated for repair. This sagittal T1 scapular-Y slice shows the four cuff muscle bellies; the muscle filling the supraspinous fossa above the scapular spine is the supraspinatus. | A. High-grade fatty infiltration here predicts an irreparable tear | B. Bright marrow signal here on T1 indicates marrow edema | C. This slice is best for measuring the acromiohumeral interval | D. This T1 slice best demonstrates a SLAP tear of the biceps anchor | A | The supraspinatus fills the supraspinous fossa, and T1 (fat bright, unsuppressed) is the sequence to grade Goutallier-Fuchs fatty infiltration; high-grade fatty atrophy of the supraspinatus predicts an irreparable tear and changes surgical planning. Bright signal in MUSCLE on T1 reflects fatty infiltration, not marrow edema (which is DARK on T1); the acromiohumeral interval is a radiographic/coronal measurement, and a SLAP tear is a labral lesion best shown on fluid-sensitive or arthrographic sequences, not this T1 muscle slice.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A 48-year-old overhead athlete has lateral shoulder pain and weakness. On this coronal slice, follow the supraspinatus tendon to the greater-tuberosity footprint and separate the thin distal crescent from the thicker rotator cable just medial to it. Why does that distinction matter when a small articular-sided tear is present?
text: A 48-year-old overhead athlete has lateral shoulder pain and weakness. On this coronal slice, follow the supraspinatus tendon to the greater-tuberosity footprint and separate the thin distal crescent from the thicker rotator cable just medial to it. Why does that distinction matter when a small articular-sided tear is present? | A. Cable integrity helps determine functional importance; an intact cable may preserve load transfer, while cable disruption makes a small-appearing tear more consequential | B. The cable is the same structure as the superior labrum, so its injury is reported as a SLAP tear | C. A crescent tear is diagnosed only on axial imaging because the coronal cannot show the footprint | D. The cable/crescent distinction applies only after surgery and should be ignored on the initial MRI | A | The rotator cable/crescent model matters because the cable is a load-sharing band just medial to the thin distal cuff crescent. A limited crescent tear with an intact cable can behave differently from a tear that disrupts the cable, especially anteriorly, so the report should not describe only the footprint gap and retraction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this coronal water (T2 fat-suppressed) image, find the femoral head marrow superior to the physeal scar — this is the screening location for marrow pathology. A 52-year-old man on chronic corticosteroids has 6 weeks of groin pain; radiographs are normal. If this femoral head marrow instead showed a serpentine subchondral line with the classic 'double-line sign' (T2-bright inner / T2-dark outer rim) and NO sclerosis on radiograph, what is the most appropriate staging and management?
text: On this coronal water (T2 fat-suppressed) image, find the femoral head marrow superior to the physeal scar — this is the screening location for marrow pathology. A 52-year-old man on chronic corticosteroids has 6 weeks of groin pain; radiographs are normal. If this femoral head marrow instead showed a serpentine subchondral line with the classic 'double-line sign' (T2-bright inner / T2-dark outer rim) and NO sclerosis on radiograph, what is the most appropriate staging and management? | A. ARCO stage I osteonecrosis (MRI-positive, radiograph-negative) — joint-preserving management may be considered before collapse | B. ARCO stage III osteonecrosis — subchondral collapse already present, proceed to arthroplasty | C. Transient bone-marrow-edema syndrome — reassure, protected weight-bearing, expect self-resolution | D. Subchondral insufficiency fracture — restricted weight-bearing for a stress-type fracture with no necrosis | A | Key: a classic double-line AVN pattern with a normal radiograph is the MRI-only stage of AVN = ARCO I; because there is no radiographic sclerosis or collapse yet, joint-preserving management may be considered before collapse. Best distractor (ARCO III) is wrong because collapse/crescent sign would be required to call it stage III, and the stem specifies a radiograph-negative pre-collapse hip. Transient BMES is wrong because it shows diffuse edema WITHOUT a demarcated double-line lesion and is self-limited. SIFH is wrong because it is a subchondral fracture line without the necrotic double-line pattern and is not staged by ARCO.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this coronal T2-FS image, find the gluteus medius and minimus tendon footprint on the greater trochanter (the medius inserts on the lateral and superoposterior facets; the minimus on the anterior facet). A 58-year-old woman has chronic lateral hip pain, point tenderness over the trochanter, and a positive single-leg-stance (Trendelenburg) test; she failed a corticosteroid injection. If this footprint instead showed tendon delamination with peritrochanteric T2 hyperintensity, which statement is most accurate?
text: On this coronal T2-FS image, find the gluteus medius and minimus tendon footprint on the greater trochanter (the medius inserts on the lateral and superoposterior facets; the minimus on the anterior facet). A 58-year-old woman has chronic lateral hip pain, point tenderness over the trochanter, and a positive single-leg-stance (Trendelenburg) test; she failed a corticosteroid injection. If this footprint instead showed tendon delamination with peritrochanteric T2 hyperintensity, which statement is most accurate? | A. The gluteus medius/minimus are the 'rotator cuff of the hip,' and most clinically labeled 'trochanteric bursitis' is actually gluteal tendinopathy/tearing | B. Isolated greater trochanteric bursa fluid is the dominant pain generator, so the tendon footprint is rarely involved | C. Lateral hip pain with a positive Trendelenburg sign points to iliopsoas (internal snapping) tendinopathy | D. A footprint tear here is a surgical emergency requiring acute open repair regardless of tendon quality | A | Key: the gluteus medius/minimus act as the abductor 'rotator cuff of the hip,' and the modern understanding is that most 'trochanteric bursitis' is really gluteal tendinopathy/footprint tearing — the core of GTPS. The bursitis distractor is wrong because isolated bursal fluid is usually secondary; the tendon footprint is the primary lesion. Iliopsoas is wrong — that is an anterior/internal snapping problem, not lateral footprint pathology, and snapping is a dynamic-ultrasound diagnosis. 'Surgical emergency' is wrong — GTPS is managed conservatively first (load management, PT), with repair reserved for refractory full-thickness/retracted tears.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this axial T2-FS image, find the anterior/anterosuperior acetabular labrum at the rim of the joint — the segment most prone to tearing in FAI. A 24-year-old hockey player has anterior groin pain and a positive anterior impingement (FADIR) test, and you suspect a labral tear. If you needed to maximize sensitivity for a labral tear, which choice best reflects the site and modality?
text: On this axial T2-FS image, find the anterior/anterosuperior acetabular labrum at the rim of the joint — the segment most prone to tearing in FAI. A 24-year-old hockey player has anterior groin pain and a positive anterior impingement (FADIR) test, and you suspect a labral tear. If you needed to maximize sensitivity for a labral tear, which choice best reflects the site and modality? | A. Anterosuperior labrum; MR arthrography is the most sensitive study for detecting labral tears | B. Posteroinferior labrum; MR arthrography is the most sensitive study for detecting labral tears | C. Anterosuperior labrum; non-contrast 1.5-T MRI is equal or superior to MR arthrography for tears | D. Anterosuperior labrum; dynamic ultrasound is the gold standard for diagnosing the tear | A | Key: acetabular labral tears are predominantly anterosuperior, and MR arthrography (intra-articular gadolinium distending the joint) is the most sensitive modality for detecting them. The posteroinferior option is wrong — that is not the classic FAI tear location. The 'non-contrast equals arthrography' option is wrong because MR arthrography outperforms standard non-contrast MRI for labral tears (especially at lower field strength). Dynamic ultrasound is wrong — it is the diagnostic tool for snapping-hip syndromes, not for intra-articular labral tears.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this axial T2-FS image, find the sciatic nerve in the deep gluteal space — between the posterior acetabulum/ischium and the gluteus maximus, just deep to the piriformis. A 40-year-old runner has buttock pain with sitting and non-dermatomal posterior thigh radiation; lumbar MRI is unremarkable. If imaging here showed an enlarged/T2-hyperintense sciatic nerve adjacent to the piriformis, which is the most accurate characterization?
text: On this axial T2-FS image, find the sciatic nerve in the deep gluteal space — between the posterior acetabulum/ischium and the gluteus maximus, just deep to the piriformis. A 40-year-old runner has buttock pain with sitting and non-dermatomal posterior thigh radiation; lumbar MRI is unremarkable. If imaging here showed an enlarged/T2-hyperintense sciatic nerve adjacent to the piriformis, which is the most accurate characterization? | A. Deep gluteal syndrome — extraspinal sciatic nerve entrapment in the subgluteal space (piriformis is one cause) | B. A lumbar L5–S1 disc radiculopathy that simply was not captured on the spine MRI | C. Meralgia paresthetica from lateral femoral cutaneous nerve compression | D. Pudendal neuralgia from entrapment within Alcock's canal | A | Key: sciatic nerve findings in the deep gluteal (subgluteal) space with buttock pain and non-radicular sciatica describe deep gluteal syndrome / extraspinal sciatic entrapment, of which piriformis syndrome is one cause. The disc-radiculopathy distractor is wrong because the spine MRI is normal and the entrapment is extraspinal at the hip. Meralgia paresthetica is wrong — that is the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve causing anterolateral thigh symptoms, not the sciatic nerve. Pudendal neuralgia is wrong — that involves the pudendal nerve/perineum, a different nerve and territory.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this sagittal PD-FS image, find the anterior femoral head–neck junction in profile and trace its contour/offset — the region that loses sphericity in cam morphology. A 27-year-old soccer player has anterior groin pain and limited internal rotation; you suspect cam FAI. Regarding measuring the alpha angle to quantify cam morphology, which statement is correct?
text: On this sagittal PD-FS image, find the anterior femoral head–neck junction in profile and trace its contour/offset — the region that loses sphericity in cam morphology. A 27-year-old soccer player has anterior groin pain and limited internal rotation; you suspect cam FAI. Regarding measuring the alpha angle to quantify cam morphology, which statement is correct? | A. The alpha angle is best measured on oblique-axial (and radial) images aligned to the femoral neck, not on the coronal plane | B. The alpha angle is measured on the straight coronal image, where the superolateral head-neck offset is shown | C. An alpha angle below ~40° is diagnostic of cam morphology and predicts a labral tear | D. Cam FAI reflects acetabular over-coverage, so the crossover sign is the measurement of choice | A | Key: the alpha angle for cam morphology is measured on oblique-axial images aligned along the femoral neck (and on radial reformats), which best profile the anterior/anterosuperior head-neck asphericity — not on the coronal plane. The coronal option is wrong because the straight coronal underestimates the anterior cam lesion. The '<40°' option is wrong — an ELEVATED alpha angle (commonly >55°) indicates cam morphology, while ~60° may be more specific; a low value is normal. The crossover-sign option is wrong because crossover/over-coverage describes PINCER morphology (acetabular retroversion), not cam, which is a femoral-side aspherical head-neck deformity. Morphology still needs clinical and chondrolabral correlation before calling symptomatic FAI.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this coronal T2-FS image, follow the joint inferomedially to the pubic symphysis and the rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurosis at the pubic body — the focus of athletic pubalgia. A 30-year-old soccer player has chronic lower-abdominal/groin pain worse with cutting and sit-ups, no palpable inguinal hernia. If imaging here showed fluid tracking inferolaterally from the symphysis along the adductor origin, what is this finding and its significance?
text: On this coronal T2-FS image, follow the joint inferomedially to the pubic symphysis and the rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurosis at the pubic body — the focus of athletic pubalgia. A 30-year-old soccer player has chronic lower-abdominal/groin pain worse with cutting and sit-ups, no palpable inguinal hernia. If imaging here showed fluid tracking inferolaterally from the symphysis along the adductor origin, what is this finding and its significance? | A. The 'secondary cleft sign' — fluid extending from the symphysis indicating a rectus–adductor aponeurosis/common-origin injury of athletic pubalgia | B. The normal 'primary cleft' — a midline symphyseal cleft that is an expected variant and excludes pathology | C. A femoral-neck tension-side stress fracture requiring urgent surgical fixation | D. An inguinal canal lipoma confirming a true (palpable) inguinal hernia as the pain source | A | Key: fluid extending inferolaterally from the symphysis along the rectus-adductor aponeurosis is the secondary cleft sign, marking a common rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurotic injury at the symphysis — the imaging hallmark of athletic pubalgia. The 'primary cleft' distractor is wrong because the primary cleft is the normal midline symphyseal cleft; it is the SECONDARY cleft that signals injury. The femoral-neck stress-fracture option is wrong — that is a superolateral (tension-side) femoral-neck process, an unrelated location and entity. The inguinal-hernia option is wrong because athletic pubalgia is a non-hernia aponeurotic/musculotendinous injury without a true palpable hernia.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this coronal T2 FS image, follow the medial side from the medial epicondyle down to the sublime tubercle of the coronoid and trace the anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament — normally a thin, taut, uniformly low-signal band reaching the sublime tubercle, where a tiny synovial recess can be a normal finding. A 22-year-old pitcher has medial elbow pain at ball release and a positive moving-valgus test. As you scroll this plane, what is the abnormal threshold that distinguishes a partial undersurface (T-sign) tear from the normal distal recess, and what study best confirms it?
text: On this coronal T2 FS image, follow the medial side from the medial epicondyle down to the sublime tubercle of the coronoid and trace the anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament — normally a thin, taut, uniformly low-signal band reaching the sublime tubercle, where a tiny synovial recess can be a normal finding. A 22-year-old pitcher has medial elbow pain at ball release and a positive moving-valgus test. As you scroll this plane, what is the abnormal threshold that distinguishes a partial undersurface (T-sign) tear from the normal distal recess, and what study best confirms it? | A. Fluid/contrast tracking along the sublime tubercle BEYOND the edge of the articular cartilage while proximal fibers stay attached — the T-sign of a partial undersurface tear; MR arthrography is the most sensitive study | B. Any fluid undercutting the most distal UCL fibers by even 1–2 mm is diagnostic of a full-thickness tear; non-contrast MRI is fully sufficient | C. Thickening and intermediate (not fluid-bright) signal of the proximal anterior bundle without any undercutting; dynamic valgus ultrasound is the most sensitive test | D. Fluid tracking along the posterior bundle in the cubital tunnel floor; CT arthrography is the most sensitive study | A | Key: the T-sign pattern is fluid/contrast undermining the distal/sublime-tubercle attachment BEYOND the articular-cartilage edge while the proximal fibers remain attached — supporting an articular-sided partial tear in the right clinical setting — and MR arthrography is the most sensitive study for it. The strongest distractor (any 1–2 mm undercutting = full-thickness) is wrong because a couple of millimetres of undercutting can be a normal synovial recess; the abnormal threshold is fluid extending past the cartilage margin, and undersurface tracking is partial, not full-thickness. Proximal thickening with intermediate signal describes chronic sprain/tendinosis, not the T-sign. The posterior bundle is the cubital-tunnel floor and contributes little to valgus restraint — the T-sign is a distal anterior-bundle phenomenon.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this coronal T2 FS image, find the radiocapitellar joint and the convex articular surface of the capitellum — the anterolateral/central capitellum is where osteochondritis dissecans lives. (Remember the normal posterolateral capitellar pseudodefect groove is a bare area, NOT a lesion.) A 14-year-old gymnast has lateral elbow pain and catching. If the capitellum here showed an osteochondral lesion, which approach and finding correctly drive the stable-vs-unstable call?
text: On this coronal T2 FS image, find the radiocapitellar joint and the convex articular surface of the capitellum — the anterolateral/central capitellum is where osteochondritis dissecans lives. (Remember the normal posterolateral capitellar pseudodefect groove is a bare area, NOT a lesion.) A 14-year-old gymnast has lateral elbow pain and catching. If the capitellum here showed an osteochondral lesion, which approach and finding correctly drive the stable-vs-unstable call? | A. Surrounding marrow edema alone confirms instability, so the lesion can be staged from this single coronal image | B. Read the lesion on coronal AND sagittal together; instability is suggested by a T2 fluid-bright line completely undercutting the fragment, a sizeable/multiple subchondral cyst, a cartilage cleft reaching the fragment, or a displaced fragment | C. A single subchondral cyst measuring exactly ≥5 mm is the validated capitellar threshold and is sufficient by itself to call the lesion unstable | D. An isolated high-T2 line at the interface without a cartilage breach or cyst is definitively unstable and mandates immediate surgery | B | Key: capitellar OCD is assessed on coronal + sagittal together (not one plane alone), and instability is suggested by a fluid-bright line completely undercutting the fragment, a sizeable or multiple subchondral cysts, a high-signal cartilage cleft reaching the fragment, or a displaced fragment/loose body. The strongest distractor (marrow edema alone = unstable) is wrong because surrounding edema is present in both stable and unstable lesions and is NOT a stability sign. The '≥5 mm cyst' option is wrong because the 5 mm figure is a knee-derived (De Smet) criterion not validated for the capitellum — teach 'sizeable or multiple,' not a hard cutoff. An isolated high-T2 line without a cartilage breach or cyst is INDETERMINATE (possibly healing granulation tissue), not definitively unstable.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this axial T2 FS image, find the ulnar nerve in the cubital tunnel — posterior to the medial epicondyle, beneath Osborne's retinaculum, and compare it with the median nerve on the anterior side of the same slice. A 35-year-old thrower has medial elbow pain with little-finger numbness. Knowing there is no universal cross-sectional-area cutoff and that mild T2 signal can be normal, what best anchors a confident diagnosis of ulnar neuritis on this plane?
text: On this axial T2 FS image, find the ulnar nerve in the cubital tunnel — posterior to the medial epicondyle, beneath Osborne's retinaculum, and compare it with the median nerve on the anterior side of the same slice. A 35-year-old thrower has medial elbow pain with little-finger numbness. Knowing there is no universal cross-sectional-area cutoff and that mild T2 signal can be normal, what best anchors a confident diagnosis of ulnar neuritis on this plane? | A. Any T2 hyperintensity of the ulnar nerve by itself, since signal alone is diagnostic of neuritis | B. Caliber enlargement plus T2 signal greater than the ipsilateral median-nerve internal control and the contralateral side, together with denervation of FCU/ulnar FDP/ulnar intrinsics — anchored to caliber change + denervation + clinical correlation, not signal alone | C. The presence of the anterior fat pad on this slice, which indicates an effusion and therefore neuritis | D. Subluxation of the radial nerve/PIN over the lateral epicondyle in flexion | B | Key: because there is no universal CSA cutoff and mild ulnar-nerve T2 brightness occurs in asymptomatic elbows, the diagnosis is anchored to caliber change plus muscle denervation plus clinical correlation, using the ipsilateral median nerve and the contralateral side as internal controls — not signal alone. The strongest distractor (any T2 hyperintensity = neuritis) is wrong precisely because isolated signal can be normal. The anterior fat pad is a different structure entirely (an effusion sign, not a nerve finding). Radial-nerve/PIN subluxation over the lateral epicondyle is the wrong nerve and side — the management-changing dynamic finding here is ULNAR-nerve flexion subluxation over the MEDIAL epicondyle (which favors transposition), best assessed on axial images in flexion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this axial T2 FS image, identify the common extensor origin off the lateral epicondyle and the common flexor-pronator origin off the medial epicondyle in cross-section — both are normally uniform low-signal tendon. A 46-year-old recreational tennis player has refractory lateral elbow pain that failed prior steroid injection. As you read these origins, which companion structure must you always trace, and why does it change management?
text: On this axial T2 FS image, identify the common extensor origin off the lateral epicondyle and the common flexor-pronator origin off the medial epicondyle in cross-section — both are normally uniform low-signal tendon. A 46-year-old recreational tennis player has refractory lateral elbow pain that failed prior steroid injection. As you read these origins, which companion structure must you always trace, and why does it change management? | A. Trace the brachialis to the coronoid, because brachialis tendinosis is the usual cause of lateral epicondylitis | B. Trace the LUCL to the supinator crest with lateral epicondylitis (and the anterior-bundle UCL with medial epicondylitis), because a deep common-extensor tear that undercuts the radiocapitellar joint and involves the LUCL implies PLRI — a surgical problem and a known complication of prior lateral steroid injection/release | C. Trace the annular ligament only, because a deep extensor tear most often avulses the annular ligament off the radial neck | D. Trace the posterior bundle of the UCL, because lateral epicondylitis destabilizes the cubital tunnel floor | B | Key: the common-extensor origin (ECRB) sits superficial to the LUCL/RCL origin, so with lateral epicondylitis you must trace the LUCL to the supinator crest — a deep/full-thickness extensor tear that undercuts the radiocapitellar joint and involves the LUCL implies posterolateral rotatory instability, converting 'tennis elbow' into a surgical instability problem (and a known complication of prior lateral steroid injection/release). On the medial side the companion is the anterior-bundle UCL. The brachialis distractor is wrong — brachialis is an anterior flexor unrelated to the extensor origin. The annular ligament is not the lesion that hides beneath a deep extensor tear (the LUCL is), and the posterior bundle of the UCL is a medial cubital-tunnel structure, not a lateral companion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this sagittal IR/STIR image, follow the anterior soft tissues to the distal biceps heading toward the radial tuberosity, and posteriorly the triceps inserting broadly on the olecranon — both normally smooth, continuous, low-signal tendons. A 50-year-old man felt a painful anterior 'pop' lifting a heavy box and has a vague antecubital mass but can still flex. What concept must you keep in mind so you do not undercall the injury on this plane?
text: On this sagittal IR/STIR image, follow the anterior soft tissues to the distal biceps heading toward the radial tuberosity, and posteriorly the triceps inserting broadly on the olecranon — both normally smooth, continuous, low-signal tendons. A 50-year-old man felt a painful anterior 'pop' lifting a heavy box and has a vague antecubital mass but can still flex. What concept must you keep in mind so you do not undercall the injury on this plane? | A. A complete distal biceps tear with an INTACT lacertus fibrosus may NOT retract and can mimic a partial tear clinically and on imaging — report partial-vs-complete AND lacertus status and state retraction in cm; likewise the triceps deep central fibers tear first while the superficial expansion stays intact | B. A balled-up, retracted stump with an empty tuberosity is the only way a complete distal biceps tear can present, so preserved flexion excludes a complete tear | C. The bicipitoradial bursa fluid is itself the diagnosis of a complete tear and no insertion assessment is needed | D. The distal biceps and triceps are best graded on the coronal plane, so a sagittal read cannot characterize either tendon | A | Key: the lacertus fibrosus tethers the distal biceps, so a COMPLETE tear with an intact lacertus may not retract and can masquerade as a partial tear (the commonly missed complete tear) — you must report partial-vs-complete AND lacertus-intact-vs-torn and state retraction in cm; analogously, the triceps deep central fibers fail first while the superficial expansion stays intact, so a clinically near-complete tear can look partial. The strongest distractor (empty tuberosity is the only presentation) is wrong because that is the DISRUPTED-lacertus pattern; an intact lacertus prevents retraction. Bicipitoradial bursitis is a clue to scrutinize the insertion, not a diagnosis by itself. Distal biceps grading is done on axial/FABS and sagittal, not the coronal plane.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this sagittal IR/STIR image, find the anterior fat pad (normally seen, hugging the coronoid fossa) and the posterior fat pad in the olecranon fossa, and sweep the coronoid fossa, olecranon fossa, and radiocapitellar recess. A 40-year-old laborer has elbow locking and catching, and you suspect a loose body. Which statement about reading this plane is correct?
text: On this sagittal IR/STIR image, find the anterior fat pad (normally seen, hugging the coronoid fossa) and the posterior fat pad in the olecranon fossa, and sweep the coronoid fossa, olecranon fossa, and radiocapitellar recess. A 40-year-old laborer has elbow locking and catching, and you suspect a loose body. Which statement about reading this plane is correct? | A. A visible anterior fat pad is always pathologic, whereas the posterior fat pad is normally seen in the olecranon fossa | B. Loose bodies are best detected on the coronal plane against dark cortex, and the posterior fat pad is normally elevated | C. Loose bodies migrate to the dependent recesses (coronoid fossa, olecranon fossa, radiocapitellar recess) and are best seen against bright joint fluid on sagittal fluid-sensitive images; a normally HIDDEN posterior fat pad that becomes visible/elevated after trauma signals an effusion/hemarthrosis (occult fracture until proven otherwise) | D. Any fluid in the olecranon fossa is a loose body, and the radiocapitellar recess never harbors bodies | C | Key: loose bodies migrate to the dependent recesses (coronoid fossa, olecranon fossa, radiocapitellar recess) and are most conspicuous against bright joint fluid on sagittal fluid-sensitive images; the anterior fat pad is NORMALLY seen, but the posterior fat pad is normally HIDDEN in the olecranon fossa, so a visible/elevated posterior fat pad after trauma means an effusion/hemarthrosis and an occult intra-articular fracture until proven otherwise. The first distractor reverses the fat-pad rule (the anterior pad is normal; the posterior pad being visible is the abnormal sign). Loose bodies are best seen on sagittal against bright fluid, not on coronal against dark cortex. And fluid in the olecranon fossa is an effusion, not itself a loose body — the radiocapitellar recess is in fact one of the classic niches that DOES harbor bodies.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On this sagittal IR/STIR image, trace the radiocapitellar articulation and the posterolateral recess. A fellow sees a thin low-signal synovial fold near the radiocapitellar joint in an otherwise normal elbow and wonders whether to call a loose body. Which interpretation is safest?
text: On this sagittal IR/STIR image, trace the radiocapitellar articulation and the posterolateral recess. A fellow sees a thin low-signal synovial fold near the radiocapitellar joint in an otherwise normal elbow and wonders whether to call a loose body. Which interpretation is safest? | A. A thin posterolateral radiocapitellar plica/fold can be normal; call it clinically relevant only if thickened, edematous, trapped, or matching snapping/catching symptoms | B. Any synovial fold at the radiocapitellar joint is a displaced loose body and should be reported as mechanical | C. The fold is a capitellar OCD instability sign even when the cartilage and subchondral interface are smooth | D. The fold is the distal UCL T-sign and should be evaluated only on coronal MR arthrography | A | A posterolateral radiocapitellar plica/synovial fold is a recognized normal structure when thin and smooth. It becomes a potential pain generator when thickened or edematous and when it matches lateral snapping/catching. A loose body should be a discrete intra-articular fragment, and OCD instability depends on the capitellar cartilage/subchondral interface, not a thin synovial fold.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Which imaging plane is the workhorse of knee MRI, and what structures does it best evaluate?
text: Which imaging plane is the workhorse of knee MRI, and what structures does it best evaluate? | front: Which imaging plane is the workhorse of knee MRI, and what structures does it best evaluate? | back: Sagittal. It is the primary plane for evaluating the cruciate ligaments, the meniscal horns (the "bow-tie" body in cross-section), cartilage surfaces, and the extensor mechanism. (Meniscal body extrusion is measured on coronal.)

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you confirm medial versus lateral orientation on coronal knee MRI images?
text: How do you confirm medial versus lateral orientation on coronal knee MRI images? | front: How do you confirm medial versus lateral orientation on coronal knee MRI images? | back: Identify the fibular head, which is always on the lateral side.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the single most important sequence for knee MRI, and why?
text: What is the single most important sequence for knee MRI, and why? | front: What is the single most important sequence for knee MRI, and why? | back: PD FS (proton density fat-suppressed). Fat suppression removes bright fat signal so edema and fluid stand out. It detects bone marrow edema, meniscal tears, ligament injuries, cartilage defects, and effusion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On T1-weighted images, what does normal marrow look like and what causes it to appear dark?
text: On T1-weighted images, what does normal marrow look like and what causes it to appear dark? | front: On T1-weighted images, what does normal marrow look like and what causes it to appear dark? | back: Normal marrow is fatty and therefore bright on T1. Any process that replaces marrow fat (fracture, tumor, infection, edema) appears dark on T1.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A finding is dark on T1 and bright on PD FS. What does this signal combination represent?
text: A finding is dark on T1 and bright on PD FS. What does this signal combination represent? | front: A finding is dark on T1 and bright on PD FS. What does this signal combination represent? | back: Almost always edema or fluid. A finding bright on both T1 and PD FS could be subacute blood (methemoglobin) or proteinaceous fluid.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is magic angle artifact, and how do you confirm it is not a true meniscal tear?
text: What is magic angle artifact, and how do you confirm it is not a true meniscal tear? | front: What is magic angle artifact, and how do you confirm it is not a true meniscal tear? | back: Magic angle artifact causes increased signal in structures oriented at 55 degrees to the main magnetic field on short TE sequences (T1, PD). It disappears on T2. If meniscal signal on PD disappears on T2, it is artifact, not a tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What artifact produces a linear bright line within the meniscus parallel to its surface, mimicking a horizontal tear?
text: What artifact produces a linear bright line within the meniscus parallel to its surface, mimicking a horizontal tear? | front: What artifact produces a linear bright line within the meniscus parallel to its surface, mimicking a horizontal tear? | back: Truncation (Gibbs) artifact. It runs parallel to, and a fixed short distance from, the meniscal surface and does NOT contact the articular surface (a true tear reaches a surface). Reduce it by increasing the acquisition matrix or changing the phase-encode direction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How should you adjust window/level differently when evaluating menisci versus cartilage?
text: How should you adjust window/level differently when evaluating menisci versus cartilage? | front: How should you adjust window/level differently when evaluating menisci versus cartilage? | back: Widen the window for meniscal evaluation to reveal internal signal changes within the dark meniscus. Narrow the window for cartilage to maximize contrast between the cartilage surface and adjacent fluid.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Name the three compartments of the knee.
text: Name the three compartments of the knee. | front: Name the three compartments of the knee. | back: Medial tibiofemoral, lateral tibiofemoral, and patellofemoral.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the Segond fracture and what is its clinical significance?
text: What is the Segond fracture and what is its clinical significance? | front: What is the Segond fracture and what is its clinical significance? | back: A small avulsion fracture of the lateral tibial rim. It is pathognomonic for ACL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the significance of a tibial eminence fracture in an adolescent?
text: What is the significance of a tibial eminence fracture in an adolescent? | front: What is the significance of a tibial eminence fracture in an adolescent? | back: It represents an avulsion equivalent of an ACL tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why is the medial meniscus more susceptible to tears than the lateral meniscus?
text: Why is the medial meniscus more susceptible to tears than the lateral meniscus? | front: Why is the medial meniscus more susceptible to tears than the lateral meniscus? | back: The medial meniscus is firmly attached to the joint capsule and deep MCL, giving it limited mobility. The lateral meniscus is more circular, more mobile, and detaches from the capsule at the popliteal hiatus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the functional consequence of a meniscal root tear?
text: What is the functional consequence of a meniscal root tear? | front: What is the functional consequence of a meniscal root tear? | back: A root tear is functionally equivalent to total meniscectomy because it eliminates the ability to distribute circumferential hoop stresses.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On sagittal MRI, what is the expected appearance and orientation of a normal ACL?
text: On sagittal MRI, what is the expected appearance and orientation of a normal ACL? | front: On sagittal MRI, what is the expected appearance and orientation of a normal ACL? | back: A taut, low-signal band extending from the lateral femoral condyle to the anterior tibial plateau, oriented approximately parallel to the Blumensaat line (intercondylar roof).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the two layers of the MCL and what structure does the deep layer attach to?
text: What are the two layers of the MCL and what structure does the deep layer attach to? | front: What are the two layers of the MCL and what structure does the deep layer attach to? | back: Superficial MCL (primary valgus stabilizer, extends 5-7 cm below the joint line) and deep MCL (meniscofemoral and meniscotibial ligaments that attach directly to the medial meniscus).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you distinguish the popliteal hiatus from a lateral meniscal tear on sagittal images?
text: How do you distinguish the popliteal hiatus from a lateral meniscal tear on sagittal images? | front: How do you distinguish the popliteal hiatus from a lateral meniscal tear on sagittal images? | back: Look for the round popliteus tendon sitting within the gap. If the tendon is present, it is the normal popliteal hiatus, not a tear. Confirm by following the tendon on consecutive slices.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where do the meniscofemoral ligaments of Humphrey and Wrisberg run in relation to the PCL?
text: Where do the meniscofemoral ligaments of Humphrey and Wrisberg run in relation to the PCL? | front: Where do the meniscofemoral ligaments of Humphrey and Wrisberg run in relation to the PCL? | back: Humphrey passes anterior to the PCL; Wrisberg passes posterior to the PCL. Both run from the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus to the medial femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the three layers of the quadriceps tendon from superficial to deep?
text: What are the three layers of the quadriceps tendon from superficial to deep? | front: What are the three layers of the quadriceps tendon from superficial to deep? | back: Rectus femoris (superficial), vastus medialis and lateralis (middle), and vastus intermedius (deep).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the MPFL and what is its primary function?
text: What is the MPFL and what is its primary function? | front: What is the MPFL and what is its primary function? | back: The medial patellofemoral ligament runs from the adductor tubercle region to the superomedial patella. It is the primary soft tissue restraint preventing lateral patellar translation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why are most missed findings on knee MRI not due to lesion invisibility?
text: Why are most missed findings on knee MRI not due to lesion invisibility? | front: Why are most missed findings on knee MRI not due to lesion invisibility? | back: Studies show that missed findings are rarely invisible. In most cases, the reader simply never looked at the area where the lesion was located. A systematic search pattern forces evaluation of every structure every time.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is satisfaction of search and how does it lead to missed diagnoses?
text: What is satisfaction of search and how does it lead to missed diagnoses? | front: What is satisfaction of search and how does it lead to missed diagnoses? | back: Once you find a significant abnormality, your brain becomes psychologically satisfied and less attentive to additional findings. The antidote is to complete your entire search pattern before constructing the report.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: You correctly diagnose an ACL tear. Name three commonly associated injuries you must still actively search for.
text: You correctly diagnose an ACL tear. Name three commonly associated injuries you must still actively search for. | front: You correctly diagnose an ACL tear. Name three commonly associated injuries you must still actively search for. | back: Lateral meniscal posterior horn tear, medial meniscal ramp lesion, posterolateral corner injury. Also: bone contusions in the lateral compartment and cartilage damage.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: List the 7 steps of the knee MRI search pattern in order.
text: List the 7 steps of the knee MRI search pattern in order. | front: List the 7 steps of the knee MRI search pattern in order. | back: 1) Overall assessment (effusion, alignment, marrow), 2) Bones and marrow, 3) Cartilage and osteochondral surfaces, 4) Menisci, 5) Ligaments, 6) Extensor mechanism and soft tissues, 7) Final review (return to clinical question).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is an anchor slice and what are the three key anchor slices for knee MRI?
text: What is an anchor slice and what are the three key anchor slices for knee MRI? | front: What is an anchor slice and what are the three key anchor slices for knee MRI? | back: An anchor slice is a specific slice where you know normal anatomy and can navigate from. Key anchors: sagittal midline intercondylar notch (cruciates), coronal mid-condylar (meniscal bodies, collaterals), and axial patellofemoral joint (MPFL, trochlea).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you differentiate a bone contusion from a fracture on MRI?
text: How do you differentiate a bone contusion from a fracture on MRI? | front: How do you differentiate a bone contusion from a fracture on MRI? | back: Both show bone marrow edema on fluid-sensitive sequences. A fracture has a discrete low-signal line on T1 within the edema; a contusion does not. This distinction changes weight-bearing status.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the pivot-shift bone contusion pattern and what injury does it suggest?
text: What is the pivot-shift bone contusion pattern and what injury does it suggest? | front: What is the pivot-shift bone contusion pattern and what injury does it suggest? | back: Edema in the posterolateral tibial plateau and the lateral femoral condyle sulcus. This pattern is present in many acute ACL tears and should trigger direct ACL evaluation plus a search for associated meniscal injuries.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the dashboard bone contusion pattern?
text: What is the dashboard bone contusion pattern? | front: What is the dashboard bone contusion pattern? | back: Edema in the anterior tibial plateau from a posterior-directed force on the flexed knee. This pattern is associated with PCL tears.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the classic bone bruise pattern of lateral patellar dislocation?
text: What is the classic bone bruise pattern of lateral patellar dislocation? | front: What is the classic bone bruise pattern of lateral patellar dislocation? | back: Edema on the medial patellar facet and the anterolateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle. This pattern results from the patella impacting the lateral condyle during transient dislocation.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is SIFK (SONK) and how does it differ from OCD?
text: What is SIFK (SONK) and how does it differ from OCD? | front: What is SIFK (SONK) and how does it differ from OCD? | back: SIFK (subchondral insufficiency fracture of the knee) occurs in older patients (typically women >55) with acute-onset pain and shows subchondral low-signal fracture line with surrounding edema, often in the medial femoral condyle. OCD occurs in younger patients (10-25) at the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle with a well-defined osteochondral fragment.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the modified ICRS grades for cartilage loss on MRI?
text: What are the modified ICRS grades for cartilage loss on MRI? | front: What are the modified ICRS grades for cartilage loss on MRI? | back: Grade 1: signal abnormality with intact surface. Grade 2: partial-thickness defect (<50%). Grade 3: partial-thickness defect (>50%) or full-thickness without bone exposure. Grade 4: full-thickness with subchondral bone exposure.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where is the most common location for OCD in the knee?
text: Where is the most common location for OCD in the knee? | front: Where is the most common location for OCD in the knee? | back: The lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle, accounting for approximately 70–80% of cases.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability?
text: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability? | front: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability? | back: A high-signal-intensity rim (fluid signal on T2) surrounding the fragment at the interface with parent bone, indicating fluid undermining the lesion. A low-signal rim on T1 is nonspecific and can represent fibrous healing or a cleavage plane.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Name four MRI signs of OCD instability.
text: Name four MRI signs of OCD instability. | front: Name four MRI signs of OCD instability. | back: Fluid-signal line at fragment-bone interface on T2, fluid-filled cyst beneath the lesion, displaced or partially displaced fragment, and disruption of the overlying articular cartilage surface.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where should you search for loose bodies on knee MRI?
text: Where should you search for loose bodies on knee MRI? | front: Where should you search for loose bodies on knee MRI? | back: Posteromedial and posterolateral recesses, the intercondylar notch, and the suprapatellar pouch. Loose bodies preferentially settle in dependent recesses.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How should you systematically report knee articular cartilage?
text: How should you systematically report knee articular cartilage? | front: How should you systematically report knee articular cartilage? | back: Compartment by compartment — medial and lateral tibiofemoral plus patellofemoral — assessing BOTH surfaces of each. Patellofemoral cartilage is best seen on axial images; the patellar median ridge and lateral facet are common defect sites. The medial compartment bears the greater load, so early loss often shows there first.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What single imaging check separates juvenile from adult OCD, and why does it matter?
text: What single imaging check separates juvenile from adult OCD, and why does it matter? | front: What single imaging check separates juvenile from adult OCD, and why does it matter? | back: The distal femoral physis on coronal images: OPEN physes = juvenile OCD (greater healing potential, more often managed conservatively); CLOSED physes = adult OCD (poorer healing, more often surgical). Caveat: in skeletally immature patients a high-T2 rim alone is unreliable for instability — it can be vascular granulation in a stable lesion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What grade of intrameniscal signal reaches the articular surface?
text: What grade of intrameniscal signal reaches the articular surface? | front: What grade of intrameniscal signal reaches the articular surface? | back: Grade 3. Grade 1 is a globular dot of mucinous degeneration, Grade 2 is linear intrameniscal signal not reaching the surface, and Grade 3 contacts the articular surface (= tear).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On PD / short-TE images, does intrameniscal signal have to be as bright as fluid to call a tear?
text: On PD / short-TE images, does intrameniscal signal have to be as bright as fluid to call a tear? | front: On PD / short-TE images, does intrameniscal signal have to be as bright as fluid to call a tear? | back: No — on PD / short-TE, surface-reaching intermediate-to-high signal is enough; it need not approach fluid intensity. (Requiring fluid-bright signal is the T2 / long-TE criterion, which is why T2 is less sensitive.) The key is signal unequivocally reaching an articular surface on ≥2 consecutive images.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the two-slice-touch rule?
text: What is the two-slice-touch rule? | front: What is the two-slice-touch rule? | back: Meniscal signal must contact the articular surface on at least two consecutive sagittal images (at standard 3-4 mm slice thickness) to be diagnosed as a tear. This reduces false positives from volume averaging and truncation artifact.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why does the two-slice-touch rule require caution at 3T?
text: Why does the two-slice-touch rule require caution at 3T? | front: Why does the two-slice-touch rule require caution at 3T? | back: At 3T, magic angle effect and magnetization transfer can cause artifactually increased intrameniscal signal, particularly in the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus near the popliteal hiatus, mimicking tears.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why are radial meniscal tears biomechanically significant even when small?
text: Why are radial meniscal tears biomechanically significant even when small? | front: Why are radial meniscal tears biomechanically significant even when small? | back: Radial tears disrupt the circumferential hoop fibers of the meniscus, eliminating its ability to distribute axial load. This is true regardless of tear size.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the "ghost meniscus" sign and what tear type does it suggest?
text: What is the "ghost meniscus" sign and what tear type does it suggest? | front: What is the "ghost meniscus" sign and what tear type does it suggest? | back: The meniscus appears absent or truncated on images through the tear. It is seen with radial tears (where the tear is parallel to the imaging plane) and with root tears on sagittal images.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the most common degenerative meniscal tear pattern and where does it typically originate?
text: What is the most common degenerative meniscal tear pattern and where does it typically originate? | front: What is the most common degenerative meniscal tear pattern and where does it typically originate? | back: Horizontal (cleavage) tear, running parallel to the tibial plateau and splitting the meniscus into upper and lower leaves. It most commonly originates in the posterior horn of the medial meniscus.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: A meniscal root tear is functionally equivalent to what procedure?
text: A meniscal root tear is functionally equivalent to what procedure? | front: A meniscal root tear is functionally equivalent to what procedure? | back: Total meniscectomy. Root tears completely disrupt the circumferential hoop stresses that allow the meniscus to distribute axial load.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the root tear triad?
text: What is the root tear triad? | front: What is the root tear triad? | back: Posterior medial root tear + meniscal extrusion (>3 mm) + subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle or tibial plateau. The root tear is the underlying biomechanical cause.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the "ghost sign" on sagittal images in the context of a root tear?
text: What is the "ghost sign" on sagittal images in the context of a root tear? | front: What is the "ghost sign" on sagittal images in the context of a root tear? | back: Absence of the normal dark posterior horn root attachment adjacent to the PCL insertion on sagittal images. It indicates the root has been avulsed or disrupted.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is a ramp lesion and how common is it in ACL-injured knees?
text: What is a ramp lesion and how common is it in ACL-injured knees? | front: What is a ramp lesion and how common is it in ACL-injured knees? | back: A peripheral longitudinal tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus at the meniscocapsular junction. It is found in approximately 16-24% of ACL-deficient knees.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the reported MRI sensitivity for detecting ramp lesions?
text: What is the reported MRI sensitivity for detecting ramp lesions? | front: What is the reported MRI sensitivity for detecting ramp lesions? | back: Only 48-65%. Ramp lesions are notoriously underdiagnosed on preoperative MRI, missed in up to half of cases. In every ACL tear, actively search the posterior meniscocapsular junction.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the three cardinal signs of a bucket-handle tear?
text: What are the three cardinal signs of a bucket-handle tear? | front: What are the three cardinal signs of a bucket-handle tear? | back: Absent bow-tie sign (fewer than two consecutive body sagittal images), double PCL sign (displaced fragment parallel to the PCL), and flipped meniscus sign (fragment adjacent to the anterior horn making it appear disproportionately large).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How many consecutive sagittal images should show the normal bow-tie configuration of the meniscal body?
text: How many consecutive sagittal images should show the normal bow-tie configuration of the meniscal body? | front: How many consecutive sagittal images should show the normal bow-tie configuration of the meniscal body? | back: At least two. Fewer than two consecutive bow-tie images suggests a bucket-handle tear with displacement of the central fragment.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How is meniscal extrusion measured, and what threshold is pathologic?
text: How is meniscal extrusion measured, and what threshold is pathologic? | front: How is meniscal extrusion measured, and what threshold is pathologic? | back: Measured on coronal images at the meniscal mid-body: distance from the outer tibial plateau margin to the outermost meniscal edge. Greater than 3 mm is pathologic; greater than 5 mm suggests severe extrusion with major loss of hoop stress function.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: At what meniscal extrusion threshold should you actively search for a meniscal root tear?
text: At what meniscal extrusion threshold should you actively search for a meniscal root tear? | front: At what meniscal extrusion threshold should you actively search for a meniscal root tear? | back: Extrusion >3 mm is pathologic and should prompt an active search for a meniscal root tear on coronal and axial images; >5 mm indicates severe meniscal dysfunction with major loss of hoop-stress function.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the relationship between parameniscal cysts and meniscal tears?
text: What is the relationship between parameniscal cysts and meniscal tears? | front: What is the relationship between parameniscal cysts and meniscal tears? | back: Most parameniscal cysts have an underlying meniscal tear acting as a one-way valve. The tear allows joint fluid to decompress into parameniscal tissues. The cyst may recur if only the cyst is treated without addressing the causative tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What complication can a large lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head cause?
text: What complication can a large lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head cause? | front: What complication can a large lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head cause? | back: Compression of the common peroneal nerve, presenting with foot drop.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After meniscal repair, what signal criterion distinguishes re-tear from expected postoperative healing?
text: After meniscal repair, what signal criterion distinguishes re-tear from expected postoperative healing? | front: After meniscal repair, what signal criterion distinguishes re-tear from expected postoperative healing? | back: Fluid-bright signal (matching joint fluid on T2) reaching the articular surface indicates re-tear. Intermediate signal at the repair site represents expected healing granulation tissue and should not be overcalled.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After partial meniscectomy, what does the normal meniscal remnant look like on MRI?
text: After partial meniscectomy, what does the normal meniscal remnant look like on MRI? | front: After partial meniscectomy, what does the normal meniscal remnant look like on MRI? | back: Truncated and blunted with a smooth, well-defined free edge and uniformly low signal. Persistent grade 3 signal extending to the new articular surface suggests re-tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the primary MRI signs of an ACL tear?
text: What are the primary MRI signs of an ACL tear? | front: What are the primary MRI signs of an ACL tear? | back: Discontinuity of ligament fibers, abnormal morphology (wavy, lax, or horizontally oriented), and abnormal signal intensity (edema/hemorrhage on T2). A complete tear shows amorphous edematous tissue replacing the ligament.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Name three secondary signs of an ACL tear.
text: Name three secondary signs of an ACL tear. | front: Name three secondary signs of an ACL tear. | back: Pivot-shift bone contusions (posterolateral tibial plateau + lateral femoral condyle sulcus), anterior tibial translation >5 mm, and PCL buckling. Others include deepened lateral femoral sulcus >1.5 mm, Segond fracture, and uncovered posterior horn sign.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the Segond fracture and what is its association with ACL tears?
text: What is the Segond fracture and what is its association with ACL tears? | front: What is the Segond fracture and what is its association with ACL tears? | back: A small avulsion of the lateral tibial rim representing anterolateral ligament avulsion. It is pathognomonic for ACL tear, with ACL injury present in approximately 75-100% of cases.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How does ACL mucoid degeneration differ from an ACL tear on MRI?
text: How does ACL mucoid degeneration differ from an ACL tear on MRI? | front: How does ACL mucoid degeneration differ from an ACL tear on MRI? | back: Mucoid degeneration shows a thickened, bulbous ACL with diffuse T2 signal but intact, continuous fibers ("celery stalk" appearance). Unlike tears, fiber tension and orientation are preserved, and secondary signs (bone contusions, tibial translation) are absent.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the ACL graft ligamentization period, and how does it appear on MRI?
text: What is the ACL graft ligamentization period, and how does it appear on MRI? | front: What is the ACL graft ligamentization period, and how does it appear on MRI? | back: During the first 6-12 months, the graft undergoes revascularization and remodeling with increased T2 signal. This is expected and should not be confused with graft failure. A mature graft (12-24 months) should be uniformly low signal.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the most common technical error leading to ACL graft failure?
text: What is the most common technical error leading to ACL graft failure? | front: What is the most common technical error leading to ACL graft failure? | back: Tunnel malposition. The two mechanisms differ: an anteriorly placed TIBIAL tunnel causes roof (notch) impingement in extension, while a too-anterior or vertical FEMORAL tunnel produces a non-isometric/vertical graft with persistent rotational (pivot-shift) instability. On sagittal MRI, the femoral tunnel should sit in the posterior aspect of the intercondylar notch.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the classic mechanism for a PCL tear?
text: What is the classic mechanism for a PCL tear? | front: What is the classic mechanism for a PCL tear? | back: A posterior-directed force on the proximal tibia with the knee flexed -- the "dashboard injury." Bone contusions are typically on the anterior tibial plateau.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When does a PCL buckle anteriorly, and why is this important to recognize?
text: When does a PCL buckle anteriorly, and why is this important to recognize? | front: When does a PCL buckle anteriorly, and why is this important to recognize? | back: The PCL may buckle anteriorly in the setting of an ACL tear with anterior tibial translation. This should not be confused with a primary PCL injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the three grades of MCL injury and their MRI findings?
text: What are the three grades of MCL injury and their MRI findings? | front: What are the three grades of MCL injury and their MRI findings? | back: Grade I (sprain): periligamentous edema with intact fibers. Grade II (partial tear): partial discontinuity with surrounding edema. Grade III (complete tear): complete fiber disruption with fluid filling the gap.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why is it important to specify whether an MCL injury involves the superficial or deep layer?
text: Why is it important to specify whether an MCL injury involves the superficial or deep layer? | front: Why is it important to specify whether an MCL injury involves the superficial or deep layer? | back: The deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) attaches directly to the medial meniscus. Deep MCL tears can cause meniscocapsular separation, which may require surgical repair -- a different management path than an isolated superficial MCL sprain.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the three primary posterolateral corner (PLC) structures?
text: What are the three primary posterolateral corner (PLC) structures? | front: What are the three primary posterolateral corner (PLC) structures? | back: LCL (fibular collateral ligament), popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the arcuate fracture and what does it indicate?
text: What is the arcuate fracture and what does it indicate? | front: What is the arcuate fracture and what does it indicate? | back: Avulsion of the fibular styloid. It is pathognomonic for a posterolateral corner injury.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why is an unrecognized PLC injury an important, frequently overlooked cause of cruciate graft failure?
text: Why is an unrecognized PLC injury an important, frequently overlooked cause of cruciate graft failure? | front: Why is an unrecognized PLC injury an important, frequently overlooked cause of cruciate graft failure? | back: Unrecognized PLC injury allows persistent external rotation and varus laxity, which overloads cruciate ligament reconstructions. PLC injuries must be identified and addressed for successful graft outcomes.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the primary structure of the posteromedial corner and what instability pattern does PMC injury create?
text: What is the primary structure of the posteromedial corner and what instability pattern does PMC injury create? | front: What is the primary structure of the posteromedial corner and what instability pattern does PMC injury create? | back: The posterior oblique ligament (POL). PMC injury contributes to anteromedial rotatory instability (AMRI), and when combined with ACL tear, creates greater rotational instability than an isolated ACL tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What bone bruise pattern is classic for transient lateral patellar dislocation?
text: What bone bruise pattern is classic for transient lateral patellar dislocation? | front: What bone bruise pattern is classic for transient lateral patellar dislocation? | back: Edema on the medial patellar facet and the anterolateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: At what location is the MPFL most commonly torn?
text: At what location is the MPFL most commonly torn? | front: At what location is the MPFL most commonly torn? | back: At its attachments far more than mid-substance. Classic adult teaching is femoral-sided predominance (~50%, Balcarek 2010), but in skeletally immature patients the patellar attachment predominates (~80-95%, Askenberger 2016) — scrutinize BOTH ends.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What three anatomic risk factors for recurrent patellar dislocation should be assessed?
text: What three anatomic risk factors for recurrent patellar dislocation should be assessed? | front: What three anatomic risk factors for recurrent patellar dislocation should be assessed? | back: Patella alta (Insall-Salvati ratio >1.2), trochlear dysplasia (Dejour classification), and increased TT-TG distance (>20 mm is pathologic).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What combination of ligament tears constitutes a knee dislocation until proven otherwise?
text: What combination of ligament tears constitutes a knee dislocation until proven otherwise? | front: What combination of ligament tears constitutes a knee dislocation until proven otherwise? | back: Combined ACL + PCL disruption, even if the knee has spontaneously reduced. This carries a limb-threatening risk of occult popliteal artery injury, requiring urgent vascular assessment.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What vascular injury must be excluded in a multiligament knee injury, and how should it be assessed?
text: What vascular injury must be excluded in a multiligament knee injury, and how should it be assessed? | front: What vascular injury must be excluded in a multiligament knee injury, and how should it be assessed? | back: Popliteal artery injury must be excluded urgently. MRI signs include loss of the normal flow void, perivascular hematoma, and intimal irregularity; CTA/MRA is used when pulses, ABI, or clinical findings are abnormal or equivocal, or per trauma protocol.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Which layer of the quadriceps tendon is most commonly involved in partial tears?
text: Which layer of the quadriceps tendon is most commonly involved in partial tears? | front: Which layer of the quadriceps tendon is most commonly involved in partial tears? | back: The deep layer (vastus intermedius). On sagittal images, look for a fluid-signal defect at the deep aspect of the tendon near the patellar insertion. The superficial fibers may appear intact.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What finding on MRI distinguishes a complete quadriceps tendon tear from a partial tear?
text: What finding on MRI distinguishes a complete quadriceps tendon tear from a partial tear? | front: What finding on MRI distinguishes a complete quadriceps tendon tear from a partial tear? | back: A complete tear shows full-thickness discontinuity with proximal retraction of the tendon stump, patella baja (low-riding patella), and often a large hemorrhagic joint effusion.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How does patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee) differ from a partial patellar tendon tear on MRI?
text: How does patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee) differ from a partial patellar tendon tear on MRI? | front: How does patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee) differ from a partial patellar tendon tear on MRI? | back: Tendinopathy shows thickening and intermediate signal (not fluid-bright on T2) with intact continuity. A partial tear shows a discrete fluid-signal defect within the tendon. This distinction matters: tendinopathy is managed conservatively; significant partial tears may need surgery.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After bone-patellar tendon-bone graft harvest for ACL reconstruction, what is the expected appearance of the patellar tendon?
text: After bone-patellar tendon-bone graft harvest for ACL reconstruction, what is the expected appearance of the patellar tendon? | front: After bone-patellar tendon-bone graft harvest for ACL reconstruction, what is the expected appearance of the patellar tendon? | back: A thinned central portion of the tendon with surrounding scar tissue. This is a normal postoperative finding and should not be called a tear.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you differentiate bipartite patella from a patellar fracture?
text: How do you differentiate bipartite patella from a patellar fracture? | front: How do you differentiate bipartite patella from a patellar fracture? | back: Bipartite patella has smooth, well-corticated margins, is located at the superolateral pole (~75% of cases), and is bilateral in ~50%. Fractures have sharp, irregular margins, most commonly occur at the mid-patella, and are usually unilateral.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is a painful bipartite patella and what finding indicates it on MRI?
text: What is a painful bipartite patella and what finding indicates it on MRI? | front: What is a painful bipartite patella and what finding indicates it on MRI? | back: When a bipartite patella becomes symptomatic from stress or trauma, bone marrow edema is seen at the synchondrosis between the accessory ossicle and the patella.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What does a lipohemarthrosis indicate and how does it appear on MRI?
text: What does a lipohemarthrosis indicate and how does it appear on MRI? | front: What does a lipohemarthrosis indicate and how does it appear on MRI? | back: Lipohemarthrosis is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture. It appears as a fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa: the superior fat layer is bright on T1, and the inferior layer is serous or hemorrhagic fluid.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When you identify a lipohemarthrosis but no obvious fracture, what should you do?
text: When you identify a lipohemarthrosis but no obvious fracture, what should you do? | front: When you identify a lipohemarthrosis but no obvious fracture, what should you do? | back: Perform a dedicated second-look fracture search of the tibial plateaus, patella, and femoral condyles. Subtle lateral tibial plateau depression fractures are the most commonly missed finding.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where does a Baker's cyst arise and what associated pathology should you search for?
text: Where does a Baker's cyst arise and what associated pathology should you search for? | front: Where does a Baker's cyst arise and what associated pathology should you search for? | back: Baker's cysts arise between the medial head of the gastrocnemius and the semimembranosus tendon posteromedially. They are commonly secondary to intra-articular pathology (meniscal tears, cartilage degeneration, inflammatory arthropathy).

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What clinical condition does a ruptured Baker's cyst mimic?
text: What clinical condition does a ruptured Baker's cyst mimic? | front: What clinical condition does a ruptured Baker's cyst mimic? | back: Deep venous thrombosis (pseudothrombophlebitis). On MRI, a ruptured Baker's cyst shows inferior extension of fluid along the medial gastrocnemius fascial planes.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where is the pes anserine bursa located and what clinical condition does pes anserine bursitis mimic?
text: Where is the pes anserine bursa located and what clinical condition does pes anserine bursitis mimic? | front: Where is the pes anserine bursa located and what clinical condition does pes anserine bursitis mimic? | back: Located approximately 4-5 cm below the medial joint line, deep to the sartorius/gracilis/semitendinosus tendons. It commonly mimics medial meniscal tear or MCL injury clinically.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the hallmark MRI finding of PVNS (tenosynovial giant cell tumor)?
text: What is the hallmark MRI finding of PVNS (tenosynovial giant cell tumor)? | front: What is the hallmark MRI finding of PVNS (tenosynovial giant cell tumor)? | back: Low signal intensity on all pulse sequences due to hemosiderin deposition, with "blooming" artifact on gradient echo sequences. Diffuse PVNS also shows bone erosions on both sides of the joint with relative preservation of joint space.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you distinguish PVNS from synovial chondromatosis on MRI?
text: How do you distinguish PVNS from synovial chondromatosis on MRI? | front: How do you distinguish PVNS from synovial chondromatosis on MRI? | back: PVNS shows low signal on all sequences with GRE blooming (hemosiderin). Synovial chondromatosis shows cartilage-signal bodies (intermediate T1, high T2) without blooming, often with ring-and-arc calcification.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On fluid-sensitive MRI, how do you distinguish a Grade 1 from a Grade 3 muscle strain?
text: On fluid-sensitive MRI, how do you distinguish a Grade 1 from a Grade 3 muscle strain? | front: On fluid-sensitive MRI, how do you distinguish a Grade 1 from a Grade 3 muscle strain? | back: Grade 1: feathery/stellate perifascial edema with intact architecture and NO fiber gap. Grade 3: complete fiber discontinuity with a retracted wavy/serpentine stump and hematoma filling the gap.

course_title: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
