course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: An os acromiale is incidentally identified. Regarding its imaging and clinical significance, which statement is correct?
text: Impingement & acromion | An os acromiale is incidentally identified. Regarding its imaging and clinical significance, which statement is correct? | It represents non-fusion of the basiacromion and is best confirmed on a sagittal-oblique MR sequence | The most common symptomatic type is the meso-acromion, representing non-union at the junction of the meso- and meta-acromion, best seen on axial images | It is always asymptomatic and never contributes to subacromial impingement | It is reliably diagnosed on a standard AP radiograph because the unfused segment lies in the coronal plane | Correct: The most common symptomatic type is the meso-acromion, representing non-union at the junction of the meso- and meta-acromion, best seen on axial images | The acromion ossifies from up to four centers (pre-, meso-, meta-, basi-acromion); failure of fusion at the meso-meta junction (meso-acromion) is the most common type (~75%) and most often symptomatic, best demonstrated on axial images, and a mobile fragment can tilt with deltoid pull to narrow the subacromial space.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 24-year-old competitive powerlifter has chronic anterior shoulder pain. MRI shows subchondral marrow edema and small subchondral cysts confined to the DISTAL CLAVICLE, with preservation of the acromial side, and no marginal osteophytes. Which feature most specifically distinguishes distal clavicular osteolysis from ordinary AC osteoarthrosis?
text: AC joint & distal clavicle | A 24-year-old competitive powerlifter has chronic anterior shoulder pain. MRI shows subchondral marrow edema and small subchondral cysts confined to the DISTAL CLAVICLE, with preservation of the acromial side, and no marginal osteophytes. Which feature most specifically distinguishes distal clavicular osteolysis from ordinary AC osteoarthrosis? | Bilateral involvement of the AC joints | Marrow edema and subchondral resorption predominantly on the clavicular side of the joint | Capsular hypertrophy and joint-space narrowing | Inferior AC joint osteophytes producing supraspinatus outlet narrowing | Correct: Marrow edema and subchondral resorption predominantly on the clavicular side of the joint | Distal clavicular osteolysis (a stress/overuse injury of weightlifters) characteristically produces marrow edema, subchondral microfracture, and resorption that PREDOMINATES on the clavicular side of the AC joint, whereas degenerative osteoarthrosis affects both sides with osteophytes and joint-space narrowing. Clavicular-side predominance is the key discriminator; secondary acromial marrow edema is not uncommon (seen in roughly a third to a half of cases on MRI: de la Puente 1999, Gulati 2025), so true acromial involvement does not exclude the diagnosis.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On shoulder arthrography (or fluid-sensitive MRI), the 'geyser sign' refers to contrast/fluid tracking from the glenohumeral joint through a rotator cuff defect into the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa and then erupting superiorly through an incompetent acromioclavicular (AC) joint capsule. What does this sign most reliably indicate?
text: AC joint & distal clavicle | On shoulder arthrography (or fluid-sensitive MRI), the 'geyser sign' refers to contrast/fluid tracking from the glenohumeral joint through a rotator cuff defect into the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa and then erupting superiorly through an incompetent acromioclavicular (AC) joint capsule. What does this sign most reliably indicate? | An isolated grade I AC sprain with intact capsule | A chronic full-thickness rotator cuff tear allowing fluid to communicate from the glenohumeral joint up through the AC joint | Os acromiale with a synovial pseudarthrosis | Coracoclavicular ligament calcification | Correct: A chronic full-thickness rotator cuff tear allowing fluid to communicate from the glenohumeral joint up through the AC joint | The geyser sign (Craig, 1984) reflects a chronic full-thickness rotator cuff tear: fluid/contrast passes from the glenohumeral joint through the cuff defect into the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa and then erupts superiorly through an incompetent (eroded) AC joint capsule, often forming an AC joint (Type II) cyst. It signals an irreparable cuff with AC erosion, classically seen on arthrography and on fluid-sensitive MRI.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Using the Rockwood classification of AC joint separation, which combination of findings defines a TYPE IV injury and distinguishes it from a Type III?
text: AC joint & distal clavicle | Using the Rockwood classification of AC joint separation, which combination of findings defines a TYPE IV injury and distinguishes it from a Type III? | Sprain of the AC ligament with intact coracoclavicular ligaments and normal alignment | Disruption of both AC and CC ligaments with posterior displacement of the distal clavicle into or through the trapezius | Disruption of both AC and CC ligaments with the clavicle displaced inferiorly beneath the coracoid | Disruption of both AC and CC ligaments with 100–300% superior coracoclavicular widening and superior clavicle displacement | Correct: Disruption of both AC and CC ligaments with posterior displacement of the distal clavicle into or through the trapezius | Type IV involves rupture of both AC and CC ligaments with POSTERIOR displacement of the distal clavicle into/through the trapezius (best appreciated on axial imaging), distinguishing it from the purely superior displacement of Type III. Inferior (subcoracoid) displacement is Type VI; massive 100–300% superior displacement is Type V.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On a non-arthrographic fat-suppressed T2 MRI of a 52-year-old with progressive shoulder stiffness and global motion loss, which combination of findings is the MOST specific for adhesive capsulitis?
text: Capsule, ligaments & adhesive capsulitis | On a non-arthrographic fat-suppressed T2 MRI of a 52-year-old with progressive shoulder stiffness and global motion loss, which combination of findings is the MOST specific for adhesive capsulitis? | T2-hyperintense edema and thickening of the capsule in the axillary recess plus soft-tissue thickening obliterating the subcoracoid fat triangle in the rotator interval | A thickened middle glenohumeral ligament with a sublabral foramen at the anterosuperior labrum | Fluid distending the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa with biceps tendon sheath fluid | Thinning and attenuation of the axillary pouch capsule with a patulous joint recess | Correct: T2-hyperintense edema and thickening of the capsule in the axillary recess plus soft-tissue thickening obliterating the subcoracoid fat triangle in the rotator interval | Adhesive capsulitis shows capsular/synovial THICKENING and T2 edema in the axillary recess plus rotator-interval soft-tissue thickening obliterating the subcoracoid fat triangle; the latter (subcoracoid triangle sign) is highly specific (Mengiardi reported ~100% specificity). Thinning/patulous capsule is the opposite (instability), bursal/biceps-sheath fluid is nonspecific, and a thickened MGHL with a sublabral foramen is a normal anterosuperior variant.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Using the inferior axillary pouch (capsule + synovium) thickness as a quantitative sign of adhesive capsulitis, which threshold best discriminates affected from normal shoulders on MRI?
text: Capsule, ligaments & adhesive capsulitis | Using the inferior axillary pouch (capsule + synovium) thickness as a quantitative sign of adhesive capsulitis, which threshold best discriminates affected from normal shoulders on MRI? | Axillary capsule thickness > 4 mm | Axillary capsule thickness > 1 mm | Axillary capsule thickness > 8 mm | Axillary capsule thickness > 12 mm | Correct: Axillary capsule thickness > 4 mm | A combined capsule-synovium thickness exceeding ~4 mm in the axillary recess is the classic discriminating threshold for adhesive capsulitis (Emig 1995: mean 5.2 mm in affected vs 2.9 mm in normals; >4 mm was 95% specific, 70% sensitive). A >1 mm cutoff overlaps the normal range (~2-3 mm), while >8 mm and >12 mm are too high and insensitive.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Regarding the coracohumeral ligament (CHL) in the rotator interval, which MRI finding most reliably supports adhesive capsulitis rather than a normal interval?
text: Capsule, ligaments & adhesive capsulitis | Regarding the coracohumeral ligament (CHL) in the rotator interval, which MRI finding most reliably supports adhesive capsulitis rather than a normal interval? | CHL thickening greater than approximately 4 mm with enhancement/edema and obliteration of the rotator interval fat | A normal CHL measuring 1-2 mm bridging the supraspinatus and subscapularis | Visualization of the superior glenohumeral ligament forming the medial sling of the biceps pulley | A high-riding biceps tendon with an intact pulley on the sagittal oblique image | Correct: CHL thickening greater than approximately 4 mm with enhancement/edema and obliteration of the rotator interval fat | In adhesive capsulitis the CHL becomes pathologically thickened (>~4 mm) with edema/enhancement and effacement of rotator-interval fat; a thin CHL and a normal biceps pulley/SGHL sling are normal anatomy.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On MR arthrography, a smooth focal outpouching of contrast at the anterior capsule near the level of the subscapularis is identified. Which capsular insertion variant does this MOST likely represent, and why is it important?
text: Capsule, ligaments & adhesive capsulitis | On MR arthrography, a smooth focal outpouching of contrast at the anterior capsule near the level of the subscapularis is identified. Which capsular insertion variant does this MOST likely represent, and why is it important? | A type II/III (medial) capsular insertion variant of the anterior capsule, which is a normal variant and should not be mistaken for a capsular/periosteal stripping injury | A reverse Bankart lesion indicating posterior instability | An ALPSA lesion requiring surgical repair | A HAGL lesion at the humeral attachment of the IGHL | Correct: A type II/III (medial) capsular insertion variant of the anterior capsule, which is a normal variant and should not be mistaken for a capsular/periosteal stripping injury | Anterior capsular insertion is classified into types I-III by how medially the capsule attaches to the scapular neck; a more medial (type II/III) insertion is a normal variant that can mimic, but must be distinguished from, true capsuloperiosteal stripping (e.g., ALPSA). Insertion type alone does not predict instability (Palmer, Radiology 1995).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 34-year-old volleyball player has a posterosuperior paralabral cyst. On MRI the cyst extends from the glenoid margin into the spinoglenoid notch but does NOT reach the suprascapular notch. Which denervation pattern is MOST expected?
text: Nerve entrapment & paralabral cysts | A 34-year-old volleyball player has a posterosuperior paralabral cyst. On MRI the cyst extends from the glenoid margin into the spinoglenoid notch but does NOT reach the suprascapular notch. Which denervation pattern is MOST expected? | Isolated infraspinatus edema/atrophy | Combined supraspinatus and infraspinatus edema/atrophy | Isolated supraspinatus edema/atrophy | Isolated teres minor edema/atrophy | Correct: Isolated infraspinatus edema/atrophy | At the spinoglenoid notch the suprascapular nerve has already given off its supraspinatus branches (which arise proximally near the suprascapular notch), so compression there denervates the infraspinatus alone; more proximal suprascapular-notch lesions affect both muscles. Teres minor is supplied by the axillary nerve.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A pitcher with chronic posterior shoulder pain shows isolated teres minor fatty atrophy on MRI, with a normal infraspinatus and an unremarkable spinoglenoid notch. The MOST likely explanation is:
text: Nerve entrapment & paralabral cysts | A pitcher with chronic posterior shoulder pain shows isolated teres minor fatty atrophy on MRI, with a normal infraspinatus and an unremarkable spinoglenoid notch. The MOST likely explanation is: | Quadrilateral space syndrome (axillary nerve, posterior branch) | Spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst | Suprascapular notch ganglion cyst | Parsonage–Turner involving the long thoracic nerve | Correct: Quadrilateral space syndrome (axillary nerve, posterior branch) | Teres minor is supplied by the posterior branch of the axillary nerve within the quadrilateral space, so isolated teres minor denervation localizes there—classic in overhead throwers. The suprascapular nerve does not innervate teres minor, and a normal infraspinatus with an unremarkable spinoglenoid notch excludes spinoglenoid and suprascapular-notch cysts; the long thoracic nerve supplies serratus anterior, not teres minor.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On a fluid-sensitive sequence, a denervated infraspinatus shows diffuse hyperintensity but preserved bulk and NO fatty infiltration on T1. Compared with established fatty atrophy, this finding indicates:
text: Nerve entrapment & paralabral cysts | On a fluid-sensitive sequence, a denervated infraspinatus shows diffuse hyperintensity but preserved bulk and NO fatty infiltration on T1. Compared with established fatty atrophy, this finding indicates: | A more acute/subacute and potentially reversible stage of denervation | A chronic irreversible process unlikely to recover with decompression | Superimposed acute strain rather than denervation | A normal variant of muscle signal with no clinical significance | Correct: A more acute/subacute and potentially reversible stage of denervation | Denervation edema (T2 hyperintensity without volume loss or T1 fatty change) reflects an earlier, often reversible phase; T1 fatty infiltration and atrophy mark chronic, less recoverable disease.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 41-year-old develops acute shoulder pain followed by weakness; MRI shows diffuse T2 hyperintense edema involving BOTH the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, plus the deltoid, with NO paralabral cyst or mass at either notch. The BEST diagnosis is:
text: Nerve entrapment & paralabral cysts | A 41-year-old develops acute shoulder pain followed by weakness; MRI shows diffuse T2 hyperintense edema involving BOTH the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, plus the deltoid, with NO paralabral cyst or mass at either notch. The BEST diagnosis is: | Parsonage–Turner syndrome (neuralgic amyotrophy) | Spinoglenoid notch ganglion cyst | Quadrilateral space syndrome | Suprascapular notch ganglion cyst | Correct: Parsonage–Turner syndrome (neuralgic amyotrophy) | Multifocal denervation edema crossing several nerve territories—here both suprascapular (supra/infraspinatus) and axillary (deltoid)—without a compressive lesion is the hallmark of Parsonage–Turner. A suprascapular-notch ganglion would give supra+infraspinatus edema but spare the deltoid; a spinoglenoid cyst confines edema to the infraspinatus, and quadrilateral space syndrome confines it to the deltoid (and teres minor). The absence of any cyst/mass and the cross-territory pattern exclude all compressive causes.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A sports-medicine fellow suspects a Perthes lesion in a young thrower with anterior instability, but the standard MR arthrogram in neutral position is equivocal. Which additional maneuver best demonstrates this lesion, and what is the mechanism?
text: Measurements, technique & normal values | A sports-medicine fellow suspects a Perthes lesion in a young thrower with anterior instability, but the standard MR arthrogram in neutral position is equivocal. Which additional maneuver best demonstrates this lesion, and what is the mechanism? | ABER (abduction-external rotation) position, because tension on the inferior glenohumeral ligament lifts the medially synovialized labrum and allows contrast undercutting | FADIR position, because internal rotation loads the posterosuperior labrum against the glenoid | Traction with a weighted hand, because it widens the subacromial space | Intravenous rather than intra-articular contrast, because it improves capsular enhancement | Correct: ABER (abduction-external rotation) position, because tension on the inferior glenohumeral ligament lifts the medially synovialized labrum and allows contrast undercutting | A Perthes lesion is an anteroinferior labral tear whose scapular periosteum stays intact but is medially stripped, so the labrum lies reduced against the glenoid and is often occult on neutral MR arthrography. ABER tensions the anterior band of the inferior glenohumeral ligament, lifting the labroligamentous complex off the glenoid so intra-articular contrast undercuts and reveals the tear. FADIR is a hip/FAI maneuver; subacromial traction doesn't affect the labrum; IV (rather than intra-articular) contrast defeats the joint distension arthrography depends on.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 63-year-old has chronic shoulder weakness. This medial sagittal slice is the scapular-Y view used to grade the rotator cuff muscle bellies in cross-section.
text: Rotator cuff - reparability | A 63-year-old has chronic shoulder weakness. This medial sagittal slice is the scapular-Y view used to grade the rotator cuff muscle bellies in cross-section. | Sagittal T2-FS | Grade fatty atrophy of the cuff muscles (Goutallier-Fuchs), which predicts repairability | Measure tendon gap and retraction to assign a Patte stage | Assess the bicipital groove for an empty groove sign | Evaluate the anteroinferior glenoid for a bony Bankart | Correct: Grade fatty atrophy of the cuff muscles (Goutallier-Fuchs), which predicts repairability | This medial Y-view shows all four cuff bellies (supraspinatus, subscapularis, infraspinatus, teres minor) in cross-section, making it THE plane to grade fatty atrophy with the Goutallier-Fuchs system, which predicts whether a cuff tear is repairable. Patte tendon-gap/retraction is measured on the coronal that follows the tendon to its footprint, the empty-groove sign is an axial finding, and the bony glenoid rim is assessed on the axial - none of which is the purpose of this medial sagittal muscle slice.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 35-year-old has anterior shoulder pain and a positive belly-press test after a fall. On this axial slice, the anterior cuff tendon coursing toward the lesser tuberosity is the subscapularis.
text: Subscapularis & biceps pulley | A 35-year-old has anterior shoulder pain and a positive belly-press test after a fall. On this axial slice, the anterior cuff tendon coursing toward the lesser tuberosity is the subscapularis. | Axial PD-FS | The long head of the biceps may subluxate or dislocate medially out of its groove | The supraspinatus footprint will show a full-thickness gap | The posterior labrum will be torn (reverse Bankart) | The acromion will show a type III hooked morphology | Correct: The long head of the biceps may subluxate or dislocate medially out of its groove | This is the subscapularis tendon; a tear here disrupts the medial wall of the biceps pulley, so the most specifically associated finding is medial subluxation or dislocation of the long head of the biceps out of the bicipital groove - the companion clue to a subscapularis tear. A supraspinatus footprint gap, a posterior (reverse Bankart) labral tear, and acromial hooking are not the direct consequence of a subscapularis tendon tear.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 23-year-old sustained an anterior shoulder dislocation in a wrestling match and now has apprehension. On this axial slice (anterior at the top), the dark triangle at the anterior glenoid rim is the anterior labrum.
text: Instability - Bankart & Hill-Sachs | A 23-year-old sustained an anterior shoulder dislocation in a wrestling match and now has apprehension. On this axial slice (anterior at the top), the dark triangle at the anterior glenoid rim is the anterior labrum. | Axial PD-FS | A posterosuperolateral humeral head impaction (Hill-Sachs lesion) | A superior labral tear extending into the biceps anchor (SLAP) | An os acromiale at the meso-meta junction | Distal clavicular osteolysis | Correct: A posterosuperolateral humeral head impaction (Hill-Sachs lesion) | A torn anteroinferior labrum (Bankart lesion) from anterior dislocation is classically paired with a Hill-Sachs lesion, the impaction fracture of the posterosuperolateral humeral head where it strikes the anterior glenoid rim. A SLAP tear involves the superior labral-biceps anchor (a different mechanism, overhead athletes); os acromiale and distal clavicular osteolysis are unrelated to the instability event shown here.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 55-year-old has chronic shoulder pain and weakness. On this coronal slice, the thin plane between the rotator cuff and the overlying acromion/deltoid is the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa.
text: Rotator cuff - full-thickness tear | A 55-year-old has chronic shoulder pain and weakness. On this coronal slice, the thin plane between the rotator cuff and the overlying acromion/deltoid is the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. | Coronal T2-FS | Combined with joint fluid, distension of this bursa suggests a full-thickness cuff tear | Isolated distension here is diagnostic of adhesive capsulitis | Fluid here indicates a SLAP tear at the biceps anchor | Fluid here localizes a quadrilateral-space syndrome | Correct: Combined with joint fluid, distension of this bursa suggests a full-thickness cuff tear | This is the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa; communicating fluid in BOTH this bursa and the glenohumeral joint strongly suggests a full-thickness rotator cuff tear that lets fluid pass from the joint into the bursa. A trace of isolated bursal fluid is nonspecific and does not diagnose a full-thickness tear or frozen shoulder (which thickens the axillary recess capsule); the bursa is unrelated to the superior labral anchor (SLAP) or the quadrilateral space.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 52-year-old diabetic woman has months of painful global stiffness with no trauma. On this coronal slice, the dependent inferior pouch of the joint capsule is the axillary recess.
text: Adhesive capsulitis | A 52-year-old diabetic woman has months of painful global stiffness with no trauma. On this coronal slice, the dependent inferior pouch of the joint capsule is the axillary recess. | Coronal T2-FS | Capsular thickening and edema here support adhesive capsulitis | Fluid distension here is a normal finding requiring no follow-up | Thickening here indicates a full-thickness supraspinatus tear | Edema here localizes a spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst | Correct: Capsular thickening and edema here support adhesive capsulitis | This is the axillary recess; thickening and T2 edema of the axillary recess capsule (together with rotator-interval soft-tissue thickening that obliterates the subcoracoid fat) is the MRI signature of adhesive capsulitis, fitting this diabetic patient with non-traumatic global stiffness. The axillary recess is not where a supraspinatus tear or a spinoglenoid notch cyst is assessed, and capsular thickening here is not a normal finding to ignore.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 60-year-old with a chronic cuff tear is being evaluated for repair. This sagittal T1 scapular-Y slice shows the four cuff muscle bellies; the muscle filling the supraspinous fossa above the scapular spine is the supraspinatus.
text: Rotator cuff - Goutallier atrophy | A 60-year-old with a chronic cuff tear is being evaluated for repair. This sagittal T1 scapular-Y slice shows the four cuff muscle bellies; the muscle filling the supraspinous fossa above the scapular spine is the supraspinatus. | Sagittal T1 | High-grade fatty infiltration here predicts an irreparable tear | Bright marrow signal here on T1 indicates marrow edema | This slice is best for measuring the acromiohumeral interval | This T1 slice best demonstrates a SLAP tear of the biceps anchor | Correct: High-grade fatty infiltration here predicts an irreparable tear | The supraspinatus fills the supraspinous fossa, and T1 (fat bright, unsuppressed) is the sequence to grade Goutallier-Fuchs fatty infiltration; high-grade fatty atrophy of the supraspinatus predicts an irreparable tear and changes surgical planning. Bright signal in MUSCLE on T1 reflects fatty infiltration, not marrow edema (which is DARK on T1); the acromiohumeral interval is a radiographic/coronal measurement, and a SLAP tear is a labral lesion best shown on fluid-sensitive or arthrographic sequences, not this T1 muscle slice.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 48-year-old overhead athlete has lateral shoulder pain and weakness. On this coronal slice, follow the supraspinatus tendon to the greater-tuberosity footprint and separate the thin distal crescent from the thicker rotator cable just medial to it. Why does that distinction matter when a small articular-sided tear is present?
text: Rotator cuff - cable/crescent | A 48-year-old overhead athlete has lateral shoulder pain and weakness. On this coronal slice, follow the supraspinatus tendon to the greater-tuberosity footprint and separate the thin distal crescent from the thicker rotator cable just medial to it. Why does that distinction matter when a small articular-sided tear is present? | Coronal T2-FS | Cable integrity helps determine functional importance; an intact cable may preserve load transfer, while cable disruption makes a small-appearing tear more consequential | The cable is the same structure as the superior labrum, so its injury is reported as a SLAP tear | A crescent tear is diagnosed only on axial imaging because the coronal cannot show the footprint | The cable/crescent distinction applies only after surgery and should be ignored on the initial MRI | Correct: Cable integrity helps determine functional importance; an intact cable may preserve load transfer, while cable disruption makes a small-appearing tear more consequential | The rotator cable/crescent model matters because the cable is a load-sharing band just medial to the thin distal cuff crescent. A limited crescent tear with an intact cable can behave differently from a tear that disrupts the cable, especially anteriorly, so the report should not describe only the footprint gap and retraction.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A fellow reviewing a shoulder MRI wants to assess the subscapularis tendon at its lesser-tuberosity insertion and confirm that the long head of the biceps tendon is seated within the bicipital groove. Which imaging plane is best suited for evaluating both of these structures together?
text: A fellow reviewing a shoulder MRI wants to assess the subscapularis tendon at its lesser-tuberosity insertion and confirm that the long head of the biceps tendon is seated within the bicipital groove. Which imaging plane is best suited for evaluating both of these structures together? | A. Axial | B. Oblique coronal | C. Oblique sagittal | D. Abduction-external-rotation (ABER) oblique | Correct: A | The axial plane displays the subscapularis tendon in cross-section at its lesser-tuberosity insertion and shows the long head of biceps within the bicipital groove, making it the workhorse plane for both. The oblique coronal plane is optimized for the supraspinatus and superior labrum and the oblique sagittal plane for muscle bulk and the cuff arch in profile, so choosing either mismatches a structure to a non-optimal plane; the ABER view is a problem-solving sequence for anteroinferior labral lesions, not a routine plane for the groove and subscapularis. | orientation-search | shoulder-mri-orientation

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: During a systematic search, a fellow wants to grade fatty infiltration and muscle bulk of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus bellies (Goutallier/Warner assessment) on a shoulder MRI. Which plane is the standard reference for this evaluation?
text: During a systematic search, a fellow wants to grade fatty infiltration and muscle bulk of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus bellies (Goutallier/Warner assessment) on a shoulder MRI. Which plane is the standard reference for this evaluation? | A. Axial | B. Oblique coronal | C. Oblique sagittal | D. True (non-oblique) coronal | Correct: C | The oblique sagittal plane (the 'Y' view, just medial to the glenoid) profiles the cuff muscle bellies within the supraspinatus and infraspinatus fossae, which is where fatty infiltration and atrophy are graded. The oblique coronal plane best depicts the supraspinatus tendon and footprint rather than muscle bulk, the axial plane is optimized for the subscapularis and biceps groove, and a true coronal is not angled to the scapular plane, so selecting those confuses tendon/insertion or non-aligned planes with the muscle-belly plane. | orientation-search | shoulder-mri-orientation

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 52-year-old with chronic shoulder pain has an MRI showing a thickened supraspinatus tendon with diffuse intermediate (not fluid-bright) signal on the fat-saturated T2 images, but no focal fluid-bright defect reaching either the articular or bursal surface. What does this finding most likely represent?
text: A 52-year-old with chronic shoulder pain has an MRI showing a thickened supraspinatus tendon with diffuse intermediate (not fluid-bright) signal on the fat-saturated T2 images, but no focal fluid-bright defect reaching either the articular or bursal surface. What does this finding most likely represent? | A. Articular-surface partial-thickness tear | B. Tendinosis (intermediate-signal degeneration) | C. Bursal-surface partial-thickness tear | D. Full-thickness tear with joint-to-bursa communication | Correct: B | Tendon thickening with intermediate (not fluid-bright) signal that does not reach a surface is the classic appearance of tendinosis, reflecting myxoid/mucoid degeneration rather than a fiber gap. A partial tear requires focal fluid-bright signal reaching the articular or bursal surface, and a full-thickness tear requires a fluid-bright defect spanning the entire tendon with joint-to-bursa communication; calling intermediate non-surfacing signal a 'tear' is the most common over-reading error. | rotator-cuff | shoulder-rotator-cuff

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 60-year-old with weakness has an MRI showing a fluid-bright gap through the full thickness of the supraspinatus tendon, with high T2 signal continuous from the glenohumeral joint into the overlying subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. Which interpretation best fits this finding?
text: A 60-year-old with weakness has an MRI showing a fluid-bright gap through the full thickness of the supraspinatus tendon, with high T2 signal continuous from the glenohumeral joint into the overlying subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. Which interpretation best fits this finding? | A. Tendinosis (intermediate-signal degeneration) | B. Intrasubstance partial-thickness tear | C. Articular-surface partial-thickness tear | D. Full-thickness tear with joint-to-bursa communication | Correct: D | A fluid-bright defect spanning the entire tendon thickness with continuity of fluid signal between the joint and the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa defines a full-thickness tear. Tendinosis shows intermediate, non-fluid signal without a gap, and an articular-surface or intrasubstance partial tear involves only part of the tendon thickness and does not create joint-to-bursa communication, so those choices underestimate a defect that clearly traverses the whole tendon. | rotator-cuff | shoulder-rotator-cuff

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: An overhead-throwing athlete has a partial supraspinatus tear. On the oblique coronal images, the fluid-bright defect reaches the deep (inferior) surface of the tendon at the footprint, while the superior (bursal) fibers remain intact. How is this partial tear best classified?
text: An overhead-throwing athlete has a partial supraspinatus tear. On the oblique coronal images, the fluid-bright defect reaches the deep (inferior) surface of the tendon at the footprint, while the superior (bursal) fibers remain intact. How is this partial tear best classified? | A. Bursal-sided tear | B. Intrasubstance (interstitial) tear | C. Articular-sided (PASTA) tear | D. Full-thickness tear | Correct: C | A defect reaching the deep/articular surface at the footprint with intact bursal fibers is an articular-sided partial tear, the PASTA lesion (partial articular-sided supraspinatus tendon avulsion), which is the most common partial tear and typical in throwers. A bursal-sided tear reaches the superior surface, and an intrasubstance tear is confined within the tendon touching neither surface, so those answers misassign which surface the fluid contacts. | rotator-cuff | shoulder-rotator-cuff

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 48-year-old laborer with impingement symptoms has a partial supraspinatus tear. On fat-saturated images the fluid-bright defect reaches the superior surface of the tendon abutting the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa, while the deep articular fibers at the footprint remain intact. What is the correct classification?
text: A 48-year-old laborer with impingement symptoms has a partial supraspinatus tear. On fat-saturated images the fluid-bright defect reaches the superior surface of the tendon abutting the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa, while the deep articular fibers at the footprint remain intact. What is the correct classification? | A. Articular-sided (PASTA) tear | B. Bursal-sided tear | C. Intrasubstance (interstitial) tear | D. Full-thickness tear | Correct: B | A partial tear whose fluid-bright defect reaches the superior surface adjacent to the subacromial bursa, with intact deep articular fibers, is a bursal-sided tear, classically associated with extrinsic impingement. An articular-sided (PASTA) tear contacts the deep surface instead, and an intrasubstance tear reaches neither surface; choosing those reverses or ignores which surface the defect contacts. | rotator-cuff | shoulder-rotator-cuff

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 67-year-old has a full-thickness supraspinatus tear. On the oblique sagittal images the supraspinatus muscle shows more fat than muscle (Goutallier grade 3-4), and on coronal images the tendon stump is retracted to the level of the glenoid. Which feature most strongly predicts poor reparability and should prompt earlier surgical discussion?
text: A 67-year-old has a full-thickness supraspinatus tear. On the oblique sagittal images the supraspinatus muscle shows more fat than muscle (Goutallier grade 3-4), and on coronal images the tendon stump is retracted to the level of the glenoid. Which feature most strongly predicts poor reparability and should prompt earlier surgical discussion? | A. Goutallier grade 3-4 fatty infiltration with retraction to the glenoid | B. A small rim of reactive subacromial bursal fluid | C. Mild tendinosis of the adjacent infraspinatus | D. A type II (curved) acromion | Correct: A | Advanced Goutallier 3-4 fatty infiltration combined with high-grade retraction (Patte stage III, to the glenoid) and muscle atrophy is largely irreversible and predicts poor healing and re-tear, so it argues for earlier surgical referral before further degeneration. Reactive bursal fluid, mild adjacent tendinosis, and acromial shape influence symptoms but are not the dominant reparability/prognosis drivers, so they are plausible but incorrect markers of irreparability. | rotator-cuff | shoulder-rotator-cuff

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 65-year-old has a full-thickness infraspinatus and supraspinatus tear. The surgeon asks which single imaging feature most strongly predicts that a durable repair is unlikely and warrants earlier surgical discussion. Which feature carries that prognostic weight?
text: A 65-year-old has a full-thickness infraspinatus and supraspinatus tear. The surgeon asks which single imaging feature most strongly predicts that a durable repair is unlikely and warrants earlier surgical discussion. Which feature carries that prognostic weight? | A. A moderate subacromial-subdeltoid bursal effusion | B. A type III (hooked) acromion on the oblique sagittal images | C. Mild tendinosis of the intact subscapularis | D. Goutallier grade 3-4 fatty infiltration with muscle atrophy and retraction to the glenoid | Correct: D | Advanced Goutallier 3-4 fatty infiltration with muscle atrophy and high-grade (Patte III) retraction to the glenoid reflects poor muscle-tendon quality that is largely irreversible and predicts low healing and high re-tear rates, so it drives earlier surgical discussion. A bursal effusion is nonspecific, a hooked acromion relates to impingement risk rather than tendon reparability, and mild subscapularis tendinosis is incidental, so each is a real but non-dominant finding that does not determine reparability. | rotator-cuff | shoulder-rotator-cuff

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 55-year-old has an MRI showing a tear of the upper subscapularis tendon at the lesser tuberosity. On the axial images the long head of the biceps tendon is no longer within the bicipital groove and instead lies medially, displaced over the torn subscapularis. This medial biceps displacement is best interpreted as which of the following?
text: A 55-year-old has an MRI showing a tear of the upper subscapularis tendon at the lesser tuberosity. On the axial images the long head of the biceps tendon is no longer within the bicipital groove and instead lies medially, displaced over the torn subscapularis. This medial biceps displacement is best interpreted as which of the following? | A. A normal variant biceps position seen with a shallow groove | B. An indirect sign of a biceps pulley lesion associated with the subscapularis tear | C. An isolated supraspinatus tear without pulley involvement | D. Mucoid degeneration of the intra-articular biceps tendon | Correct: B | The subscapularis tendon contributes to the medial wall of the biceps pulley/reflection, so an upper subscapularis tear frequently disrupts the pulley and allows the biceps to sublux medially out of the groove, a recognized indirect sign of subscapularis/pulley injury. A shallow groove alone does not displace a seated tendon over a torn subscapularis, supraspinatus tears do not explain medial biceps subluxation, and mucoid degeneration alters signal rather than position, so those distractors miss the pulley mechanism. | subscapularis-biceps | shoulder-biceps-interval-ac

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A fellow notices on axial images that the long head of the biceps tendon has subluxed medially and appears to lie deep to (medial to) the lesser tuberosity. Recognizing the anatomic relationship of the biceps pulley, which additional tendon should be scrutinized most closely for an associated tear?
text: A fellow notices on axial images that the long head of the biceps tendon has subluxed medially and appears to lie deep to (medial to) the lesser tuberosity. Recognizing the anatomic relationship of the biceps pulley, which additional tendon should be scrutinized most closely for an associated tear? | A. Infraspinatus | B. Teres minor | C. Subscapularis | D. Supraspinatus | Correct: C | The subscapularis tendon forms the medial sling of the biceps pulley, so medial subluxation of the biceps out of the groove should trigger close evaluation of the subscapularis, which is commonly torn in this pattern. The infraspinatus and teres minor are posterior cuff and do not stabilize the biceps medially, and an isolated supraspinatus tear does not produce medial biceps subluxation, so those choices overlook the pulley's medial anatomy. | subscapularis-biceps | shoulder-biceps-interval-ac

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 24-year-old overhead athlete has an MR arthrogram for clicking and deep pain. At the superior labrum, contrast tracks into a cleft that is smooth, oriented medially, parallels the glenoid rim, and stops at the biceps anchor without lateral extension. Which interpretation is best supported?
text: A 24-year-old overhead athlete has an MR arthrogram for clicking and deep pain. At the superior labrum, contrast tracks into a cleft that is smooth, oriented medially, parallels the glenoid rim, and stops at the biceps anchor without lateral extension. Which interpretation is best supported? | A. Type II SLAP tear | B. Anterosuperior sublabral foramen | C. Normal sublabral recess | D. Bucket-handle labral tear | Correct: C | A sublabral recess is a normal variant: a smooth, medially oriented cleft that parallels the glenoid and stops at the biceps anchor without lateral extension or irregular margins, exactly as described. A type II SLAP tear would show contrast extending laterally into the labral substance with irregular margins posterior to the anchor, and a bucket-handle tear shows a displaced labral fragment; calling this smooth medial recess a tear is the most common over-reading error, while a sublabral foramen is an anterosuperior (not superior-at-the-anchor) variant. | labrum-slap | shoulder-labrum-instability

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 27-year-old pitcher has an MR arthrogram. At the superior labrum, gadolinium extends laterally into the labral substance with irregular margins and tracks posterior to the biceps anchor. Which interpretation is best supported?
text: A 27-year-old pitcher has an MR arthrogram. At the superior labrum, gadolinium extends laterally into the labral substance with irregular margins and tracks posterior to the biceps anchor. Which interpretation is best supported? | A. Normal sublabral recess | B. Type II SLAP tear | C. Normal sublabral foramen | D. Mucoid degeneration of the labrum | Correct: B | Lateral extension into the labral substance, irregular margins, and tracking posterior to the biceps anchor distinguish a SLAP tear from a normal recess, which is smooth, medially oriented, and ends at the anchor. A sublabral recess and a sublabral foramen are both smooth variants that follow the glenoid rather than extending irregularly into the labrum, so labeling this finding either of those is the classic benign-variant pitfall; mucoid degeneration produces ill-defined intrasubstance signal without a discrete margin-irregular contrast-filled cleft. | labrum-slap | shoulder-labrum-instability

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 30-year-old with recurrent anterior dislocations has an MR arthrogram. The anteroinferior labroligamentous complex is displaced medially and 'rolled up' on the glenoid neck along an intact but stripped scapular periosteum. Which lesion does this describe?
text: A 30-year-old with recurrent anterior dislocations has an MR arthrogram. The anteroinferior labroligamentous complex is displaced medially and 'rolled up' on the glenoid neck along an intact but stripped scapular periosteum. Which lesion does this describe? | A. Bankart lesion, with a disrupted anterior scapular periosteum | B. ALPSA lesion, with the complex rolled up medially on an intact stripped periosteum | C. Perthes lesion, with an intact periosteum and a non-displaced labrum resynovialized in place | D. HAGL lesion, with avulsion of the inferior glenohumeral ligament from the humerus | Correct: B | ALPSA (anterior labroligamentous periosteal sleeve avulsion) is medial displacement of the labroligamentous sleeve along an intact but stripped periosteum, classically chronic/recurrent with the labrum 'rolled up' on the glenoid neck. A Bankart has a torn periosteum so the labrum is not held medially, a Perthes keeps the periosteum intact but the labrum stays anatomically positioned rather than rolled up medially, and a HAGL is a humeral-sided ligament avulsion rather than a labral lesion. | instability | shoulder-labrum-instability

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 22-year-old has anterior instability. On MR arthrogram the inferior glenohumeral ligament is avulsed from its humeral neck attachment, the axillary pouch takes on a 'J' configuration, and contrast leaks extra-articularly on the humeral side. The anteroinferior labrum is intact. Which lesion is this?
text: A 22-year-old has anterior instability. On MR arthrogram the inferior glenohumeral ligament is avulsed from its humeral neck attachment, the axillary pouch takes on a 'J' configuration, and contrast leaks extra-articularly on the humeral side. The anteroinferior labrum is intact. Which lesion is this? | A. Bankart lesion, with anteroinferior labral avulsion from the glenoid | B. GLAD lesion, with a glenolabral articular cartilage defect and intact ligaments | C. Perthes lesion, with a non-displaced anteroinferior labral tear on an intact periosteum | D. HAGL lesion, with humeral avulsion of the inferior glenohumeral ligament and a J-shaped axillary pouch | Correct: D | A HAGL lesion is humeral-sided avulsion of the inferior glenohumeral ligament, producing a 'J-sign' of the axillary pouch and an extra-articular contrast leak on the humeral side, with the glenoid labrum intact. A Bankart and a Perthes are glenoid/labral-sided lesions and a GLAD is a glenolabral cartilage injury, so all three represent the classic pitfall of fixating on the glenoid side while missing the humeral-sided ligament avulsion; none produces a humeral contrast leak or a J-shaped pouch. | instability | shoulder-labrum-instability

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 26-year-old has had four anterior dislocations. MRI suggests substantial anterior glenoid bone loss and a Hill-Sachs lesion that appears to engage. The surgeon wants precise osseous quantification to decide between an arthroscopic soft-tissue repair and a Latarjet. What is the most appropriate next step?
text: A 26-year-old has had four anterior dislocations. MRI suggests substantial anterior glenoid bone loss and a Hill-Sachs lesion that appears to engage. The surgeon wants precise osseous quantification to decide between an arthroscopic soft-tissue repair and a Latarjet. What is the most appropriate next step? | A. Proceed to arthroscopic Bankart repair on clinical grounds alone | B. Obtain a radionuclide bone scan of the glenoid rim | C. Repeat MRI with intra-articular gadolinium to re-image the labrum | D. Obtain CT to quantify glenoid bone loss and the Hill-Sachs track | Correct: D | When MRI suggests significant glenoid bone loss (approaching ~20% or more) or an engaging/off-track Hill-Sachs, CT — often 3D with the humeral head digitally subtracted — best quantifies the osseous defect and drives the choice between soft-tissue repair and a bony procedure such as Latarjet. Proceeding to isolated Bankart repair ignores that high bone loss predicts soft-tissue-repair failure, a bone scan gives no quantification of bone loss, and an MR arthrogram characterizes soft tissue but underestimates osseous defects compared with CT. | instability | shoulder-labrum-instability

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 19-year-old collision-sport athlete has recurrent anterior dislocations. CT shows ~25% anterior glenoid bone loss and an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion. Based on these osseous findings, which surgical strategy is most appropriate?
text: A 19-year-old collision-sport athlete has recurrent anterior dislocations. CT shows ~25% anterior glenoid bone loss and an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion. Based on these osseous findings, which surgical strategy is most appropriate? | A. Isolated arthroscopic Bankart repair, the labrum being the primary stabilizer | B. Nonoperative management with bracing and activity modification | C. A bony augmentation procedure such as Latarjet to restore the deficient glenoid arc | D. Arthroscopic capsular plication to retension the anterior capsule | Correct: C | Critical glenoid bone loss (around 20-25% or more) with an off-track/engaging Hill-Sachs predicts high failure rates for soft-tissue repair, so a bony procedure such as Latarjet — which lengthens the glenoid arc and adds a sling effect — is favored. Isolated Bankart repair reflects the misconception that labral repair suffices despite bone loss, bracing wrongly assumes bone loss is irrelevant to recurrence, and capsular plication addresses soft tissue but does not restore the deficient bony track. | instability | shoulder-labrum-instability

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 54-year-old with diabetes has months of progressive global stiffness and a marked loss of passive external rotation. Which MRI finding most specifically supports adhesive capsulitis rather than a rotator cuff tear?
text: A 54-year-old with diabetes has months of progressive global stiffness and a marked loss of passive external rotation. Which MRI finding most specifically supports adhesive capsulitis rather than a rotator cuff tear? | A. Fluid-bright signal spanning the supraspinatus from articular to bursal surface | B. Thickening and edema of the coracohumeral ligament and rotator interval with obliteration of the subcoracoid fat triangle | C. Intermediate signal and thickening of the supraspinatus tendon without a surface defect | D. Fluid in the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa | Correct: B | Adhesive capsulitis shows soft-tissue thickening and edema at the rotator interval and coracohumeral ligament with loss of the normal subcoracoid fat triangle, plus axillary pouch/inferior capsule thickening — findings that explain the global loss of motion. Fluid-bright transmural supraspinatus signal describes a full-thickness cuff tear (the mimic to be excluded), intermediate non-surfacing tendon signal describes tendinosis, and bursal fluid is nonspecific and does not indicate capsulitis. | capsule-adhesive | shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 49-year-old has 4 months of painful shoulder stiffness limiting motion in all planes. MRI is ordered to distinguish adhesive capsulitis from a cuff problem. Which constellation of findings is most specific for adhesive capsulitis?
text: A 49-year-old has 4 months of painful shoulder stiffness limiting motion in all planes. MRI is ordered to distinguish adhesive capsulitis from a cuff problem. Which constellation of findings is most specific for adhesive capsulitis? | A. Retraction of the supraspinatus to the glenoid with Goutallier grade 3 fatty infiltration | B. A laterally extending superior labral contrast cleft with irregular margins | C. Thickening and edema of the axillary pouch and inferior glenohumeral ligament with rotator-interval and coracohumeral ligament thickening | D. Isolated subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid without capsular changes | Correct: C | The specific MRI signature of adhesive capsulitis is thickening and edema of the axillary pouch/inferior glenohumeral ligament combined with rotator-interval and coracohumeral ligament thickening and obliteration of the subcoracoid fat triangle, often with capsular enhancement. Supraspinatus retraction with fatty infiltration describes a chronic cuff tear (the mimic), a laterally extending irregular superior labral cleft describes a SLAP tear, and isolated bursal fluid is nonspecific and does not establish capsulitis. | capsule-adhesive | shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 35-year-old volleyball player has posterior shoulder weakness. MRI shows a posterosuperior labral tear with a paralabral cyst extending into the spinoglenoid notch. Which additional finding best explains the weakness and should be reported?
text: A 35-year-old volleyball player has posterior shoulder weakness. MRI shows a posterosuperior labral tear with a paralabral cyst extending into the spinoglenoid notch. Which additional finding best explains the weakness and should be reported? | A. Denervation of both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, from compression at the suprascapular notch | B. Denervation of the deltoid, from axillary nerve compression in the quadrilateral space | C. Denervation edema or fatty atrophy of the infraspinatus alone, from suprascapular nerve compression at the spinoglenoid notch | D. Denervation of the subscapularis, from upper subscapular nerve involvement | Correct: C | A cyst at the spinoglenoid notch compresses the suprascapular nerve after its branch to the supraspinatus has departed, so denervation is isolated to the infraspinatus (edema acutely, fatty atrophy chronically). Denervation of both muscles is the more proximal suprascapular-notch pattern, and deltoid or subscapularis denervation involves different nerves (axillary, subscapular) not affected by a spinoglenoid notch cyst; the teaching point is to look beyond the labrum to muscle denervation signal. | nerve-entrapment | shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 41-year-old has shoulder pain and weakness of both abduction and external rotation. MRI shows a paralabral cyst arising from a superior labral tear that extends medially into the suprascapular notch. Which pattern of muscle denervation do you most expect?
text: A 41-year-old has shoulder pain and weakness of both abduction and external rotation. MRI shows a paralabral cyst arising from a superior labral tear that extends medially into the suprascapular notch. Which pattern of muscle denervation do you most expect? | A. Isolated infraspinatus denervation, the cyst lying distal to the supraspinatus branch | B. Isolated teres minor denervation, from axillary nerve compression | C. Isolated supraspinatus denervation, the cyst sparing the infraspinatus branch | D. Denervation of both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, the cyst compressing the suprascapular nerve at the suprascapular notch | Correct: D | A cyst at the suprascapular notch compresses the nerve proximal to both motor branches, so both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus show denervation, matching the combined abduction and external-rotation weakness. Isolated infraspinatus denervation is the more distal spinoglenoid-notch pattern, isolated supraspinatus denervation inverts the anatomy because a proximal lesion cannot spare the infraspinatus, and teres minor denervation involves a different nerve; the key is to localize the cyst along the nerve's course. | nerve-entrapment | shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Five months after supraspinatus repair, a patient has persistent pain. On fluid-sensitive images the repaired tendon shows intermediate-to-heterogeneous signal and mild thickening, with a little subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid and focal susceptibility artifact near the anchors. How should this be interpreted?
text: Five months after supraspinatus repair, a patient has persistent pain. On fluid-sensitive images the repaired tendon shows intermediate-to-heterogeneous signal and mild thickening, with a little subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid and focal susceptibility artifact near the anchors. How should this be interpreted? | A. Recurrent full-thickness tear, because persistent intratendinous signal indicates failure | B. Recurrent full-thickness tear, because subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid confirms a gap | C. Recurrent full-thickness tear, because anchor susceptibility artifact represents a tendon defect | D. Expected postoperative change, because heterogeneous signal, mild bursal fluid, and anchor artifact are normal here | Correct: D | Repaired cuff tendons normally remain thickened and heterogeneous with intermediate-to-increased signal for many months, and mild bursal fluid and anchor susceptibility artifact are expected, so none of these indicates failure. Options A, B, and C each reflect the common error of overcalling a recurrent tear from nonspecific postoperative signal, reactive bursal fluid, or hardware artifact; a recurrent full-thickness tear instead requires a fluid-bright gap spanning the tendon with fluid communication. | postoperative | shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Seven months after rotator cuff repair, a patient has recurrent pain and weakness. On fluid-sensitive fat-saturated images there is a fluid-bright (fluid-equivalent) defect spanning the full thickness of the repaired tendon, with fluid communicating between the joint and the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. Which interpretation is best supported?
text: Seven months after rotator cuff repair, a patient has recurrent pain and weakness. On fluid-sensitive fat-saturated images there is a fluid-bright (fluid-equivalent) defect spanning the full thickness of the repaired tendon, with fluid communicating between the joint and the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. Which interpretation is best supported? | A. Recurrent full-thickness tear, because a fluid-bright gap spans the tendon with joint-to-bursa communication | B. Expected postoperative change, because some increased intratendinous signal is normal after repair | C. Expected postoperative change, because anchor susceptibility artifact mimics a defect | D. Tendinosis of the repaired tendon, because the signal is intermediate rather than fluid-bright | Correct: A | A fluid-bright (fluid-equivalent) defect spanning the full tendon thickness with fluid communication between the joint and the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa is the reliable sign of a recurrent full-thickness tear. Options B and C reflect the opposite error of dismissing a true fluid-bright gap as benign postoperative signal or artifact, and option D mischaracterizes the defect because the finding is fluid-equivalent, not the intermediate signal of tendinosis. | postoperative | shoulder-arthritis-bursa-dontmiss

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
item_id: HAGL (humeral avulsion of the glenohumeral ligament)
title: HAGL (humeral avulsion of the glenohumeral ligament)
text: The IGHL torn off the HUMERAL neck (J-sign axillary pouch) — the classic miss when attention is on the glenoid. A surgical instability lesion.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Posterior dislocation (reverse Hill-Sachs + reverse Bankart)
text: Seizure, electrocution, or fall on an adducted arm — easily missed; the head locks posteriorly. Read it on the axial.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
item_id: Critical glenoid bone loss / off-track Hill-Sachs
title: Critical glenoid bone loss / off-track Hill-Sachs
text: ≥20–25% glenoid loss or an engaging Hill-Sachs shifts a soft-tissue Bankart repair toward a bony procedure (Latarjet). CT quantifies best.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
item_id: Irreparable cuff tear (high Goutallier + narrowed AHI)
title: Irreparable cuff tear (high Goutallier + narrowed AHI)
text: Goutallier ≥3 fatty infiltration with an acromiohumeral interval <7 mm signals a chronic/irreparable tear — changes the surgical conversation.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Resorptive calcific tendinitis
text: Hydroxyapatite is LOW signal on T1 AND T2 — easy to overlook on MRI; correlate the radiograph/ultrasound. It's an acute pain crisis, not a cuff-repair problem.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Supraspinatus footprint — MRI ↔ arthroscopy
text: structure: Rotator cuff footprint | title: Supraspinatus footprint — MRI ↔ arthroscopy | body: Coronal MRI shows the cuff inserting on the greater-tuberosity footprint; a full-thickness tear is a fluid-filled gap. From the subacromial space at arthroscopy the same footprint is bared bone, and the tear pattern (crescent, U-, L-shaped) plus tendon mobility guide the repair construct.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: SLAP — MRI ↔ arthroscopic probe
text: structure: Superior labrum / biceps anchor | title: SLAP — MRI ↔ arthroscopic probe | body: On MR-arthrography the superior labrum–biceps anchor is the SLAP zone, and a smooth sublabral recess can mimic a tear. Arthroscopy settles it — a probe tests whether the superior labrum is abnormally displaceable off the glenoid (vs a normal, smoothly contoured sublabral recess), and the dynamic peel-back sign (arm in ~90° abduction + maximal external rotation) shows the posterosuperior labrum peeling medially off the glenoid in a true type II SLAP.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Planes — what each one answers
text: label: Oblique coronal (parallel to supraspinatus) | detail: Workhorse for supraspinatus and infraspinatus tears, tendon footprint, retraction, marrow edema, and the AC joint. Trace the cuff from musculotendinous junction to the greater tuberosity. | label: Oblique sagittal (parallel to the glenoid face) | detail: Best for muscle bulk and fatty infiltration (Goutallier grading on the 'Y' view), localizing which tendon is torn, the rotator interval, and acromial morphology / os acromiale. | label: Axial | detail: Best for the subscapularis, the long head of biceps in/out of its groove (medial subluxation), the labrum (Bankart, reverse Bankart, ALPSA), glenoid bone loss, and Hill-Sachs / reverse Hill-Sachs.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sequences — what they're for
text: label: Fluid-sensitive fat-saturated (T2 / PD FS) | detail: Shows edema, bursitis, fluid filling a cuff defect, and marrow injury. A tear is more convincing when fluid-bright signal reaches a tendon surface. | label: T1-weighted | detail: Anatomy, marrow replacement, muscle fatty infiltration, and arthrogram contrast (bright) when present. | label: Intermediate / PD (non-fat-sat) | detail: Helpful for tendon morphology and labral signal. Confirm a suspected finding on at least two planes.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Normal rotator cuff
text: label: Normal tendon | detail: Uniformly low signal, smooth, inserting on the footprint without surface-reaching fluid-bright defect. Tendinosis = thickening + intermediate signal WITHOUT a surface defect. | label: Magic-angle pitfall | detail: Intermediate signal in the distal supraspinatus where the tendon curves ~55° to B0 on short-TE (T1/PD) sequences. It resolves on T2 — do not call it a tear.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Normal labrum & 'don't-overcall' variants
text: label: Normal labrum | detail: Triangular, uniformly low signal, firmly attached to the glenoid rim. Interpret superior labral signal in the context of age and sport before calling a SLAP tear. | label: Sublabral recess | detail: Normal smooth cleft under the superior labrum that parallels the glenoid and stops at the biceps anchor. A SLAP extends laterally into the labrum or posterior to the anchor with irregular margins. | label: Sublabral foramen & Buford complex | detail: Anterosuperior variants (1–3 o'clock). A Buford complex = absent anterosuperior labrum + cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament. Do not mistake either for a torn/detached labrum.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Normal biceps, pulley & capsule
text: label: Long head of biceps | detail: Centered in the bicipital groove on axial images. Medial perching/subluxation should trigger a search for an upper-border subscapularis / pulley lesion. | label: Rotator interval & capsule | detail: Normal subcoracoid fat triangle and a thin coracohumeral ligament. Obliteration of that fat + CHL thickening + a thickened axillary pouch supports adhesive capsulitis.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Rotator cuff tear — what to report every time
text: label: Five-part description | detail: (1) Which tendon(s); (2) surface — articular-sided, bursal-sided, or full-thickness; (3) AP tear size; (4) retraction (Patte); (5) muscle atrophy / fatty infiltration (Goutallier). Surface + muscle quality are what change management. | label: Tendinosis vs partial vs full-thickness | detail: Tendinosis = thickening + intermediate signal, no surface defect. Partial = fluid-bright signal reaching ONE surface. Full-thickness = defect spanning articular-to-bursal with joint–bursa fluid communication. | label: Partial-tear depth | detail: Low-grade < 50% vs high-grade > 50% of tendon thickness. A high-grade partial tear behaves clinically closer to a significant structural lesion — not 'just tendinosis.'

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Goutallier fatty infiltration (0–4)
text: label: Grade 0 | detail: Normal muscle, no fat. | label: Grade 1 | detail: Some fatty streaks within the muscle. | label: Grade 2 | detail: Fatty infiltration present, but muscle > fat. | label: Grade 3 | detail: Fat = muscle (50/50). | label: Grade 4 | detail: Fat > muscle. | label: Why it matters | detail: Grade ≥ 3 predicts poor healing / higher retear and may indicate an irreparable tear. Assess on the oblique-sagittal 'Y' view at the scapular spine.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Patte retraction stage (1–3)
text: label: Stage 1 | detail: Tendon stump near the footprint / greater tuberosity (minimal). | label: Stage 2 | detail: Retraction to the level of the humeral head (moderate). | label: Stage 3 | detail: Retraction to the glenoid level (advanced). | label: Why it matters | detail: Greater retraction + high Goutallier grade = harder repair and earlier surgical conversation.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Acromiohumeral interval
text: label: Normal > 7 mm | detail: Distance from the humeral head to the undersurface of the acromion on the coronal image. | label: < 7 mm | detail: Suggests cuff insufficiency with superior humeral migration. | label: < 5 mm | detail: Severe — consistent with chronic massive cuff tear / cuff-tear arthropathy.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Instability — bone loss & the glenoid track
text: label: Critical glenoid bone loss | detail: Attritional or fracture-related glenoid bone loss approaching ~20–25% shifts management from isolated soft-tissue (Bankart) repair toward a bony procedure (e.g., Latarjet). | label: Glenoid track / engaging Hill-Sachs | detail: An 'off-track' Hill-Sachs engages the anterior glenoid rim and predicts recurrent instability — it changes the surgical plan even with limited glenoid loss. | label: Best test for bone loss | detail: CT (often 3D with the humeral head subtracted) quantifies glenoid and humeral bone loss most accurately. MRI/MRA may underestimate it.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Lesion vocabulary — anterior instability
text: label: Bankart | detail: Anteroinferior labrum detached with disrupted/torn periosteum (± bony Bankart = glenoid rim fracture). | label: Perthes | detail: Anteroinferior labrum stripped but the periosteum is INTACT and the labrum is minimally displaced — easily missed; ABER view helps. | label: ALPSA | detail: Labroligamentous complex displaced MEDIALLY along an intact periosteum and 'rolled up' on the glenoid neck — chronic/recurrent. | label: Hill-Sachs | detail: Posterolateral humeral head impaction from an anterior dislocation. | label: HAGL | detail: Humeral Avulsion of the Glenohumeral Ligament — IGHL torn from the HUMERAL neck with a 'J-sign' axillary pouch. A classic miss because attention is on the glenoid.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Protocol — MRI vs MR arthrogram vs CT
text: label: Non-contrast MRI (most primary care questions) | detail: Excellent for cuff tendinosis/tears, subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis, AC joint, marrow/occult fracture, and adhesive-capsulitis support. | label: MR arthrogram | detail: Improves subtle labral/instability lesions, partial undersurface (articular-sided) cuff tears, and the postoperative labrum — when non-contrast MRI is equivocal or discordant with the exam. | label: CT (often 3D) | detail: Add when precise glenoid/humeral bone-loss quantification will change instability management.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Don't-miss: calcific tendinitis
text: label: MRI appearance | detail: A hydroxyapatite (calcific) deposit is LOW signal on T1 AND T2/fluid-sensitive sequences, so it is easy to overlook on MRI. Look for a focal low-signal focus in the tendon (often supraspinatus near the footprint) with surrounding edema. Radiographs and ultrasound show the deposit best — correlate. | label: Acute pain crisis | detail: Resorptive-phase calcific tendinitis causes severe atraumatic pain out of proportion to routine tendinopathy, often with florid peritendinous edema ± subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis (occasionally bursal rupture). | label: Management differs from tendinosis | detail: Usually conservative first (NSAIDs, activity modification, physical therapy); image-guided barbotage/aspiration or a subacromial injection is added for refractory or acute resorptive-phase pain. The point: it is not a structural cuff-repair problem — recognizing it changes the next step.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Patte stage the retraction of a full-thickness supraspinatus tear on the coronal: stage 1 = stump near the footprint, stage 2 = at the humeral-head level, stage 3 = at the glenoid level (or more medial). Stage 3 retraction predicts poor reparability.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Goutallier/Fuchs fatty atrophy is the key reparability marker: grade 3-4 (fat equal to or exceeding muscle) on the sagittal Y-view predicts irreparability and re-tear regardless of tear size. Always comment on muscle quality, not just the tendon.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Distinguish tendinosis (intermediate T2 signal, no fluid-bright gap) from a partial tear (fluid-bright defect not spanning the full thickness) from a full-thickness tear (fluid-signal cleft from articular to bursal surface). Only the last communicates the joint with the subacromial bursa.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Grade articular-surface partial tears by depth: a defect greater than 50% of the ~12 mm footprint (a high-grade PASTA lesion, roughly >6 mm) generally crosses the surgical threshold for repair rather than debridement.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: The supraspinatus 'critical zone,' about 1 cm proximal to the footprint, is hypovascular and the most common site of degenerative tears. Scrutinize it on every fluid-sensitive coronal and sagittal image.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: On a fluid-sensitive sagittal at the level of the coracoid base (the 'Y' or Mercedes view), the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis are all profiled — the standard plane for grading Goutallier fatty atrophy and muscle bulk.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A torn subscapularis is easy to miss: look for tendon discontinuity at the lesser tuberosity on axial images, fatty atrophy on the sagittal Y-view, and medial subluxation of the long head of biceps as a secondary sign.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: The biceps pulley (CHL + SGHL sling) stabilizes the long head of biceps at the groove entrance; a pulley lesion lets it sublux medially over or under the subscapularis. Assess it on axial images at the top of the bicipital groove, and if the biceps sits medial to the lesser tuberosity, interrogate the subscapularis insertion directly.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A normal sublabral recess is a smooth, medially-oriented cleft at the 11-1 o'clock position that follows the glenoid contour; a SLAP tear extends laterally/posteriorly, is irregular and wide (>2-3 mm), and tracks fluid or contrast into the labral substance.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: The anteroinferior labroligamentous complex (3-6 o'clock) is the instability hot spot: a Bankart tear here in a young patient after anterior dislocation predicts recurrence. Trace the IGHL anterior band to its labral attachment.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Differentiate anteroinferior labral variants: Bankart = detached labrum with periosteal disruption; ALPSA = labroligamentous sleeve avulsed and displaced medially, healing malpositioned; Perthes = labrum detached but periosteum intact (may only show on ABER); GLAD = labral tear with an articular cartilage divot.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A HAGL lesion (humeral avulsion of the glenohumeral ligament) produces a 'J-sign' axillary pouch on coronal images and is a commonly missed cause of instability — it is repaired on the humeral side, not at the labrum, so it must be reported.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Quantify glenoid bone loss before instability surgery: roughly >20-25% loss favors a bony augmentation (Latarjet) over isolated soft-tissue Bankart repair. CT with 3D 'en-face' glenoid reconstruction is the reference standard for measurement.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Apply the glenoid-track concept: the track measures ~83% of the glenoid diameter minus glenoid bone loss. A Hill-Sachs whose medial margin lies medial to the track is 'off-track,' engages, and mandates remplissage or a bone procedure rather than isolated Bankart repair.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A Hill-Sachs lesion is a posterolateral humeral head impaction fracture, typically at or above the level of the coracoid; the normal bare area lies more inferiorly. Measure its width and depth and pair it with the bony Bankart to determine on-track versus off-track status.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: An empty bicipital groove on axial images means the long head of biceps has dislocated (usually medially, with a subscapularis tear) or fully ruptured and retracted. Follow the tendon distally toward the myotendinous junction to distinguish dislocation from rupture.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid alone is nonspecific and accompanies bursitis or a cuff tear; call a full-thickness cuff tear only when a fluid-signal gap connects the joint to the bursa. Do not diagnose impingement off bursal fluid in isolation.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A laterally downsloping or hooked (Bigliani type III) acromion and subacromial spurs narrow the supraspinatus outlet and correlate with chronic impingement and cuff degeneration; report acromial morphology when there is bursal-surface fraying.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Os acromiale is an unfused acromial apophysis (most commonly meso-type) seen on axial images as a corticated ossicle; a mobile fragment can tilt into the outlet and cause impingement, and it must be flagged before acromioplasty.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Adhesive capsulitis is a soft-tissue MR diagnosis: thickening and edema of the axillary recess capsule (>4 mm), obliteration of the rotator-interval fat by enhancing soft tissue, and thickening of the coracohumeral ligament. The cuff itself is typically normal.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Calcific tendinosis shows blooming low-signal hydroxyapatite within the cuff (usually supraspinatus) on all sequences; surrounding edema and bursal fluid signal the painful resorptive phase. Correlate with radiographs or CT, as small calcifications are easily masked on MRI.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Quadrilateral space syndrome shows isolated fatty atrophy or denervation edema of the teres minor (± deltoid) from axillary nerve compression. Isolated teres minor changes should prompt a search for a paralabral cyst, fibrous band, or mass in the quadrilateral space.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Parsonage-Turner (neuralgic amyotrophy) presents as diffuse T2 denervation edema across muscles in a non-compressive, often multi-nerve distribution (commonly supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and deltoid) with no compressive mass — pain preceding weakness is the clinical tip-off.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A spinoglenoid-notch paralabral cyst compresses the suprascapular nerve distally, denervating the infraspinatus alone; a suprascapular-notch cyst affects both supraspinatus and infraspinatus. Always trace a posterior paralabral cyst to its parent posterosuperior labral tear.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Order direct MR arthrography for suspected labral pathology, SLAP tears, and subtle instability lesions; the distended joint and ABER positioning unmask non-displaced Perthes lesions and partial undersurface cuff tears that conventional MRI misses.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A sublabral foramen is a normal unattached anterosuperior labrum (1-3 o'clock) with the labrum intact below the equator; do not call it a Bankart. Mistaking this variant for a tear above the 3 o'clock position is a classic false positive.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: The Buford complex is a congenital variant: absent anterosuperior labrum with a thick, cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament. Recognize it and do NOT surgically reattach the 'detached' labrum, which would restrict external rotation.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Anterosuperior labral variants (sublabral recess, sublabral foramen, Buford complex) are normal only above the glenoid equator (above 3 o'clock); any labral detachment at or below the equator (3-6 o'clock) is pathologic until proven otherwise.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
