text: Massive retracted cuff tears with a high-riding humerus (acromiohumeral interval <7 mm) and grade 3-4 fatty atrophy define cuff-tear arthropathy; these are generally irreparable and steer management toward reverse total shoulder arthroplasty rather than repair.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A reverse Hill-Sachs (anteromedial humeral head impaction) plus a reverse Bankart (posteroinferior labral tear) signals prior posterior dislocation — easily overlooked because the humeral head can look 'reduced.' Suspect it after seizures, electrocution, or a posteriorly directed fall.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: When is an MR arthrogram preferred over a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI?
text: When is an MR arthrogram preferred over a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI? | When subtle labral/instability lesions, an articular-sided (undersurface) partial cuff tear, or the postoperative labrum are in question and non-contrast MRI is equivocal or discordant with the exam. Intra-articular contrast distends the joint and outlines the labrum.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What does a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI answer well?
text: What does a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI answer well? | Most primary care questions: cuff tendinosis/tears, subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis, AC joint disease, marrow/occult fracture, and findings supporting adhesive capsulitis.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which plane is the workhorse for supraspinatus/infraspinatus tears and the AC joint?
text: Which plane is the workhorse for supraspinatus/infraspinatus tears and the AC joint? | Oblique coronal (parallel to the supraspinatus). Trace the cuff from the musculotendinous junction to the greater tuberosity footprint.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which plane best shows the subscapularis, the biceps in/out of its groove, the labrum (Bankart), and Hill-Sachs?
text: Which plane best shows the subscapularis, the biceps in/out of its groove, the labrum (Bankart), and Hill-Sachs? | Axial. The oblique sagittal is best for muscle bulk/fatty infiltration (Goutallier 'Y' view), which tendon is torn, the rotator interval, and acromial morphology.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which sequence makes a cuff tear most convincing, and why?
text: Which sequence makes a cuff tear most convincing, and why? | Fluid-sensitive fat-saturated (T2/PD FS): a tear is convincing when fluid-bright signal reaches a tendon surface or fills a defect.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the magic-angle pitfall in the shoulder?
text: What is the magic-angle pitfall in the shoulder? | Intermediate signal in the distal supraspinatus where it curves ~55° to the main field on short-TE (T1/PD) sequences. It resolves on T2 — do not call it a tear.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: For primary care sports medicine, what is the goal when reading a shoulder MRI?
text: For primary care sports medicine, what is the goal when reading a shoulder MRI? | Answer the management question — is there a structural/surgical lesion, a red flag, or a finding that changes the next step (referral, injection, rehab)? — rather than cataloguing every degenerative change.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which high-miss areas is a shoulder search pattern designed to catch?
text: Which high-miss areas is a shoulder search pattern designed to catch? | Upper-border subscapularis, the biceps pulley / medial biceps subluxation, the posterior and posterosuperior labrum, suprascapular/spinoglenoid denervation, and the AC joint.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is satisfaction-of-search, and how do you guard against it on shoulder MRI?
text: What is satisfaction-of-search, and how do you guard against it on shoulder MRI? | Stopping after the first obvious finding (e.g., a supraspinatus tear) and missing a second lesion. Finish the full checklist every time — a cuff tear does not exclude a labral tear, a biceps lesion, or denervation.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Name three superior-labral 'don't-overcall' variants.
text: Name three superior-labral 'don't-overcall' variants. | Sublabral recess, sublabral foramen, and the Buford complex (absent anterosuperior labrum + a cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you distinguish a sublabral recess from a SLAP tear?
text: How do you distinguish a sublabral recess from a SLAP tear? | A recess is smooth, medial, parallels the glenoid, and stops at the biceps anchor. A SLAP extends laterally into the labrum or posterior to the anchor with irregular margins.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How does tendinosis look versus a cuff tear?
text: How does tendinosis look versus a cuff tear? | Tendinosis = tendon thickening + intermediate signal WITHOUT a surface-reaching fluid-bright defect. A tear shows fluid-bright signal reaching a surface (partial) or spanning articular-to-bursal (full-thickness).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Articular- versus bursal-sided partial tear — which surface is which, and which is more common?
text: Articular- versus bursal-sided partial tear — which surface is which, and which is more common? | Articular = the deep surface facing the joint (the most common partial tear, 'PASTA'). Bursal = the superficial surface under the subacromial bursa.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How is partial-tear depth graded, and why does it matter?
text: How is partial-tear depth graded, and why does it matter? | Low-grade <50% vs high-grade >50% of tendon thickness. A high-grade partial behaves clinically closer to a significant structural lesion — not 'just tendinosis.'

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What five things should every full-thickness cuff tear report include?
text: What five things should every full-thickness cuff tear report include? | (1) which tendon(s); (2) surface/thickness; (3) AP tear size; (4) retraction (Patte stage); (5) muscle atrophy / fatty infiltration (Goutallier grade). Surface and muscle quality are what change management.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What does the Patte stage describe?
text: What does the Patte stage describe? | Tendon retraction: Stage 1 near the footprint, Stage 2 to the humeral head, Stage 3 to the glenoid. More retraction = harder repair.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What Goutallier grade predicts poor healing / an irreparable tear, and where is it assessed?
text: What Goutallier grade predicts poor healing / an irreparable tear, and where is it assessed? | Grade ≥3 (fat ≥ muscle). Assess on the oblique-sagittal 'Y' view at the scapular spine.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Medial subluxation of the biceps out of its groove should trigger a search for what?
text: Medial subluxation of the biceps out of its groove should trigger a search for what? | An upper-border subscapularis tear / biceps pulley lesion — the classic 'hidden lesion.' Inspect the subscapularis on axial and sagittal images.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What does a normal labrum look like?
text: What does a normal labrum look like? | Triangular, uniformly low signal, firmly attached to the glenoid rim. Interpret superior labral signal in the context of age and sport before calling a SLAP tear.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is found — what associated finding must you check?
text: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is found — what associated finding must you check? | Infraspinatus denervation edema (acute) or fatty atrophy (chronic) from compression of the suprascapular nerve branch.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the classic bipolar lesion of an anterior shoulder dislocation?
text: What is the classic bipolar lesion of an anterior shoulder dislocation? | An anteroinferior labral Bankart lesion plus a posterolateral humeral-head Hill-Sachs impaction.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Define Bankart, Perthes, ALPSA, and HAGL.
text: Define Bankart, Perthes, ALPSA, and HAGL. | Bankart (soft-tissue) = anteroinferior labrum detached with torn periosteum; the osseous Bankart variant is an anteroinferior glenoid rim fracture (it drives the bone-loss/surgical-referral assessment). Perthes = labrum stripped but periosteum intact and minimally displaced (easily missed). ALPSA = labroligamentous complex displaced medially and 'rolled up' on the glenoid neck (chronic). HAGL = inferior glenohumeral ligament avulsed from the HUMERAL side ('J-sign' axillary pouch).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which instability findings push toward surgery and warrant CT quantification?
text: Which instability findings push toward surgery and warrant CT quantification? | Significant glenoid bone loss (approaching ~20%, with lower subcritical thresholds increasingly used) or an engaging / off-track Hill-Sachs. CT (often 3D) quantifies bone loss best — MRI/MRA may underestimate it.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Where do you check the long head of biceps, and what is normal versus abnormal?
text: Where do you check the long head of biceps, and what is normal versus abnormal? | Centered in the bicipital groove on axial images. Fluid tracking only around the tendon reflects the joint's fluid; medial perching/subluxation out of the groove is abnormal and a red flag.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Medial biceps subluxation implies injury to what, and which tendon should you scrutinize?
text: Medial biceps subluxation implies injury to what, and which tendon should you scrutinize? | The biceps pulley (coracohumeral / superior glenohumeral ligament sling). Scrutinize the upper-border subscapularis — the 'hidden lesion.'

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What MRI findings support adhesive capsulitis?
text: What MRI findings support adhesive capsulitis? | Obliteration of the subcoracoid fat triangle in the rotator interval, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and a thickened/edematous axillary pouch and capsule. Correlate with painful global ROM loss — the diagnosis is clinical.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: When does AC joint arthrosis matter on MRI?
text: When does AC joint arthrosis matter on MRI? | When it correlates with focal AC tenderness: look for capsular hypertrophy, marrow edema on BOTH sides of the joint, and inferior osteophytes contacting the cuff. Asymptomatic AC degeneration is common — don't over-attribute pain to it.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What acromiohumeral interval values signal cuff insufficiency and cuff-tear arthropathy?
text: What acromiohumeral interval values signal cuff insufficiency and cuff-tear arthropathy? | Normal >7 mm. <7 mm suggests cuff insufficiency with superior humeral migration. <5 mm is consistent with a chronic massive tear / cuff-tear arthropathy.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the imaging clue and the management pivot for calcific tendinitis?
text: What is the imaging clue and the management pivot for calcific tendinitis? | A focal LOW-signal deposit on T1 AND T2 (easy to miss) with surrounding edema, often in the supraspinatus near the footprint. It is usually managed conservatively first (NSAIDs, activity modification, physical therapy); ultrasound-guided barbotage/aspiration or a subacromial injection is added for refractory or acutely painful (resorptive-phase) cases. The key point: it is NOT a structural cuff-repair problem. Radiographs/ultrasound show the deposit best.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Match the denervated muscle to the paralabral cyst location.
text: Match the denervated muscle to the paralabral cyst location. | Spinoglenoid notch cyst → infraspinatus denervation. Suprascapular notch cyst → BOTH supraspinatus and infraspinatus.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Name shoulder MRI red flags that change management urgently.
text: Name shoulder MRI red flags that change management urgently. | Suspected septic arthritis/osteomyelitis, an aggressive marrow-replacing or soft-tissue mass, and acute occult fracture. Communicate directly and don't anchor on degenerative findings.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What makes a shoulder MRI impression 'management-changing'?
text: What makes a shoulder MRI impression 'management-changing'? | It states the structural diagnosis, its severity/repairability (tendon, size, retraction, muscle quality; bone loss; denervation), and the implied next step (rehab, injection, referral) — rather than a list of every degenerative change.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What are the three MRI grades of a muscle strain, and where do strains most commonly occur?
text: What are the three MRI grades of a muscle strain, and where do strains most commonly occur? | Grade 1 = perifascial 'feathery' edema with intact architecture; Grade 2 = partial tear with fiber disruption plus fluid/hematoma; Grade 3 = complete tear with retraction. The myotendinous junction is the most common site.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the classic mechanism and the most surgically relevant tear location for a pectoralis major rupture?
text: What is the classic mechanism and the most surgically relevant tear location for a pectoralis major rupture? | Eccentric load with the arm extended/abducted — classically the bottom of a bench press. The most surgically relevant location is avulsion of the tendon from its humeral insertion at the lateral lip of the bicipital (intertubercular) groove; the sternocostal head tears most often (inferior sternocostal fibers first).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What MRI sign indicates a complete humeral-sided avulsion of the pectoralis major, and what should the report state?
text: What MRI sign indicates a complete humeral-sided avulsion of the pectoralis major, and what should the report state? | The 'empty bicipital groove' — the avulsed tendon is absent from the lateral lip of the intertubercular groove (partial/MTJ tears retain tendon there). Report which head(s), tear location (intramuscular vs MTJ vs humeral avulsion), completeness, and retraction; acute complete avulsions in active patients are referred for surgical repair.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A primary care sports medicine fellow is reviewing shoulder MRI for suspected instability and subtle labral pathology. Which protocol most often improves labral and capsuloligamentous evaluation compared with routine non-contrast MRI?
text: A primary care sports medicine fellow is reviewing shoulder MRI for suspected instability and subtle labral pathology. Which protocol most often improves labral and capsuloligamentous evaluation compared with routine non-contrast MRI? | A. MR arthrography | B. T1-only non-contrast MRI | C. Ultrasound of the biceps groove only | D. Radiographs with internal rotation view only | A | MR arthrography distends the joint and can improve visualization of subtle labral, capsular, and undersurface cuff abnormalities. Routine MRI remains excellent for many cuff, bursa, marrow, arthritis, and adhesive capsulitis questions.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which shoulder MRI plane is most useful for assessing rotator cuff muscle bulk and fatty infiltration across the supraspinatus and infraspinatus fossae?
text: Which shoulder MRI plane is most useful for assessing rotator cuff muscle bulk and fatty infiltration across the supraspinatus and infraspinatus fossae? | A. Axial | B. Oblique sagittal | C. Coronal localizer only | D. Scout images only | B | Oblique sagittal images are parallel to the glenoid face (i.e., perpendicular to the long axis of the supraspinatus tendon) and are the key plane for evaluating rotator cuff muscle bulk, atrophy, and fatty infiltration (Goutallier grading on the scapular-Y view).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A fellow is reading a shoulder MRI for a possible full-thickness supraspinatus tear. The protocol includes T1-weighted, proton-density fat-suppressed (PD FS), and PD sequences. Which statement best describes how these sequences complement one another to confirm a surface-reaching fluid-filled defect and assess associated muscle/marrow changes?
text: A fellow is reading a shoulder MRI for a possible full-thickness supraspinatus tear. The protocol includes T1-weighted, proton-density fat-suppressed (PD FS), and PD sequences. Which statement best describes how these sequences complement one another to confirm a surface-reaching fluid-filled defect and assess associated muscle/marrow changes? | A. PD FS best demonstrates the bright fluid signal tracking through a full-thickness defect into the subacromial bursa, while non-fat-suppressed PD/T1 best depict tendon and muscle anatomy and any fatty muscle atrophy. | B. PD FS alone is sufficient; the non-fat-suppressed sequences add no diagnostic value and only waste acquisition time. | C. T1 alone suffices, because joint and bursal fluid appears bright on T1 and is therefore the most conspicuous sign of a tear. | D. Gradient-echo sequences alone are preferred, because their magnetic-susceptibility effects maximize conspicuity of cuff tears and fluid. | A | On fluid-sensitive PD FS, fluid is bright and tracks through a full-thickness cuff defect into the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa, confirming a surface-reaching tear. Non-fat-suppressed PD and T1 give higher-SNR anatomic detail and show fatty muscle atrophy (Goutallier). Fluid is not bright on T1, and GRE blooming degrades cuff/fluid assessment.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 58-year-old primary care sports medicine patient presents with 3 weeks of severe shoulder pain and global passive range-of-motion loss after a minor rotator cuff strain. You obtain a shoulder MRI and want to assess whether adhesive capsulitis findings support the clinical presentation. Which sequences and imaging findings are most useful for this question?
text: A 58-year-old primary care sports medicine patient presents with 3 weeks of severe shoulder pain and global passive range-of-motion loss after a minor rotator cuff strain. You obtain a shoulder MRI and want to assess whether adhesive capsulitis findings support the clinical presentation. Which sequences and imaging findings are most useful for this question? | A. T1-weighted images alone, because they show anatomy best and are sufficient to rule out a tear. | B. Axial and coronal fluid-sensitive fat-suppressed sequences to visualize rotator interval edema, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and axillary pouch capsular thickening—findings that support adhesive capsulitis in the appropriate clinical setting and help guide injection or therapy planning. | C. Gradient-echo sequences are essential because adhesive capsulitis produces blooming artifact that proves the diagnosis. | D. Sagittal images are the only plane needed; axial and coronal images are redundant for assessing capsular inflammation. | B | Adhesive capsulitis imaging findings—rotator interval edema, CHL thickening, axillary recess capsular thickening—are best seen on fluid-sensitive sequences and axial/coronal planes. MRI supports but does not replace the clinical diagnosis of global ROM loss; these imaging findings are management-relevant when they match the exam and guide injection-planning or exclusion of competing pathology.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: After finding mild supraspinatus tendinosis, the reader stops and misses medial biceps subluxation from an upper-border subscapularis tear. What cognitive error does the search pattern help prevent?
text: After finding mild supraspinatus tendinosis, the reader stops and misses medial biceps subluxation from an upper-border subscapularis tear. What cognitive error does the search pattern help prevent? | A. Satisfaction of search | B. Recency bias | C. Lead-time bias | D. Verification bias | A | Satisfaction of search occurs when finding one abnormality reduces vigilance for additional findings. The shoulder search pattern forces a complete review even after an obvious pain generator is found.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which impression is most useful for a primary care sports medicine clinician?
text: Which impression is most useful for a primary care sports medicine clinician? | A. Abnormal shoulder MRI. | B. Signal abnormality in the cuff and labrum. | C. Acute full-thickness supraspinatus tear with mild retraction and no advanced fatty atrophy; timely surgical referral is appropriate. | D. Degenerative changes are present. | C | Management-relevant impressions name the dominant diagnosis, severity, chronicity/reparability clues, and clinical implication. Vague signal descriptions do not help the next-step decision.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: You are implementing a systematic shoulder MRI search pattern as a primary care sports medicine fellow. Which step sequence is most logical for ensuring you capture management-changing pathology while avoiding satisfaction-of-search errors?
text: You are implementing a systematic shoulder MRI search pattern as a primary care sports medicine fellow. Which step sequence is most logical for ensuring you capture management-changing pathology while avoiding satisfaction-of-search errors? | A. Start with the clinically suspected pathology, then skip any regions that appear normal to save time and move to the next case quickly. | B. Read the supraspinatus alone first; if it is normal, conclude the rotator cuff is intact and move to the labrum without checking the subscapularis, infraspinatus, or muscles. | C. Begin with protocol and clinical context, then evaluate bones and alignment, followed by rotator cuff tendons and muscles, labrum and instability lesions, biceps/pulley/rotator interval, capsule and bursa, and finally nerves and red flags—in that fixed order every time. | D. Use a different search order for each patient based on the clinical question, adapting the pattern to what you think is most likely to be abnormal. | C | A fixed eight-step checklist is not a crutch—it is how experts protect themselves from predictable human misses like medial biceps subluxation, posterior labral tears, and denervation. Finishing the full pattern on every case is what keeps satisfactory findings (mild tendinosis) from hiding management-changing lesions (subscapularis tear with pulley involvement).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A fellow dictates an impression: 'Slight supraspinatus signal change; consider tendinosis versus small tear.' This phrasing has several problems for a primary care clinician. Which revised impression is most useful?
text: A fellow dictates an impression: 'Slight supraspinatus signal change; consider tendinosis versus small tear.' This phrasing has several problems for a primary care clinician. Which revised impression is most useful? | A. Signal abnormality present in the supraspinatus; clinical correlation needed. | B. Possible rotator cuff pathology; recommendation: advance imaging if symptoms persist. | C. Mild degenerative signal change in the cuff; significance unknown from imaging alone. | D. Low-grade supraspinatus tendinosis without discrete surface-reaching tear or retraction; no acute traumatic features; rehab-first strategy remains reasonable, though patient correlation advised. | D | Structured impressions answer four questions: (1) What is the dominant diagnosis? (2) How severe? (3) What associated findings matter? (4) What is the management implication? Vague phrases like 'signal change' and 'consider' place the clinical burden back on the referring provider. Instead, name the severity, state whether a surface is involved, and guide the next visit (rehab-first vs. injection vs. referral).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On an MR arthrogram, you identify a smooth band of contrast undercutting the superior labrum at the biceps-labral junction, following the glenoid contour and stopping precisely at the biceps anchor. The margins are sharp and regular. What is the correct interpretation?
text: On an MR arthrogram, you identify a smooth band of contrast undercutting the superior labrum at the biceps-labral junction, following the glenoid contour and stopping precisely at the biceps anchor. The margins are sharp and regular. What is the correct interpretation? | A. Normal sublabral recess—a common variant that should not be reported as a tear and does not require surgical follow-up. | B. SLAP tear with posterior extension; recommend arthroscopy for confirmation. | C. Sublabral foramen with partial labral detachment; borderline for referral. | D. Type II SLAP with biceps anchor instability; surgical consultation advised. | A | A sublabral recess is a normal variant with a smooth, medial cleft that parallels the glenoid contour and stops at the biceps anchor. A true SLAP tear, by contrast, extends LATERALLY into the labral substance or POSTERIOR to the anchor with irregular, jagged margins. Mistaking a recess for a tear is a classic overcall that exposes an asymptomatic patient to unnecessary imaging and referral; two-plane confirmation and arthrographic morphology are key to the differentiation.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A supraspinatus tendon is thickened with intermediate signal but has no fluid-filled surface defect or fiber discontinuity. What is the best description?
text: A supraspinatus tendon is thickened with intermediate signal but has no fluid-filled surface defect or fiber discontinuity. What is the best description? | A. Full-thickness tear | B. Tendinosis | C. Complete tendon rupture with retraction | D. Bankart lesion | B | Tendinosis produces tendon thickening and intermediate signal without a discrete articular-sided, bursal-sided, or full-thickness defect.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which additional information is most important when reporting a full-thickness rotator cuff tear?
text: Which additional information is most important when reporting a full-thickness rotator cuff tear? | A. Only whether bursal fluid is present | B. Only the patient age | C. Tendons involved, tear size, retraction, muscle atrophy, and fatty infiltration | D. The number of localizer images | C | Tendons involved, AP size, retraction, and muscle quality are management-changing details that influence referral urgency and repairability.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A partial-thickness supraspinatus tear shows fluid-bright signal reaching only the deep surface that faces the glenohumeral joint. How is this best classified?
text: A partial-thickness supraspinatus tear shows fluid-bright signal reaching only the deep surface that faces the glenohumeral joint. How is this best classified? | A. Bursal-sided partial tear | B. Articular-sided (PASTA) partial tear | C. Full-thickness tear | D. Tendinosis | B | The articular (deep) surface faces the joint; an articular-sided partial tear (PASTA) is the most common partial tear. Bursal-sided tears involve the superficial surface, and a full-thickness tear reaches both surfaces.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On axial images the long head of biceps is displaced medially out of the bicipital groove. Which tendon should you deliberately scrutinize?
text: On axial images the long head of biceps is displaced medially out of the bicipital groove. Which tendon should you deliberately scrutinize? | A. Infraspinatus | B. Teres minor | C. Upper-border subscapularis | D. Supraspinatus only | C | Medial biceps displacement is a red flag for a biceps pulley / upper-border subscapularis lesion — the classic "hidden lesion." Inspect the subscapularis on axial and sagittal images.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 58-year-old with a full-thickness supraspinatus tear is referred for surgical evaluation. On oblique-sagittal MRI at the scapular Y-view, the supraspinatus muscle shows intramuscular fat streaks that are approximately equal to the residual muscle volume. Using Goutallier grading, what does this finding indicate, and how should it influence surgical decision-making?
text: A 58-year-old with a full-thickness supraspinatus tear is referred for surgical evaluation. On oblique-sagittal MRI at the scapular Y-view, the supraspinatus muscle shows intramuscular fat streaks that are approximately equal to the residual muscle volume. Using Goutallier grading, what does this finding indicate, and how should it influence surgical decision-making? | A. Goutallier grade 1 (minimal streaking); this is a favorable prognostic sign that predicts excellent repair outcomes regardless of tear size. | B. Goutallier grade 3 (fat ≥ muscle); this predicts poor healing and may indicate an irreparable tear or mandate low-expectation repair counseling because advanced fatty infiltration significantly reduces repairability and functional outcome. | C. Goutallier grade 2 (fat < muscle); this is irrelevant to repair potential and does not change the surgical recommendation beyond standard repair. | D. Goutallier grade 4 (complete fatty replacement); although severe, grade 4 is still suitable for routine rotator cuff repair with the same success rates as lower grades. | B | Goutallier grades reflect muscle quality and predict repairability: grades 0–2 generally favor repair; grades 3–4 indicate potential irreparability or poor functional outcome even if repair is technically possible. Assessment on the oblique-sagittal Y-view is essential for accurate grading and informs preoperative counseling.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A first-time anterior shoulder dislocation MRI shows an anteroinferior labral detachment and a posterolateral humeral head impaction injury. What is the most coherent diagnosis?
text: A first-time anterior shoulder dislocation MRI shows an anteroinferior labral detachment and a posterolateral humeral head impaction injury. What is the most coherent diagnosis? | A. Adhesive capsulitis | B. Bankart lesion with Hill-Sachs injury | C. Isolated AC joint arthritis | D. Quadrilateral space syndrome | B | Anterior instability commonly produces an anteroinferior labral Bankart lesion and a posterolateral humeral head Hill-Sachs impaction injury.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is identified. What associated finding should be actively checked?
text: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is identified. What associated finding should be actively checked? | A. Infraspinatus denervation edema or atrophy | B. Patellar tendon rupture | C. Medial meniscal extrusion | D. Achilles tendinosis | A | A spinoglenoid notch cyst can compress the suprascapular nerve branch to infraspinatus, causing denervation edema acutely or fatty atrophy chronically.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On an MR arthrogram of the superior labrum, which feature favors a true SLAP tear over a normal sublabral recess?
text: On an MR arthrogram of the superior labrum, which feature favors a true SLAP tear over a normal sublabral recess? | A. A smooth medial cleft that parallels the glenoid and stops at the biceps anchor | B. Contrast extending laterally into the labral substance or posterior to the biceps anchor with irregular margins | C. A cleft located at the 12 o’clock position | D. A cleft that measures less than 2 mm | B | A sublabral recess is smooth, medial, parallels the glenoid, and stops at the biceps anchor. Lateral extension into the labrum or posterior to the anchor with irregular margins favors a SLAP tear. A 12 o’clock location is common to both and does not discriminate.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: In recurrent anterior instability, which finding most increases the importance of surgical referral and may warrant CT for precise quantification?
text: In recurrent anterior instability, which finding most increases the importance of surgical referral and may warrant CT for precise quantification? | A. A small soft-tissue Bankart with no osseous injury | B. Significant glenoid bone loss or an engaging / off-track Hill-Sachs lesion | C. Mild subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis | D. A normal sublabral recess | B | Significant glenoid bone loss (approaching ~20%) or an engaging / off-track Hill-Sachs shifts management toward a bony procedure (e.g., Latarjet) and is best quantified by CT, often 3D. An isolated soft-tissue Bankart does not carry the same implication.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 28-year-old overhead athlete with posterior shoulder pain undergoes shoulder MRI with non-contrast and MR arthrography. Axial images show a hypoattenuating cleft at the posterior labrum with irregular margins and edema in the adjacent soft tissue. The referring clinician's note only states "labral tear." Which additional information is essential to guide the next diagnostic and management step?
text: A 28-year-old overhead athlete with posterior shoulder pain undergoes shoulder MRI with non-contrast and MR arthrography. Axial images show a hypoattenuating cleft at the posterior labrum with irregular margins and edema in the adjacent soft tissue. The referring clinician's note only states "labral tear." Which additional information is essential to guide the next diagnostic and management step? | A. Report only the tear size in millimeters, because that is the only measurement that influences surgical decision-making. | B. Describe the signal intensity of the tear relative to fluid, since signal alone is sufficient to communicate the clinical urgency. | C. Specify the quadrant location (e.g., posterior or 6-9 o'clock position) and pattern (tear vs. degenerative fraying vs. reverse Bankart), as posterior labral injuries require different imaging follow-up and surgical considerations than anterior or superior patterns. | D. Simply confirm that a labral abnormality is present and defer all further characterization to the orthopedic surgeon, as MRI interpretation should not include quadrant or pattern details. | C | Labral pathology must be localized by quadrant and classified by pattern (traumatic tear, degenerative fraying, instability variant, or associated with cyst/denervation) because the clinical significance, likelihood of symptoms, and surgical approach differ between anterior (Bankart), superior (SLAP), and posterior labral injuries. 'Labral tear' alone does not guide management.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Medial subluxation of the long head biceps tendon from the groove should trigger a deliberate search for which associated lesion?
text: Medial subluxation of the long head biceps tendon from the groove should trigger a deliberate search for which associated lesion? | A. Upper-border subscapularis tear or biceps pulley lesion | B. Posterior horn medial meniscus tear | C. Distal radius fracture | D. Achilles rupture | A | The biceps pulley and upper subscapularis stabilize the long head biceps. Medial biceps subluxation is a red flag for pulley/subscapularis pathology.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: MRI shows rotator interval edema, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and axillary capsular thickening in a patient with global passive range-of-motion loss. What clinical diagnosis do these findings support?
text: MRI shows rotator interval edema, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and axillary capsular thickening in a patient with global passive range-of-motion loss. What clinical diagnosis do these findings support? | A. Adhesive capsulitis | B. Anterior shoulder dislocation | C. Massive acute pectoralis tear | D. Normal variant with no clinical relevance | A | Rotator interval edema, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and axillary capsular thickening support adhesive capsulitis when the clinical exam shows global passive range-of-motion loss.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain and clicking in the bicipital groove. MRI shows signal abnormality and mild thickening of the long head biceps tendon in the intra-articular segment with surrounding synovial fluid. The biceps anchor is intact and the tendon remains centered in the groove. No rotator cuff tear is identified. Which term best describes this finding, and what management approach is most appropriate?
text: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain and clicking in the bicipital groove. MRI shows signal abnormality and mild thickening of the long head biceps tendon in the intra-articular segment with surrounding synovial fluid. The biceps anchor is intact and the tendon remains centered in the groove. No rotator cuff tear is identified. Which term best describes this finding, and what management approach is most appropriate? | A. Biceps rupture; this requires urgent surgical referral and tenodesis to restore elbow flexion and supination strength. | B. SLAP tear; this requires MR arthrography confirmation and typically surgical referral in active patients, as an anchor tear would show contrast undercutting the labral attachment. | C. Biceps pulley failure; this would present with medial biceps subluxation or dislocation out of the groove, which is not seen here with the tendon centered. | D. Biceps tenosynovitis; this is a synovial inflammatory response around the tendon that is typically managed conservatively with NSAIDs, activity modification, and physical therapy focused on scapular stability and rotator cuff strengthening. | D | Biceps tenosynovitis manifests as fluid surrounding an intact, centered tendon without surface-reaching defects or rupture. The distinction between tendinosis (tendon thickening and signal change) versus tenosynovitis (sheath fluid) versus tear is critical—tenosynovitis and low-grade tendinosis are often rehab-first findings, whereas partial or complete rupture and pulley failure warrant referral consideration.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 58-year-old with superior shoulder pain localized to the AC joint on cross-body adduction testing undergoes shoulder MRI. Coronal and axial images show joint-space narrowing, hypertrophic osteophytes, and marrow edema in both the distal clavicle and the lateral acromial process. There is no AC joint capsular hypertrophy, and the adjacent supraspinatus tendon is intact. How should you interpret these findings in your report?
text: A 58-year-old with superior shoulder pain localized to the AC joint on cross-body adduction testing undergoes shoulder MRI. Coronal and axial images show joint-space narrowing, hypertrophic osteophytes, and marrow edema in both the distal clavicle and the lateral acromial process. There is no AC joint capsular hypertrophy, and the adjacent supraspinatus tendon is intact. How should you interpret these findings in your report? | A. AC joint osteoarthritis with acute-on-chronic change; the marrow edema in both articulating surfaces indicates recent stress or microtrauma. This finding is clinically relevant and should be matched to the patient's focal AC tenderness and cross-body adduction pain before concluding it is the pain generator, and inferior osteophytes should be assessed for contact with the cuff or bursa. | B. Incidental AC joint changes of no clinical significance; AC osteophytes are common incidental findings, but the presence of marrow edema indicates acute inflammation that may be symptomatic and warrant correlation with clinical exam. | C. AC joint septic arthritis requiring urgent intervention; while aggressive edema and effusion can suggest infection, uncomplicated osteoarthritis with reactive marrow edema is far more common in this age group without systemic signs. | D. Supraspinatus impingement from AC osteophytes requiring surgical distal clavicle resection; inferior AC osteophytes can contact the cuff, but this finding alone does not mandate surgery—correlation with symptoms, exam, and conservative treatment response guides referral. | A | AC joint osteoarthritis is common, but the clinically relevant finding is often marrow edema or capsular hypertrophy, not the mere presence of osteophytes. Match MRI findings to focal AC tenderness and cross-body adduction pain; imaging alone should not drive surgery. Always assess whether inferior osteophytes contact the rotator cuff or bursa, as this influences the surgical discussion. The distinction between asymptomatic degenerative changes and clinically important AC pathology rests on imaging sign (edema, capsular thickening) plus clinical correlation.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A massive chronic supraspinatus/infraspinatus tear has retraction to the glenoid, advanced fatty infiltration, and superior humeral migration. What is the best interpretation?
text: A massive chronic supraspinatus/infraspinatus tear has retraction to the glenoid, advanced fatty infiltration, and superior humeral migration. What is the best interpretation? | A. Low-grade tendinosis suitable for routine reassurance only | B. Cuff tear arthropathy pattern with limited primary repairability | C. Normal age-related finding that should not be reported | D. Isolated SLAP tear | B | Massive chronic cuff tear with advanced fatty atrophy and superior migration suggests cuff tear arthropathy and changes referral expectations away from simple primary repair.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A paralabral cyst with infraspinatus denervation edema is seen on shoulder MRI. Why is this management-relevant?
text: A paralabral cyst with infraspinatus denervation edema is seen on shoulder MRI. Why is this management-relevant? | A. It may indicate suprascapular nerve compression and should not be dismissed as an incidental small labral tear. | B. It proves the ACL is torn. | C. It means all rotator cuff tendons are normal. | D. It is always a contraindication to physical therapy. | A | Paralabral cysts can compress the suprascapular nerve, producing denervation changes. This is a referral-relevant finding even when the labral tear itself appears small.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 52-year-old with chronic shoulder pain undergoes MRI. Oblique coronal images show a fluid-distended subacromial-subdeltoid bursa with multiple small intra-bursal filling defects. The supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons are intact without full-thickness tear. Which additional finding should prompt you to reconsider the diagnosis beyond simple mechanical bursitis?
text: A 52-year-old with chronic shoulder pain undergoes MRI. Oblique coronal images show a fluid-distended subacromial-subdeltoid bursa with multiple small intra-bursal filling defects. The supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons are intact without full-thickness tear. Which additional finding should prompt you to reconsider the diagnosis beyond simple mechanical bursitis? | A. Mild AC joint osteophytes contacting the bursa, which explains reactive bursitis from subacromial impingement. | B. Synovitis with rice bodies on the bursal fluid-sensitive images, raising suspicion for an inflammatory or rheumatologic process rather than a structural cuff tear or impingement. | C. Low-signal calcific deposit within the supraspinatus tendon, indicating acute calcific tendinitis with secondary reactive bursitis. | D. Small bursal fluid collection measuring <2 mL, which is a normal variant not requiring clinical follow-up. | B | Bursitis is a finding, not always a diagnosis. Always pair bursal fluid assessment with cuff integrity, synovial character (rice bodies, enhancement), and marrow/capsular changes to direct the differential toward mechanical, inflammatory, or infectious etiologies.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 68-year-old male presents with acute shoulder pain after a minor fall. MRI shows diffuse humeral head marrow edema on PD FS images with a subtle subchondral low-signal band on T1 at the weight-bearing surface of the humeral head. The overlying articular cartilage appears intact. There is no history of prior shoulder trauma or steroid use. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: A 68-year-old male presents with acute shoulder pain after a minor fall. MRI shows diffuse humeral head marrow edema on PD FS images with a subtle subchondral low-signal band on T1 at the weight-bearing surface of the humeral head. The overlying articular cartilage appears intact. There is no history of prior shoulder trauma or steroid use. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Acute traumatic full-thickness rotator cuff tear with superior humeral migration, which would show a tendon defect with retraction. | B. Avascular necrosis of the humeral head, which typically shows a larger geographic zone of necrosis and is associated with corticosteroid use or sickle cell disease. | C. Subchondral insufficiency fracture (analogous to SIFK in the knee), occurring in osteoporotic bone under normal loading without a known prior major trauma. | D. Simple bone contusion from the fall, which would show diffuse marrow edema without a discrete low-T1 subchondral line. | C | A discrete low-T1 subchondral line (best seen on T1) with surrounding marrow edema in the appropriate clinical context (older patient, acute onset, no major trauma) should raise suspicion for insufficiency fracture. This changes weight-bearing and surgical considerations and may not fit the typical sports-medicine pain pattern.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: You are formulating the MRI impression for a primary care sports medicine clinician. Which statement best reflects how to structure a high-yield shoulder MRI impression for maximum clinical utility?
text: You are formulating the MRI impression for a primary care sports medicine clinician. Which statement best reflects how to structure a high-yield shoulder MRI impression for maximum clinical utility? | A. List all anatomic findings in order of location (AC joint, bursa, labrum, cuff) regardless of clinical relevance, to ensure comprehensive documentation. | B. Focus exclusively on the most obvious finding (e.g., 'mild AC joint arthritis') and defer mention of coexisting pathology to avoid cluttering the impression. | C. Avoid any reference to severity or urgency unless the finding is clearly life-threatening, so as not to over-triage routine findings. | D. Lead with the finding most likely to change management (e.g., 'Spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst with infraspinatus denervation edema') and include severity language, associated findings, and the next-step hierarchy so the clinician can prioritize referral and treatment decisions. | D | A useful shoulder MRI impression answers: (1) What is the dominant diagnosis? (2) How severe is it? (3) What associated findings matter? (4) What should not be missed? Lead with the finding that changes management, not the most obvious one—the second finding often matters more than the first.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 28-year-old competitive powerlifter feels a sudden 'pop' at the bottom of a heavy bench press, with the arm extended and abducted. He develops medial chest bruising and a soft, dropped-out anterior axillary fold. MRI shows a hematoma with a medially retracted tendon and no tissue at the lateral lip of the intertubercular groove. Which finding most directly indicates the need for surgical referral?
text: A 28-year-old competitive powerlifter feels a sudden 'pop' at the bottom of a heavy bench press, with the arm extended and abducted. He develops medial chest bruising and a soft, dropped-out anterior axillary fold. MRI shows a hematoma with a medially retracted tendon and no tissue at the lateral lip of the intertubercular groove. Which finding most directly indicates the need for surgical referral? | A. Feathery perifascial edema within the pectoralis major belly with intact architecture | B. Complete avulsion of the tendon from its humeral insertion with an empty bicipital groove | C. Fluid in the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa communicating with the joint | D. Intermediate signal and thickening of the pectoralis tendon without a discrete gap | B | The mechanism (eccentric load, arm extended/abducted at the bottom of a bench press), medial ecchymosis, and axillary-fold defect are classic for pectoralis major rupture, and the empty bicipital groove with a retracted tendon localizes a complete avulsion at the humeral insertion — the surgically relevant lesion that warrants repair in an active patient. Feathery intramuscular edema (A) is a grade 1 strain managed with rehab, and intermediate tendon signal without a gap (D) is tendinosis, not an avulsion; bursal fluid (C) is a cuff/bursa finding unrelated to the pectoralis.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 24-year-old wakeboarder is jerked forward when the tow rope catches with the arm flexed and abducted, and reports posterior axillary pain. MRI shows feathery edema PLUS a discrete fluid cleft with partial fiber disruption at the myotendinous junction of the latissimus dorsi/teres major, with most fibers intact and no retraction. How is this best characterized and managed?
text: A 24-year-old wakeboarder is jerked forward when the tow rope catches with the arm flexed and abducted, and reports posterior axillary pain. MRI shows feathery edema PLUS a discrete fluid cleft with partial fiber disruption at the myotendinous junction of the latissimus dorsi/teres major, with most fibers intact and no retraction. How is this best characterized and managed? | A. Grade 1 overstretch injury with no fiber disruption, managed with rest | B. Grade 2 partial myotendinous strain, generally managed conservatively | C. Deltoid denervation from axillary nerve injury | D. Pectoralis major humeral avulsion with an empty bicipital groove | B | Partial fiber disruption with a discrete fluid cleft but preserved continuity and no retraction is a grade 2 partial strain, and the myotendinous junction is the expected site; latissimus dorsi/teres major strains in water-skiers/wakeboarders and throwers are typically treated conservatively. A grade 1 injury (A) shows only perifascial feathery edema with intact architecture and no fiber disruption or fluid cleft, deltoid denervation (C) produces diffuse muscle edema in a nerve territory rather than an MTJ cleft, and a pectoralis avulsion (D) involves a different muscle and the bicipital groove.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: When is an MR arthrogram preferred over a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI?
text: When is an MR arthrogram preferred over a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI? | front: When is an MR arthrogram preferred over a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI? | back: When subtle labral/instability lesions, an articular-sided (undersurface) partial cuff tear, or the postoperative labrum are in question and non-contrast MRI is equivocal or discordant with the exam. Intra-articular contrast distends the joint and outlines the labrum.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What does a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI answer well?
text: What does a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI answer well? | front: What does a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI answer well? | back: Most primary care questions: cuff tendinosis/tears, subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis, AC joint disease, marrow/occult fracture, and findings supporting adhesive capsulitis.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which plane is the workhorse for supraspinatus/infraspinatus tears and the AC joint?
text: Which plane is the workhorse for supraspinatus/infraspinatus tears and the AC joint? | front: Which plane is the workhorse for supraspinatus/infraspinatus tears and the AC joint? | back: Oblique coronal (parallel to the supraspinatus). Trace the cuff from the musculotendinous junction to the greater tuberosity footprint.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which plane best shows the subscapularis, the biceps in/out of its groove, the labrum (Bankart), and Hill-Sachs?
text: Which plane best shows the subscapularis, the biceps in/out of its groove, the labrum (Bankart), and Hill-Sachs? | front: Which plane best shows the subscapularis, the biceps in/out of its groove, the labrum (Bankart), and Hill-Sachs? | back: Axial. The oblique sagittal is best for muscle bulk/fatty infiltration (Goutallier 'Y' view), which tendon is torn, the rotator interval, and acromial morphology.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which sequence makes a cuff tear most convincing, and why?
text: Which sequence makes a cuff tear most convincing, and why? | front: Which sequence makes a cuff tear most convincing, and why? | back: Fluid-sensitive fat-saturated (T2/PD FS): a tear is convincing when fluid-bright signal reaches a tendon surface or fills a defect.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the magic-angle pitfall in the shoulder?
text: What is the magic-angle pitfall in the shoulder? | front: What is the magic-angle pitfall in the shoulder? | back: Intermediate signal in the distal supraspinatus where it curves ~55° to the main field on short-TE (T1/PD) sequences. It resolves on T2 — do not call it a tear.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: For primary care sports medicine, what is the goal when reading a shoulder MRI?
text: For primary care sports medicine, what is the goal when reading a shoulder MRI? | front: For primary care sports medicine, what is the goal when reading a shoulder MRI? | back: Answer the management question — is there a structural/surgical lesion, a red flag, or a finding that changes the next step (referral, injection, rehab)? — rather than cataloguing every degenerative change.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which high-miss areas is a shoulder search pattern designed to catch?
text: Which high-miss areas is a shoulder search pattern designed to catch? | front: Which high-miss areas is a shoulder search pattern designed to catch? | back: Upper-border subscapularis, the biceps pulley / medial biceps subluxation, the posterior and posterosuperior labrum, suprascapular/spinoglenoid denervation, and the AC joint.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is satisfaction-of-search, and how do you guard against it on shoulder MRI?
text: What is satisfaction-of-search, and how do you guard against it on shoulder MRI? | front: What is satisfaction-of-search, and how do you guard against it on shoulder MRI? | back: Stopping after the first obvious finding (e.g., a supraspinatus tear) and missing a second lesion. Finish the full checklist every time — a cuff tear does not exclude a labral tear, a biceps lesion, or denervation.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Name three superior-labral 'don't-overcall' variants.
text: Name three superior-labral 'don't-overcall' variants. | front: Name three superior-labral 'don't-overcall' variants. | back: Sublabral recess, sublabral foramen, and the Buford complex (absent anterosuperior labrum + a cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you distinguish a sublabral recess from a SLAP tear?
text: How do you distinguish a sublabral recess from a SLAP tear? | front: How do you distinguish a sublabral recess from a SLAP tear? | back: A recess is smooth, medial, parallels the glenoid, and stops at the biceps anchor. A SLAP extends laterally into the labrum or posterior to the anchor with irregular margins.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How does tendinosis look versus a cuff tear?
text: How does tendinosis look versus a cuff tear? | front: How does tendinosis look versus a cuff tear? | back: Tendinosis = tendon thickening + intermediate signal WITHOUT a surface-reaching fluid-bright defect. A tear shows fluid-bright signal reaching a surface (partial) or spanning articular-to-bursal (full-thickness).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Articular- versus bursal-sided partial tear — which surface is which, and which is more common?
text: Articular- versus bursal-sided partial tear — which surface is which, and which is more common? | front: Articular- versus bursal-sided partial tear — which surface is which, and which is more common? | back: Articular = the deep surface facing the joint (the most common partial tear, 'PASTA'). Bursal = the superficial surface under the subacromial bursa.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How is partial-tear depth graded, and why does it matter?
text: How is partial-tear depth graded, and why does it matter? | front: How is partial-tear depth graded, and why does it matter? | back: Low-grade <50% vs high-grade >50% of tendon thickness. A high-grade partial behaves clinically closer to a significant structural lesion — not 'just tendinosis.'

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What five things should every full-thickness cuff tear report include?
text: What five things should every full-thickness cuff tear report include? | front: What five things should every full-thickness cuff tear report include? | back: (1) which tendon(s); (2) surface/thickness; (3) AP tear size; (4) retraction (Patte stage); (5) muscle atrophy / fatty infiltration (Goutallier grade). Surface and muscle quality are what change management.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What does the Patte stage describe?
text: What does the Patte stage describe? | front: What does the Patte stage describe? | back: Tendon retraction: Stage 1 near the footprint, Stage 2 to the humeral head, Stage 3 to the glenoid. More retraction = harder repair.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What Goutallier grade predicts poor healing / an irreparable tear, and where is it assessed?
text: What Goutallier grade predicts poor healing / an irreparable tear, and where is it assessed? | front: What Goutallier grade predicts poor healing / an irreparable tear, and where is it assessed? | back: Grade ≥3 (fat ≥ muscle). Assess on the oblique-sagittal 'Y' view at the scapular spine.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Medial subluxation of the biceps out of its groove should trigger a search for what?
text: Medial subluxation of the biceps out of its groove should trigger a search for what? | front: Medial subluxation of the biceps out of its groove should trigger a search for what? | back: An upper-border subscapularis tear / biceps pulley lesion — the classic 'hidden lesion.' Inspect the subscapularis on axial and sagittal images.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What does a normal labrum look like?
text: What does a normal labrum look like? | front: What does a normal labrum look like? | back: Triangular, uniformly low signal, firmly attached to the glenoid rim. Interpret superior labral signal in the context of age and sport before calling a SLAP tear.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is found — what associated finding must you check?
text: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is found — what associated finding must you check? | front: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is found — what associated finding must you check? | back: Infraspinatus denervation edema (acute) or fatty atrophy (chronic) from compression of the suprascapular nerve branch.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the classic bipolar lesion of an anterior shoulder dislocation?
text: What is the classic bipolar lesion of an anterior shoulder dislocation? | front: What is the classic bipolar lesion of an anterior shoulder dislocation? | back: An anteroinferior labral Bankart lesion plus a posterolateral humeral-head Hill-Sachs impaction.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Define Bankart, Perthes, ALPSA, and HAGL.
text: Define Bankart, Perthes, ALPSA, and HAGL. | front: Define Bankart, Perthes, ALPSA, and HAGL. | back: Bankart (soft-tissue) = anteroinferior labrum detached with torn periosteum; the osseous Bankart variant is an anteroinferior glenoid rim fracture (it drives the bone-loss/surgical-referral assessment). Perthes = labrum stripped but periosteum intact and minimally displaced (easily missed). ALPSA = labroligamentous complex displaced medially and 'rolled up' on the glenoid neck (chronic). HAGL = inferior glenohumeral ligament avulsed from the HUMERAL side ('J-sign' axillary pouch).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which instability findings push toward surgery and warrant CT quantification?
text: Which instability findings push toward surgery and warrant CT quantification? | front: Which instability findings push toward surgery and warrant CT quantification? | back: Significant glenoid bone loss (approaching ~20%, with lower subcritical thresholds increasingly used) or an engaging / off-track Hill-Sachs. CT (often 3D) quantifies bone loss best — MRI/MRA may underestimate it.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Where do you check the long head of biceps, and what is normal versus abnormal?
text: Where do you check the long head of biceps, and what is normal versus abnormal? | front: Where do you check the long head of biceps, and what is normal versus abnormal? | back: Centered in the bicipital groove on axial images. Fluid tracking only around the tendon reflects the joint's fluid; medial perching/subluxation out of the groove is abnormal and a red flag.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Medial biceps subluxation implies injury to what, and which tendon should you scrutinize?
text: Medial biceps subluxation implies injury to what, and which tendon should you scrutinize? | front: Medial biceps subluxation implies injury to what, and which tendon should you scrutinize? | back: The biceps pulley (coracohumeral / superior glenohumeral ligament sling). Scrutinize the upper-border subscapularis — the 'hidden lesion.'

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What MRI findings support adhesive capsulitis?
text: What MRI findings support adhesive capsulitis? | front: What MRI findings support adhesive capsulitis? | back: Obliteration of the subcoracoid fat triangle in the rotator interval, coracohumeral ligament thickening, and a thickened/edematous axillary pouch and capsule. Correlate with painful global ROM loss — the diagnosis is clinical.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: When does AC joint arthrosis matter on MRI?
text: When does AC joint arthrosis matter on MRI? | front: When does AC joint arthrosis matter on MRI? | back: When it correlates with focal AC tenderness: look for capsular hypertrophy, marrow edema on BOTH sides of the joint, and inferior osteophytes contacting the cuff. Asymptomatic AC degeneration is common — don't over-attribute pain to it.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What acromiohumeral interval values signal cuff insufficiency and cuff-tear arthropathy?
text: What acromiohumeral interval values signal cuff insufficiency and cuff-tear arthropathy? | front: What acromiohumeral interval values signal cuff insufficiency and cuff-tear arthropathy? | back: Normal >7 mm. <7 mm suggests cuff insufficiency with superior humeral migration. <5 mm is consistent with a chronic massive tear / cuff-tear arthropathy.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the imaging clue and the management pivot for calcific tendinitis?
text: What is the imaging clue and the management pivot for calcific tendinitis? | front: What is the imaging clue and the management pivot for calcific tendinitis? | back: A focal LOW-signal deposit on T1 AND T2 (easy to miss) with surrounding edema, often in the supraspinatus near the footprint. It is usually managed conservatively first (NSAIDs, activity modification, physical therapy); ultrasound-guided barbotage/aspiration or a subacromial injection is added for refractory or acutely painful (resorptive-phase) cases. The key point: it is NOT a structural cuff-repair problem. Radiographs/ultrasound show the deposit best.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Match the denervated muscle to the paralabral cyst location.
text: Match the denervated muscle to the paralabral cyst location. | front: Match the denervated muscle to the paralabral cyst location. | back: Spinoglenoid notch cyst → infraspinatus denervation. Suprascapular notch cyst → BOTH supraspinatus and infraspinatus.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Name shoulder MRI red flags that change management urgently.
text: Name shoulder MRI red flags that change management urgently. | front: Name shoulder MRI red flags that change management urgently. | back: Suspected septic arthritis/osteomyelitis, an aggressive marrow-replacing or soft-tissue mass, and acute occult fracture. Communicate directly and don't anchor on degenerative findings.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What makes a shoulder MRI impression 'management-changing'?
text: What makes a shoulder MRI impression 'management-changing'? | front: What makes a shoulder MRI impression 'management-changing'? | back: It states the structural diagnosis, its severity/repairability (tendon, size, retraction, muscle quality; bone loss; denervation), and the implied next step (rehab, injection, referral) — rather than a list of every degenerative change.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What are the three MRI grades of a muscle strain, and where do strains most commonly occur?
text: What are the three MRI grades of a muscle strain, and where do strains most commonly occur? | front: What are the three MRI grades of a muscle strain, and where do strains most commonly occur? | back: Grade 1 = perifascial 'feathery' edema with intact architecture; Grade 2 = partial tear with fiber disruption plus fluid/hematoma; Grade 3 = complete tear with retraction. The myotendinous junction is the most common site.

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the classic mechanism and the most surgically relevant tear location for a pectoralis major rupture?
text: What is the classic mechanism and the most surgically relevant tear location for a pectoralis major rupture? | front: What is the classic mechanism and the most surgically relevant tear location for a pectoralis major rupture? | back: Eccentric load with the arm extended/abducted — classically the bottom of a bench press. The most surgically relevant location is avulsion of the tendon from its humeral insertion at the lateral lip of the bicipital (intertubercular) groove; the sternocostal head tears most often (inferior sternocostal fibers first).

course_title: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What MRI sign indicates a complete humeral-sided avulsion of the pectoralis major, and what should the report state?
text: What MRI sign indicates a complete humeral-sided avulsion of the pectoralis major, and what should the report state? | front: What MRI sign indicates a complete humeral-sided avulsion of the pectoralis major, and what should the report state? | back: The 'empty bicipital groove' — the avulsed tendon is absent from the lateral lip of the intertubercular groove (partial/MTJ tears retain tendon there). Report which head(s), tear location (intramuscular vs MTJ vs humeral avulsion), completeness, and retraction; acute complete avulsions in active patients are referred for surgical repair.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A primary care sports medicine hip MRI course focused on management-changing interpretation: FAI and the labrum, cartilage, femoral-neck stress fracture and AVN, the abductor/hamstring/iliopsoas/adductor tendons, athletic pubalgia, and red flags. | Primary care sports medicine fellows

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Verify Protocol & Clinical Question
text: Confirm side, protocol, arthrogram status, available planes/sequences, and the exact management question before narrowing your attention. Let the symptom location steer the read. | Confirm correct patient, side, and any comparison imaging | Identify protocol: large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip, non-contrast vs MR arthrogram | Inventory planes and sequences: axial, coronal, sagittal, oblique-axial along the neck, T1, PD/T2 FS, STIR | Localize the symptom: anterior/groin, lateral, or posterior/buttock pain | State the management question: rehab, injection, surgical/ortho referral, or urgent escalation | Pain location tells you where to spend your time: groin = intra-articular or adductor/symphysis, lateral = abductor/GTPS, posterior = hamstring origin or sacrum. | If FAI or a labral tear is the question, confirm the oblique-axial-along-the-neck images exist before committing to an alpha-angle judgment.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Bones, Marrow & Stress Injury
text: Sweep the proximal femur, acetabulum, and pelvic ring first: T1 for marrow replacement, fluid-sensitive (STIR/T2 FS) for edema and fracture lines. This is where the highest-stakes findings hide. | Femoral neck for a stress fracture line, explicitly noting compression (inferomedial) vs TENSION (superolateral) side | Femoral head for a subchondral double-line/serpiginous lesion (AVN) or subchondral insufficiency-fracture line | Diffuse marrow edema pattern: BMES vs AVN vs insufficiency fracture | Apophyses/avulsion sites: ASIS, AIIS, ischial tuberosity, lesser trochanter | Sacrum and pubic rami for insufficiency/stress fracture; T1 review for marrow replacement | Tension-side (superolateral) femoral-neck stress fracture = non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral; compression-side is usually conservative. Always state the side. | Isolated femoral-head marrow edema is a differential, not a diagnosis: chase a subchondral line, a double-line rim, or a transient pattern before reassuring. A lesser-trochanter avulsion in an adult without trauma is a tumor red flag.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cartilage, Joint Surface & Effusion
text: Trace acetabular and femoral cartilage for delamination vs full-thickness loss, assess subchondral change and joint space, and characterize any effusion before turning to the labrum. | Acetabular cartilage, especially anterosuperior, for delamination (undermining fluid cleft) vs full-thickness defect | Femoral head cartilage surface integrity and the central/fovea region | Subchondral cysts and subchondral marrow change as load markers | Joint-space narrowing and osteophytes (early OA staging) | Characterize any effusion/synovitis; in the right clinical setting consider septic arthritis | Hip cartilage is thin; early delamination hides under an intact-looking surface and is the case where MR arthrography (with traction) earns its place. | Diffuse cartilage loss and joint-space narrowing predict poor outcomes from labral surgery and shift the referral target from preservation toward arthroplasty.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Labrum & FAI Morphology
text: Assess the labrum by quadrant, trace any paralabral cyst to its tear, and read the femoral/acetabular morphology (cam/pincer) that drives FAI management. Measure the alpha angle on the oblique-axial neck. | Anterosuperior labrum for a tear; distinguish it from a normal sublabral recess or chondrolabral variant | Paralabral cyst as a secondary sign pointing to an underlying tear | Cam: anterosuperior head-neck bump and elevated alpha angle (>55 degrees) on oblique-axial/radial images | Pincer: crossover sign, coxa profunda, acetabular retroversion/over-coverage (LCEA) | Adjacent acetabular rim cartilage for chondrolabral injury/delamination; note os acetabuli and herniation pit | Asymptomatic cam/pincer morphology and labral signal changes are common: report the morphology, but FAI is a clinical-plus-imaging diagnosis. | A paralabral cyst is your cue to hunt for the labral tear you may have skimmed past; the adjacent chondral status, not the tear alone, often drives the surgical decision.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Abductors, Lateral & Posterior Soft Tissue
text: Evaluate the gluteus medius/minimus footprint and peritrochanteric tissues for lateral pain, then the proximal hamstring origin for posterior pain, grading tears and retraction. | Gluteus medius/minimus footprints on the greater trochanter for tendinopathy, partial or full-thickness retracted tear, atrophy/fatty infiltration | Peritrochanteric bursal fluid and ITB / external snapping-hip clues | Proximal hamstring origin at the ischial tuberosity: COUNT tendons involved and measure retraction | Apply muscle-strain grading (1 feathery edema, 2 partial with fluid, 3 complete with retraction) | Sciatic nerve proximity to a retracted hamstring stump | Lateral hip pain (GTPS) is usually abductor tendinopathy/tearing, not primary bursitis: name the tendon, because a full-thickness retracted abductor tear is a surgical referral. | For the proximal hamstring, tendon count and retraction distance decide rehab vs surgical referral, so always state both.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Flexors, Adductors & Pubic Symphysis
text: Work through the anterior and groin structures: iliopsoas, rectus femoris/AIIS, adductors, and the pubic symphysis / rectus-adductor aponeurosis. | Iliopsoas: tendinosis, bursa distension, and internal snapping-hip clues | Rectus femoris / AIIS origin: strain, avulsion, or apophyseal injury (direct and indirect heads) | Adductor (especially longus) origin: tendinosis, partial or complete tear | Pubic symphysis: edema, secondary cleft sign, aponeurotic plate disruption (athletic pubalgia) | Differentiate intra-articular groin pain from extra-articular adductor/symphysis sources | Athletic pubalgia / core-muscle injury at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis is extra-articular and managed differently from a labral tear: inspect the symphysis on every groin-pain study and do not call the normal central primary cleft pathologic. | Anterior snapping hip is usually the iliopsoas tendon: pair tendinosis with bursa distension and the clinical snap, since the snap itself is a dynamic diagnosis.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Don't-Miss & Final Management Review
text: Run the safety sweep for findings that change disposition today, then translate the whole study into a clear management next step. | Re-check for tension-side femoral-neck stress fracture, occult/insufficiency fracture, and pre-collapse AVN | Effusion + synovitis + periarticular edema: consider septic arthritis (urgent aspiration) | Confluent low-T1 marrow replacement, periosteal reaction, or a soft-tissue mass: consider neoplasm/metastasis | Skeletally immature: SCFE (urgent) and Legg-Calve-Perthes; consider referred pelvic/oncologic pathology | Summarize: rehab/injection candidate, surgical/ortho referral, or urgent escalation | Negative radiographs do not exclude an occult or tension-side femoral-neck fracture; MRI is the sensitive test and the disposition can be surgical. | Confluent low-T1 marrow darker than muscle or an intervertebral disc, or a soft-tissue mass, outranks every degenerative finding; do not let satisfaction of search bury it. A subtle adolescent SCFE is non-weight-bearing plus urgent referral.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Hip MRI Basics
text: Protocols, planes, sequences, and when arthrography changes management | Large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip protocol | Planes: axial, coronal, sagittal, and oblique-axial along the femoral neck | Core sequences: T1, PD/T2 fat-sat, STIR, and what each is best for | Fluid vs fat: reading signal behavior across sequences | Non-contrast MRI vs MR arthrography: when labral/chondral detail changes management | Differentiate a large-FOV pelvis screen from a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol and state which the clinical question requires | Identify the oblique-axial plane prescribed along the femoral neck and explain why it is essential for cam morphology and the alpha angle | Match each sequence (T1 for marrow/anatomy, PD/T2 FS and STIR for fluid/edema) to the tissue question it best answers, and distinguish STIR from chemical fat-sat over a large pelvic FOV | Decide when direct MR arthrography adds management-relevant labral or chondral detail versus when non-contrast MRI suffices

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Ordering or reading a dedicated small-FOV hip when the pain is poorly localized or possibly referred
text: Use a large-FOV pelvis screen first when the pain generator is unclear or extra-articular/referred causes are in play (sacrum, marrow, contralateral); reserve the small-FOV dedicated hip (with oblique-axial along the neck) for a focused intra-articular question.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Defaulting to MR arthrography for every labral question, or assuming it is always superior
text: Ask whether the added detail changes the next step. Most 3T non-contrast hips answer the labral/chondral question; reserve direct arthrography (optionally with traction) for equivocal cases where confirming a tear or chondral delamination would shift management toward surgical referral.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Treating any bright signal on a T2/PD FS sequence as pathologic fluid
