text: Confirm fluid on more than one sequence: true fluid follows CSF (dark on T1, bright on fluid-sensitive); fat is bright on T1/PD and suppresses on FS/STIR. Magic-angle and chemical-shift artifacts can mimic signal abnormality.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip protocol
text:
### Large-FOV Pelvis Screen vs Small-FOV Dedicated Hip Protocol

The first decision when you open a hip study: **what protocol was actually ordered?** These are not interchangeable.

**Large-FOV pelvis screen** (~32–40 cm FOV, both hips on every slice):
- Best for **bilateral and referred problems**: insufficiency/sacral fractures, sacroiliitis, occult marrow lesions, muscle/tendon injury, an unclear hip-vs-back-vs-pelvis story
- Catches the **contralateral hip** and the pelvic ring you would otherwise miss
- Trade-off: lower spatial resolution — labrum and cartilage are blurred

**Small-FOV dedicated hip** (~16–20 cm FOV, single joint, thin slices):
- Built to resolve the **labrum, chondral surface, and cam/pincer morphology**
- The protocol you want for **FAI, labral tear, chondral delamination** workups

| Question | Order |
|---|---|
| "Where is the pain coming from?" | Large-FOV screen |
| "Is the labrum/cartilage torn?" | Small-FOV dedicated |

If the clinical question is intra-articular, a screen alone will underdiagnose it. | If you are chasing a labral or chondral diagnosis, a large-FOV pelvis screen is the wrong tool — its resolution blurs the joint surfaces; reorder a small-FOV dedicated hip before you commit to a management plan.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Planes: axial, coronal, sagittal, and oblique-axial along the femoral neck
text:
### Planes: Axial, Coronal, Sagittal, and Oblique-Axial Along the Femoral Neck

**Coronal** — your workhorse for **marrow, femoral head/neck, abductors, and superior labrum**; STIR/T2 FS here is the screen for stress fractures and AVN.

**Sagittal** — anterior–posterior labrum, anterior femoral head-neck junction, and chondral surfaces.

**Axial (true)** — anterior/posterior structures, iliopsoas, and anterior/posterior labrum.

**Oblique-axial along the femoral neck** — slices prescribed *parallel to the long axis of the femoral neck* (off the coronal). This is the **money plane for cam morphology**:
- It lays the **anterosuperior head–neck junction** flat so you can measure the **alpha angle** without obliquity error
- **Alpha angle >55°** = cam morphology (loss of femoral head–neck offset)
- Radial reformats add the clock-face map of where the bump is

Without this plane, a true-axial slice cuts the neck obliquely and **over- or under-estimates the alpha angle**, leading you to mislabel FAI. | Cam morphology lives at the anterosuperior head-neck junction — only the oblique-axial (or radial) plane measures the alpha angle correctly; never quote an alpha angle off a standard axial slice.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Core sequences: T1, PD/T2 fat-sat, STIR, and what each is best for
text:
### Core Sequences: T1, PD/T2 Fat-Sat, STIR — and What Each Is Best For

Match the **sequence to the tissue question**:

| Sequence | Reads | Use it for |
|---|---|---|
| **T1** | Anatomy + **marrow fat** | Normal fatty marrow = bright; **red flag = confluent low-T1 darker than muscle/disc** (tumor/marrow replacement); fracture lines |
| **PD/T2 fat-sat** | **Fluid + edema**, cartilage | Labral/chondral detail, joint effusion, bone marrow edema |
| **STIR** | **Fluid/edema**, robust fat suppression | Stress fracture, AVN, marrow edema over a **large FOV** |

**STIR vs chemical (spectral) fat-sat:** chemical fat-sat is sharper and higher-resolution but **fails at the edges of a large FOV and near metal** (heterogeneous B0). STIR suppresses fat uniformly across a wide field and around hardware, so it is the **edema screen of choice on large-FOV pelvis** imaging. On a tight, dedicated hip, PD FS gives you the crisp cartilage/labral picture.

Think: **T1 = anatomy/marrow, fluid-sensitive sequences = pathology/edema.** | On a large-FOV pelvis or any study near hardware, trust STIR over chemical fat-sat — failed fat suppression at the field edge can hide or mimic marrow edema and send you down the wrong path.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Fluid vs fat: reading signal behavior across sequences
text:
### Fluid vs Fat: Reading Signal Behavior Across Sequences

You confirm what a structure *is* by tracking its signal across sequences, not from one image.

**Fluid** (effusion, edema, cyst, simple fluid):
- **T1 dark**, **T2/PD FS bright**, **STIR bright**
- "Follows fluid" = bright on fluid-sensitive, dark on T1

**Fat** (normal marrow, lipoma, fat within a lesion):
- **T1 bright**, bright on conventional (non-fat-sat) **T2/PD**, and **drops out (goes dark) on fat-sat / STIR**
- The fat-sat "drop" is the proof it is fat

| | T1 | T2/PD (non-FS) | Fat-sat / STIR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fluid/edema | dark | bright | bright |
| Fat | bright | bright | **dark** |

**Why it matters:** a **T1-bright** marrow signal that **suppresses** on fat-sat is reassuring fat. A lesion staying **bright on fat-sat** is not fat — characterize it. And **edema-only** marrow (bright on fluid-sensitive, no T1 fracture line) is a different management animal than a true subchondral fracture. | Always pair a fat-sat sequence with T1: a marrow signal that is bright on T1 and suppresses on fat-sat is fat (benign); one that stays bright on fat-sat demands characterization.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Non-contrast MRI vs MR arthrography: when labral/chondral detail changes management
text:
### Non-Contrast MRI vs MR Arthrography: When It Changes Management

**MR arthrography (MRA)** = intra-articular dilute gadolinium that **distends the joint and outlines the labrum and chondral surface**, letting contrast track into tears.

**When MRA earns its needle:**
- The clinical question is a **surgically relevant labral or chondrolabral tear** (younger patient, FAI, positive impingement signs, considering arthroscopy)
- You need to confirm a **subtle labral detachment or chondral delamination** that non-contrast may miss
- High-resolution **3T non-contrast** can approach MRA for the labrum and is often tried first

**When non-contrast is enough:**
- **Marrow** questions — stress fracture, AVN, transient BME, occult fracture, tumor — contrast adds nothing
- **Tendon/muscle** injury (abductors, hamstrings)
- **Advanced OA** — once cartilage is gone, distending the joint changes no decision

**Management hinge:** order MRA when a positive labral/chondral finding would **push toward arthroscopy or change the operative plan** — not as a reflex. | Reach for MR arthrography only when a labral or chondral finding would change the surgical decision; for marrow, tendon, or advanced-OA questions it adds cost and a needle with no management payoff.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Hip & Pelvis Anatomy
text: Labrum, capsuloligamentous complex, the muscle-tendon groups, and variants that mimic pathology | Acetabulum, labrum, chondrolabral junction, femoral head/neck, and articular cartilage | Capsule, zona orbicularis, iliofemoral ligament, and ligamentum teres | Muscle-tendon groups: abductors (gluteus medius/minimus), flexors (iliopsoas, rectus femoris/AIIS) | Hamstring origin, adductors, and the pubic symphysis / rectus abdominis-adductor aponeurosis | Bursae around the hip and pelvis (trochanteric, iliopsoas) | Normal variants that mimic pathology: os acetabuli, supra-acetabular fossa, sublabral sulcus, stellate lesion, herniation pit | Identify the acetabular labrum, chondrolabral junction, articular cartilage, ligamentum teres, and the capsuloligamentous complex on standard planes | Locate the major periarticular muscle-tendon groups (abductors, iliopsoas, rectus femoris/AIIS, hamstring origin, adductors) and their attachment sites, including the gluteus medius/minimus footprints on the greater trochanter | Distinguish common normal variants (sublabral sulcus, supra-acetabular fossa, stellate lesion, herniation pit, os acetabuli) from true labral, chondral, or marrow pathology | Recognize the rectus abdominis-adductor aponeurosis at the pubic symphysis as the anatomic crossroads for athletic pubalgia

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling a supra-acetabular fossa, central acetabular fossa, or normal stellate lesion a cartilage defect
text: These are predictable midline/bare-area variants with no surrounding marrow edema; the cotyloid fossa is non-articular and normally contains fat and ligamentum teres. True chondral loss is eccentric, weight-bearing, and usually has subchondral reaction.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reading a herniation pit at the femoral neck as an aggressive lesion
text: A herniation pit is a small, well-corticated subcortical cyst at the anterosuperior head-neck junction (T1-low/T2-high), classically benign and often a marker of cam-type FAI; note it but do not escalate to a tumor workup.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling every focal fluid-signal cleft at the labral base a tear
text: A normal sublabral sulcus/recess follows fluid signal but lacks the morphology, displacement, and paralabral cyst of a true tear; smooth contrast that does not extend into labral substance on arthrography favors a normal recess. Correlate with the typical anterosuperior location of true tears.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Acetabulum, labrum, chondrolabral junction, femoral head/neck, and articular cartilage
text:
### Acetabulum, Labrum, Chondrolabral Junction, Femoral Head/Neck & Cartilage

The **acetabular labrum** is a triangular fibrocartilaginous rim — uniformly **low signal** on all sequences — best seen on **radial and oblique-axial** images. It deepens the socket and seals the joint. The **chondrolabral junction** is where labrum meets hyaline cartilage; this is the **anterosuperior** weight-bearing zone where most pathologic tears and FAI rim chondral damage occur.

- **Femoral head**: spherical, fatty marrow (bright T1). Loss of sphericity at the head-neck junction (asphericity/bump) suggests **cam morphology**.
- **Femoral neck**: scrutinize cortex on T1/fluid-sensitive — tension-side (superolateral) edema or a fracture line is a red flag for a high-risk stress fracture.
- **Articular cartilage**: thin, intermediate signal; assess on fluid-sensitive and (best) **MR arthrogram**.

| Structure | Normal signal | Key sequence |
|---|---|---|
| Labrum | Uniform low | Radial / oblique-axial |
| Cartilage | Intermediate | Fluid-sensitive / arthrogram |

**Management:** anterosuperior chondrolabral disruption with adjacent cartilage delamination shifts FAI toward surgical referral; an intact labrum and cartilage favor conservative care. | Anterosuperior chondrolabral-junction tears with adjacent cartilage delamination drive the surgical weighting in FAI — location of the labral abnormality matters more than its mere presence.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Capsule, zona orbicularis, iliofemoral ligament, and ligamentum teres
text:
### Capsule, Zona Orbicularis, Iliofemoral Ligament & Ligamentum Teres

The **capsuloligamentous complex** stabilizes the hip and is read on oblique-axial and coronal images.

- **Zona orbicularis**: circular low-signal capsular condensation that **waists** the femoral neck on coronal images — looks like a constriction; do not call it a tear or a paralabral cyst.
- **Iliofemoral ligament (Y of Bigelow)**: the strongest anterior capsular thickening; primary restraint to **extension and external rotation**. Anterior capsule/iliofemoral integrity matters after dislocation or hip arthroscopy (an unrepaired capsulotomy can cause **microinstability**).
- **Ligamentum teres**: runs from the **fovea capitis** to the **transverse acetabular ligament / margins of the acetabular notch**; carries a small artery. Best seen on oblique-axial/coronal; tears (sprain → full rupture) cause mechanical pain and can be a hidden source of groin pain in athletes.

**Management:** a full ligamentum teres tear or an unrepaired anterior capsulotomy with instability symptoms warrants arthroscopy referral; an isolated zona orbicularis is **normal** and needs no action. | The zona orbicularis is a normal circular capsular waist around the neck — mistaking it for a tear or cyst is a classic over-call that sends a normal hip to arthroscopy.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Muscle-tendon groups: abductors (gluteus medius/minimus), flexors (iliopsoas, rectus femoris/AIIS)
text:
### Muscle-Tendon Groups: Abductors & Flexors

**Abductors (gluteus medius/minimus):** insert on the **greater trochanter** — medius onto the lateral and superoposterior facets, minimus onto the anterior facet. They are the **rotator cuff of the hip**. On fluid-sensitive coronal/axial images look for tendinosis (thickening/signal), partial tears (footprint signal), and **full-thickness retracted tears** with a fluid gap.

**Flexors:**
- **Iliopsoas**: psoas + iliacus joining to insert on the **lesser trochanter**; the tendon lies anterior to the hip and can cause snapping or bursitis.
- **Rectus femoris**: two heads — **direct head from the AIIS** (anterior inferior iliac spine) and **indirect/reflected head from the superior acetabular ridge/rim**. Proximal strains and AIIS apophyseal avulsions are common in kicking/sprinting athletes.

**Management:** a **full-thickness, retracted gluteus medius/minimus tear** (true cuff failure) is a surgical-referral lesion; tendinosis and low-grade partial tears get rehab. Distinguish an abductor tear from simple trochanteric bursitis — management diverges. | A full-thickness retracted abductor (gluteus medius/minimus) tear is the hip's rotator-cuff failure and earns surgical referral — don't bury it under the label 'trochanteric bursitis.'

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Hamstring origin, adductors, and the pubic symphysis / rectus abdominis-adductor aponeurosis
text:
### Hamstring Origin, Adductors & the Rectus Abdominis–Adductor Aponeurosis

**Proximal hamstring origin**: the **conjoint tendon (semitendinosus + biceps femoris long head)** and the **semimembranosus** arise from the **ischial tuberosity**. On oblique-coronal/axial fluid-sensitive images, document **how many of the tendons are torn** and the **retraction distance** — both drive surgery.

**Adductors**: longus, brevis, magnus, gracilis, and pectineus arise from the **pubic body/inferior ramus**; adductor longus strains are the classic groin injury.

**Rectus abdominis–adductor aponeurosis** at the **pubic symphysis**: the rectus abdominis and adductor longus blend into a common aponeurotic plate. This is the **athletic-pubalgia ("sports hernia") crossroads** — disruption here produces the **secondary cleft sign** (fluid tracking inferolaterally from the symphysis on coronal fluid-sensitive images).

**Management:** proximal hamstring avulsion of **≥2 tendons with ≥2 cm retraction** warrants surgical referral; partial/single-tendon injuries get rehab. Aponeurotic/secondary-cleft injuries go to a core-muscle-injury (athletic pubalgia) specialist. | Proximal hamstring referral threshold: ≥2 tendons torn AND ≥2 cm retraction → surgeon; below that, rehab. Always report tendon count and retraction explicitly.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Bursae around the hip and pelvis (trochanteric, iliopsoas)
text:
### Bursae: Trochanteric & Iliopsoas

Bursae are normally collapsed; a thin sliver of fluid is physiologic. They become conspicuous (bright on fluid-sensitive, fluid-equivalent) when inflamed.

- **Trochanteric (peritrochanteric) bursa**: lies superficial to the greater trochanter beneath the gluteus maximus/IT band. Distension is part of **greater trochanteric pain syndrome**. Crucially, **always interrogate the underlying gluteus medius/minimus tendons** — bursal fluid is frequently the visible flag for an abductor tendon tear, not an isolated diagnosis.
- **Iliopsoas bursa**: largest in the body; lies deep to the iliopsoas tendon, anterior to the joint. Communicates with the hip in ~15%, so a **distended iliopsoas bursa can be a marker of intra-articular pathology** (effusion, synovitis, OA). Anteromedial location; can cause snapping or femoral-nerve/vessel mass effect when large.

**Management:** image-guided injection helps both diagnosis and treatment, but treat the **driver** — repair an underlying abductor tear; address the joint pathology feeding an iliopsoas bursa rather than just aspirating. | Peritrochanteric bursal fluid is a signpost, not a diagnosis — always check the abductor tendons underneath before settling on 'trochanteric bursitis.'

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Normal variants that mimic pathology: os acetabuli, supra-acetabular fossa, sublabral sulcus, stellate lesion, herniation pit
text:
### Normal Variants — Don't Call Them Pathology

These benign findings are routinely over-called. Recognize them to avoid needless arthrograms and referrals.

| Variant | Location | How to call it normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Os acetabuli** | Anterosuperior acetabular rim | Corticated ossicle, smooth margins (vs. acute rim fracture — sharp margins, marrow edema) |
| **Supra-acetabular fossa** | Acetabular **dome (12 o'clock)** | Midline cartilage-filled notch, smooth — not a chondral defect |
| **Sublabral sulcus/recess** | Posteroinferior / anteroinferior junction | Smooth linear cleft paralleling the labral base, **no displacement or paralabral cyst** |
| **Stellate lesion** | **Superomedial** acetabular roof — medial to the supra-acetabular fossa, continuous with the acetabular notch | Star-shaped bare area of absent cartilage, clean margins, no marrow edema — a midline/bare-area variant, NOT the anterosuperior weight-bearing zone where true tears live |
| **Herniation pit (fibrocystic pit)** | Anterosuperior femoral neck | Small T2-bright, T1-dark corticated cyst; common with **cam FAI** but often incidental |

**Pitfall:** a cleft at the **anterosuperior** chondrolabral junction is NOT a safe "normal variant" — that's where real tears live. Location separates variant from lesion.

**Management:** isolated variants → reassure, correlate clinically, no surgical referral. A symptomatic herniation pit should prompt a look for **cam morphology / alpha angle >55°**. | Variant vs. pathology comes down to location: a posteroinferior sublabral sulcus is benign, but the same-looking cleft anterosuperiorly is the prime site for a true tear — never reflexively call an anterosuperior cleft a normal variant.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Search Pattern Overview
text: A repeatable, satisfaction-of-search-proof read for groin, lateral, and posterior hip pain | Why a fixed search order beats lesion-spotting | The seven-step hip/pelvis search pattern | Anchor slices: the oblique-axial neck and the mid-coronal joint | Localizing by region: anterior/groin vs lateral vs posterior/buttock pain | Satisfaction-of-search traps and blind spots (tension-side femoral neck, sacrum, symphysis, marrow) | Translating findings into rehab vs injection vs referral vs urgent | Apply a reproducible seven-step hip and pelvis MRI search pattern to every study | Use pain location (groin, lateral, posterior) to weight the search toward the likely pain generator | Explain how satisfaction-of-search causes missed second findings (e.g., a tension-side stress fracture behind an obvious labral tear) and adopt a habit that defeats it | Convert the read into a management hierarchy rather than an anatomic list

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Stopping the read once a labral tear or trochanteric bursitis is found
text: Labral tears are common and frequently incidental; the obvious finding is exactly when a tension-side femoral neck stress fracture, early AVN, marrow-replacing lesion, or sacral stress fracture gets missed. Finish all seven steps every time.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reviewing only the small-FOV hip and ignoring the pelvic ring, contralateral side, and soft-tissue edges
text: Sacral stress fractures, contralateral pathology, marrow disease, and soft-tissue masses live outside the small FOV. Always review the large-FOV/coronal STIR of the whole pelvis and weight the read by symptom location.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Why a fixed search order beats lesion-spotting
text:
### Why a Fixed Search Order Beats Lesion-Spotting

The natural instinct is to **chase the obvious finding** — a labral tear, a big effusion — and then quit. That is **satisfaction of search**, and on hip/pelvis MRI it is how the high-stakes lesion gets missed: the eye that locks onto a labral tear glides right past a **tension-side femoral-neck stress fracture** two slices away.

A reproducible order forces you to **visit every compartment regardless of where your attention is pulled**, so the second (often more dangerous) finding still gets seen.

**Why it works:**
- **Coverage, not recall** — you check structures because they are next on the list, not because you remembered them.
- **Calibrates attention** — the dangerous compartment (bone/marrow) is visited *early*, while attention is freshest.
- **Reproducible dictation** — the report writes itself in a fixed sequence reviewers can audit.

**Management impact:** lesion-spotting tends to over-call the symptomatic structure and under-call the silent emergency. A fixed order keeps you from discharging a hip to rehab when the marrow held a surgical fracture. | The first finding you see is rarely the one that changes disposition — a fixed order exists precisely to make you keep looking after you have already found something.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: The seven-step hip/pelvis search pattern
text:
### The Seven-Step Hip/Pelvis Search Pattern

Drive every study through the same sequence. **Bone and marrow go early** (step 2), while attention is freshest, because that is where the disposition-changing lesions hide.

| Step | Region | High-yield target |
|---|---|---|
| **1 Verify** | Protocol & question | Side, arthrogram status, oblique-axial neck present, pain location |
| **2 Bones/Marrow** | Femur, acetabulum, ring | **Tension-side neck fracture**, AVN, SIFK, T1 marrow replacement |
| **3 Cartilage** | Joint surface, effusion | Delamination vs full-thickness loss, complex/septic effusion |
| **4 Labrum/FAI** | Labrum, morphology | Tear by quadrant, **alpha angle >55 deg**, rim chondral injury |
| **5 Abductors/Hamstring** | Lateral & posterior | Full-thickness retracted abductor tear, hamstring tendon count |
| **6 Flexors/Adductors** | Anterior & groin | Iliopsoas, rectus/AIIS, **rectus-adductor aponeurosis** |
| **7 Don't-Miss** | Safety sweep + plan | Re-check neck/AVN/marrow/ring, then state disposition |

**Use both T1 and fluid-sensitive on every step** — T1 for marrow replacement, STIR/T2 FS for edema and fracture lines. | Always run bone/marrow as step 2, not last — the femoral-neck fracture and the confluent marrow lesion are the findings that change today's disposition, and fatigue-at-the-end is where they get missed.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Anchor slices: the oblique-axial neck and the mid-coronal joint
text:
### Anchor Slices: The Oblique-Axial Neck and the Mid-Coronal Joint

Two slices orient the entire read. Find them first; everything else hangs off them.

**Mid-coronal joint** — the workhorse for:
- **Femoral-neck fracture line** — and which cortex it touches (inferomedial **compression** vs superolateral **tension**).
- **Superior acetabular cartilage** and the superolateral labrum.
- **Abductor footprints** on the greater trochanter.
- Marrow signal of head, neck, and acetabular roof on one image.

**Oblique-axial along the femoral neck** — the dedicated plane for **FAI cam morphology**:
- Anterosuperior **head-neck junction bump** (cam).
- **Alpha angle**, measured here (or on radial reformats): **>55 deg is abnormal**.
- A standard (non-oblique) axial foreshortens the neck and **misrepresents** the bump and angle.

**Before you commit to an alpha-angle call, confirm the oblique-axial-along-the-neck (or radial) images actually exist** — large-FOV pelvis screens often lack them. | An alpha angle measured on a routine axial instead of a dedicated oblique-axial-along-the-neck or radial plane is unreliable — if the dedicated plane is missing, say so rather than report a number you cannot defend.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Localizing by region: anterior/groin vs lateral vs posterior/buttock pain
text:
### Localizing by Region: Anterior/Groin vs Lateral vs Posterior/Buttock

Pain location tells you **where to spend your time** — it weights the search without replacing it.

| Pain location | Weight the search toward | Common culprits |
|---|---|---|
| **Anterior / groin** | Intra-articular + adductor/symphysis | Labral tear, FAI, **athletic pubalgia (rectus-adductor aponeurosis)**, iliopsoas |
| **Lateral** | Peritrochanteric | **Abductor (glute med/min) tendinopathy/tear**, GTPS, ITB |
| **Posterior / buttock** | Hamstring origin + sacrum | **Proximal hamstring tear**, ischial bursitis, **sacral stress fracture**, deep gluteal/sciatic |

**Key discriminator for groin pain:** intra-articular (labrum/cartilage) vs extra-articular (**adductor longus origin, pubic symphysis**). They look adjacent but are managed completely differently.

Weighting means you read *more carefully* in the suspected region — it does **not** mean skipping the rest. Lateral pain still gets a marrow sweep; the sacrum and symphysis are exactly where referred pain and second lesions hide. | Groin pain has an intra-articular and an extra-articular answer — read the labrum AND the rectus-adductor aponeurosis/symphysis on every groin study, because the management diverges sharply.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Satisfaction-of-search traps and blind spots (tension-side femoral neck, sacrum, symphysis, marrow)
text:
### Satisfaction-of-Search Traps (Tension-Side Neck, Sacrum, Symphysis, Marrow)

These are the lesions that **hide behind the obvious finding** and change disposition. Defeat satisfaction of search by *always* completing the fixed order and revisiting these on step 7.

**The classic traps:**
- **Tension-side (superolateral) femoral-neck stress fracture** — a *surgical emergency*; easy to skim past once you have found a labral tear. Always state which cortex the line touches.
- **Sacrum / pubic rami** — insufficiency and stress fractures sit at the edge of FOV and field of attention; check T1 marrow there.
- **Pubic symphysis** — edema and the **secondary cleft sign** (athletic pubalgia) are missed when the eye stops at the hip joint.
- **Marrow** — **confluent low-T1 marrow darker than muscle or disc** is a tumor red flag that outranks every degenerative finding. A **lesser-trochanter avulsion in an adult without trauma** is a marrow-lesion red flag (pathologic fracture), not a sprain.

**Defeat tactic:** a deliberate second pass dedicated only to bone/marrow and the pelvic ring, after the soft-tissue read. | After you have found the finding that explains the symptoms, do one more dedicated marrow/bone pass — that is the pass that catches the tension-side neck fracture and the confluent low-T1 tumor.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Translating findings into rehab vs injection vs referral vs urgent
text:
### Translating Findings Into Rehab vs Injection vs Referral vs Urgent

Every read ends with a **disposition**, ordered from most to least urgent. Sort the study into one bucket.

**URGENT (escalate today):**
- **Tension-side (superolateral) femoral-neck stress fracture** — non-weight-bearing + urgent surgical referral.
- **Pre-collapse AVN** (double-line sign) — the **ARCO collapse hinge**; pre-collapse is joint-preserving, post-collapse (subchondral fracture/crescent) trends to arthroplasty.
- **SIFK** (subchondral fracture line, collapse risk), septic effusion, SCFE, **confluent low-T1 marrow / mass**.

**SURGICAL/ORTHO REFERRAL:**
- **Full-thickness retracted abductor tear**; proximal hamstring tear by **tendon count + retraction** threshold.
- FAI where **rim chondral injury** drives the surgical weighting.

**INJECTION:** focal tendinopathy, bursitis, symptomatic labral tear as a temporizing/diagnostic step.

**REHAB:** **compression-side (inferomedial)** neck stress reaction, **transient BME (no fracture line)**, muscle strains grade 1-2, asymptomatic cam/pincer. | Transient bone marrow edema (no fracture line) is rehab; a subchondral fracture line (SIFK) or a superolateral neck line is urgent — the presence or absence of a fracture line is the hinge between conservative and surgical.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Bones, Marrow & Stress Injury
text: Tension-side stress fracture, AVN, marrow-edema syndromes, insufficiency fracture, and avulsions | Femoral-neck stress fracture: compression (inferomedial) vs TENSION (superolateral) side — a surgical emergency | AVN of the femoral head: double-line sign, pattern, and ARCO staging (pre- vs post-collapse) | Transient osteoporosis / bone-marrow-edema syndrome (BMES) | Subchondral insufficiency fracture of the femoral head | Apophyseal avulsions (ASIS, AIIS, ischial tuberosity, lesser trochanter) | Sacral and pubic-ramus stress / insufficiency fracture | Differentiate compression-side from tension-side femoral-neck stress fractures and recognize the tension side as a surgical emergency requiring urgent referral and protected weight-bearing | Recognize the AVN double-line sign and stage femoral-head osteonecrosis using ARCO (pre- vs post-collapse) | Distinguish transient bone-marrow-edema syndrome from AVN and from subchondral insufficiency fracture | Identify apophyseal avulsion sites and the associated muscle origin, and recognize a lesser-trochanter avulsion in an adult without major trauma as a red flag for an underlying marrow-replacing lesion | Recognize sacral and pubic-ramus stress fractures as causes of buttock/groin pain in runners and the female athlete triad

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling all femoral-neck marrow edema a stress reaction and clearing the athlete to load
text: Find and side the fracture line. A superolateral (tension-side) line is at high risk of completion and displacement: it is non-weight-bearing and an urgent orthopedic referral, not progressive loading. The side determines management and must be stated.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reading marrow edema with a subchondral double line as simply transient osteoporosis
text: A double-line sign (outer low-signal sclerotic rim + inner T2-high granulation line) and a serpiginous demarcated lesion indicate AVN, not BMES; BMES shows diffuse edema without a demarcating interface and is self-limited. Misclassification changes prognosis and follow-up.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling diffuse femoral-head/neck marrow edema pure BMES and stopping
text: In an older or osteopenic patient, exclude a subchondral insufficiency fracture (low-signal subchondral line paralleling the cortex with surrounding edema); it carries a real collapse risk and is managed differently from self-limited BMES.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Femoral-neck stress fracture: compression (inferomedial) vs TENSION (superolateral) side — a surgical emergency
text:
### Femoral-Neck Stress Fracture: Compression vs Tension

A runner or recruit with groin/anterior-hip pain and a normal radiograph needs **fluid-sensitive MRI** (coronal STIR/T2 fat-sat + T1). Marrow edema alone is a stress *reaction*; a discrete **low-signal fracture line** on T1 defines a stress *fracture*.

Location drives management:

| Type | Site | Risk | Plan |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compression** | **Infero-medial** neck | Low (compressive load) | Protected weight-bearing, close follow-up |
| **Tension** | **Supero-lateral** neck | High — completion/displacement | **Surgical emergency → percutaneous fixation** |

- Grade the line: extent across the neck and any cortical break predict instability.
- Any **superolateral** line, a line crossing **>50%** of the neck width, or a fracture gap warrants urgent orthopedic referral and **non-weight-bearing** until seen.
- Watch for the **female athlete triad** (RED-S) as the driver. | A superolateral (tension-side) femoral-neck fracture line is a surgical emergency — make the patient non-weight-bearing and call ortho the same day, because completion can displace and cause AVN.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: AVN of the femoral head: double-line sign, pattern, and ARCO staging (pre- vs post-collapse)
text:
### AVN of the Femoral Head: Double-Line Sign & ARCO Staging

Suspect osteonecrosis with steroid use, alcohol, sickle cell, or prior trauma. The earliest MRI change is a **serpiginous reactive interface** rimming the necrotic antero-superior segment.

- **Double-line sign** (T2): outer **low-signal** sclerotic rim + inner **high-signal** hyperemic/granulation line — essentially pathognomonic.
- T1 shows a geographic subchondral lesion with a low-signal margin; the enclosed marrow may retain near-normal fat early.

**ARCO staging — the hinge is collapse:**

| Stage | Finding |
|---|---|
| I | MRI+ / radiograph normal |
| II | Sclerosis/cysts, **no** collapse |
| **III** | **Subchondral fracture / crescent sign → collapse** |
| IV | Secondary osteoarthritis |

- **Pre-collapse (I–II)** = joint-preserving window (core decompression).
- **Post-collapse (III–IV)** = articular surface lost → trends toward **arthroplasty**.
- Always look at the **contralateral hip**; AVN is frequently bilateral. | The pre- vs post-collapse line (ARCO II→III, the subchondral crescent) is the single most management-relevant finding: pre-collapse hips can be salvaged, post-collapse usually head toward replacement.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Transient osteoporosis / bone-marrow-edema syndrome (BMES)
text:
### Transient Osteoporosis / Bone-Marrow-Edema Syndrome (BMES)

Classically a **middle-aged man** or a **third-trimester pregnant woman** with acute atraumatic hip pain and a limp. MRI shows **diffuse marrow edema** of the femoral head extending into the neck/intertrochanteric region — **bright on STIR/T2, dark on T1** — with a possible reactive effusion.

Key distinguishing features:

- **No subchondral fracture line, no focal necrotic segment, no double-line sign** (vs AVN/SIFK).
- Edema is **diffuse and homogeneous**, often crossing into the neck — not the geographic antero-superior lesion of AVN.
- It is **self-limiting** (resolves over weeks to months).

**Management:** protected weight-bearing and analgesia; it is a **diagnosis of exclusion**, so the radiologist's job is to actively rule out a fracture line. BMES can be a precursor to or overlap with subchondral insufficiency fracture, so any developing subchondral low-signal line changes the diagnosis and the risk. | BMES is diffuse marrow edema with NO fracture line and NO necrotic segment — but always scrutinize the subchondral bone, because a low-signal line converts it to a subchondral insufficiency fracture with real collapse risk.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Subchondral insufficiency fracture of the femoral head
text:
### Subchondral Insufficiency Fracture (SIFK) of the Femoral Head

Typically an **older osteopenic woman** (or post-arthroscopy patient) with abrupt hip pain out of proportion to imaging. Unlike BMES, there is a structural fracture.

MRI hallmarks:

- **Subchondral low-signal line** paralleling the articular surface on T1 and T2, surrounded by **extensive marrow edema** on fluid-sensitive sequences.
- The line is **subchondral and convex to the surface** — not the serpiginous, segment-bounding interface of AVN.
- Look for **flattening / subtle collapse** of the articular contour; a **rim of detached fragment** signals advanced disease.

| Feature | BMES | SIFK | AVN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fracture line | No | **Yes (subchondral)** | Crescent if collapsing |
| Double-line | No | No | **Yes** |
| Collapse risk | No | **Yes** | Yes (III–IV) |

**Management:** strict **protected/non-weight-bearing** and treat the osteoporosis; large lesions or collapse may need surgery. | SIFK = subchondral fracture line + edema in an osteopenic patient — it carries real collapse risk, so offload the joint; do not dismiss it as transient marrow edema.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Apophyseal avulsions (ASIS, AIIS, ischial tuberosity, lesser trochanter)
text:
### Apophyseal Avulsions (ASIS, AIIS, Ischial Tuberosity, Lesser Trochanter)

In **adolescents/young athletes**, sudden muscle pull avulses the un-fused apophysis. MRI shows a **displaced ossific fragment**, marrow edema, and tendon/muscle strain at the donor site.

| Site | Muscle | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **ASIS** | Sartorius | Sprinting |
| **AIIS** | Rectus femoris (direct/straight head) | Kicking |
| **Ischial tuberosity** | Hamstring origin | Hurdling/splits |
| **Lesser trochanter** | Iliopsoas | Sudden flexion |

- Most are managed **conservatively**; large displacement (often cited **>1.5–2 cm**, especially ischial) prompts surgical discussion.
- **Red flag:** a **lesser-trochanter avulsion in an ADULT without significant trauma** is a **pathologic-fracture marker** — assume an underlying metastasis/marrow lesion until proven otherwise. | A lesser-trochanter avulsion in an adult with no real trauma is a malignancy red flag — scrutinize the marrow for confluent low-T1 infiltration (darker than muscle/disc) and work up for metastatic disease.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sacral and pubic-ramus stress / insufficiency fracture
text:
### Sacral & Pubic-Ramus Stress / Insufficiency Fractures

Two populations: **young athletes** (fatigue fracture of normal bone under load — distance runners, the female athlete triad) and **older/osteoporotic or post-radiation** patients (insufficiency fracture of weak bone under normal load).

MRI findings:

- **Sacrum:** marrow edema in the **sacral ala** with a **vertical low-signal line** paralleling the SI joint; bilateral ala edema **± a horizontal component = the H/Honda sign** (classic for insufficiency).
- **Pubic ramus:** edema and a low-signal line; can mimic or coexist with athletic pubalgia, so read the whole pelvis.
- Radiographs are often negative — **MRI (STIR) or bone scan** makes the diagnosis.

**Management:** relative rest/protected weight-bearing and pain control. Critically, **work up the cause**: bone-density evaluation, and in young women screen for the **female athlete triad / RED-S** (energy deficiency, menstrual dysfunction, low bone density). | Sacral ala or pubic-ramus stress fracture in a young female athlete should trigger a female-athlete-triad (RED-S) work-up; in an older patient, bilateral ala edema with the Honda/H sign points to an osteoporotic insufficiency fracture needing bone-health treatment.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: FAI & the Acetabular Labrum
text: Cam vs pincer morphology, the alpha angle, labral tears, paralabral cysts, and chondral injury | Cam morphology and the alpha angle (oblique-axial/radial measurement) | Pincer morphology: crossover sign, coxa profunda, acetabular retroversion/over-coverage (LCEA) | Anterosuperior labral tears: appearance and pitfalls vs normal sublabral recess | Paralabral cysts as a secondary sign of a tear, and os acetabuli | FAI-associated acetabular rim chondral injury and delamination at the chondrolabral junction | Measure the alpha angle in the correct plane (oblique-axial along the neck or radial) and apply the commonly cited >55-degree threshold for cam morphology | Differentiate cam from pincer (and mixed) FAI using femoral and acetabular morphologic signs | Identify an anterosuperior labral tear, distinguish it from a normal sublabral recess, and recognize a paralabral cyst as a secondary indicator that should trigger a directed search for the tear | Localize associated acetabular chondral injury/delamination and explain why its presence (more than the labral tear alone) weights management toward surgical referral | Recognize that asymptomatic cam/pincer morphology is common, so FAI is a clinical-plus-imaging diagnosis

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Using a single universal alpha-angle cutoff or measuring it on a standard axial slice
text: Measure the alpha angle on an oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck or on radial images; >55 degrees is the common threshold, with borderline values near the cutoff. A non-aligned plane over- or under-estimates the angle.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reporting cam or pincer morphology as 'FAI' without symptoms, or calling every anterosuperior labral signal a tear
text: Cam/pincer morphology and labral signal changes (including the normal sublabral recess) are common in asymptomatic hips. FAI is a clinical-imaging diagnosis: describe the morphology and require fluid/contrast undercutting into labral substance before calling a tear, then correlate with exam.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reporting a labral tear but not searching the adjacent acetabular cartilage
text: Anterosuperior labral tears are frequently accompanied by chondrolabral-junction chondral injury or delamination, which is often the more management-relevant finding for surgical planning and prognosis.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cam morphology and the alpha angle (oblique-axial/radial measurement)
text:
### Cam Morphology and the Alpha Angle

**Cam morphology** is asphericity at the anterosuperior **femoral head-neck junction** — a bump that loses the normal concave waist. During flexion/internal rotation it jams into the acetabulum, shearing the chondrolabral junction.

**Measuring the alpha angle:**
- Center a circle on the femoral head. Draw line 1 from head center down the **femoral neck axis**; line 2 from head center to the point where the head first exits the circle. The angle between them is alpha.
- Plane matters: measure on **oblique-axial** images angled along the neck, or **radial** reformats. A standard axial under-samples the **anterosuperior (12–3 o'clock)** peak and falsely reassures.

| Finding | Threshold |
|---|---|
| Normal | <50° |
| Borderline | 50–55° |
| **Cam (pathologic)** | **>55°** |

**Management:** alpha angle is one input. Cam morphology with positive impingement signs and a labral/chondral lesion supports arthroscopic **osteochondroplasty (femoral cam resection)**; an incidental bump in an asymptomatic patient does not — asymptomatic cam morphology is common. | Measure the alpha angle on oblique-axial/radial images where the anterosuperior bump actually lives — a normal straight-axial alpha angle can hide a clinically important cam at 1–2 o'clock.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Pincer morphology: crossover sign, coxa profunda, acetabular retroversion/over-coverage (LCEA)
text:
### Pincer: Crossover, Coxa Profunda, Retroversion, Over-coverage

**Pincer** is acetabular **over-coverage** — the rim contacts the neck and levers the labrum. Read it from the bony acetabulum, not just the labrum.

**Patterns to recognize:**
- **Crossover sign** — a radiographic sign on a true AP pelvis where the anterior rim line crosses the posterior rim line near the top, indicating focal **cranial acetabular retroversion** (anterior over-coverage). On MRI, correlate version and rim coverage on axial/oblique slices.
- **Coxa profunda / protrusio** — deep socket; in coxa profunda the acetabular floor touches or passes the **ilioischial line**, and in protrusio the femoral head crosses it. Global over-coverage.
- **LCEA (lateral center-edge angle) >40°** = lateral over-coverage (vs. <20–25° = dysplasia/under-coverage).

**MRI clues:** rim-side labral degeneration/ossification, a **contrecoup** posteroinferior chondral injury (the femoral head pivots away from the impinging anterior rim).

**Management:** pincer is treated by **rim trimming with labral refixation**; over-resection risks iatrogenic instability. Most FAI is **mixed (cam + pincer)** — name both because each drives a different part of the bony correction. | A contrecoup posteroinferior chondral injury is the tell of pincer impingement — the lesion sits opposite the impinging anterior rim, not at the point of contact.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Anterosuperior labral tears: appearance and pitfalls vs normal sublabral recess
text:
### Anterosuperior Labral Tear vs. Normal Sublabral Recess

The acetabular labrum is a **low-signal triangle** on the rim. Tears and a normal recess both show fluid signal at the chondrolabral junction — distinguishing them prevents over-calling.

| Feature | Normal sublabral recess | Labral tear |
|---|---|---|
| Margins | Smooth, follows cartilage, no displacement | Frayed, blunted, displaced |
| Contrast extension | Partial, smooth, at the **base** only | Into labral **substance**, irregular |
| Adjacent findings | None | Paralabral cyst, chondral loss, FAI morphology |

- A recess is a **smooth-walled cleft** paralleling the cartilage; it does not enter the body of the labrum. Sublabral sulci/recesses are recognized at the **anterosuperior** rim as well as posteroinferiorly, so location alone does not settle it.
- **MR arthrography** (or traction/leg-pull) improves sensitivity for the anterosuperior tear typical of cam FAI.
- The classic cam tear zone is **anterosuperior (12–3 o'clock)** — weigh trajectory, margins, and secondary signs rather than location alone.

**Management:** isolated degenerative fraying is treated conservatively; an unstable, symptomatic anterosuperior tear with FAI is refixed at arthroscopy. | Trajectory and margins decide it, not location: contrast that tracks smoothly along the cartilage base is a recess; contrast extending INTO the labral substance with frayed margins — especially with a paralabral cyst — is a tear.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Paralabral cysts as a secondary sign of a tear, and os acetabuli
text:
### Paralabral Cysts and Os Acetabuli

**Paralabral cyst** — a **T2-bright, lobulated** fluid collection adjacent to the labrum, often tracking along the rim or into the surrounding soft tissues. It forms when joint fluid is pumped through a **full-thickness labral tear** (one-way valve).

- **Key role: a secondary sign.** A paralabral cyst is highly specific for an underlying tear — find the cyst, then scrutinize the adjacent labrum, even if the tear itself looks subtle.
- Large anterior cysts can abut the iliopsoas or extend toward adjacent neurovascular structures (e.g., femoral neurovascular bundle) — note mass effect.

**Os acetabuli** — an ossicle at the acetabular rim. It may be an unfused secondary ossification center or, in FAI, a **rim stress fracture / chronic pincer apophyseal fragment**. On MRI: a corticated bony fragment at the anterosuperior rim, sometimes with adjacent marrow edema if active.

**Management:** the cyst confirms the tear and supports labral repair. An os acetabuli matters surgically — it can represent rim overload, and the surgeon decides excision vs. incorporation/fixation based on size and fragment viability. | Treat a paralabral cyst as a flag, not a diagnosis — it almost always means a full-thickness labral tear is feeding it, so go back and find the tear you may have under-called.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: FAI-associated acetabular rim chondral injury and delamination at the chondrolabral junction
text:
### Rim Chondral Injury / Delamination at the Chondrolabral Junction

FAI damages cartilage where the impingement lands: the **anterosuperior acetabular rim**, at the **chondrolabral junction**. This is the lesion that pushes management toward surgery.

**What to look for on MRI:**
- **Delamination** — fluid/contrast undercutting the cartilage, separating it from subchondral bone (the "carpet lifting off the floor"); the surface can look deceptively intact.
- **Wave sign** — a mobile cartilage flap. Look for an **outer-edge chondral fissure** at the chondrolabral junction adjacent to a cam lesion.
- **Subchondral changes** — rim marrow edema, cysts, early sclerosis.
- Cam → **acetabular-sided** anterosuperior delamination; pincer → narrower rim chondral injury plus **contrecoup** posteroinferior loss.

**Why it weights toward surgery:** intact labrum + reparable chondral lesion = good candidate for **osteochondroplasty + chondrolabral repair**. **Diffuse full-thickness cartilage loss / Tönnis 2–3 (joint-space narrowing)** predicts poor arthroscopic outcomes — that patient is steered toward conservative care or arthroplasty, not scope. | The amount and depth of rim chondral damage — not the labral tear — is the strongest imaging driver of the surgical decision; delamination is repairable, but diffuse full-thickness loss predicts a failed scope.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cartilage & Early OA
text: Chondral delamination, subchondral change, and the joint-preservation-vs-arthroplasty conversation | Systematic acetabular and femoral-head cartilage assessment | Chondral delamination vs full-thickness loss (the cam-related shear injury) | Subchondral cysts and subchondral marrow change as load markers | Joint-space narrowing and the early-osteoarthritis pattern | When MR arthrography (with traction) improves chondral detection and changes management | Assess acetabular and femoral cartilage systematically and grade chondral loss as partial-thickness, full-thickness, or delamination separate from surface fibrillation | Differentiate focal FAI-related rim chondral injury from diffuse early osteoarthritis, and a degenerative subchondral cyst/geode from an aggressive lytic process | Recognize the degree of cartilage loss and joint-space narrowing at which joint preservation becomes less effective, shifting referral from arthroscopy toward arthroplasty | State when direct MR arthrography, particularly with traction, increases sensitivity for chondral and chondrolabral lesions and will change management

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Assuming standard non-contrast MRI reliably excludes early acetabular chondral delamination
text: Hip cartilage is thin and closely apposed; delamination is a fluid cleft undermining an intact-looking surface and is easy to miss. Direct MR arthrography (optionally with leg traction) improves detection of chondral/chondrolabral lesions when this will change management.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Equating any subchondral cyst or marrow edema with early OA, or a labral tear with a surgically correctable problem regardless of cartilage
text: Interpret subchondral cysts alongside cartilage status, joint space, and morphology; isolated edema without chondral loss may be a stress/insufficiency phenomenon. Diffuse cartilage loss predicts poor outcomes from labral repair and shifts management away from joint preservation.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Systematic acetabular and femoral-head cartilage assessment
text:
### Systematic acetabular and femoral-head cartilage assessment

Grade hip cartilage the same way every time so you don't miss the focal lesion that changes the operation. Use **fluid-sensitive sequences** (intermediate-weighted/PD fat-sat, T2 fat-sat) where cartilage is intermediate signal against bright fluid and dark subchondral bone.

A reproducible system:
- **Clock-face the acetabulum** on radial/sagittal images (anterosuperior, roughly the 12-3 o'clock zone, is where FAI damage clusters).
- Track each surface: **acetabular** then **femoral head**, looking for surface fibrillation, fissures, thinning, focal full-thickness defects, and subchondral signal.
- Note **location** (anterosuperior vs global), **depth** (partial vs full-thickness), and **size**.

| Finding | Read as |
|---|---|
| Focal anterosuperior loss | FAI/cam shear |
| Diffuse circumferential loss | Early OA |

**Management:** a small, well-shouldered focal defect with preserved joint space favors **joint-preserving arthroscopy/osteoplasty**; diffuse loss does not. | Always localize cartilage damage on a clock face: focal anterosuperior loss points to a correctable FAI lesion, while diffuse loss signals OA and shifts you away from arthroscopy.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Chondral delamination vs full-thickness loss (the cam-related shear injury)
text:
### Chondral delamination vs full-thickness loss (cam shear injury)

The **cam bump** shears the acetabular cartilage off the subchondral plate during impingement, so the earliest FAI cartilage injury is often a **delamination** with an intact-looking surface, which is easy to miss.

**Delamination** = cartilage separates from bone at the tidemark while the surface stays continuous. Look for:
- A thin line of **fluid/high signal undercutting the cartilage** (the "carpet lifting off the floor").
- Cartilage that looks normal in thickness but is **unstable/wavy** or shows a hypointense interface.
- Typically **anterosuperior**, adjacent to the chondrolabral junction.

**Full-thickness loss** = focal defect down to bone, often with reactive subchondral edema or cyst.

| | Surface | Depth | Detection |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delamination | Intact | Deep cleavage | Often needs arthrography/traction |
| Full-thickness | Disrupted | To bone | Visible on standard MR |

**Management:** delaminated flaps are unstable and arthroscopically treatable (debridement, microfracture/fixation) **together with** cam osteoplasty; flagging them changes the surgical plan. | A delaminated flap can look like normal-thickness cartilage on standard MRI; the giveaway is a thin fluid signal undercutting it, and missing it leaves an unstable flap that fails after isolated cam correction.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Subchondral cysts and subchondral marrow change as load markers
text:
### Subchondral cysts and marrow change as load markers

Subchondral signal tells you where the joint is **overloaded**, not just where cartilage is gone.

- **Subchondral BME-like marrow change**: ill-defined T2-hyperintense/T1-hypointense signal beneath a cartilage defect = active focal overload, usually anterosuperior in FAI.
- **Subchondral cysts (geodes)**: well-defined, rounded, **T2-bright/T1-dark** lesions, typically **at or near the cartilage defect** and the bony stress point.

Geode vs aggressive lesion, the reassuring features:

| Geode (benign) | Aggressive (red flag) |
|---|---|
| Small, rounded, well-corticated | Confluent, ill-defined |
| At joint surface/load point | Away from surface, marrow-replacing |
| Paired with cartilage loss | Low T1 **darker than muscle/disc** |
| No soft-tissue mass | Cortical breakthrough, mass |

**Management:** cysts/BME tracking the load point confirm a mechanical, treatable problem. A **confluent low-T1 marrow lesion darker than muscle or disc** is a tumor red flag, so stop and work it up rather than calling it OA. | A confluent marrow lesion lower in T1 signal than adjacent muscle or disc is marrow replacement, not a degenerative geode: treat it as a tumor red flag, not early OA.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Joint-space narrowing and the early-osteoarthritis pattern
text:
### Joint-space narrowing and the early-OA pattern

Early OA on MRI is **diffuse**, not focal: circumferential cartilage thinning, **global joint-space narrowing**, marginal osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis/cysts, and effusion/synovitis. Contrast this with FAI's focal anterosuperior wear.

Reading early OA:
- **Cartilage**: diffuse thinning across acetabular and femoral surfaces, not one clock-position.
- **Joint space**: narrowing best confirmed on the **weightbearing radiograph**; MRI tends to overcall preserved space because it is non-weightbearing.
- **Osteophytes, sclerosis, subchondral cysts** at multiple sites.

The threshold that matters:

| Pattern | Procedure |
|---|---|
| Focal lesion, **joint space preserved** | Joint-preserving arthroscopy/osteoplasty |
| Diffuse loss, **joint space ≤2 mm / Tönnis grade ≥2** | Arthroplasty territory |

**Management:** advanced/diffuse cartilage loss with established narrowing predicts **arthroscopy failure**; these patients are headed for arthroplasty, and recognizing it prevents an operation that won't help. | Confirm joint-space narrowing on a weightbearing radiograph, not MRI: diffuse loss with narrowing (Tönnis grade ≥2) moves the patient out of arthroscopy and into arthroplasty planning.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: When MR arthrography (with traction) improves chondral detection and changes management
text:
### When MR arthrography with traction improves chondral detection

Native cartilage surfaces are apposed, so subtle defects and **delamination** hide. **Direct MR arthrography (MRA)** distends the joint with dilute gadolinium; adding **axial traction** pulls the surfaces apart and lets contrast fill defects and undercut delaminated flaps.

Use MRA ± traction when:
- You **suspect FAI/labral or chondral pathology** but standard MRI is equivocal.
- You need to characterize a **delamination** or surface flap before surgery.
- The clinical picture (young athlete, mechanical symptoms) outpaces the standard study.

| | Standard MRI | MRA + traction |
|---|---|---|
| Surfaces | Apposed | Separated by contrast |
| Delamination | Often missed | Contrast undercuts flap |
| Sensitivity | Lower for early chondral | Higher |

**Management:** a traction MRA that confirms a stable, focal, treatable lesion in a young patient supports **joint preservation**; if it instead shows diffuse loss, it appropriately steers toward non-operative or arthroplasty pathways. It is an invasive test, so reserve it for cases where the result changes the plan. | Traction MR arthrography unmasks delamination and early chondral defects by pulling apposed surfaces apart: order it when standard MRI is equivocal and the answer will change whether you operate.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tendons & Muscles
text: Abductor cuff/GTPS, hamstring origin, iliopsoas/snapping hip, and athletic pubalgia | Gluteus medius/minimus ('rotator cuff of the hip') and greater trochanteric pain syndrome (GTPS) | Proximal hamstring origin: tendinosis, partial tear, and tendon count/retraction that triggers surgical referral | Rectus femoris / AIIS injury and the universal muscle-strain grading framework | Iliopsoas: tendinosis, internal snapping hip, and iliopsoas bursa | Adductor injury and athletic pubalgia / core-muscle injury (rectus-adductor aponeurosis, secondary cleft sign) | Recognize gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy and tears at the trochanteric footprint and explain why GTPS is usually a rehab/injection problem until a full-thickness retracted abductor tear shifts it toward referral | Grade proximal hamstring origin injury and identify the features (number of tendons involved, retraction) that change management from rehab to surgical referral | Apply the universal muscle-strain grading framework (grade 1 feathery edema, grade 2 partial with fluid, grade 3 complete with retraction) to rectus femoris/AIIS and adductor injuries | Differentiate internal (iliopsoas) snapping hip from intra-articular causes of anterior hip pain and recognize iliopsoas tendinosis/bursa findings | Identify athletic pubalgia / core-muscle injury at the rectus abdominis-adductor aponeurosis (secondary cleft sign) and distinguish it from a labral tear

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Attributing all lateral hip pain to 'trochanteric bursitis'
text: GTPS is most often gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy or tearing (the hip's rotator cuff), with peritrochanteric fluid usually secondary. Read the abductor footprint for tendon signal/thickness change and undersurface tears; a full-thickness retracted tear is the finding that prompts surgical referral.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reporting a proximal hamstring tear without tendon count and retraction
text: Management hinges on how many of the three tendons are involved and how far the stump has retracted. Acute multi-tendon avulsions with retraction are surgical-referral injuries; isolated tendinosis is rehab. Always state both, and note sciatic nerve proximity.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling groin pain a labral tear when the problem is at the pubic symphysis, or mistaking the normal primary cleft for the secondary cleft sign
text: Athletic pubalgia / core-muscle injury at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis (secondary cleft sign — fluid extending inferolaterally from the symphysis — and aponeurotic plate disruption) is extra-articular and managed differently. The central primary cleft is normal; inspect the symphysis on every groin-pain study.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Gluteus medius/minimus ('rotator cuff of the hip') and greater trochanteric pain syndrome (GTPS)
text:
### Gluteus Medius/Minimus — the 'Rotator Cuff of the Hip' and GTPS

**Greater trochanteric pain syndrome (GTPS)** is usually abductor **tendinopathy/tearing**, not bursitis. The gluteus medius/minimus insert on the **greater trochanter** at named facets (minimus → anterior facet; medius → lateral + superoposterior facets).

**What to read on MRI (fluid-sensitive fat-sat coronal/axial):**
- **Tendinopathy:** thickened tendon, intermediate T2 signal, peritrochanteric edema.
- **Partial tear:** focal fluid signal at the facet, fraying, often with reactive **trochanteric bursal fluid**.
- **Full-thickness tear:** fluid-filled gap, **tendon retraction**, fatty atrophy of the muscle belly (best on **T1**).

| Finding | Management |
|---|---|
| Tendinopathy / low-grade partial | PT, load management, image-guided injection |
| Full-thickness **retracted** tear + atrophy | **Surgical referral** |

The classic look: peritrochanteric T2 hyperintensity tracking along the facets. Don't call it "bursitis" reflexively — interrogate the tendon footprint itself. | A full-thickness, **retracted** abductor tear with fatty muscle atrophy is a surgical-referral lesion — an isolated "trochanteric bursitis" report should prompt you to scrutinize the gluteus medius/minimus tendon footprint before settling for conservative care.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Proximal hamstring origin: tendinosis, partial tear, and tendon count/retraction that triggers surgical referral
text:
### Proximal Hamstring Origin — Tendinosis, Partial Tear, and the Referral Triggers

The hamstrings (**semitendinosus + biceps femoris conjoint tendon, semimembranosus**) arise from the **ischial tuberosity**. Injury ranges from chronic insertional **tendinosis** to acute **avulsion**.

**MRI read (oblique-coronal/axial fat-sat):**
- **Tendinosis:** thickened, intermediate-signal tendon at the tuberosity, no fiber gap.
- **Partial tear:** focal fluid signal, some intact fibers.
- **Complete avulsion:** all tendons discontinuous, fluid gap, **measurable retraction**.

**Two numbers that change management:**
1. **Tendon count** — how many of the **3 tendons** are torn (free-tendon avulsion of ≥2 tendons trends surgical).
2. **Retraction** — measured from the tuberosity; **>2 cm** retraction of a 2–3 tendon avulsion favors **surgical repair**, especially in active patients.

| Pattern | Management |
|---|---|
| Tendinosis / low-grade partial | PT, eccentric load |
| 2–3 tendon avulsion, retraction >2 cm | **Surgical referral** | | For proximal hamstring avulsions, your report needs **two data points the surgeon cares about: tendon count and retraction distance.** A 2–3 tendon avulsion retracted >2 cm in an active patient is a repair candidate — vague "high-grade tear" wording underserves the decision.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Rectus femoris / AIIS injury and the universal muscle-strain grading framework
text:
### Rectus Femoris / AIIS Injury and the Universal Muscle-Strain Grading Framework

The **rectus femoris** has a **direct head (AIIS)** and an **indirect/reflected head (superior acetabular rim)**. Its central intramuscular tendon (the "bull's-eye" on axial) is a common strain site; in skeletally immature athletes the **AIIS apophysis avulses** instead.

**Universal muscle-strain grades (apply to ANY muscle — rectus, adductor, hamstring):**

| Grade | MRI finding | Management |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Feathery T2 edema, **<5%** fibers, no architectural disruption | Relative rest, RTP days–~2 wk |
| **2** | Partial tear, fluid at myotendinous junction, some fiber discontinuity | PT, RTP weeks |
| **3** | **Complete** rupture ± retraction, hematoma | Consider surgical referral (esp. proximal/retracted) |

Most strains occur at the **myotendinous junction**. Read fat-sat fluid-sensitive sequences for the edema pattern and look for a discrete fiber gap that upgrades a grade 1 to grade 2/3. | Strain grading is **universal** — grade 1 is edema only, grade 2 is a partial tear, grade 3 is complete rupture. In an adolescent, the same mechanism avulses the **AIIS apophysis** rather than tearing muscle, so check the apophysis on T1 before calling it a simple strain.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Iliopsoas: tendinosis, internal snapping hip, and iliopsoas bursa
text:
### Iliopsoas — Tendinosis, Internal Snapping Hip, and the Iliopsoas Bursa

The **iliopsoas tendon** crosses the anterior hip to insert on the **lesser trochanter**; the **iliopsoas bursa** sits between the tendon and the joint and communicates with the hip in ~15%.

**MRI read:**
- **Tendinosis:** thickened, intermediate-signal tendon anterior to the joint.
- **Iliopsoas bursitis:** fluid-distended bursa anteromedial to the hip — can be reactive to intra-articular pathology.
- **Internal snapping hip:** a **dynamic** diagnosis (tendon snapping over the **iliopectineal eminence / femoral head**). MRI is often normal at rest; **dynamic ultrasound** is the confirmatory test.

**Differentiating snapping causes:**

| Type | Cause | Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal** | Iliopsoas over iliopectineal eminence | Dynamic US |
| **External** | IT band/gluteus maximus over greater trochanter | Exam/US |
| **Intra-articular** | Labral tear, loose body | MR arthrogram | | **Internal snapping hip is a dynamic, often MRI-negative diagnosis** — confirm with dynamic ultrasound, and don't attribute the snap to an incidental labral tear. A tense iliopsoas bursa should prompt a hunt for the intra-articular process driving it.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Adductor injury and athletic pubalgia / core-muscle injury (rectus-adductor aponeurosis, secondary cleft sign)
text:
### Adductor Injury and Athletic Pubalgia ("Sports Hernia")

The **adductor longus** origin at the pubis is the classic strain site; apply the **universal 1–3 grading**. **Athletic pubalgia** is an overlapping but distinct entity centered on the **rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurosis** at the pubic symphysis — *not* a true hernia.

**MRI read (fluid-sensitive, large FOV pelvis):**
- **Adductor strain:** myotendinous/origin edema, ± partial tear.
- **Athletic pubalgia:** disruption/edema at the **rectus-adductor aponeurosis**; the **secondary cleft sign** — fluid extending **inferolaterally** from the symphysis along the adductor origin — is the key marker.
- **Osteitis pubis:** symmetric subchondral marrow edema about the symphysis (often coexists).

**Why it matters:** athletic pubalgia is groin pain from the **aponeurosis**, managed with targeted PT/core rehab and sometimes surgery — a **labral tear** is intra-articular and presents with mechanical/C-sign symptoms. Distinguishing them changes the whole pathway. | The **secondary cleft sign** (fluid tracking inferolaterally from the symphysis) localizes pain to the **rectus-adductor aponeurosis** of athletic pubalgia — anatomically and managerially distinct from an intra-articular **labral tear**, so don't let an incidental labral finding hijack the workup.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Don't-Miss / Red Flags
text: Findings that escalate management today: tension-side fracture, AVN, septic joint, tumor, SCFE/Perthes | Tension-side femoral-neck stress fracture and occult/insufficiency fracture in the elderly | AVN of the femoral head before collapse | Septic arthritis and osteomyelitis pattern | Soft-tissue mass / sarcoma and marrow-replacement / metastasis | Athletic pubalgia as a commonly missed groin-pain source | SCFE and Legg-Calve-Perthes in the skeletally immature; referred pelvic/oncologic pathology | Triage the hip MRI findings that require urgent escalation (tension-side femoral-neck fracture, septic arthritis, aggressive marrow/soft-tissue lesion) versus routine referral, and route each to the correct disposition | Recognize an occult or subchondral insufficiency fracture in the elderly that explains a 'negative x-ray' painful hip and changes weight-bearing instructions | Identify imaging red flags for tumor (confluent low-T1 marrow replacement darker than muscle or disc, soft-tissue mass, periosteal reaction) that warrant oncologic workup rather than a sports diagnosis | Recognize SCFE and Legg-Calve-Perthes in the skeletally immature and explain the urgency of SCFE (non-weight-bearing, prompt pediatric-ortho referral)

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Dismissing hip/groin pain with negative radiographs as soft-tissue strain
text: A negative radiograph does not exclude an occult femoral-neck fracture, tension-side stress fracture, AVN, or a marrow-replacing lesion. MRI (T1 plus fluid-sensitive) is the sensitive test; the tension-side fracture and occult elderly fracture change weight-bearing and referral immediately.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Anchoring on a sports diagnosis and overlooking a marrow-replacing lesion or treating an effusion with synovitis as a degenerative flare
text: Confluent low-T1 marrow that is darker than skeletal muscle or an intervertebral disc, or a soft-tissue mass/periosteal reaction, should trigger an oncologic workup. In the right clinical setting (fever, raised inflammatory markers), a tense effusion with synovitis and periarticular edema is septic arthritis until proven otherwise — urgent aspiration, not injection.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Treating a subtle SCFE as a benign physeal variant
text: In an adolescent, even a subtle slipped capital femoral epiphysis is an orthopedic urgency: make non-weight-bearing and refer promptly to prevent further slip and AVN. Look for physeal widening/edema and posteromedial epiphyseal slip.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tension-side femoral-neck stress fracture and occult/insufficiency fracture in the elderly
text:
### Tension-Side Femoral-Neck Stress Fracture and Occult/Insufficiency Fracture

Not all femoral-neck stress fractures are equal — **location dictates urgency.**

**Compression-side (inferomedial)** fractures are stable and managed with protected weight-bearing.

**Tension-side (superolateral)** fractures are a **surgical emergency** — they propagate across the neck and risk complete displacement with AVN.

**MRI signs:**
- Diffuse femoral-neck **marrow edema** (T2/STIR bright, T1 dark) — sensitive but nonspecific
- The diagnosis-changer is a **linear T1-hypointense fracture line**; note which cortex it abuts
- Any fracture line touching the **superolateral cortex** = tension-side

**Occult / insufficiency fracture in the elderly:**
- Hip pain + **negative radiograph** + can't bear weight → MRI, do not stop at the x-ray
- Look for a **subcapital/intertrochanteric T1-hypointense line** within marrow edema
- MRI detects fractures **plain films and even CT can miss** in the first days

| Finding | Management |
|---|---|
| Edema only, no line | Relative rest, monitor |
| Compression-side line | Protected weight-bearing |
| Tension-side line | **NWB + urgent ortho** | | A superolateral (tension-side) femoral-neck fracture line is a surgical emergency — make the patient non-weight-bearing and call ortho the same day. In an elderly patient who can't bear weight with a negative x-ray, the next test is MRI, not reassurance.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: AVN of the femoral head before collapse
text:
### AVN of the Femoral Head Before Collapse

The entire goal is to catch osteonecrosis **before the articular surface collapses** — that is the window where joint-preserving surgery (core decompression) still works.

**The pathognomonic sign:**
- **Double-line sign** on T2 — an inner **bright** line (hyperemic granulation tissue) flanking an outer **dark** sclerotic rim, demarcating the necrotic segment, classically in the **anterosuperior** weight-bearing head
- On T1, a **serpentine low-signal line** encircles the lesion

**The collapse hinge (ARCO staging):**
- **Pre-collapse (ARCO I–II):** signal change only, intact spherical contour → joint-preserving options
- **Post-collapse (ARCO III+):** **subchondral fracture / crescent sign**, flattening, step-off → trending toward arthroplasty

**What changes management on every read:**
- Hunt for the **subchondral lucent crescent** and any loss of sphericity — this is the line between salvage and replacement
- **Bilateral** disease is common; screen the contralateral hip
- Note risk factors: **steroids, alcohol, sickle cell**

Report stage, lesion size, and percent of the weight-bearing surface involved. | The double-line sign confirms AVN, but the read that changes management is the presence or absence of a subchondral fracture (crescent sign) — pre-collapse hips can be offered core decompression, post-collapse hips are headed for arthroplasty. Always check the other hip.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Septic arthritis and osteomyelitis pattern
text:
### Septic Arthritis and Osteomyelitis Pattern

A hot, painful hip with fever is a **can't-miss, joint-threatening emergency** — cartilage is destroyed within hours to days. **Imaging supports, but never delays, joint aspiration.**

**Septic arthritis on MRI:**
- **Joint effusion** with thick, **avidly enhancing synovium** (give contrast if sepsis is suspected)
- **Perisynovial / soft-tissue edema** and reactive marrow edema on both sides of the joint
- A complex or loculated effusion raises concern but cannot exclude infection

**Osteomyelitis on MRI:**
- **Confluent T1-hypointense marrow** replacing normal fat, bright on T2/STIR, with **post-contrast enhancement**
- **Rim-enhancing intraosseous/soft-tissue abscess**, cortical breakthrough, sinus tract
- Adjacent **myositis** or fluid collection

**Management triggers:**
- Suspicion of septic arthritis → **urgent aspiration** for cell count/Gram stain/culture; do not wait on MRI
- Confirmed infection → surgical **drainage/washout** + organism-directed antibiotics
- Distinguish from transient synovitis (especially in children): septic hips have higher fever, refusal to bear weight, and inflammatory markers | MRI can suggest septic arthritis, but the diagnosis is made by aspiration — never let imaging delay tapping a hot hip. Treat a confluent marrow-replacing T1-dark, enhancing lesion adjacent to an effusion as osteomyelitis until proven otherwise.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Soft-tissue mass / sarcoma and marrow-replacement / metastasis
text:
### Soft-Tissue Mass / Sarcoma and Marrow Replacement / Metastasis

The hip and pelvis are common sites for incidental — and dangerous — masses. Your job is to separate benign from **aggressive** and route the patient correctly, **without biopsying through the wrong plane.**

**Marrow-replacement red flag:**
- **Confluent low-T1 marrow that is DARKER than adjacent skeletal muscle or disc** = abnormal marrow replacement (tumor/infiltration), not benign red marrow
- Benign hematopoietic marrow stays **brighter than muscle** on T1 — this single comparison is the highest-yield check

**Aggressive lesion features:**
- **Cortical destruction**, **periosteal reaction** (lamellated/sunburst), wide zone of transition
- Associated **soft-tissue mass** extending beyond bone
- Enhancing soft-tissue component

**Soft-tissue sarcoma red flags:**
- **Deep**, **> 5 cm**, **enlarging**, heterogeneous, non-fatty mass — these are sarcoma until proven otherwise

**Management:**
- Do **not** biopsy or resect locally — refer to a **sarcoma/orthopedic-oncology center** so the biopsy tract can be planned and excised en bloc
- Confluent marrow replacement in an at-risk patient → **metastatic/myeloma workup** | On T1, normal marrow is brighter than muscle; marrow that is darker than muscle or disc is replaced and abnormal — that is your tumor red flag. A deep, enlarging soft-tissue mass over 5 cm goes to a sarcoma center, never to a local excision, so the biopsy tract isn't contaminated.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Athletic pubalgia as a commonly missed groin-pain source
text:
### Athletic Pubalgia — A Commonly Missed Groin-Pain Source

**Athletic pubalgia ("sports hernia")** is chronic activity-related groin/lower-abdominal pain that is **routinely missed** because it is *not* an inguinal hernia and the hip joint itself looks normal. The injury is at the **rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurosis** at its pubic attachment.

**Where to look (dedicated pubic-symphysis protocol):**
- Edema/disruption at the **common rectus-adductor aponeurosis** on the symphysis
- **Secondary cleft sign** — abnormal fluid/contrast extending inferolaterally from the symphysis along the adductor/rectus origin, indicating a microtear of the aponeurosis
- **Adductor longus** enthesopathy/tendinosis or partial tear at the pubic origin
- Often coexists with **osteitis pubis** (subchondral symphyseal marrow edema)

**Why it's missed:**
- A standard hip MRI may **not cover the symphysis** adequately
- Coexisting **FAI/labral findings** distract from the true pain source

**Management:** Most respond to **rehab/core-and-adductor program**; refractory cases → surgical repair. Reporting the secondary cleft sign directs the surgeon to the aponeurosis. | Anterior groin pain in an athlete with a normal-looking hip joint — look at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis on the pubic symphysis.
