The location is important: sublabral sulci/recesses are most accepted as normal variants posteroinferiorly and at the anteroinferior junction, whereas a true cleft at the anterosuperior junction is the site where pathologic tears predominate and should not be casually dismissed as a variant. A bucket-handle tear (A) shows a displaced fragment, which is absent. There is no cyst to aspirate (B). A normal-shaped labrum with a thin cleft is not a mass (D). | hip-anatomy | hip-anatomy

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 35-year-old recreational athlete has mild intermittent groin ache. MRI demonstrates a thin, regular high-signal line at the posteroinferior chondrolabral junction paralleling the labral base, with a triangular labrum of normal contour, no fragment displacement, and no surrounding cyst. How should this finding most appropriately direct management?
text: A 35-year-old recreational athlete has mild intermittent groin ache. MRI demonstrates a thin, regular high-signal line at the posteroinferior chondrolabral junction paralleling the labral base, with a triangular labrum of normal contour, no fragment displacement, and no surrounding cyst. How should this finding most appropriately direct management? | A. Diagnose a full-thickness radial labral tear and refer for reconstruction | B. Interpret as a normal sublabral recess; manage symptoms clinically without surgical referral | C. Recommend MR arthrography solely to drain an associated paralabral cyst | D. Initiate a marrow-replacement tumor protocol for a labral lesion | Correct: B | A thin regular line at the posteroinferior chondrolabral junction paralleling the labral base with a normally contoured labrum and no cyst or displacement indicates a normal sublabral recess variant; manage clinically. Location matters: a posteroinferior/inferior cleft is a recognized normal-variant site, in contrast to the anterosuperior junction where most true symptomatic tears occur and a cleft cannot be safely called a variant. A radial tear (A) disrupts and irregularly clefts the labrum, not seen here. There is no cyst to drain (C), and a normal-appearing labrum is not a tumor (D). | hip-anatomy | hip-anatomy

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 30-year-old marathoner has 2 weeks of groin pain. MRI shows marrow edema in the femoral neck with a linear T1-hypointense fracture line along the superolateral (tension) cortex extending partway across the neck. What is the most appropriate management?
text: A 30-year-old marathoner has 2 weeks of groin pain. MRI shows marrow edema in the femoral neck with a linear T1-hypointense fracture line along the superolateral (tension) cortex extending partway across the neck. What is the most appropriate management? | A. Allow continued running with a 10% weekly mileage reduction and recheck in 6 weeks | B. Make the patient non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic surgical referral | C. Start protected weight-bearing with a cane and routine clinic follow-up in 3 months | D. Inject the hip with corticosteroid to control pain and resume training | Correct: B | A tension-side (superolateral) femoral neck stress fracture is at high risk of completion and displacement and is treated as a surgical emergency with non-weight-bearing and urgent referral. Continued running (A), partial weight-bearing with routine follow-up (C), or steroid injection (D) all risk catastrophic completion of the fracture. | hip-bones-stress | hip-bones-stress

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 26-year-old triathlete reports 3 weeks of groin pain. MRI reveals femoral neck marrow edema with a short fracture line along the inferomedial (compression) cortex and no cortical break or displacement. What is the most appropriate management?
text: A 26-year-old triathlete reports 3 weeks of groin pain. MRI reveals femoral neck marrow edema with a short fracture line along the inferomedial (compression) cortex and no cortical break or displacement. What is the most appropriate management? | A. Immediate operative fixation that day as a surgical emergency | B. Continue full training with anti-inflammatories and no imaging follow-up | C. Protected weight-bearing with activity modification and close outpatient orthopedic follow-up | D. Same-day corticosteroid injection followed by return to competition | Correct: C | A compression-side (inferomedial) femoral neck stress fracture without a cortical break is typically managed conservatively with protected weight-bearing, activity modification, and close follow-up. Emergent fixation (A) is reserved for tension-side or displaced fractures. Continuing full training (B) or steroid injection then competing (D) risk progression. The tension-vs-compression distinction is what changes management. | hip-bones-stress | hip-bones-stress

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 67-year-old woman with osteoporosis fell and has groin pain and inability to bear weight, but radiographs are read as negative. MRI shows an incomplete linear T1-hypointense fracture line with surrounding marrow edema in the femoral neck. What is the most appropriate management?
text: A 67-year-old woman with osteoporosis fell and has groin pain and inability to bear weight, but radiographs are read as negative. MRI shows an incomplete linear T1-hypointense fracture line with surrounding marrow edema in the femoral neck. What is the most appropriate management? | A. Discharge home with full weight-bearing since radiographs were negative | B. Provide a corticosteroid injection for presumed bursitis and mobilize | C. Schedule routine outpatient physiotherapy with weight-bearing as tolerated | D. Keep her non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic referral for an occult femoral neck fracture | Correct: D | An MRI-detected occult femoral neck fracture in an osteoporotic patient who cannot bear weight requires non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral because of the high risk of displacement. Discharging with full weight-bearing (A), steroid injection (B), or weight-bearing-as-tolerated physiotherapy (C) all risk fracture completion and displacement. | hip-bones-stress | hip-bones-stress

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: An 82-year-old man fell from standing and has groin pain with inability to bear weight; two views of the hip are read as negative. MRI shows an incomplete T1-hypointense fracture line through the femoral neck with surrounding marrow edema and no displacement. What is the most appropriate management?
text: An 82-year-old man fell from standing and has groin pain with inability to bear weight; two views of the hip are read as negative. MRI shows an incomplete T1-hypointense fracture line through the femoral neck with surrounding marrow edema and no displacement. What is the most appropriate management? | A. Keep him non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic referral for the occult femoral neck fracture | B. Discharge with weight-bearing as tolerated because the plain films were negative | C. Treat presumptively for greater trochanteric bursitis with a corticosteroid injection and mobilize | D. Arrange routine outpatient physiotherapy with a follow-up radiograph in 6 weeks | Correct: A | An MRI-detected occult femoral neck fracture in an older adult who cannot bear weight needs non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral because of the high risk of completion and displacement, even when radiographs are negative. Weight-bearing as tolerated (B), a steroid injection for presumed bursitis (C), or routine delayed physiotherapy (D) all risk the fracture completing and displacing. | hip-bones-stress | hip-bones-stress

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 48-year-old former athlete has groin pain. MRI shows DIFFUSE acetabular and femoral cartilage thinning with several subchondral cysts and global joint-space narrowing, rather than a single focal FAI rim lesion. The patient asks whether hip arthroscopy will fix the joint. Which interpretation best guides the management conversation?
text: A 48-year-old former athlete has groin pain. MRI shows DIFFUSE acetabular and femoral cartilage thinning with several subchondral cysts and global joint-space narrowing, rather than a single focal FAI rim lesion. The patient asks whether hip arthroscopy will fix the joint. Which interpretation best guides the management conversation? | A. Diffuse cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing indicates early/established OA, shifting management away from arthroscopy toward joint-preservation/arthroplasty pathways | B. The subchondral cysts represent an aggressive lytic tumor requiring biopsy before any treatment | C. The findings are a focal FAI rim injury that arthroscopic labral repair will durably correct | D. The pattern is transient bone marrow edema syndrome that will fully resolve with observation | Correct: A | Diffuse bipolar cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing reflects OA, which predicts poor arthroscopic outcomes and pushes the conversation toward preservation or arthroplasty pathways. Subchondral geodes in OA are not aggressive tumors (B). The diffuse pattern is not a focal FAI rim lesion amenable to arthroscopy (C). OA with structural cartilage loss is not self-limited transient edema (D). | hip-cartilage-oa | hip-cartilage-oa

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 52-year-old recreational cyclist has progressive groin pain. MRI demonstrates widespread bipolar cartilage thinning, multiple subchondral geodes, and superior joint-space narrowing, without a discrete focal rim lesion. The patient hopes arthroscopy will restore the joint. Which interpretation best frames the management discussion?
text: A 52-year-old recreational cyclist has progressive groin pain. MRI demonstrates widespread bipolar cartilage thinning, multiple subchondral geodes, and superior joint-space narrowing, without a discrete focal rim lesion. The patient hopes arthroscopy will restore the joint. Which interpretation best frames the management discussion? | A. The geodes are an aggressive lytic process mandating urgent oncologic biopsy | B. This is an isolated focal chondral flap that arthroscopic debridement will permanently resolve | C. Diffuse bipolar cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing represents OA, moving care toward arthroplasty rather than arthroscopic labral surgery | D. This represents reversible transient marrow edema needing only rest | Correct: C | Widespread bipolar cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing indicates OA; arthroscopy is poorly suited and the discussion shifts toward arthroplasty. OA geodes are not aggressive tumors (A). A diffuse process is not an isolated focal flap fixable by debridement (B), and structural OA cartilage loss is not reversible transient edema (D). | hip-cartilage-oa | hip-cartilage-oa

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 58-year-old man on chronic corticosteroids has 6 weeks of groin pain and a normal radiograph. MRI shows a serpentine subchondral line in the anterosuperior femoral head with a 'double-line sign' and preserved spherical head contour without subchondral collapse. What management does this finding most appropriately drive?
text: A 58-year-old man on chronic corticosteroids has 6 weeks of groin pain and a normal radiograph. MRI shows a serpentine subchondral line in the anterosuperior femoral head with a 'double-line sign' and preserved spherical head contour without subchondral collapse. What management does this finding most appropriately drive? | A. Reassure and discharge, since the head contour is preserved and no action is needed | B. Treat as routine transient marrow edema with observation and full activity | C. Refer to orthopedics for pre-collapse AVN, including protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving options | D. Start antibiotics for presumed osteomyelitis based on the marrow signal | Correct: C | A double-line sign with a serpentine subchondral line in a steroid-exposed patient is osteonecrosis (AVN); identifying it before collapse opens a window for protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving procedures, so referral is key. Discharge (A) or treating as transient edema (B) misses a progressive disease. There is no infectious picture to justify antibiotics (D). | hip-dont-miss | hip-dont-miss

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 44-year-old woman with a history of heavy alcohol use has 2 months of groin pain and normal radiographs. MRI demonstrates a serpiginous subchondral 'double-line sign' in the superior femoral head with maintained sphericity and no subchondral fracture or flattening. What management does this finding most appropriately direct?
text: A 44-year-old woman with a history of heavy alcohol use has 2 months of groin pain and normal radiographs. MRI demonstrates a serpiginous subchondral 'double-line sign' in the superior femoral head with maintained sphericity and no subchondral fracture or flattening. What management does this finding most appropriately direct? | A. Discharge with no follow-up because the femoral head remains spherical | B. Manage as benign transient bone marrow edema with unrestricted activity | C. Begin empiric IV antibiotics for suspected septic osteomyelitis | D. Orthopedic referral for pre-collapse osteonecrosis with protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving consideration | Correct: D | A double-line sign with maintained sphericity in an at-risk patient indicates pre-collapse AVN; catching it before collapse allows protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving surgery, so referral is appropriate. Discharge (A) or calling it transient edema (B) misses progressive necrosis. No infectious features justify antibiotics (C). | hip-dont-miss | hip-dont-miss

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 13-year-old boy with obesity has 3 weeks of dull groin and knee pain and now walks with the foot externally rotated. MRI shows widening and irregularity of the proximal femoral physis with posteromedial slip of the epiphysis relative to the metaphysis. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
text: A 13-year-old boy with obesity has 3 weeks of dull groin and knee pain and now walks with the foot externally rotated. MRI shows widening and irregularity of the proximal femoral physis with posteromedial slip of the epiphysis relative to the metaphysis. What is the most appropriate immediate management? | A. Make him non-weight-bearing and arrange urgent orthopedic surgical referral | B. Begin outpatient physical therapy and recheck in 6 weeks | C. Allow full activity with NSAIDs and clinic follow-up in 3 months | D. Inject the hip with corticosteroid and resume sports | Correct: A | Physeal widening with posteromedial epiphyseal slip is a slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE), which is an urgent, non-weight-bearing diagnosis requiring prompt surgical fixation to prevent further slip and AVN. Physical therapy (B), full activity (C), or steroid injection with return to sport (D) all risk progression and avascular necrosis. | hip-dont-miss | hip-dont-miss

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: An 11-year-old boy with obesity has several weeks of vague knee and thigh pain and now limps with the leg turned outward. MRI of the hips shows widening and irregularity of the proximal femoral physis with posteromedial displacement of the epiphysis. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
text: An 11-year-old boy with obesity has several weeks of vague knee and thigh pain and now limps with the leg turned outward. MRI of the hips shows widening and irregularity of the proximal femoral physis with posteromedial displacement of the epiphysis. What is the most appropriate immediate management? | A. Begin a knee-focused physiotherapy program and reassess in 6 weeks | B. Allow unrestricted activity with NSAIDs and routine follow-up in 3 months | C. Make him non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic surgical referral | D. Aspirate the knee to evaluate the referred knee pain | Correct: C | Physeal widening with posteromedial epiphyseal displacement is a slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE); referred knee and thigh pain is classic, and the diagnosis is missed when attention stays on the knee. It requires non-weight-bearing and prompt surgical fixation to prevent further slip and avascular necrosis. Knee physiotherapy (A), unrestricted activity (B), or knee aspiration (D) all delay fixation and risk progression and AVN. | hip-dont-miss | hip-dont-miss

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 25-year-old hockey player has activity-related anterior groin pain. Dedicated hip MRI shows an alpha angle of 68 degrees on the oblique-axial neck plane, an anterosuperior labral tear, AND adjacent acetabular rim cartilage delamination. Conservative rehab has failed over 4 months. What does the imaging add that most supports operative referral over continued rehab?
text: A 25-year-old hockey player has activity-related anterior groin pain. Dedicated hip MRI shows an alpha angle of 68 degrees on the oblique-axial neck plane, an anterosuperior labral tear, AND adjacent acetabular rim cartilage delamination. Conservative rehab has failed over 4 months. What does the imaging add that most supports operative referral over continued rehab? | A. The 68-degree alpha angle alone proves symptomatic disease and mandates surgery regardless of cartilage | B. The rim chondral delamination accompanying cam morphology and a labral tear weights toward arthroscopic referral | C. The labral tear by itself indicates urgent same-week surgery to prevent dislocation | D. The findings indicate diffuse end-stage OA, so arthroplasty rather than arthroscopy is appropriate | Correct: B | Associated rim chondral injury/delamination alongside cam morphology and a labral tear is a feature that shifts decision-making toward arthroscopic surgery, especially after failed rehab. Cam morphology is common and often asymptomatic, so the alpha angle alone is not dispositive (A). A labral tear is not a dislocation emergency (C). Focal rim delamination is not diffuse end-stage OA requiring arthroplasty (D). | hip-fai-labrum | hip-fai-labrum

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 29-year-old dancer has deep flexion-provoked groin pain. Dedicated hip MRI shows an alpha angle of 64 degrees, an anterosuperior labral tear, and focal full-thickness acetabular rim cartilage loss with delamination at the same location, after 5 months of failed physiotherapy. Which imaging feature most strongly supports proceeding to arthroscopic referral rather than further conservative care?
text: A 29-year-old dancer has deep flexion-provoked groin pain. Dedicated hip MRI shows an alpha angle of 64 degrees, an anterosuperior labral tear, and focal full-thickness acetabular rim cartilage loss with delamination at the same location, after 5 months of failed physiotherapy. Which imaging feature most strongly supports proceeding to arthroscopic referral rather than further conservative care? | A. The 64-degree alpha angle alone confirms the pathology is symptomatic and dictates surgery | B. The isolated labral tear mandates emergent operative repair within days | C. The combination indicates global joint-space collapse warranting total hip arthroplasty | D. The focal rim chondral delamination co-located with cam morphology and a labral tear favors surgical referral | Correct: D | Co-located rim chondral delamination with cam morphology and a labral tear, after failed conservative care, weights toward arthroscopic referral. The alpha angle alone does not establish symptomatic disease because cam morphology is frequently asymptomatic (A). A labral tear is not an emergency (B). Focal rim loss is not global collapse requiring arthroplasty (C). | hip-fai-labrum | hip-fai-labrum

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 27-year-old with anterior groin pain has a dedicated hip MRI. On the oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck, the alpha angle measures 70 degrees with an anterosuperior osseous bump, but the labrum and cartilage are intact and the patient is currently asymptomatic at rest. How should this isolated finding influence management?
text: A 27-year-old with anterior groin pain has a dedicated hip MRI. On the oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck, the alpha angle measures 70 degrees with an anterosuperior osseous bump, but the labrum and cartilage are intact and the patient is currently asymptomatic at rest. How should this isolated finding influence management? | A. Treat cam morphology as a common, often asymptomatic finding and correlate clinically rather than refer for surgery on imaging alone | B. Refer for prophylactic arthroscopic osteoplasty because the alpha angle exceeds 55 degrees | C. Order an urgent oncologic workup for the femoral neck osseous bump | D. Place the patient non-weight-bearing for an impending femoral neck fracture | Correct: A | Cam morphology (alpha angle >55 degrees) is common and frequently asymptomatic; isolated morphology without chondrolabral injury or concordant symptoms should be correlated clinically, not taken to surgery. Prophylactic osteoplasty on morphology alone (B) is not indicated. A cam bump is not a tumor (C) or a fracture (D). | hip-fai-labrum | hip-fai-labrum

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 32-year-old recreational swimmer has a dedicated hip MRI for lateral pain later attributed to the abductors. Incidentally, on the oblique-axial neck plane the alpha angle measures 66 degrees with an anterosuperior osseous bump, but the labrum and chondral surfaces are intact and there is no impingement-type pain with deep flexion. How should this isolated cam finding influence management?
text: A 32-year-old recreational swimmer has a dedicated hip MRI for lateral pain later attributed to the abductors. Incidentally, on the oblique-axial neck plane the alpha angle measures 66 degrees with an anterosuperior osseous bump, but the labrum and chondral surfaces are intact and there is no impingement-type pain with deep flexion. How should this isolated cam finding influence management? | A. Refer for prophylactic arthroscopic osteoplasty because the alpha angle is above 55 degrees | B. Treat cam morphology as a common, frequently asymptomatic finding and correlate clinically rather than operate on imaging alone | C. Order a staging workup for an aggressive osseous lesion of the femoral neck | D. Place the patient non-weight-bearing for an impending femoral neck fracture | Correct: B | Cam morphology (alpha angle >55 degrees) is common and frequently asymptomatic; an isolated bump without chondrolabral injury or concordant impingement symptoms should be correlated clinically, not taken to surgery — especially when found incidentally. Prophylactic osteoplasty on morphology alone (A) is not indicated; a cam bump is neither an aggressive osseous lesion (C) nor a fracture (D). | hip-fai-labrum | hip-fai-labrum

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 24-year-old runner has deep anterior groin pain with pivoting. The ordered study is a large-FOV pelvis MRI read as 'no fracture, mild bursitis.' You suspect cam-type FAI and want to measure the alpha angle reliably. Which acquisition change most directly enables an accurate alpha-angle measurement to guide the FAI workup?
text: A 24-year-old runner has deep anterior groin pain with pivoting. The ordered study is a large-FOV pelvis MRI read as 'no fracture, mild bursitis.' You suspect cam-type FAI and want to measure the alpha angle reliably. Which acquisition change most directly enables an accurate alpha-angle measurement to guide the FAI workup? | A. Add a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol with an oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck | B. Repeat the large-FOV pelvis sequence with thinner coronal slices only | C. Add IV gadolinium to the existing large-FOV pelvis sequences | D. Switch the large-FOV T1 sequences to STIR for better fat suppression | Correct: A | Alpha angle requires an oblique-axial plane prescribed along the femoral neck on a dedicated small-FOV hip study; the large-FOV pelvis screen lacks the resolution and plane to measure cam morphology. Thinner coronal slices (B) still do not give the neck-axis plane needed. IV gadolinium (C) does not address geometry/resolution. STIR (D) changes contrast, not the plane or FOV needed for the measurement. | hip-mri-basics | hip-mri-basics

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 31-year-old soccer player has anterior hip pain catching with deep flexion. Outside imaging is a large-FOV pelvis MRI labeled 'unremarkable hips.' You want to confirm or exclude cam morphology before deciding on a surgical referral. Which protocol adjustment most directly provides the information needed to measure cam morphology?
text: A 31-year-old soccer player has anterior hip pain catching with deep flexion. Outside imaging is a large-FOV pelvis MRI labeled 'unremarkable hips.' You want to confirm or exclude cam morphology before deciding on a surgical referral. Which protocol adjustment most directly provides the information needed to measure cam morphology? | A. Order a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol including an oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck | B. Re-run the large-FOV axial sequence with a larger matrix and the same coverage | C. Convert the large-FOV PD sequences to chemically fat-saturated images | D. Add post-contrast large-FOV imaging through both hips | Correct: A | Cam morphology and the alpha angle are measured on a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol with an oblique-axial plane aligned to the femoral neck. A larger matrix on the large-FOV axial (B) still does not provide the neck-axis plane. Chemical fat-sat (C) changes contrast, not the plane/FOV. Contrast (D) adds nothing for measuring osseous cam shape. | hip-mri-basics | hip-mri-basics

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 40-year-old distance runner has worsening anterior groin pain, now with night pain and pain on weight-bearing. The reading radiologist anchors on an incidental small labral fray and stops. Given a fixed search-pattern discipline, which additional structure must be deliberately checked before translating the read into a return-to-run plan?
text: A 40-year-old distance runner has worsening anterior groin pain, now with night pain and pain on weight-bearing. The reading radiologist anchors on an incidental small labral fray and stops. Given a fixed search-pattern discipline, which additional structure must be deliberately checked before translating the read into a return-to-run plan? | A. The tension (superolateral) side of the femoral neck for a stress fracture line and marrow edema | B. The contralateral asymptomatic acetabular labrum for incidental fraying | C. The trochanteric bursa for fluid before scanning the femoral neck | D. The skin and subcutaneous fat for postoperative seroma | Correct: A | Satisfaction-of-search after spotting a minor labral fray risks missing a superolateral (tension-side) femoral neck stress fracture, which is a surgical-urgency finding that changes a runner from 'return to run' to non-weight-bearing and referral. The asymptomatic contralateral labrum (B), bursa first (C), or skin (D) do not carry the same management-changing red-flag weight in this presentation. | hip-search-pattern | hip-search-pattern

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 22-year-old military recruit has 3 weeks of escalating groin pain after ramping up marching, now with rest pain. The MRI report fixates on a small herniation pit and concludes 'benign.' Applying a disciplined search pattern, which area most needs deliberate inspection before clearing the recruit to continue training?
text: A 22-year-old military recruit has 3 weeks of escalating groin pain after ramping up marching, now with rest pain. The MRI report fixates on a small herniation pit and concludes 'benign.' Applying a disciplined search pattern, which area most needs deliberate inspection before clearing the recruit to continue training? | A. The opposite hip's herniation pit for symmetry | B. The iliopsoas bursa for distension before assessing marrow | C. The gluteal subcutaneous tissues for cellulitis | D. The superolateral femoral neck for a tension-side stress fracture and reactive marrow edema | Correct: D | Anchoring on a benign herniation pit can mask a tension-side (superolateral) femoral neck stress fracture in a high-risk recruit; finding it converts management to non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral. The contralateral pit (A), bursa first (B), or subcutaneous tissue (C) do not change this high-stakes decision the way the tension-side neck does. | hip-search-pattern | hip-search-pattern

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 70-year-old woman has chronic lateral hip pain, a limp, and weak abduction. MRI shows a FULL-THICKNESS gluteus medius tendon tear with 2 cm of tendon retraction and fatty muscle atrophy at the greater trochanter. Prior corticosteroid injection and physiotherapy failed. What does this imaging most appropriately direct?
text: A 70-year-old woman has chronic lateral hip pain, a limp, and weak abduction. MRI shows a FULL-THICKNESS gluteus medius tendon tear with 2 cm of tendon retraction and fatty muscle atrophy at the greater trochanter. Prior corticosteroid injection and physiotherapy failed. What does this imaging most appropriately direct? | A. Another image-guided peritrochanteric corticosteroid injection as definitive treatment | B. Reassurance that this is simple trochanteric bursitis managed with stretching | C. Urgent escalation for suspected septic arthritis of the hip | D. Orthopedic surgical referral for consideration of abductor tendon repair | Correct: D | A full-thickness, retracted gluteus medius tear with atrophy that has failed conservative care warrants surgical referral for possible tendon repair. Repeat steroid injection (A) does not address a retracted full-thickness tear and may worsen tendon quality. This is not simple bursitis (B). There is no septic picture (C). | hip-tendons-muscles | hip-tendons-muscles

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 64-year-old woman has persistent lateral hip pain and a Trendelenburg gait. MRI reveals a FULL-THICKNESS gluteus minimus and medius tendon tear with retraction and grade 2 fatty atrophy, after 6 months of physiotherapy and one peritrochanteric injection. What management does this imaging most appropriately support?
text: A 64-year-old woman has persistent lateral hip pain and a Trendelenburg gait. MRI reveals a FULL-THICKNESS gluteus minimus and medius tendon tear with retraction and grade 2 fatty atrophy, after 6 months of physiotherapy and one peritrochanteric injection. What management does this imaging most appropriately support? | A. Repeat peritrochanteric corticosteroid injection as the definitive next step | B. Referral to orthopedic surgery to evaluate for abductor tendon reconstruction | C. Labeling the process as greater trochanteric bursitis treated with a home program | D. Emergent admission for presumed osteomyelitis of the trochanter | Correct: B | A retracted full-thickness abductor tendon tear with fatty atrophy refractory to conservative measures should prompt surgical referral for possible reconstruction. Another injection (A) will not restore a retracted tendon. This is more than bursitis (C). No infectious findings are described to warrant osteomyelitis admission (D). | hip-tendons-muscles | hip-tendons-muscles

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 45-year-old water-skier had a sudden hamstring 'pop' while the hip flexed and the knee extended. MRI shows all three proximal hamstring tendons avulsed from the ischial tuberosity with 3 cm of retraction and a surrounding hematoma. What is the most appropriate management?
text: A 45-year-old water-skier had a sudden hamstring 'pop' while the hip flexed and the knee extended. MRI shows all three proximal hamstring tendons avulsed from the ischial tuberosity with 3 cm of retraction and a surrounding hematoma. What is the most appropriate management? | A. Provide a home stretching program and clear for return to sport in 2 weeks | B. Refer promptly to orthopedic surgery for operative repair of the complete retracted avulsion | C. Provide an image-guided corticosteroid injection at the ischial tuberosity | D. Reassure as a grade 1 strain and continue normal activity | Correct: B | A complete three-tendon proximal hamstring avulsion with significant (3 cm) retraction is a surgical lesion; timely referral is favored because the window for primary repair is best within a few weeks before further retraction and scarring. A stretching program with rapid return (A) or calling it a grade 1 strain (D) understages a complete avulsion. A corticosteroid injection (C) does not address tendon retraction and may impair healing. | hip-tendons-muscles | hip-tendons-muscles

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 41-year-old man slipped on a boat ramp with the hip suddenly flexed and the knee held straight, feeling a painful pop in the buttock. MRI shows all three proximal hamstring tendons avulsed off the ischial tuberosity with 4 cm of retraction and a surrounding hematoma. What is the most appropriate management?
text: A 41-year-old man slipped on a boat ramp with the hip suddenly flexed and the knee held straight, feeling a painful pop in the buttock. MRI shows all three proximal hamstring tendons avulsed off the ischial tuberosity with 4 cm of retraction and a surrounding hematoma. What is the most appropriate management? | A. Refer promptly to orthopedic surgery for operative repair of the complete retracted avulsion | B. Prescribe a home eccentric-stretching program and clear for sport in 2 weeks | C. Inject the ischial tuberosity with corticosteroid to control pain | D. Reassure that this is a low-grade strain and continue normal activity | Correct: A | A complete three-tendon proximal hamstring avulsion with significant (4 cm) retraction is a surgical lesion; prompt referral is favored because primary-repair outcomes are best within a few weeks before further retraction and scarring. A stretching program with early return (B) or calling it a low-grade strain (D) understages a complete avulsion. A corticosteroid injection (C) does not address tendon retraction and can impair healing. | hip-tendons-muscles | hip-tendons-muscles

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tension-side femoral-neck stress fracture
text: A line on the SUPEROLATERAL (tension) cortex is the surgical-urgency pattern — prone to displacement and AVN. Protected/non-weight-bearing + prompt ortho.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: AVN pre-collapse (double-line sign)
text: A radiograph-negative double-line is ARCO I — the joint-preservation window. Refer promptly and image the contralateral hip (often bilateral).

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIFK)
text: A subchondral low-signal fracture line (± flattening) carries collapse risk — it is NOT benign transient marrow edema. Offload and refer.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Marrow-replacing lesion
text: Low-T1 marrow that isn't a stress/edema pattern — tumor or infiltration until proven otherwise.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Proximal hamstring avulsion
text: ≥2 tendons torn AND ≥2 cm retraction meets the surgical-referral window (the sciatic nerve sits right there) — flag it before retraction worsens.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Labral tear — MR-arthrography ↔ hip arthroscopy
text: structure: Acetabular labrum | title: Labral tear — MR-arthrography ↔ hip arthroscopy | body: MR-arthrography is most sensitive for the anterosuperior labral tear; at arthroscopy the same lesion is probed for chondrolabral separation, and tissue quality decides labral repair vs selective debridement.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cam morphology — alpha angle ↔ femoroplasty
text: structure: Cam lesion (FAI) | title: Cam morphology — alpha angle ↔ femoroplasty | body: The aspherical anterior head-neck bump (alpha angle >~55° on oblique-axial/radial) is the cam lesion. At arthroscopy it is burred back (femoroplasty/osteochondroplasty) to restore the concave head-neck offset and the dynamic clearance lost in impingement.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Planes — what each one answers
text: label: Large-FOV coronal (both hips + pelvis) | detail: The screen. T2 FS Dixon across both hips, sacrum, and symphysis catches marrow lesions, occult/stress fractures, AVN, SI joints, and athletic pubalgia — and lets you compare sides. Use this first when pain is poorly localized or possibly referred. At the symphysis, look for the secondary cleft sign; the normal midline (primary) cleft is not pathology. | label: Dedicated single-hip axial | detail: Anterior and posterior labrum, iliopsoas tendon, and the sciatic nerve. Workhorse for anterior/posterior structures once the question is focused and intra-articular. | label: Dedicated single-hip sagittal | detail: Anterior head-neck contour (cam bump) and the anterior joint recess / capsule. Profiles the anterosuperior head-neck junction and anterior chondral surfaces. | label: Oblique-axial (along the femoral neck) / radial | detail: Added specifically to measure the alpha angle. Prescribed parallel to the femoral neck long axis (or as radial reformats) so the anterosuperior head-neck junction lies flat. Cam = alpha angle >~55°. NEVER quote an alpha angle off a straight axial or coronal — it foreshortens the neck and mis-measures the bump.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sequences — what they're for
text: label: Fluid-sensitive fat-sat (T2 FS / STIR) | detail: Edema, marrow injury, effusion, stress-fracture lines, and AVN reactive edema. A finding is more convincing when fluid-bright signal points to it — read these on every step. | label: T1-weighted | detail: Anatomy, marrow replacement (a low-T1 marrow lesion is the red flag), and subtle/occult fracture lines. Fatty marrow is normally bright on T1; loss of that brightness is the warning. | label: Dixon — water vs fat | detail: Separates water (edema, fluid) from fat in one acquisition. The water image behaves like a fat-sat fluid sequence; the fat image confirms true marrow fat and fatty muscle infiltration — reliable fat-sat across the wide pelvic FOV. | label: MR arthrography (the labrum) | detail: Most sensitive modality for the acetabular labrum and subtle anterosuperior tears; intra-articular contrast undercuts a tear and distends the joint. Add it when non-contrast MRI is equivocal or discordant with the exam.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Normal anatomy checklist
text: label: Femoral head & marrow | detail: Spherical with uniformly bright fatty marrow on T1. Asphericity / a bump at the anterosuperior head-neck junction suggests cam morphology; a low-T1 marrow lesion is never normal. A radiograph-negative double-line hip is AVN ARCO I (MRI-positive, pre-collapse). | label: Acetabulum, sourcil & coverage | detail: Smooth subchondral 'sourcil' (the weight-bearing roof on coronal); assess coverage — undercoverage (dysplasia) vs overcoverage (pincer). The cotyloid (acetabular) fossa is non-articular and normally holds fat + ligamentum teres. | label: Acetabular labrum | detail: Triangular, uniformly LOW signal, firmly attached to the rim — best on radial / oblique-axial. The anterosuperior chondrolabral junction is the weight-bearing zone where true tears live. | label: Articular cartilage | detail: Thin, intermediate signal over head and acetabulum; assess on fluid-sensitive sequences and (best) MR arthrogram. Look for focal anterosuperior loss / delamination. | label: Capsule, iliofemoral ligament & anterior recess | detail: Iliofemoral ligament (Y of Bigelow) is the strong anterior restraint; zona orbicularis normally 'waists' the neck — don't call it a tear. A thin anterior recess is normal; capsule integrity matters post-arthroscopy/dislocation. | label: Abductors & greater trochanter | detail: Gluteus medius and minimus — the hip's 'rotator cuff' — insert on the greater trochanter facets with smooth low-signal footprints. This is the GTPS site; most 'trochanteric bursitis' is really gluteal tendinopathy or a footprint tear. | label: Iliopsoas | detail: Tendon crosses the anterior joint / pelvic brim toward the lesser trochanter; a small iliopsoas bursa can be normal. Internal (iliopsoas) snapping is a dynamic ultrasound diagnosis, not a static-MRI one. | label: Hamstring origin | detail: Conjoint tendon on the ischial tuberosity — uniformly low signal at its footprint. Count the tendons: surgical avulsion = ≥2 tendons torn AND ≥2 cm retraction (sciatic nerve is right there). | label: Sciatic nerve | detail: Posterior to the hip, just lateral to the ischial tuberosity (near the hamstring origin) — trace it on axial images for caliber, signal, and perineural edema.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Normal variants — don't overcall
text: label: Os acetabuli | detail: Corticated ossicle at the anterosuperior acetabular rim with smooth margins. Distinguish from an acute rim fracture (sharp margins + marrow edema); in FAI it can represent a chronic rim stress fragment. | label: Supra-acetabular fossa (12 o'clock) | detail: Midline cartilage-filled notch in the acetabular dome at 12 o'clock with smooth margins and no marrow edema — not a chondral defect. | label: Sublabral sulcus / recess | detail: Smooth linear cleft paralleling the labral base with NO displacement and NO paralabral cyst. The posteroinferior/inferior junction is the classic benign site; an anterosuperior cleft cannot be called a variant on location alone — smooth margins and absent secondary signs do that. | label: Stellate lesion (superomedial) | detail: Normal star-shaped BARE AREA of absent cartilage on the SUPEROMEDIAL acetabular roof — medial to the supra-acetabular fossa, continuous with the acetabular notch. Clean margins, no marrow edema. NOT the anterosuperior weight-bearing dome, so it is not where true tears live. | label: Herniation pit (fibrocystic pit) | detail: Small, well-corticated subcortical cyst at the anterosuperior femoral neck (T1-dark, T2-bright). Classically benign and a marker of cam-type FAI — note it and check the alpha angle, but don't escalate to a tumor workup.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Normal labrum vs tear
text: label: Where true tears live | detail: The ANTEROSUPERIOR chondrolabral junction (roughly 12–3 o'clock) is the weight-bearing FAI shear zone and the prime site for a real tear. MR arthrography is the most sensitive modality. | label: Normal labrum / smooth sublabral sulcus | detail: Low-signal triangle firmly attached to the rim; a sulcus is a SMOOTH-walled cleft at the base that parallels the cartilage and does NOT enter the labral substance. | label: Tear — irregular & undercutting | detail: Frayed/blunted/displaced margins with contrast (or fluid) extending INTO the labral substance, often with a paralabral cyst or adjacent chondral loss. A paralabral cyst is a high-yield secondary sign — find the cyst, then scrutinize the adjacent labrum. | label: Don't reflexively call an anterosuperior cleft 'normal' | detail: A posteroinferior smooth sulcus is benign, but the same-looking cleft anterosuperiorly is exactly where tears hide. Weigh margins, displacement, and secondary signs — not location alone.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: FAI measurements — cam, pincer & version
text: label: Alpha angle (cam) | detail: Measured on OBLIQUE-AXIAL (along the femoral neck) or RADIAL images — NOT the straight coronal. >~55° defines cam morphology (anterosuperior head-neck bump with loss of the normal concave waist). Don't eyeball it on a standard axial; measure it on the neck-axis plane. | label: Lateral center-edge angle (LCEA) | detail: Acetabular coverage of the femoral head. <~20–25° = dysplasia/undercoverage; ~25–40° = normal; >~40° = overcoverage/pincer (often with coxa profunda). | label: Crossover sign | detail: Anterior wall projecting lateral to the posterior wall before they meet = acetabular retroversion, a focal anterosuperior pincer pattern. It is an AP-PELVIS RADIOGRAPH sign (confirm on a well-positioned AP film, not on coronal MRI). | label: Cam morphology is common and often asymptomatic | detail: An elevated alpha angle alone does NOT prove symptomatic disease. Correlate with concordant symptoms and chondrolabral injury before any surgical conversation — morphology is not the diagnosis. | label: Femoral version (note) | detail: Excessive femoral anteversion OR retroversion of the neck modifies impingement and instability risk; report it when measured, as it co-determines hip-preservation planning.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Acetabular labral tear
text: label: Location — anterosuperior | detail: True labral tears live at the ANTEROSUPERIOR chondrolabral junction (roughly 12–3 o'clock). Signal extending to the articular margin with irregular morphology, ideally confirmed on two planes. | label: Most sensitive modality | detail: MR ARTHROGRAPHY is the most sensitive study for subtle labral tears (intra-articular contrast outlines the tear and distends the joint). Use it when non-contrast MRI is equivocal. | label: Paralabral cyst | detail: A paralabral cyst is a secondary sign of an adjacent tear — trace the cyst back to its labral defect. Its presence raises confidence even when the tear itself is subtle. | label: Don't overcall the sulcus | detail: A smooth labral recess/sulcus is a normal variant; a tear has irregular margins and reaches the articular surface at the anterosuperior chondrolabral junction.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: AVN — ARCO staging & the collapse hinge
text: label: Stage I | detail: MRI-POSITIVE / radiograph-NEGATIVE, pre-collapse. Double-line sign with maintained sphericity and NO subchondral crescent. A radiograph-negative double-line hip is strictly ARCO I. | label: Stage II | detail: Radiographic sclerosis and/or cysts appear, but STILL pre-collapse (no crescent, no flattening). | label: Stage III | detail: Subchondral crescent / early collapse — the articular surface is no longer congruent. This is the management hinge: post-collapse changes the conversation toward salvage/arthroplasty. | label: Stage IV | detail: Secondary osteoarthritis with joint-space loss and acetabular involvement. | label: Double-line sign | detail: On T2/fluid-sensitive: inner hyperintense granulation tissue paralleling an outer hypointense sclerotic rim. Highly specific for osteonecrosis; distinguishes AVN from transient marrow edema, which lacks it. | label: Lesion size / Kerboul | detail: Necrotic-segment size (e.g., modified Kerboul combined-angle, or >2/3 weight-bearing head involvement) predicts collapse risk and lower core-decompression success. The hinge for joint preservation is catching it PRE-collapse — and AVN is frequently bilateral, so image the contralateral hip.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: SIFK vs transient bone-marrow-edema syndrome
text: label: The distinguishing feature | detail: A subchondral low-signal FRACTURE LINE paralleling the articular surface (with or without focal subchondral collapse/flattening) marks subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIFK). Transient BME syndrome shows diffuse marrow edema WITHOUT a discrete fracture line or collapse. | label: SIFK — collapse risk | detail: The subchondral line plus any flattening signals structural failure and collapse risk; it is not benign self-limited edema. Protected weight-bearing and orthopedic referral, especially in older/osteopenic patients. | label: Transient BME syndrome | detail: Self-limited diffuse femoral-head/neck edema that resolves over months. Isolated marrow edema is a differential, not a diagnosis — chase a subchondral line or a double-line rim before reassuring.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Femoral-neck stress fracture — which side
text: label: Superolateral (tension side) | detail: Tension-side fracture line along the SUPEROLATERAL cortex is the SURGICAL-URGENCY pattern — prone to displacement and AVN. Treat as urgent: protected/non-weight-bearing and prompt orthopedic referral. | label: Inferomedial (compression side) | detail: Compression-side line along the INFEROMEDIAL cortex is lower-risk and more often managed conservatively with activity modification and protected weight-bearing. | label: Look for the line, not just edema | detail: A discrete low-signal fracture line within marrow edema (confirmed on T1) defines and locates the stress fracture and changes the weight-bearing protocol. Estimate the percentage of neck width involved.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cartilage grading (Outerbridge-type 0–IV)
text: label: Grade 0 | detail: Normal cartilage — uniform thickness, smooth surface, homogeneous signal. | label: Grade I | detail: Softening/signal heterogeneity (swelling) with an intact surface. | label: Grade II | detail: Partial-thickness defect/fissuring <50% of cartilage depth. | label: Grade III | detail: Deep defect >50% of depth, extending to but not through the subchondral plate. | label: Grade IV | detail: Full-thickness loss with exposed subchondral bone, often with reactive marrow edema. | label: Chondrolabral junction in FAI | detail: In cam-type FAI, the surgically significant lesion is anterosuperior acetabular RIM chondral delamination at the chondrolabral junction. Co-located rim chondral injury + cam + labral tear after failed rehab weights toward arthroscopic referral. | label: Don't overcall the stellate lesion | detail: The stellate lesion is a NORMAL bare-area variant of the SUPEROMEDIAL acetabular roof (medial to the supra-acetabular fossa, continuous with the acetabular notch) — not the anterosuperior weight-bearing dome. Do not mistake this medial bare cartilage gap for a chondral defect; true chondrolabral pathology is anterosuperior.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: GTPS / abductor tear — the hip's rotator cuff
text: label: What GTPS really is | detail: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome is usually gluteus medius/minimus (the abductor 'rotator cuff of the hip') tendinopathy or footprint tear — most labeled 'trochanteric bursitis' is actually gluteal tendinopathy. Name the tendon. | label: Grade the abductor lesion | detail: Distinguish tendinopathy (thickening/signal, intact fibers) from partial-thickness footprint tear from full-thickness tear with retraction and muscle fatty atrophy. | label: What changes management | detail: A full-thickness, retracted abductor tear (especially with fatty atrophy) is a SURGICAL referral, analogous to a rotator cuff tear — not a bursitis to inject and forget.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Proximal hamstring avulsion & athletic pubalgia
text: label: Hamstring — surgical-referral criteria | detail: Count tendons and measure retraction at the ischial tuberosity. Surgical referral when at least 2 tendons are torn AND at least 2 cm of retraction; the sciatic nerve sits nearby a retracted stump. Always state both numbers. | label: Repair window | detail: The window for primary repair is best within a few weeks before further retraction and scarring — so flag the surgical pattern promptly. | label: Athletic pubalgia — secondary cleft sign | detail: Rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurosis injury at the symphysis. The SECONDARY cleft sign (fluid tracking inferolaterally from the symphysis) indicates the tear. The PRIMARY cleft is the normal midline symphyseal cleft — don't confuse them. | label: Snapping hip is dynamic, not static MRI | detail: Internal (iliopsoas) and external (ITB over the trochanter) snapping hip are DYNAMIC ULTRASOUND diagnoses. Static MRI may be normal — recognize the limitation.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Protocol — when each study changes management
text: label: Non-contrast MRI (most questions) | detail: Dedicated small-FOV hip with oblique-axial neck sequences with or without radial reformats. Best first study for AVN, SIFK/stress fracture, marrow edema, abductor and hamstring tendons, and cam morphology (alpha angle). | label: MR arthrogram | detail: Most sensitive for subtle labral tears and chondrolabral pathology; add when non-contrast MRI is equivocal or discordant with the exam in suspected FAI/instability. | label: CT | detail: Best for precise bony morphology and version (cam/pincer, acetabular version, femoral version) when osseous quantification will change hip-preservation surgical planning. | label: Radiograph | detail: First-line and decisive for AVN staging (radiograph-negative double-line = ARCO I; sclerosis/cysts = II; crescent/collapse = III; secondary OA = IV). Also screens for OA, dysplasia (LCEA), and pincer overcoverage, and is where the crossover sign of retroversion is read. A large-FOV pelvis screen cannot reliably measure the alpha angle — order the dedicated hip study for that.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: An alpha angle >55 degrees defines cam morphology, but measure it on radial reformats around the head-neck junction (maximal between 1 and 2 o'clock) — a single straight axial slice misses the anterosuperior bump and underestimates the deformity.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Cam FAI is the convex bump and loss of head-neck offset at the anterosuperior junction; pincer is acetabular over-coverage. The distinction matters because cam drives anterosuperior chondrolabral delamination while pincer drives circumferential labral degeneration and contrecoup chondral loss.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: On radiographs/CT, a crossover sign, posterior wall sign, or prominent ischial spine signals acetabular retroversion (focal pincer); coxa profunda or protrusio signals global over-coverage. Flag these because over-resection of the rim risks iatrogenic instability.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A lateral center-edge angle <20 degrees indicates dysplasia (undercoverage, instability risk) and >40 degrees indicates pincer over-coverage; the identical labral tear is managed very differently depending on which side of that range the hip sits.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Pathologic labral tears live anterosuperiorly and show fluid undercutting the labral base; the key mimic is a smooth posteroinferior sublabral recess following the labral contour. Anchor the call on location, full-thickness extension, and an associated paralabral cyst — not signal alone.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A paralabral cyst is a secondary sign of a labral tear even when the tear itself is subtle; trace the cyst neck back to the labral base on the source images to confirm the communicating tear.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Rim chondral delamination at the anterosuperior chondrolabral junction is the lesion that drives surgical decision-making in cam FAI; look for a fluid cleft undermining cartilage from subchondral bone, which conventional MRI routinely under-calls.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: In pincer FAI, search for contrecoup chondral injury at the posteroinferior acetabulum and femoral head — leverage damage opposite the impinging anterosuperior rim — which signals more advanced disease than the rim lesion alone.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: MR arthrography with axial traction distracts the joint and opens the chondrolabral cleft, markedly improving detection and grading of cartilage delamination versus conventional MRA; an undermining fluid cleft indicates unstable delamination that reorders the surgical plan. Request it when chondral mapping is the question.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A superolateral (tension-side) femoral neck stress fracture is a surgical emergency: this side is under distraction, prone to displacement and AVN, and warrants urgent fixation referral — not the conservative course used for compression-side fractures.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A compression-side (inferomedial) femoral neck stress fracture without a discrete fracture line can be managed with protected weight-bearing; but a fracture line crossing more than 50 percent of the neck width shifts the conversation toward fixation regardless of side.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: AVN's near-pathognomonic finding is the double-line sign on T2 — a hyperintense inner granulation line paralleled by a hypointense sclerotic outer line at the necrosis interface; its presence distinguishes AVN from nonspecific marrow edema.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: The hinge in AVN management is subchondral collapse: a subchondral fracture (crescent sign) or articular flattening marks ARCO stage III and ends the window for joint-preserving core decompression, pushing toward arthroplasty.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Transient bone marrow edema syndrome shows diffuse femoral head and neck edema with NO subchondral fracture line and is self-limiting; a low-signal subchondral line instead points to subchondral insufficiency fracture, which carries collapse risk.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Subchondral insufficiency fracture shows a serpiginous low-signal line parallel to the articular surface with surrounding edema, typically in older osteopenic patients; a line length >4 mm or any articular flattening predicts collapse.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Gluteus medius and minimus tears begin as undersurface partial tears at the greater trochanter footprint (lateral and anterior facets) with peritendinous T2 signal — the MRI substrate of recalcitrant greater trochanteric pain, distinct from simple trochanteric bursitis.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A full-thickness retracted gluteus medius/minimus tear with fatty muscle atrophy is the surgically relevant 'rotator cuff of the hip' lesion; measure the tendon gap and grade atrophy, because retraction plus high-grade fatty infiltration predicts irreparability and warrants referral.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Proximal hamstring avulsions hinge on tendon count and retraction: a complete 2-tendon (semitendinosus plus biceps) or 3-tendon avulsion with more than 2 cm retraction is a repair candidate, whereas partial or non-retracted tears are managed conservatively.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: In athletic pubalgia, scrutinize the rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurosis at the pubic symphysis; the secondary cleft sign (fluid tracking inferolaterally from the symphyseal cleft) marks the microtear at this common aponeurotic plate.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Internal snapping hip is the iliopsoas tendon flicking over the iliopectineal eminence or femoral head; on MRI look for iliopsoas bursal distension and peritendinous edema, and remember dynamic ultrasound confirms the snap that static MRI cannot show.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Apophyseal avulsions map to muscle origins: ASIS = sartorius, AIIS = rectus femoris (direct head), ischial tuberosity = hamstrings, lesser trochanter = iliopsoas — match the displaced ossicle to the pulling tendon in the adolescent athlete.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A lesser trochanter avulsion in an adult without significant trauma is a pathologic fracture until proven otherwise — search the proximal femur and pelvis for an underlying metastasis or marrow-replacing lesion before calling it a simple iliopsoas avulsion.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A herniation pit (Pitt's pit) is a small well-corticated subchondral cyst at the anterosuperior head-neck junction; usually a benign incidental finding, but in the right setting it flags cam morphology and chronic impingement biomechanics.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome on MRI is a spectrum — bursal fluid, gluteal tendinosis, and footprint partial tears — and identifying an actual tendon tear rather than isolated bursitis is what redirects refractory cases toward targeted injection or surgery.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Iliopsoas bursa distension communicates with the hip joint in roughly 15 percent of people; a large fluid collection medial to the femoral neck can be a joint effusion decompressing into the bursa — check the joint for synovitis or a labral source rather than calling isolated bursitis.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: For suspected FAI, report all three legs of the triad — femoral cam (alpha angle), acetabular coverage (LCEA, crossover, profunda), and chondrolabral damage (anterosuperior delamination plus paralabral cyst) — because surgery must address the bony deformity and the soft-tissue lesion together.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A normal hyaline cartilage stripe is thicker on the acetabular side than the femoral side at the hip; asymmetric thinning, full-thickness defects, or subchondral cysts at the anterosuperior rim signal the advanced chondral wear that lowers the yield of joint preservation.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Ischiofemoral impingement is suggested by quadratus femoris edema with narrowing of both the ischiofemoral space (<17 mm) and the quadratus femoris space (<8 mm); it is an under-recognized cause of deep buttock pain that mimics hamstring or sciatic pathology.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: Os acetabuli (an unfused or stress-related rim ossicle) can represent a chronic rim fracture in pincer FAI; distinguish it from a benign accessory ossification center, because a painful os acetabuli may be addressed at the time of rim trimming.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: On a hip effusion, a complex or T2-heterogeneous effusion with synovial thickening and rim enhancement after contrast should raise septic arthritis over a bland reactive effusion — aspirate rather than image again, because delay risks rapid cartilage destruction.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip — which for which question?
text: Large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip — which for which question? | Large-FOV screen for bilateral/referred problems (insufficiency fractures, sacroiliitis, occult marrow lesions, unclear pain source) and the contralateral hip. Small-FOV dedicated hip for intra-articular detail (labrum, cartilage, cam/pincer/FAI). A screen will underdiagnose labral/chondral disease.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which MRI plane is essential for measuring the alpha angle, and what is the cam-morphology threshold?
text: Which MRI plane is essential for measuring the alpha angle, and what is the cam-morphology threshold? | Oblique-axial along the femoral neck (or radial reformats), which lays the anterosuperior head-neck junction flat. Alpha angle >55 degrees indicates cam morphology. Never measure it on a standard true-axial slice.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you prove a bright marrow signal is benign fat rather than pathology?
text: How do you prove a bright marrow signal is benign fat rather than pathology? | Pair T1 with a fat-sat sequence: fat is bright on T1 and drops out (goes dark) on fat-sat/STIR. A signal that stays bright on fat-sat is not fat and must be characterized. A confluent low-T1 lesion darker than muscle/disc is a tumor red flag.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: When does MR arthrography change hip management versus non-contrast MRI?
text: When does MR arthrography change hip management versus non-contrast MRI? | Order MRA when a labral/chondral finding would push toward arthroscopy or alter the operative plan (FAI, suspected labral detachment/chondral delamination). For marrow (stress fracture, AVN, tumor), tendon/muscle, or advanced OA questions, non-contrast suffices and MRA adds a needle with no payoff.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On hip MRI, where do most pathologic labral tears and FAI rim chondral injuries occur, and why does location matter for management?
text: On hip MRI, where do most pathologic labral tears and FAI rim chondral injuries occur, and why does location matter for management? | At the anterosuperior chondrolabral junction (weight-bearing zone). Anterosuperior disruption with adjacent cartilage delamination drives surgical weighting in FAI; the same-appearing cleft posteroinferiorly is usually a benign sublabral sulcus.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the zona orbicularis and what is the classic pitfall?
text: What is the zona orbicularis and what is the classic pitfall? | A normal circular low-signal capsular condensation that waists the femoral neck on coronal images. The pitfall is mistaking it for a capsular tear or paralabral cyst and over-referring a normal hip.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What MRI-based threshold for a proximal hamstring origin tear triggers surgical referral?
text: What MRI-based threshold for a proximal hamstring origin tear triggers surgical referral? | ≥2 of the 3 tendons (conjoint of semitendinosus + biceps femoris long head, plus semimembranosus) torn AND ≥2 cm retraction → refer to surgeon. Below that, rehab. Always report tendon count and retraction distance.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you distinguish a benign herniation pit from concerning pathology on hip MRI?
text: How do you distinguish a benign herniation pit from concerning pathology on hip MRI? | A herniation (fibrocystic) pit is a small, corticated, T2-bright/T1-dark cyst at the anterosuperior femoral neck — often incidental but associated with cam FAI. If symptomatic, look for cam morphology (alpha angle >55°); it is not a marrow-replacing lesion.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: In the 7-step hip/pelvis search pattern, why is bone/marrow read as step 2 rather than last?
text: In the 7-step hip/pelvis search pattern, why is bone/marrow read as step 2 rather than last? | The disposition-changing lesions hide in bone/marrow — tension-side neck fracture, AVN, SIFK, confluent low-T1 marrow. Reading them early (while attention is freshest) instead of at the fatigued end of the study prevents the highest-stakes miss. Use both T1 (marrow replacement) and fluid-sensitive (edema/fracture line).

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On which plane is the alpha angle measured, and what value is abnormal?
text: On which plane is the alpha angle measured, and what value is abnormal? | Oblique-axial along the femoral neck (or radial reformats); alpha angle >55 degrees is abnormal (cam morphology). A routine axial foreshortens the neck and misrepresents the bump — confirm the dedicated oblique-axial/radial images exist before reporting a number.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Name the four classic satisfaction-of-search traps on hip/pelvis MRI and the defeat tactic.
text: Name the four classic satisfaction-of-search traps on hip/pelvis MRI and the defeat tactic. | Tension-side (superolateral) femoral-neck stress fracture, sacral/pubic ramus insufficiency fracture, pubic symphysis (secondary cleft/athletic pubalgia), and marrow (confluent low-T1 darker than muscle/disc = tumor; adult atraumatic lesser-trochanter avulsion = pathologic-fracture red flag). Defeat with a deliberate second bone/marrow + pelvic-ring pass after the soft-tissue read.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How does the presence or absence of a subchondral fracture line change hip management?
text: How does the presence or absence of a subchondral fracture line change hip management? | No fracture line = transient bone marrow edema = rehab. A subchondral fracture line (SIFK) carries collapse risk and is urgent; a superolateral femoral-neck line is a surgical emergency. The fracture line is the hinge between conservative and surgical management.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On hip MRI, which femoral-neck stress fracture location is a surgical emergency, and what is the immediate management?
text: On hip MRI, which femoral-neck stress fracture location is a surgical emergency, and what is the immediate management? | A TENSION-side (superolateral) fracture line — high risk of completion/displacement and AVN. Make the patient non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic referral for fixation. Infero-medial (compression) lines are lower risk and managed with protected weight-bearing.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What MRI finding marks the ARCO pre-collapse to post-collapse transition in femoral-head AVN, and why does it matter?
text: What MRI finding marks the ARCO pre-collapse to post-collapse transition in femoral-head AVN, and why does it matter? | The subchondral fracture / crescent sign (ARCO II→III). Pre-collapse hips (I–II) are candidates for joint-preserving treatment (core decompression); post-collapse hips (III–IV) generally progress to arthroplasty. Always check the contralateral hip.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you distinguish transient BME syndrome (BMES) from AVN and SIFK on MRI?
text: How do you distinguish transient BME syndrome (BMES) from AVN and SIFK on MRI? | BMES = diffuse homogeneous marrow edema of the head/neck with NO subchondral fracture line, NO necrotic segment, and NO double-line sign; it is self-limiting. AVN shows a geographic lesion with a double-line sign; SIFK shows a subchondral low-signal fracture line with collapse risk.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A young female athlete has a sacral ala stress fracture on MRI. What additional work-up is essential?
text: A young female athlete has a sacral ala stress fracture on MRI. What additional work-up is essential? | Screen for the female athlete triad / RED-S — relative energy deficiency, menstrual dysfunction, and low bone mineral density — and address bone health, since this underlies the fatigue fracture. In older patients, bilateral ala edema with the Honda/H sign signals an osteoporotic insufficiency fracture.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: In which plane should the alpha angle be measured, and what value defines pathologic cam morphology?
text: In which plane should the alpha angle be measured, and what value defines pathologic cam morphology? | Measure on oblique-axial/radial images angled along the femoral neck (a straight axial under-samples the anterosuperior bump). Alpha angle >55° = pathologic cam; 50–55° borderline; <50° normal. Treat as one input alongside symptoms and a chondrolabral lesion — asymptomatic cam morphology is common.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you distinguish a normal sublabral recess from an anterosuperior labral tear on MR arthrogram?
text: How do you distinguish a normal sublabral recess from an anterosuperior labral tear on MR arthrogram? | A recess is a smooth-walled cleft at the labral base that parallels the cartilage and does not enter the labral body. A tear shows contrast extending into the labral substance with frayed/blunted margins and secondary signs (paralabral cyst, chondral loss, FAI morphology). Trajectory and margins decide it, not location, since sublabral sulci also occur anterosuperiorly.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What does a paralabral cyst tell you, and what should you do when you see one?
text: What does a paralabral cyst tell you, and what should you do when you see one? | It is a specific secondary sign of an underlying full-thickness labral tear (joint fluid pumped through a one-way valve). Use it as a flag: go back and scrutinize the adjacent labrum for the tear, even if subtle, and note any mass effect on adjacent structures.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Why does rim chondral injury at the chondrolabral junction drive the FAI surgical decision?
text: Why does rim chondral injury at the chondrolabral junction drive the FAI surgical decision? | It is the strongest imaging predictor of arthroscopic outcome. Reparable delamination/wave sign with an intact labrum favors osteochondroplasty + chondrolabral repair; diffuse full-thickness loss or Tönnis 2–3 joint-space narrowing predicts scope failure and steers toward conservative care or arthroplasty.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On MRI, how does cam-shear chondral delamination differ from full-thickness loss, and why does it matter surgically?
text: On MRI, how does cam-shear chondral delamination differ from full-thickness loss, and why does it matter surgically? | Delamination = a deep cartilage-bone cleavage (thin fluid undercutting an intact, normal-thickness anterosuperior surface); full-thickness = a focal defect down to bone. Delaminated flaps are unstable and must be addressed (debridement/fixation) along with cam osteoplasty, or isolated cam correction fails.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What MRI feature distinguishes a benign subchondral geode from a tumor red flag?
text: What MRI feature distinguishes a benign subchondral geode from a tumor red flag? | A geode is small, rounded, well-corticated, T2-bright/T1-dark, and sits at the cartilage defect/load point. The red flag is confluent marrow signal lower in T1 than adjacent muscle or disc (marrow replacement): work it up, don't call it OA.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What cartilage/joint-space threshold shifts a hip from arthroscopy to arthroplasty?
text: What cartilage/joint-space threshold shifts a hip from arthroscopy to arthroplasty? | Diffuse circumferential cartilage loss with established joint-space narrowing (Tönnis grade ≥2, space ≤~2 mm), confirmed on a weightbearing radiograph, predicts arthroscopy failure and moves the patient into arthroplasty territory.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: When does MR arthrography with traction improve chondral detection?
text: When does MR arthrography with traction improve chondral detection? | When standard MRI is equivocal and you suspect FAI/labral or chondral pathology: traction separates the apposed surfaces so contrast fills defects and undercuts delaminated flaps. Reserve this invasive test for cases where the result changes whether you operate.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which gluteus medius/minimus tear pattern mandates surgical referral rather than conservative GTPS management?
text: Which gluteus medius/minimus tear pattern mandates surgical referral rather than conservative GTPS management? | A full-thickness, retracted abductor tendon tear with fatty muscle atrophy — the 'rotator cuff tear of the hip.' Tendinopathy and low-grade partial tears get PT and image-guided injection.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What two numbers must a proximal hamstring avulsion report include for surgical planning?
text: What two numbers must a proximal hamstring avulsion report include for surgical planning? | Tendon count (how many of the 3 tendons are torn) and retraction distance from the ischial tuberosity. A 2–3 tendon avulsion retracted >2 cm in an active patient favors surgical repair.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: State the universal muscle-strain grades 1–3 and the key management shift.
text: State the universal muscle-strain grades 1–3 and the key management shift. | Grade 1 = feathery/interstitial edema without architectural fiber disruption, relative rest. Grade 2 = partial tear with fiber discontinuity at the myotendinous junction, PT. Grade 3 = complete rupture ± retraction, consider surgical referral. In adolescents the same mechanism avulses the AIIS apophysis.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you confirm internal snapping hip, and why is MRI often unhelpful?
text: How do you confirm internal snapping hip, and why is MRI often unhelpful? | Internal snapping (iliopsoas over the iliopectineal eminence) is a dynamic diagnosis; resting MRI is frequently normal. Confirm with dynamic ultrasound. Don't blame an incidental labral tear.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On femoral-neck stress fracture, which side of the neck makes it a surgical emergency, and what do you do?
text: On femoral-neck stress fracture, which side of the neck makes it a surgical emergency, and what do you do? | A fracture line on the SUPEROLATERAL (tension) side is unstable and a surgical emergency — make the patient non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent ortho referral. Compression-side (inferomedial) fractures are managed with protected weight-bearing.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: In femoral-head AVN, which MRI finding separates joint-preserving surgery from arthroplasty?
text: In femoral-head AVN, which MRI finding separates joint-preserving surgery from arthroplasty? | The subchondral fracture / crescent sign with loss of sphericity. Pre-collapse hips (intact contour, double-line sign only) can be offered core decompression; once the subchondral fracture/collapse appears, the hip trends toward arthroplasty. Check the contralateral hip — AVN is often bilateral.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the highest-yield T1 check for distinguishing benign marrow from tumor replacement in the hip?
text: What is the highest-yield T1 check for distinguishing benign marrow from tumor replacement in the hip? | Compare marrow to muscle/disc on T1. Normal red marrow is BRIGHTER than skeletal muscle; confluent marrow that is DARKER than muscle or disc is replaced/abnormal (tumor red flag) and warrants metastatic/myeloma workup. Refer soft-tissue masses (deep, >5 cm, enlarging) to a sarcoma center before any biopsy.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which MRI sign localizes athletic pubalgia, and where is the lesion?
