text: Which MRI sign localizes athletic pubalgia, and where is the lesion? | The secondary cleft sign — fluid/contrast tracking inferolaterally from the pubic symphysis — localizes a microtear of the rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurosis at the pubic attachment. The hip joint typically looks normal; a dedicated symphysis protocol is needed. Most cases respond to a core/adductor rehab program.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 19-year-old soccer player has deep groin pain and a positive FADIR impingement test. The ordering note asks: "Cam lesion? Measure alpha angle." The tech is about to acquire a standard large-FOV pelvis screen with true-axial images only.
text: A 19-year-old soccer player has deep groin pain and a positive FADIR impingement test. The ordering note asks: "Cam lesion? Measure alpha angle." The tech is about to acquire a standard large-FOV pelvis screen with true-axial images only. | A. Proceed — a true-axial large-FOV screen reliably measures the alpha angle | B. Switch to a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol and add oblique-axial images along the femoral neck | C. Add intravenous contrast to the screen to see the cam lesion | D. Cancel imaging; cam morphology cannot be measured on MRI | B | Cam morphology and the alpha angle are assessed at the anterosuperior head-neck junction, which is laid flat only on oblique-axial (or radial) planes off a high-resolution dedicated hip protocol. A true-axial slice cuts the neck obliquely and mismeasures the angle (A wrong). IV contrast does not define cam bone morphology (C). MRI measures the alpha angle well when the correct plane and FOV are used (D).

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 55-year-old with bilateral buttock and groin ache after marathon training gets a pelvis MRI. On the chemical fat-sat coronal, fat suppression looks patchy and failed at the lateral edges of the field, obscuring the sacrum and proximal femurs.
text: A 55-year-old with bilateral buttock and groin ache after marathon training gets a pelvis MRI. On the chemical fat-sat coronal, fat suppression looks patchy and failed at the lateral edges of the field, obscuring the sacrum and proximal femurs. | A. The chemical fat-sat is adequate; read it as is | B. Repeat with a small-FOV dedicated single-hip protocol | C. Rely on the STIR sequence, which suppresses fat uniformly across the large FOV | D. Add MR arthrography to evaluate the marrow | C | Chemical (spectral) fat-sat fails at the edges of a large FOV due to B0 inhomogeneity; STIR provides uniform fat suppression across a wide field and is the edema screen of choice here, ideal for a bilateral stress-fracture question. Reading failed fat-sat risks missing or mimicking marrow edema (A). A single-hip protocol abandons the bilateral question (B). Arthrography is intra-articular and adds nothing to a marrow evaluation (D).

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 16-year-old gymnast with FAI signs has a 3T non-contrast hip MRI suggesting a possible anterosuperior labral detachment, but the surgeon wants confirmation before offering arthroscopy. Marrow and tendons are normal.
text: A 16-year-old gymnast with FAI signs has a 3T non-contrast hip MRI suggesting a possible anterosuperior labral detachment, but the surgeon wants confirmation before offering arthroscopy. Marrow and tendons are normal. | A. Order an MR arthrogram to better outline the labrum and confirm a surgically relevant tear | B. Order a STIR-only pelvis screen to characterize the marrow | C. No further imaging is useful; labral tears cannot be confirmed by MRI | D. Order a large-FOV pelvis screen to improve labral resolution | A | MR arthrography distends the joint and lets contrast track into a labral detachment, adding management-relevant detail when a positive finding would push toward arthroscopy. A STIR marrow screen does not address the labrum, which is already normal here (B). MRA reliably demonstrates labral tears, so further imaging is in fact useful (C). A large-FOV screen has lower spatial resolution and would blur the labrum further (D).

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 24-year-old soccer player has chronic groin pain. MR arthrogram shows a smooth, corticated ossicle along the anterosuperior acetabular rim with no marrow edema and intact adjacent cartilage. The labrum is normal. What is the best management implication?
text: A 24-year-old soccer player has chronic groin pain. MR arthrogram shows a smooth, corticated ossicle along the anterosuperior acetabular rim with no marrow edema and intact adjacent cartilage. The labrum is normal. What is the best management implication? | A. Treat as a normal os acetabuli variant; reassure and correlate clinically rather than refer for acute fracture fixation | B. Refer urgently for open reduction and internal fixation of an acute rim fracture | C. Start a marrow-replacement tumor protocol for an osseous lesion | D. Aspirate an associated paralabral cyst | A | A smooth, corticated ossicle at the anterosuperior rim without marrow edema is an os acetabuli, a benign secondary-ossification variant — reassure and correlate clinically. An acute rim fracture (B) has sharp, non-corticated margins with marrow edema, absent here. A normal corticated ossicle is not a marrow-replacing tumor (C), and there is no cyst to aspirate (D).

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 62-year-old woman has lateral hip pain and a Trendelenburg gait. Coronal fluid-sensitive MRI shows distended peritrochanteric bursal fluid AND a full-thickness gap with tendon retraction at the gluteus medius footprint on the greater trochanter. What is the most appropriate management?
text: A 62-year-old woman has lateral hip pain and a Trendelenburg gait. Coronal fluid-sensitive MRI shows distended peritrochanteric bursal fluid AND a full-thickness gap with tendon retraction at the gluteus medius footprint on the greater trochanter. What is the most appropriate management? | A. Diagnose isolated trochanteric bursitis and offer only a bursal corticosteroid injection | B. Refer for surgical evaluation of a full-thickness retracted abductor tendon tear | C. Reassure that bursal fluid is always a normal physiologic finding | D. Begin anticoagulation for suspected venous thrombosis | B | A full-thickness, retracted gluteus medius tear is the hip's rotator-cuff failure and warrants surgical referral; the bursal fluid is the flag for the underlying tendon tear, not an isolated bursitis. Treating it as isolated bursitis (A) misses the surgical lesion. Distended bursal fluid is not always physiologic (C), and the findings are musculotendinous, not vascular (D).

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 27-year-old hockey player has insidious lower-abdominal and proximal-adductor groin pain that worsens with cutting and resisted sit-ups. Coronal fluid-sensitive MRI shows fluid tracking inferolaterally from the pubic symphysis at the rectus abdominis–adductor aponeurosis (secondary cleft sign). Which structure and referral pathway is implicated?
text: A 27-year-old hockey player has insidious lower-abdominal and proximal-adductor groin pain that worsens with cutting and resisted sit-ups. Coronal fluid-sensitive MRI shows fluid tracking inferolaterally from the pubic symphysis at the rectus abdominis–adductor aponeurosis (secondary cleft sign). Which structure and referral pathway is implicated? | A. Ligamentum teres tear; refer for hip arthroscopy | B. Tension-side femoral neck stress fracture; make non-weight-bearing emergently | C. Rectus abdominis–adductor aponeurosis (athletic pubalgia); refer to a core-muscle-injury specialist | D. Iliopsoas bursitis; aspirate the iliopsoas bursa | C | The secondary cleft sign at the rectus abdominis–adductor aponeurosis localizes athletic pubalgia (core muscle injury) at the symphysis — refer to a core-muscle-injury/sports-hernia specialist. The ligamentum teres (A) is intra-articular at the fovea, not the symphysis. There is no femoral-neck fracture line (B). The iliopsoas bursa (D) is anteromedial to the joint, not at the symphyseal aponeurosis.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 24-year-old runner has an MRI for groin pain. You immediately spot an anterosuperior labral tear and a small paralabral cyst, and begin dictating. Before you finalize, which action best protects against satisfaction of search on this study?
text: A 24-year-old runner has an MRI for groin pain. You immediately spot an anterosuperior labral tear and a small paralabral cyst, and begin dictating. Before you finalize, which action best protects against satisfaction of search on this study? | A. Re-measure the alpha angle on a third radial image to confirm the cam | B. Complete the fixed search order, including a dedicated bone/marrow and pelvic-ring pass | C. Add T1 post-contrast sequences to better characterize the paralabral cyst | D. Recommend a follow-up arthrogram to grade the labral tear | B | Satisfaction of search is defeated by completing the fixed order regardless of the first finding — a deliberate marrow/bone pass catches a tension-side neck stress fracture or confluent low-T1 marrow that the labral tear distracted you from. A is redundant characterization of an already-seen finding; C and D further work up the lesion you already found, which is exactly the trap — they do nothing to surface a silent second lesion.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 19-year-old distance runner has 3 weeks of activity-related groin pain and normal radiographs. MRI shows marrow edema in the femoral neck with a thin hypointense fracture line abutting the superolateral cortex. What is the appropriate disposition?
text: A 19-year-old distance runner has 3 weeks of activity-related groin pain and normal radiographs. MRI shows marrow edema in the femoral neck with a thin hypointense fracture line abutting the superolateral cortex. What is the appropriate disposition? | A. Begin a progressive running rehab program with follow-up in 6 weeks | B. Image-guided corticosteroid injection of the hip joint | C. Make non-weight-bearing and refer urgently for surgical evaluation | D. Reassure that this is transient bone marrow edema and continue training | C | A fracture line touching the superolateral (tension) cortex is a tension-side femoral-neck stress fracture — a surgical emergency at high risk of completion and displacement; it requires non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral. A and D would be reasonable only for a compression-side reaction or transient BME with NO fracture line; the line on the tension side is the hinge. B does not address an unstable fracture and could worsen it.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 31-year-old hockey player has anterior groin pain. The hip joint, labrum, and cartilage are unremarkable. Where should you focus next, given the pain location, before calling the study negative?
text: A 31-year-old hockey player has anterior groin pain. The hip joint, labrum, and cartilage are unremarkable. Where should you focus next, given the pain location, before calling the study negative? | A. The rectus-adductor aponeurosis and pubic symphysis for athletic pubalgia | B. The gluteus medius and minimus footprints on the greater trochanter | C. The proximal hamstring origin at the ischial tuberosity | D. The sciatic nerve in the deep gluteal space | A | Groin pain has both an intra-articular and an extra-articular answer; with a clean joint, the extra-articular source — athletic pubalgia at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis (look for the secondary cleft sign) and the symphysis — must be read before calling the study negative. B is for lateral pain (abductors/GTPS); C and D are for posterior/buttock pain — wrong region for this anterior/groin presentation.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 22-year-old female cross-country runner has 4 weeks of groin pain and a normal radiograph. Coronal STIR shows neck marrow edema, and T1 reveals a discrete low-signal fracture line along the SUPEROLATERAL femoral neck.
text: A 22-year-old female cross-country runner has 4 weeks of groin pain and a normal radiograph. Coronal STIR shows neck marrow edema, and T1 reveals a discrete low-signal fracture line along the SUPEROLATERAL femoral neck. | A. Reassure and allow return to running as tolerated | B. Order a follow-up MRI in 6 weeks to reassess | C. Make her non-weight-bearing and arrange urgent surgical fixation | D. Begin a graded swimming program and recheck radiographs monthly | C | A superolateral (tension-side) femoral-neck fracture line is at high risk of completion and displacement with resulting AVN; it is a surgical emergency requiring non-weight-bearing and same-day orthopedic referral for percutaneous fixation. Conservative paths (A, B, D) are appropriate only for infero-medial compression-side injuries, not a tension-side line, so they are unsafe here.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 41-year-old man on chronic corticosteroids has hip pain. MRI shows a geographic antero-superior subchondral lesion with an outer low-signal rim and an inner high-signal line on T2, but the articular surface is intact and contour is preserved.
text: A 41-year-old man on chronic corticosteroids has hip pain. MRI shows a geographic antero-superior subchondral lesion with an outer low-signal rim and an inner high-signal line on T2, but the articular surface is intact and contour is preserved. | A. ARCO III — refer for arthroplasty | B. Pre-collapse AVN (ARCO I–II) — joint-preserving options such as core decompression | C. Transient osteoporosis — protected weight-bearing only | D. Subchondral insufficiency fracture — treat osteoporosis | B | The double-line sign with an intact subchondral plate and preserved contour is pre-collapse AVN — the joint-preserving window — where the femoral head can still be salvaged with treatment like core decompression. On MRI alone this is ARCO I (radiograph normal); ARCO II requires radiographic sclerosis/cysts, and both stages are pre-collapse. ARCO III (A) requires a subchondral fracture/crescent with collapse, which is absent. BMES (C) lacks a double-line and necrotic segment, and SIFK (D) shows a subchondral fracture line, not a serpiginous double-line interface.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 58-year-old woman, no major trauma, presents with limping hip pain. Pelvic MRI shows a displaced ossific fragment at the lesser trochanter, and the visualized proximal femur and pelvis contain patchy confluent low-T1 marrow that is darker than the adjacent muscle and intervertebral disc.
text: A 58-year-old woman, no major trauma, presents with limping hip pain. Pelvic MRI shows a displaced ossific fragment at the lesser trochanter, and the visualized proximal femur and pelvis contain patchy confluent low-T1 marrow that is darker than the adjacent muscle and intervertebral disc. | A. Treat as a benign iliopsoas avulsion with rest and physical therapy | B. Diagnose transient osteoporosis and start protected weight-bearing | C. Attribute findings to age-related red-marrow reconversion and reassure | D. Treat as a pathologic fracture and work up for metastatic disease | D | A lesser-trochanter avulsion in an adult without significant trauma is a classic pathologic-fracture red flag, and confluent low-T1 marrow darker than muscle/disc indicates tumor infiltration rather than normal hematopoietic marrow. This mandates a metastatic/malignancy work-up. A and C dangerously dismiss it; B describes a diffuse-edema syndrome, not an avulsion with marrow replacement.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 24-year-old soccer player has anterior hip pain reproduced by flexion-adduction-internal rotation. On standard axial MR images the alpha angle measures 48°. You suspect cam FAI clinically. What is the most appropriate next interpretive step?
text: A 24-year-old soccer player has anterior hip pain reproduced by flexion-adduction-internal rotation. On standard axial MR images the alpha angle measures 48°. You suspect cam FAI clinically. What is the most appropriate next interpretive step? | A. Report no cam morphology, since 48° is below threshold | B. Remeasure the alpha angle on oblique-axial/radial images angled along the femoral neck | C. Diagnose pincer FAI instead based on the symptoms | D. Recommend hip arthroplasty given the positive impingement test | B | The cam bump peaks anterosuperiorly (1–2 o'clock), which a straight axial under-samples and can falsely normalize. Oblique-axial/radial reformats angled along the neck capture the peak; the pathologic threshold is >55°. A is wrong because a single normal axial value does not exclude cam. C is unsupported — the symptoms fit cam, and pincer requires bony over-coverage findings. D skips conservative management and is far premature.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On a true AP pelvis radiograph the anterior acetabular rim line crosses the posterior rim line cranially, the acetabular floor touches the ilioischial line, and the lateral center-edge angle is 43°. On MRI the femoral head-neck junction is normally concave. Which diagnosis best fits?
text: On a true AP pelvis radiograph the anterior acetabular rim line crosses the posterior rim line cranially, the acetabular floor touches the ilioischial line, and the lateral center-edge angle is 43°. On MRI the femoral head-neck junction is normally concave. Which diagnosis best fits? | A. Cam-type FAI | B. Acetabular dysplasia | C. Pincer-type FAI | D. Normal hip morphology | C | Crossover sign (cranial retroversion/anterior over-coverage), coxa profunda (floor at the ilioischial line), and LCEA >40° are all over-coverage findings — pincer FAI. A is excluded because the head-neck junction is concave (no cam bump). B is wrong: dysplasia is under-coverage with LCEA <20–25°, the opposite. D is excluded by three abnormal over-coverage signs. Pincer is corrected by rim trimming with labral refixation.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: MR arthrogram in a 30-year-old runner shows contrast extending into the substance of the anterosuperior labrum with a frayed, blunted margin, plus an adjacent T2-bright lobulated fluid collection along the rim. How should you interpret these findings?
text: MR arthrogram in a 30-year-old runner shows contrast extending into the substance of the anterosuperior labrum with a frayed, blunted margin, plus an adjacent T2-bright lobulated fluid collection along the rim. How should you interpret these findings? | A. Normal sublabral recess with an incidental bursa | B. Anterosuperior labral tear, with the paralabral cyst confirming a full-thickness tear | C. Contrecoup chondral injury of pincer FAI | D. Os acetabuli with reactive marrow edema | B | Contrast entering the labral substance (not just tracking smoothly along the cartilage base) with irregular margins indicates a tear, and the anterosuperior location is the classic cam zone. The lobulated T2-bright paralabral cyst is a specific secondary sign of a full-thickness tear acting as a one-way valve. A is wrong because a recess is smooth and does not enter the labral body. C describes posteroinferior cartilage loss, not a labral tear. D is a corticated bony fragment, not a fluid collection.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 24-year-old soccer player has cam morphology and anterior groin pain. Standard MRI shows anterosuperior acetabular cartilage of normal thickness but with a thin line of fluid signal beneath it at the chondrolabral junction. What is the most likely lesion?
text: A 24-year-old soccer player has cam morphology and anterior groin pain. Standard MRI shows anterosuperior acetabular cartilage of normal thickness but with a thin line of fluid signal beneath it at the chondrolabral junction. What is the most likely lesion? | A. Chondral delamination from cam shear | B. Diffuse early osteoarthritis | C. Aggressive subchondral tumor | D. Normal cartilage, no lesion | A | A thin fluid signal undercutting normal-thickness anterosuperior cartilage at the chondrolabral junction is the classic cam-shear delamination (the "carpet lifting off the floor") and is arthroscopically treatable alongside cam osteoplasty. Early OA (B) is diffuse and circumferential, not a focal undercut flap. A tumor (C) would show marrow-replacing confluent low-T1 signal darker than muscle/disc, not a surface cleavage line. Calling it normal (D) misses an unstable flap that fails after isolated cam correction.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 38-year-old runner has hip pain. MRI shows a well-defined, rounded, T2-bright/T1-dark subchondral lesion located right at an anterosuperior cartilage defect, with no soft-tissue mass. How should you characterize and manage this?
text: A 38-year-old runner has hip pain. MRI shows a well-defined, rounded, T2-bright/T1-dark subchondral lesion located right at an anterosuperior cartilage defect, with no soft-tissue mass. How should you characterize and manage this? | A. Aggressive lytic lesion requiring biopsy | B. Benign geode confirming mechanical overload | C. Infection requiring aspiration | D. Insufficiency fracture needing protected weightbearing | B | A small, well-corticated subchondral cyst at the cartilage defect/load point is a degenerative geode that confirms a mechanical, treatable problem. The red flag for an aggressive lesion is confluent marrow signal darker than muscle/disc and away from the joint surface (A), which is absent here. There is no rim-enhancing collection or surrounding marrow edema to suggest infection (C), and a geode is a fluid-filled cyst, not a subchondral fracture line (D).

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 52-year-old has groin pain and a non-weightbearing MRI suggesting preserved joint space but diffuse cartilage thinning of both acetabular and femoral surfaces with marginal osteophytes. A surgeon is considering hip arthroscopy. What is the best next step to guide the procedure decision?
text: A 52-year-old has groin pain and a non-weightbearing MRI suggesting preserved joint space but diffuse cartilage thinning of both acetabular and femoral surfaces with marginal osteophytes. A surgeon is considering hip arthroscopy. What is the best next step to guide the procedure decision? | A. Proceed directly to arthroscopy based on the MRI | B. Order traction MR arthrography to find a focal flap | C. Obtain a weightbearing radiograph to confirm joint-space narrowing | D. Repeat the MRI with contrast in 6 weeks | C | MRI is non-weightbearing and overcalls preserved joint space, so diffuse loss with osteophytes should be confirmed on a weightbearing radiograph; established narrowing (Tönnis grade ≥2) shifts the patient toward arthroplasty and predicts arthroscopy failure, so A is wrong. Traction MRA (B) helps unmask focal delamination in equivocal focal disease, not a diffuse-OA picture. A repeat contrast MRI (D) adds nothing to the weightbearing question.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 64-year-old woman has 6 months of lateral hip pain and a limp. MRI shows a fluid-filled gap at the lateral facet of the greater trochanter with 2.5 cm retraction of the gluteus medius tendon and fatty atrophy of the muscle belly. What is the best next step?
text: A 64-year-old woman has 6 months of lateral hip pain and a limp. MRI shows a fluid-filled gap at the lateral facet of the greater trochanter with 2.5 cm retraction of the gluteus medius tendon and fatty atrophy of the muscle belly. What is the best next step? | A. Reassure and treat as routine trochanteric bursitis | B. Surgical referral for abductor tendon repair | C. Repeat MRI in 6 months to assess progression | D. Intra-articular hip corticosteroid injection | B | A full-thickness, retracted gluteus medius tear with fatty muscle atrophy is the 'rotator cuff tear of the hip' and warrants surgical referral, especially when atrophy signals chronicity. Calling it bursitis (A) misses the tendon pathology; surveillance (C) wastes the window before further atrophy worsens repair outcomes; an intra-articular injection (D) targets the wrong compartment for an extra-articular abductor tear.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 38-year-old recreational sprinter felt a pop while hurdling. MRI shows discontinuity of all three proximal hamstring tendons at the ischial tuberosity with 3 cm retraction and a fluid-filled gap. Which feature most strongly drives the decision to refer for surgery?
text: A 38-year-old recreational sprinter felt a pop while hurdling. MRI shows discontinuity of all three proximal hamstring tendons at the ischial tuberosity with 3 cm retraction and a fluid-filled gap. Which feature most strongly drives the decision to refer for surgery? | A. Presence of peri-tendinous edema | B. Intermediate T2 signal within the tendon | C. A 2–3 tendon avulsion retracted >2 cm | D. Bone marrow edema at the ischial tuberosity | C | Tendon count and retraction distance are the two surgical decision metrics: a 2–3 tendon avulsion retracted >2 cm in an active patient favors operative repair. Peri-tendinous edema (A) and intermediate intratendinous signal (B) indicate tendinopathy/partial injury without quantifying the surgical lesion; marrow edema (D) is a nonspecific reactive finding.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 22-year-old soccer player has chronic groin pain that worsens with sit-ups and kicking. A large-FOV pelvic MRI shows fluid tracking inferolaterally from the pubic symphysis along the adductor longus origin, with edema at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis. The hip joint and labrum are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
text: A 22-year-old soccer player has chronic groin pain that worsens with sit-ups and kicking. A large-FOV pelvic MRI shows fluid tracking inferolaterally from the pubic symphysis along the adductor longus origin, with edema at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis. The hip joint and labrum are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis? | A. Athletic pubalgia at the rectus-adductor aponeurosis | B. Internal snapping hip from the iliopsoas | C. Anterosuperior labral tear | D. Femoral neck stress fracture | A | The secondary cleft sign (fluid extending inferolaterally from the symphysis) plus aponeurotic edema localizes the pathology to the rectus-adductor aponeurosis — athletic pubalgia. Internal snapping (B) is a dynamic iliopsoas diagnosis, typically MRI-negative and without an aponeurotic cleft; a labral tear (C) is intra-articular with mechanical/C-sign symptoms and is reported normal here; a femoral neck stress fracture (D) would show a marrow-edema/fracture line in the neck, not this peri-symphyseal distribution.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 22-year-old female collegiate distance runner has 3 weeks of progressive groin pain and now pain at rest. Radiographs are normal. MRI shows femoral-neck marrow edema with a linear T1-hypointense fracture line abutting the superolateral cortex of the neck. What is the most appropriate next step?
text: A 22-year-old female collegiate distance runner has 3 weeks of progressive groin pain and now pain at rest. Radiographs are normal. MRI shows femoral-neck marrow edema with a linear T1-hypointense fracture line abutting the superolateral cortex of the neck. What is the most appropriate next step? | A. Make the patient non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic surgical referral | B. Continue running at reduced volume and repeat MRI in 6 weeks | C. Begin protected weight-bearing with crutches and routine follow-up in 3 months | D. Treat as routine osteitis pubis with a core-strengthening program | A | A fracture line along the superolateral (tension-side) femoral neck is unstable and prone to displacement with AVN — it is a surgical emergency requiring non-weight-bearing status and same-day ortho referral. Continued running (B) or merely protected weight-bearing with routine follow-up (C) is appropriate for compression-side or edema-only injuries, not a tension-side line. Osteitis pubis (D) is symphyseal and unrelated to a femoral-neck fracture line.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 58-year-old man with prostate cancer has new left hip pain. MRI shows diffuse low signal replacing the left femoral and acetabular marrow on T1 images; the abnormal marrow is darker than the adjacent obturator muscle and the visualized intervertebral disc. What is the most appropriate interpretation and action?
text: A 58-year-old man with prostate cancer has new left hip pain. MRI shows diffuse low signal replacing the left femoral and acetabular marrow on T1 images; the abnormal marrow is darker than the adjacent obturator muscle and the visualized intervertebral disc. What is the most appropriate interpretation and action? | A. Benign red-marrow reconversion; reassure and treat the pain symptomatically | B. Marrow replacement (metastatic disease) is likely; pursue oncologic staging/workup | C. Transient bone-marrow edema syndrome; protected weight-bearing only | D. Normal post-treatment marrow; no further action needed | B | Normal hematopoietic (red) marrow stays brighter than skeletal muscle on T1; marrow that is DARKER than muscle or disc indicates replacement and is a tumor red flag — in a prostate-cancer patient this is metastatic until proven otherwise and warrants oncologic workup. Red-marrow reconversion (A) stays brighter than muscle. Transient marrow edema (C) is bright on T2/STIR with preserved T1 fat signal, not confluent T1 replacement. Calling it normal (D) misses metastatic disease.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A 13-year-old boy with obesity reports several weeks of left groin and medial knee pain and walks with the leg externally rotated. MRI shows widening of the proximal femoral physis with metaphyseal marrow edema and slight posteromedial displacement of the capital epiphysis. What is the most appropriate management?
text: A 13-year-old boy with obesity reports several weeks of left groin and medial knee pain and walks with the leg externally rotated. MRI shows widening of the proximal femoral physis with metaphyseal marrow edema and slight posteromedial displacement of the capital epiphysis. What is the most appropriate management? | A. Outpatient physical therapy for a hip flexor strain | B. Continue sports with activity modification and recheck in 4 weeks | C. Reassure that referred knee pain indicates a benign knee problem | D. Make the patient non-weight-bearing and refer urgently for in-situ pinning | D | These findings are diagnostic of SCFE, which is urgent: continued weight-bearing risks further slippage and AVN, so the patient should be made non-weight-bearing and referred for prompt in-situ pinning. PT for a strain (A) or continued sport (B) allows progression of the slip. The referred medial-knee pain (C) is a classic SCFE presentation, not a benign knee problem, and must not anchor the workup away from the hip.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip — which for which question?
text: Large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip — which for which question? | front: Large-FOV pelvis screen vs small-FOV dedicated hip — which for which question? | back: Large-FOV screen for bilateral/referred problems (insufficiency fractures, sacroiliitis, occult marrow lesions, unclear pain source) and the contralateral hip. Small-FOV dedicated hip for intra-articular detail (labrum, cartilage, cam/pincer/FAI). A screen will underdiagnose labral/chondral disease.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which MRI plane is essential for measuring the alpha angle, and what is the cam-morphology threshold?
text: Which MRI plane is essential for measuring the alpha angle, and what is the cam-morphology threshold? | front: Which MRI plane is essential for measuring the alpha angle, and what is the cam-morphology threshold? | back: Oblique-axial along the femoral neck (or radial reformats), which lays the anterosuperior head-neck junction flat. Alpha angle >55 degrees indicates cam morphology. Never measure it on a standard true-axial slice.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you prove a bright marrow signal is benign fat rather than pathology?
text: How do you prove a bright marrow signal is benign fat rather than pathology? | front: How do you prove a bright marrow signal is benign fat rather than pathology? | back: Pair T1 with a fat-sat sequence: fat is bright on T1 and drops out (goes dark) on fat-sat/STIR. A signal that stays bright on fat-sat is not fat and must be characterized. A confluent low-T1 lesion darker than muscle/disc is a tumor red flag.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: When does MR arthrography change hip management versus non-contrast MRI?
text: When does MR arthrography change hip management versus non-contrast MRI? | front: When does MR arthrography change hip management versus non-contrast MRI? | back: Order MRA when a labral/chondral finding would push toward arthroscopy or alter the operative plan (FAI, suspected labral detachment/chondral delamination). For marrow (stress fracture, AVN, tumor), tendon/muscle, or advanced OA questions, non-contrast suffices and MRA adds a needle with no payoff.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On hip MRI, where do most pathologic labral tears and FAI rim chondral injuries occur, and why does location matter for management?
text: On hip MRI, where do most pathologic labral tears and FAI rim chondral injuries occur, and why does location matter for management? | front: On hip MRI, where do most pathologic labral tears and FAI rim chondral injuries occur, and why does location matter for management? | back: At the anterosuperior chondrolabral junction (weight-bearing zone). Anterosuperior disruption with adjacent cartilage delamination drives surgical weighting in FAI; the same-appearing cleft posteroinferiorly is usually a benign sublabral sulcus.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the zona orbicularis and what is the classic pitfall?
text: What is the zona orbicularis and what is the classic pitfall? | front: What is the zona orbicularis and what is the classic pitfall? | back: A normal circular low-signal capsular condensation that waists the femoral neck on coronal images. The pitfall is mistaking it for a capsular tear or paralabral cyst and over-referring a normal hip.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What MRI-based threshold for a proximal hamstring origin tear triggers surgical referral?
text: What MRI-based threshold for a proximal hamstring origin tear triggers surgical referral? | front: What MRI-based threshold for a proximal hamstring origin tear triggers surgical referral? | back: ≥2 of the 3 tendons (conjoint of semitendinosus + biceps femoris long head, plus semimembranosus) torn AND ≥2 cm retraction → refer to surgeon. Below that, rehab. Always report tendon count and retraction distance.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you distinguish a benign herniation pit from concerning pathology on hip MRI?
text: How do you distinguish a benign herniation pit from concerning pathology on hip MRI? | front: How do you distinguish a benign herniation pit from concerning pathology on hip MRI? | back: A herniation (fibrocystic) pit is a small, corticated, T2-bright/T1-dark cyst at the anterosuperior femoral neck — often incidental but associated with cam FAI. If symptomatic, look for cam morphology (alpha angle >55°); it is not a marrow-replacing lesion.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: In the 7-step hip/pelvis search pattern, why is bone/marrow read as step 2 rather than last?
text: In the 7-step hip/pelvis search pattern, why is bone/marrow read as step 2 rather than last? | front: In the 7-step hip/pelvis search pattern, why is bone/marrow read as step 2 rather than last? | back: The disposition-changing lesions hide in bone/marrow — tension-side neck fracture, AVN, SIFK, confluent low-T1 marrow. Reading them early (while attention is freshest) instead of at the fatigued end of the study prevents the highest-stakes miss. Use both T1 (marrow replacement) and fluid-sensitive (edema/fracture line).

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On which plane is the alpha angle measured, and what value is abnormal?
text: On which plane is the alpha angle measured, and what value is abnormal? | front: On which plane is the alpha angle measured, and what value is abnormal? | back: Oblique-axial along the femoral neck (or radial reformats); alpha angle >55 degrees is abnormal (cam morphology). A routine axial foreshortens the neck and misrepresents the bump — confirm the dedicated oblique-axial/radial images exist before reporting a number.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Name the four classic satisfaction-of-search traps on hip/pelvis MRI and the defeat tactic.
text: Name the four classic satisfaction-of-search traps on hip/pelvis MRI and the defeat tactic. | front: Name the four classic satisfaction-of-search traps on hip/pelvis MRI and the defeat tactic. | back: Tension-side (superolateral) femoral-neck stress fracture, sacral/pubic ramus insufficiency fracture, pubic symphysis (secondary cleft/athletic pubalgia), and marrow (confluent low-T1 darker than muscle/disc = tumor; adult atraumatic lesser-trochanter avulsion = pathologic-fracture red flag). Defeat with a deliberate second bone/marrow + pelvic-ring pass after the soft-tissue read.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How does the presence or absence of a subchondral fracture line change hip management?
text: How does the presence or absence of a subchondral fracture line change hip management? | front: How does the presence or absence of a subchondral fracture line change hip management? | back: No fracture line = transient bone marrow edema = rehab. A subchondral fracture line (SIFK) carries collapse risk and is urgent; a superolateral femoral-neck line is a surgical emergency. The fracture line is the hinge between conservative and surgical management.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On hip MRI, which femoral-neck stress fracture location is a surgical emergency, and what is the immediate management?
text: On hip MRI, which femoral-neck stress fracture location is a surgical emergency, and what is the immediate management? | front: On hip MRI, which femoral-neck stress fracture location is a surgical emergency, and what is the immediate management? | back: A TENSION-side (superolateral) fracture line — high risk of completion/displacement and AVN. Make the patient non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic referral for fixation. Infero-medial (compression) lines are lower risk and managed with protected weight-bearing.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What MRI finding marks the ARCO pre-collapse to post-collapse transition in femoral-head AVN, and why does it matter?
text: What MRI finding marks the ARCO pre-collapse to post-collapse transition in femoral-head AVN, and why does it matter? | front: What MRI finding marks the ARCO pre-collapse to post-collapse transition in femoral-head AVN, and why does it matter? | back: The subchondral fracture / crescent sign (ARCO II→III). Pre-collapse hips (I–II) are candidates for joint-preserving treatment (core decompression); post-collapse hips (III–IV) generally progress to arthroplasty. Always check the contralateral hip.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you distinguish transient BME syndrome (BMES) from AVN and SIFK on MRI?
text: How do you distinguish transient BME syndrome (BMES) from AVN and SIFK on MRI? | front: How do you distinguish transient BME syndrome (BMES) from AVN and SIFK on MRI? | back: BMES = diffuse homogeneous marrow edema of the head/neck with NO subchondral fracture line, NO necrotic segment, and NO double-line sign; it is self-limiting. AVN shows a geographic lesion with a double-line sign; SIFK shows a subchondral low-signal fracture line with collapse risk.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A young female athlete has a sacral ala stress fracture on MRI. What additional work-up is essential?
text: A young female athlete has a sacral ala stress fracture on MRI. What additional work-up is essential? | front: A young female athlete has a sacral ala stress fracture on MRI. What additional work-up is essential? | back: Screen for the female athlete triad / RED-S — relative energy deficiency, menstrual dysfunction, and low bone mineral density — and address bone health, since this underlies the fatigue fracture. In older patients, bilateral ala edema with the Honda/H sign signals an osteoporotic insufficiency fracture.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: In which plane should the alpha angle be measured, and what value defines pathologic cam morphology?
text: In which plane should the alpha angle be measured, and what value defines pathologic cam morphology? | front: In which plane should the alpha angle be measured, and what value defines pathologic cam morphology? | back: Measure on oblique-axial/radial images angled along the femoral neck (a straight axial under-samples the anterosuperior bump). Alpha angle >55° = pathologic cam; 50–55° borderline; <50° normal. Treat as one input alongside symptoms and a chondrolabral lesion — asymptomatic cam morphology is common.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you distinguish a normal sublabral recess from an anterosuperior labral tear on MR arthrogram?
text: How do you distinguish a normal sublabral recess from an anterosuperior labral tear on MR arthrogram? | front: How do you distinguish a normal sublabral recess from an anterosuperior labral tear on MR arthrogram? | back: A recess is a smooth-walled cleft at the labral base that parallels the cartilage and does not enter the labral body. A tear shows contrast extending into the labral substance with frayed/blunted margins and secondary signs (paralabral cyst, chondral loss, FAI morphology). Trajectory and margins decide it, not location, since sublabral sulci also occur anterosuperiorly.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What does a paralabral cyst tell you, and what should you do when you see one?
text: What does a paralabral cyst tell you, and what should you do when you see one? | front: What does a paralabral cyst tell you, and what should you do when you see one? | back: It is a specific secondary sign of an underlying full-thickness labral tear (joint fluid pumped through a one-way valve). Use it as a flag: go back and scrutinize the adjacent labrum for the tear, even if subtle, and note any mass effect on adjacent structures.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Why does rim chondral injury at the chondrolabral junction drive the FAI surgical decision?
text: Why does rim chondral injury at the chondrolabral junction drive the FAI surgical decision? | front: Why does rim chondral injury at the chondrolabral junction drive the FAI surgical decision? | back: It is the strongest imaging predictor of arthroscopic outcome. Reparable delamination/wave sign with an intact labrum favors osteochondroplasty + chondrolabral repair; diffuse full-thickness loss or Tönnis 2–3 joint-space narrowing predicts scope failure and steers toward conservative care or arthroplasty.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On MRI, how does cam-shear chondral delamination differ from full-thickness loss, and why does it matter surgically?
text: On MRI, how does cam-shear chondral delamination differ from full-thickness loss, and why does it matter surgically? | front: On MRI, how does cam-shear chondral delamination differ from full-thickness loss, and why does it matter surgically? | back: Delamination = a deep cartilage-bone cleavage (thin fluid undercutting an intact, normal-thickness anterosuperior surface); full-thickness = a focal defect down to bone. Delaminated flaps are unstable and must be addressed (debridement/fixation) along with cam osteoplasty, or isolated cam correction fails.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What MRI feature distinguishes a benign subchondral geode from a tumor red flag?
text: What MRI feature distinguishes a benign subchondral geode from a tumor red flag? | front: What MRI feature distinguishes a benign subchondral geode from a tumor red flag? | back: A geode is small, rounded, well-corticated, T2-bright/T1-dark, and sits at the cartilage defect/load point. The red flag is confluent marrow signal lower in T1 than adjacent muscle or disc (marrow replacement): work it up, don't call it OA.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What cartilage/joint-space threshold shifts a hip from arthroscopy to arthroplasty?
text: What cartilage/joint-space threshold shifts a hip from arthroscopy to arthroplasty? | front: What cartilage/joint-space threshold shifts a hip from arthroscopy to arthroplasty? | back: Diffuse circumferential cartilage loss with established joint-space narrowing (Tönnis grade ≥2, space ≤~2 mm), confirmed on a weightbearing radiograph, predicts arthroscopy failure and moves the patient into arthroplasty territory.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: When does MR arthrography with traction improve chondral detection?
text: When does MR arthrography with traction improve chondral detection? | front: When does MR arthrography with traction improve chondral detection? | back: When standard MRI is equivocal and you suspect FAI/labral or chondral pathology: traction separates the apposed surfaces so contrast fills defects and undercuts delaminated flaps. Reserve this invasive test for cases where the result changes whether you operate.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which gluteus medius/minimus tear pattern mandates surgical referral rather than conservative GTPS management?
text: Which gluteus medius/minimus tear pattern mandates surgical referral rather than conservative GTPS management? | front: Which gluteus medius/minimus tear pattern mandates surgical referral rather than conservative GTPS management? | back: A full-thickness, retracted abductor tendon tear with fatty muscle atrophy — the 'rotator cuff tear of the hip.' Tendinopathy and low-grade partial tears get PT and image-guided injection.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What two numbers must a proximal hamstring avulsion report include for surgical planning?
text: What two numbers must a proximal hamstring avulsion report include for surgical planning? | front: What two numbers must a proximal hamstring avulsion report include for surgical planning? | back: Tendon count (how many of the 3 tendons are torn) and retraction distance from the ischial tuberosity. A 2–3 tendon avulsion retracted >2 cm in an active patient favors surgical repair.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: State the universal muscle-strain grades 1–3 and the key management shift.
text: State the universal muscle-strain grades 1–3 and the key management shift. | front: State the universal muscle-strain grades 1–3 and the key management shift. | back: Grade 1 = feathery/interstitial edema without architectural fiber disruption, relative rest. Grade 2 = partial tear with fiber discontinuity at the myotendinous junction, PT. Grade 3 = complete rupture ± retraction, consider surgical referral. In adolescents the same mechanism avulses the AIIS apophysis.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How do you confirm internal snapping hip, and why is MRI often unhelpful?
text: How do you confirm internal snapping hip, and why is MRI often unhelpful? | front: How do you confirm internal snapping hip, and why is MRI often unhelpful? | back: Internal snapping (iliopsoas over the iliopectineal eminence) is a dynamic diagnosis; resting MRI is frequently normal. Confirm with dynamic ultrasound. Don't blame an incidental labral tear.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: On femoral-neck stress fracture, which side of the neck makes it a surgical emergency, and what do you do?
text: On femoral-neck stress fracture, which side of the neck makes it a surgical emergency, and what do you do? | front: On femoral-neck stress fracture, which side of the neck makes it a surgical emergency, and what do you do? | back: A fracture line on the SUPEROLATERAL (tension) side is unstable and a surgical emergency — make the patient non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent ortho referral. Compression-side (inferomedial) fractures are managed with protected weight-bearing.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: In femoral-head AVN, which MRI finding separates joint-preserving surgery from arthroplasty?
text: In femoral-head AVN, which MRI finding separates joint-preserving surgery from arthroplasty? | front: In femoral-head AVN, which MRI finding separates joint-preserving surgery from arthroplasty? | back: The subchondral fracture / crescent sign with loss of sphericity. Pre-collapse hips (intact contour, double-line sign only) can be offered core decompression; once the subchondral fracture/collapse appears, the hip trends toward arthroplasty. Check the contralateral hip — AVN is often bilateral.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the highest-yield T1 check for distinguishing benign marrow from tumor replacement in the hip?
text: What is the highest-yield T1 check for distinguishing benign marrow from tumor replacement in the hip? | front: What is the highest-yield T1 check for distinguishing benign marrow from tumor replacement in the hip? | back: Compare marrow to muscle/disc on T1. Normal red marrow is BRIGHTER than skeletal muscle; confluent marrow that is DARKER than muscle or disc is replaced/abnormal (tumor red flag) and warrants metastatic/myeloma workup. Refer soft-tissue masses (deep, >5 cm, enlarging) to a sarcoma center before any biopsy.

course_title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which MRI sign localizes athletic pubalgia, and where is the lesion?
text: Which MRI sign localizes athletic pubalgia, and where is the lesion? | front: Which MRI sign localizes athletic pubalgia, and where is the lesion? | back: The secondary cleft sign — fluid/contrast tracking inferolaterally from the pubic symphysis — localizes a microtear of the rectus abdominis–adductor longus aponeurosis at the pubic attachment. The hip joint typically looks normal; a dedicated symphysis protocol is needed. Most cases respond to a core/adductor rehab program.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
text: A primary care sports medicine elbow MRI course focused on management-changing interpretation: the thrower's elbow (UCL valgus overload, capitellar OCD, ulnar neuritis, olecranon stress), the LCL complex and posterolateral rotatory instability, the coronoid/anteromedial-facet axis, the distal biceps and triceps, lateral and medial epicondylitis, the three nerves, and don't-miss findings. | Primary care sports medicine fellows

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Verify Protocol & Clinical Question
text: Confirm side, protocol, and the planes/sequences you actually have, then let the symptom location steer the read before you narrow your attention. | Confirm correct patient, side, and any comparison / contralateral imaging | Identify protocol: non-contrast vs MR arthrogram; is a FABS view present for the distal biceps? | Inventory planes and sequences (axial, coronal, sagittal, FABS; T1, PD/T2 FS, STIR) and note artifact | State the exact question: UCL vs medial epicondylitis vs ulnar nerve vs OCD vs distal biceps vs post-trauma instability | Map the symptom: medial (valgus triad), lateral (epicondylitis / LCL / PLRI), anterior (biceps), posterior (triceps / olecranon / valgus-extension overload), or mechanical (loose body / plica) | Coronal answers the UCL, the epicondyle origins, and the radiocapitellar (OCD) face; axial answers the nerves; sagittal answers loose bodies and the anterior-to-posterior extent of an OCD. Confirm a finding in ≥2 planes. | MR arthrography earns its place only when it will change the decision — confirming a partial undersurface UCL tear (T-sign), judging OCD stability, or counting loose bodies. Non-contrast 3T answers most cases.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Bones, Marrow & Osteochondral
text: Sweep the radial head, capitellum, trochlea, olecranon, and coronoid first — T1 for marrow replacement, fluid-sensitive for edema and fracture lines. Tie every edema signal to a line, physis, cyst, or fragment. | Radial head, capitellum, trochlea, olecranon, coronoid: marrow edema tied to a fracture line, physis, cyst, or fragment | Capitellar OCD vs Panner; judge OCD stability (fluid undercutting the fragment, sizeable or multiple subchondral cysts, cartilage breach, displaced fragment) — read on CORONAL + SAGITTAL together | Thrower stress: medial epicondyle apophysis (Little League elbow), olecranon stress reaction vs fracture / persistent physis, posteromedial olecranon (valgus-extension-overload) osteophyte | Occult fracture (posterior fat-pad sign; lateral condyle in children); use CRITOE and the radiocapitellar line | Sweep the coronoid + olecranon fossae and the radiocapitellar recess for loose bodies; in a non-thrower, consider primary OA as the source | Stability changes management in capitellar OCD: a T2-bright line undercutting the fragment, a sizeable/multiple subchondral cysts, a breached cartilage surface, or a displaced fragment/loose body all argue unstable → surgical referral. (The 5 mm cyst figure is a knee-derived criterion, not validated for the capitellum — teach 'sizeable or multiple.') | Panner disease is the younger child (≈5–10 yr), is reversible, and spares the surface; OCD is the older adolescent thrower/gymnast with a discrete fragment that can become unstable.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Medial Ligament — UCL & Valgus Overload
text: Trace the anterior bundle of the UCL from the medial epicondyle to the sublime tubercle, then read it together with the flexor-pronator origin and the ulnar nerve — the medial 'valgus triad.' | Anterior bundle from the medial epicondyle to the sublime tubercle on coronal | Partial undersurface tear (T-sign: fluid tracking beyond the articular-cartilage edge at the distal attachment) vs full-thickness; record location proximal / mid / distal | Chronic attenuation, thickening, or ossification; sublime-tubercle marrow edema or a traction spur | Link to the flexor-pronator origin and the ulnar nerve — valgus overload injures all three together | The T-sign is the partial undersurface tear — fluid/contrast undercuts the most distal fibers at the sublime tubercle. A couple of millimetres of undercutting can be a normal recess; MR arthrography is more sensitive than non-contrast for this. | In a thrower, every medial-pain read is UCL + flexor-pronator origin + ulnar nerve. A concomitant UCL tear shifts a 'medial epicondylitis' diagnosis toward UCL reconstruction.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Lateral Ligament — LCL Complex, PLRI & Coronoid
text: Trace the lateral ulnar collateral ligament and the radial collateral / annular ligaments, then read the coronoid and radial head as a post-traumatic circle. Comment on LUCL integrity in EVERY post-dislocation or post-lateral-surgery elbow. | LUCL (epicondyle → posterior to the radial head → supinator crest), RCL, and annular ligament; trace on coronal + axial | Humeral-origin avulsion and the undersurface tear deep to the common extensor; secondary PLRI signs (posterolateral radial subluxation, radiocapitellar incongruity) | Coronoid: the anteromedial-facet fracture (small but unstable → varus posteromedial rotatory instability) and Regan–Morrey height; the terrible-triad pattern | Post-dislocation Osborne–Cotterill posterolateral capitellar impaction and heterotopic ossification | A deep/full-thickness common-extensor tear that undercuts toward the radiocapitellar joint and involves the LUCL implies posterolateral rotatory instability → this is a ligament repair/reconstruction problem, not just tendinosis (and a known complication of prior lateral steroid injection or release). | Small does not mean stable at the coronoid: the anteromedial-facet fracture implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability and is surgical; the terrible triad (dislocation + radial head + coronoid) hinges on the coronoid and the LCL.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tendons — Biceps, Triceps & Epicondyle Origins
text: Grade the distal biceps and triceps for partial vs complete, and the common flexor and extensor origins for tendinosis vs tear — always tracing the adjacent ligament (UCL with medial, LUCL with lateral). | Distal biceps (best on FABS): partial vs complete, retraction in cm, lacertus fibrosus intact vs torn; bicipitoradial bursitis as a clue | Triceps: partial (deep-fiber, report % width) vs complete avulsion ± an olecranon flake fragment | Lateral (ECRB) epicondylitis grade — trace the LUCL to the supinator crest; medial (flexor-pronator) epicondylitis grade — trace the UCL | Apply a grade 1 / 2 / 3 muscle-strain framework to flexor-pronator and extensor strains | An empty radial tuberosity with retraction is a complete distal biceps tear — but a complete tear with an INTACT lacertus fibrosus may NOT retract and is the classic mimic; comment on the lacertus, because it informs the surgical approach. Repair timing is a guideline (earlier is technically easier), not a hard deadline. | Don't undercall the triceps: a complete rupture is surgical, and a high-grade partial (commonly >50% width, or any tear with extension weakness against resistance) is repaired.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Nerves — Ulnar, Median / AIN, Radial / PIN
text: Read the three nerves by caliber + signal against an internal control, and let muscle denervation be the localizing secondary sign. Anchor the diagnosis to a structural change, not signal alone. | Ulnar nerve in the cubital tunnel: caliber/signal vs the contralateral side and the ipsilateral median nerve; flexion subluxation; anconeus epitrochlearis; snapping triceps | Median / AIN: isolated pronator-quadratus / FPL / FDP (index–middle) denervation — consider Parsonage–Turner before calling mechanical compression | Radial / PIN at the arcade of Frohse: extensor-compartment denervation (supinator variably spared); radial tunnel = pain with a normal MRI | Use muscle denervation edema (subacute) and fatty atrophy (chronic) as the localizing sign; hunt a mass/ganglion as the cause | A structural cause changes the operation: dynamic ulnar-nerve subluxation, an anconeus epitrochlearis, or snapping triceps favor transposition (and the triceps must be addressed) over in-situ decompression. | An AIN palsy is frequently neuralgic amyotrophy (Parsonage–Turner), not mechanical compression → usually no surgery; pivot to EMG/observation. Denervation shows no MRI change for the first ~2–4 weeks, then T2/STIR edema, then chronic T1 fatty atrophy.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cartilage, Joint, Capsule, Bursa & Synovium
text: Trace the radiocapitellar and ulnotrochlear cartilage, characterize effusion/synovitis, and clear the dependent recesses for loose bodies — watching for the plica that mimics them. | Radiocapitellar and ulnotrochlear cartilage; effusion and synovitis | Radiocapitellar plica / posterolateral synovial fold (pathologic when thickened beyond ~3 mm with mechanical symptoms) as a mimic of a loose body or OCD | Olecranon bursitis — aseptic vs septic (aspirate the warm bursa; MRI cannot exclude infection) | Loose bodies in the dependent recesses; primary OA osteophytes at the coronoid and olecranon tips | A red/warm/fluctuant olecranon bursa needs aspiration, not just imaging — MRI cannot reliably exclude a septic bursa. | The radiocapitellar plica is a real mechanical cause of lateral catching, but it is also a loose-body/OCD mimic — call it pathologic only when it's thickened (>~3 mm) with concordant mechanical symptoms.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Masses, Management Review & Red Flags
text: Before you sign off, hunt a mass as the hidden cause, exclude the infection/tumor red flags, and name the single management-changing finding. | Masses / ganglia (often the real entrapment cause); a distended bicipitoradial bursa compressing the PIN | Confluent low-T1 marrow replacement, cortical destruction, or a soft-tissue mass: infection / tumor red flags | Confirm any surgically urgent injury (complete biceps / triceps / UCL / LUCL avulsion, anteromedial coronoid facet, an instability pattern) | Summarize the single management-changing finding: rehab/injection, surgical referral, or urgent escalation | When a nerve looks denervated without an obvious tunnel cause, look one more time for a ganglion or mass — it is frequently the surgically treatable cause. | End every read with the disposition, not just the finding: what does this change — continue rehab, inject, refer, or escalate urgently?

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Elbow MRI Orientation
text: Protocols, planes, and sequences — and translating the clinical question into a focused, complete search | Plane-to-structure map: axial = nerves/origins/biceps footprint, coronal = UCL/LCL/epicondyles/radiocapitellar, sagittal = fossae/triceps | FABS positioning: laying the distal biceps footprint flat for partial-vs-complete grading and retraction | MR arthrography vs non-contrast: the T-sign, loose bodies, and when contrast changes the operation | Sequences: fluid-sensitive fat-sat for edema/tears vs T1 for marrow/anatomy; confirm in ≥2 planes | Normal-variant pitfalls: the capitellar pseudodefect, transverse trochlear ridge, and distal-UCL synovial recess | Assign each imaging plane (axial, coronal, sagittal, FABS) to the structures it best shows, and state why capitellar OCD must be read on coronal and sagittal together rather than sagittal alone | Choose the right protocol — routine non-contrast elbow, FABS positioning, or MR arthrography — and recognize the specific clinical questions (distal biceps grading, the UCL T-sign, loose bodies, OCD stability) that justify each | Match fluid-sensitive fat-sat and T1 sequences to the tissue question they answer, and confirm every finding in at least two planes | Translate the clinical question (UCL vs medial epicondylitis vs ulnar nerve vs OCD vs distal biceps) into a focused but complete search, and avoid over-calling the capitellar pseudodefect, transverse trochlear ridge, and the normal distal-UCL synovial recess

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Staging a capitellar OCD off the sagittal plane alone (or off a single plane)
text: Read capitellar OCD on coronal AND sagittal together — the coronal face shows the radiocapitellar contact zone where the lesion lives, while the sagittal defines its anterior-to-posterior extent and contour. Confirm any osteochondral finding in ≥2 planes before calling it; the course now has coronal, axial, and sagittal elbow stacks, but dedicated arthrographic/FABS views are still separate protocol decisions.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reflexively ordering MR arthrography for every elbow, or treating an elbow 'ABER'/valgus-applied sequence as a validated routine like the shoulder
text: Non-contrast 3T MRI answers most elbow questions (tendons, OCD, nerves, occult fracture, loose bodies). Add direct MR arthrography specifically when a UCL undersurface (T-sign) tear is suspected but non-contrast is equivocal, or when loose-body count/OCD stability will change the operation. A valgus-applied or flexed positioning adjunct can open the medial joint, but the elbow evidence base is thin compared with the shoulder ABER — present it as an adjunct, not a routine sequence.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling the capitellar pseudodefect, the transverse trochlear ridge, or a small distal-UCL synovial recess a lesion
text: The pseudodefect of the capitellum is a normal posterolateral/postero-inferior bare-area groove at the capitellum–lateral-epicondyle junction — not an osteochondral lesion; the transverse trochlear ridge/groove is likewise a normal contour. A small synovial recess near the distal UCL is normal — the abnormal T-sign threshold is fluid tracking BEYOND the articular-cartilage edge, not any sliver of fluid at the attachment.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Plane-to-structure map: axial = nerves/origins/biceps footprint, coronal = UCL/LCL/epicondyles/radiocapitellar, sagittal = fossae/triceps
text:
### Plane-to-Structure Map: Axial, Coronal, Sagittal

The first orientation skill is knowing **which plane answers which question** — because most elbow structures have one plane that displays them in profile and others that average through them.

| Plane | Best for | The clinical question it answers |
|---|---|---|
| **Axial** | Nerves (ulnar in the cubital tunnel, radial/PIN at the radiocapitellar level), common flexor/extensor origins in cross-section, the **annular ligament** around the radial neck, the distal biceps footprint | "Is there ulnar neuritis or flexion subluxation?" "Where is the entrapment?" |
| **Coronal** | The **UCL anterior bundle** to the sublime tubercle, the RCL/LUCL complex, the common flexor and extensor origins, and the **radiocapitellar contact zone** | "Is there a UCL tear (the T-sign), epicondylitis, or an LCL-origin tear?" |
| **Sagittal** | Olecranon/coronoid **fossae and the radiocapitellar recess** for loose bodies, the olecranon/triceps insertion, the anterior/posterior fat pads | "Is there a loose body or a triceps tear?" |

**Capitellar OCD is the key cross-plane rule:** read it on **coronal + sagittal together**, not sagittal alone. The coronal shows the lesion at the radiocapitellar surface where it lives; the sagittal defines its anterior-to-posterior extent and contour. A lesion convincing on one plane but absent on the orthogonal plane is more likely a partial-volume artifact than a real defect.

**Course-image note:** the normal elbow workstation now includes coronal, axial, and sagittal stacks, so the course can teach the plane map visually. Dedicated **FABS** views for distal-biceps footprint grading and **MR arthrography** for subtle UCL undersurface tears or equivocal OCD stability remain protocol choices that are not represented by the normal stacks. | Each elbow plane owns a question: axial for nerves and the biceps footprint, coronal for the UCL and the radiocapitellar OCD face, sagittal for loose bodies and the triceps — and capitellar OCD must be read on coronal AND sagittal together, never sagittal alone.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: FABS positioning: laying the distal biceps footprint flat for partial-vs-complete grading and retraction
text:
### FABS Positioning: Profiling the Distal Biceps Footprint

The distal biceps tendon runs **obliquely** to its radial-tuberosity footprint, so on standard planes it is foreshortened and partial-volumed — you cannot reliably grade a partial tear or measure retraction. **FABS** solves this.

**FABS = Flexed elbow, ABducted shoulder, forearm Supinated**, with the patient **prone** and the arm overhead. This position lays the distal biceps tendon **flat and in-plane all the way to its radial-tuberosity footprint**, so you can:
- Distinguish **partial** (some fibers still reaching the tuberosity) from **complete** (full discontinuity) tears
- **Measure retraction** — where the stump sits, in cm
- Assess the **bifurcated footprint** (short head distal/anterior, long head proximal/posterior)

**Order FABS when the question is the distal biceps** — a middle-aged patient with a flexion-loading injury, antecubital ecchymosis, weak supination, or a positive hook test. The two management-changing variables FABS lets you report are **partial-vs-complete** and **retraction**, the features that drive repair urgency. (The lacertus-fibrosus status, the other key variable, is read on axial.)

**Pitfall to avoid:** FABS is for the **distal biceps**, not the UCL — don't confuse the two when selecting positioning. | When the distal biceps is the question, add the FABS view (Flexed elbow, ABducted shoulder, Supinated forearm, prone) — it lays the tendon flat to its radial-tuberosity footprint so you can grade partial-vs-complete and measure retraction, which standard planes foreshorten.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: MR arthrography vs non-contrast: the T-sign, loose bodies, and when contrast changes the operation
text:
### MR Arthrography vs Non-Contrast: When Contrast Changes the Operation

**Non-contrast 3T MRI is the default** and answers most elbow questions — tendons, OCD, nerves, occult fracture, loose bodies. **Direct MR arthrography** (intra-articular dilute gadolinium) earns its needle only when the added detail will change a decision.

**MR arthrography is the most sensitive study for:**
- The **partial undersurface UCL tear — the T-sign**: intra-articular contrast undercuts the deep fibers at the sublime tubercle, making an articular-sided partial tear conspicuous that non-contrast MRI undercalls. This is the classic throwing injury.
- **Intra-articular loose bodies and chondral delamination/flaps**: contrast outlines bodies and tracks under a delaminated flap.

**Add it when:**
- A thrower's exam says UCL but **non-contrast MRI is equivocal**
- **OCD stability** will change the surgical decision
- You need to **count loose bodies** pre-op (CT/CT arthrography is also strong for ossified bodies)

**The shoulder-ABER caveat (don't over-promise):** an abduction/valgus-applied or flexed positioning adjunct *can* open the medial joint to improve undersurface-tear conspicuity, but the **elbow evidence base is thin** compared with the shoulder ABER. Present it as an **adjunct, not a validated routine sequence**.

**Course-image caveat:** the local normal stacks are non-arthrographic, so the T-sign is taught as a concept with the explicit reminder that **MR arthrography is more sensitive** when the clinical question is a subtle undersurface UCL tear. | Reach for MR arthrography only when it changes the operation — confirming the partial undersurface UCL T-sign, detecting/counting loose bodies, or settling OCD stability when non-contrast is equivocal; non-contrast 3T answers most elbow cases, and an elbow valgus/ABER adjunct is not a validated routine.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sequences: fluid-sensitive fat-sat for edema/tears vs T1 for marrow/anatomy; confirm in ≥2 planes
text:
### Sequences: Fluid-Sensitive Fat-Sat vs T1 — and Confirming in ≥2 Planes

Match the **sequence to the tissue question**, exactly as in the other joints.

| Sequence | Reads | Use it for |
|---|---|---|
| **Fluid-sensitive fat-sat** (PD/T2 FS or **STIR**) | **Fluid + edema** | Tendon/ligament tears, the **OCD fluid rim**, marrow injury/stress, effusions, and **subacute muscle denervation edema** |
| **T1** | **Marrow, fat, anatomy** | Marrow **replacement** (confluent low-T1 below muscle = red flag), arthrogram contrast, occult fracture lines, chronic **fatty muscle atrophy** |

Think **T1 = anatomy/marrow, fluid-sensitive = pathology/edema.** A fracture line is a low-signal line on T1 with **fat-suppressed-bright** marrow edema around it; a bone *bruise* shows edema **without** a line.

**The orientation discipline: confirm every finding in at least two planes.** A signal abnormality on one plane that disappears on the orthogonal plane is usually **partial-volume averaging or a normal contour**, not pathology. This is why the OCD rule (coronal + sagittal) and the LUCL rule (coronal + axial) exist — partial-volume averaging is the single most common reason a real lesion is missed *or* a normal variant is over-called.

**Available in the normal workstation:** **coronal T2-FS**, **axial T2-FS**, and **sagittal IR/STIR** stacks. They are enough to teach orientation and two-plane confirmation, while T1, MR arthrography, and FABS remain protocol-specific additions when the clinical question requires them. | Fluid-sensitive fat-sat shows the pathology (tears, edema, the OCD rim) and T1 shows the anatomy and the marrow red flag — and a finding seen on only one plane is partial-volume artifact until you confirm it on a second.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Normal-variant pitfalls: the capitellar pseudodefect, transverse trochlear ridge, and distal-UCL synovial recess
text:
### Normal-Variant Pitfalls — Don't Call Them Lesions

Three normal findings are routinely over-called at the elbow. Recognizing them is part of orientation, because each lives exactly where a real lesion would and each sends a normal elbow toward unnecessary imaging or referral.

| Variant | Location | How to call it normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Pseudodefect of the capitellum** | **Posterolateral / postero-inferior** bare-area groove at the capitellum–lateral-epicondyle junction | A normal contour notch where the articular cartilage ends posteriorly — **not** an osteochondral lesion. True capitellar OCD is **anterolateral/central** (the radiocapitellar contact zone), with a fluid rim, cyst, or fragment |
| **Transverse trochlear ridge / trochlear groove** | Trochlea | A normal bony ridge/groove in the trochlear contour — not an osteochondral defect |
| **Normal distal-UCL synovial recess** | Just distal to the UCL attachment at the sublime tubercle | A small synovial recess can normally exist; the abnormal **T-sign** threshold is fluid/contrast tracking **beyond the articular-cartilage edge**, not a couple of millimeters undercutting the most distal fibers |

**The location lesson:** for capitellar OCD, the real lesion is **anterolateral/central** and the mimic (pseudodefect) is **posterolateral** — location separates variant from pathology. For the UCL, a normal recess sits at the attachment but a true partial tear has contrast extending **past the cartilage margin**. Anchor each call to **location + a corroborating sign** (fluid rim, cyst, fragment, contrast beyond the cartilage edge), not to signal alone. | The capitellar pseudodefect is a posterolateral bare-area groove — true OCD is anterolateral/central — and a small distal-UCL synovial recess is normal unless fluid tracks beyond the articular-cartilage edge; location plus a corroborating sign, not signal alone, separates variant from lesion.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Elbow Search Pattern
text: A repeatable bones-first read that defeats satisfaction-of-search across the thrower's, the tennis, and the post-trauma elbow | Why a fixed, bones-first order beats lesion-spotting | The eight-step elbow search pattern | Satisfaction-of-search traps: the epicondylitis that hides a ligament | Linked-structure habits: the medial valgus triad and the post-trauma O'Driscoll circle | Sweeping for loose bodies and the plica mimic, then writing a management-grade impression | Apply a reproducible eight-step elbow MRI search pattern that reads context and bones/marrow before ligaments, tendons, and nerves | Explain how satisfaction-of-search produces the classic elbow misses (a deep common-extensor/LUCL tear behind lateral epicondylitis; the UCL and ulnar nerve behind medial epicondylitis) and adopt a habit that defeats it | Read medial pain as the valgus triad (UCL + flexor-pronator + ulnar nerve) and the post-dislocation elbow as the O'Driscoll circle (LCL/LUCL, coronoid including the anteromedial facet, radial head, Osborne-Cotterill, heterotopic ossification) | Produce a structured impression that names the structure, the grade/severity, and the management consequence

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling lateral epicondylitis (or medial epicondylitis) and stopping the read
text: The symptomatic tendinosis is rarely the management-changing lesion. Trace the LUCL to the supinator crest beneath every common-extensor tendinosis (a deep undersurface tear there means PLRI), and trace the UCL to the sublime tubercle plus interrogate the ulnar nerve beneath every common-flexor tendinosis. Finish all eight steps every time.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Dismissing a small coronoid fragment in a post-dislocation elbow because it looks small on radiographs
text: Small does not mean stable at the coronoid. An anteromedial-facet fracture is highly unstable, implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability, and is surgical; untreated it drives early arthrosis. In any dislocation/terrible-triad elbow, close the O'Driscoll circle — LCL/LUCL, coronoid, radial head, Osborne-Cotterill impaction, and heterotopic ossification.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reading tendons and ligaments first and leaving bones/marrow for the end
text: Read context and bones/marrow as steps 1-2 while attention is freshest. The high-consequence osseous lesions (unstable OCD, olecranon stress fracture, occult radial-head fracture, the anteromedial coronoid facet, a marrow-replacing lesion) are exactly what gets lost to fatigue and satisfaction-of-search once an obvious tendinopathy is found.

course_title: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Why a fixed, bones-first order beats lesion-spotting
text:
### Why a Fixed, Bones-First Order Beats Lesion-Spotting

The natural instinct is to **chase the obvious finding** — the thickened, bright common-extensor origin in a tennis player, the fluid-filled olecranon bursa — and then quit. That is **satisfaction of search**, and at the elbow it is precisely how the management-changing lesion gets missed: the eye that locks onto lateral epicondylitis glides right past the **deep common-extensor/LUCL tear** sitting underneath it, and the elbow that should have gone to a ligament surgeon goes to rehab instead.

A reproducible order forces you to **visit every compartment regardless of where your attention is pulled**, so the second — often more dangerous — finding still gets seen.

**Why it works:**
- **Coverage, not recall** — you check a structure because it is next on the list, not because you happened to remember it.
